# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1: Define the term "component crash test" and state TWO examples. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Model Answer:**

A **component crash test** (also called a *subsystem-level* or *part-level* test) is a standardized, laboratory-based destructive evaluation performed on an individual safety-relevant automotive part — independently of a complete vehicle — to verify compliance with regulatory injury criteria, structural integrity limits, and functional performance thresholds. The test typically uses a dedicated fixture, calibrated loading apparatus, and biomechanical criteria (HIC, Nij, Fz) defined in regulations such as **ECE R16, FMVSS 209, ECE R94**, etc.

**Two Examples:**
1. **Seat belt webbing tensile test (ECE R16 §6.1):** A 100 mm specimen is pulled at 100 mm/min until failure; breaking force must exceed **15 kN**.
2. **Steering wheel headform impact test (FMVSS 203):** A 6.8 kg headform is impacted at **24.1 km/h** into the steering wheel hub; resulting **HIC must be ≤ 1000**.

---

### Question 2: What is HIC? State its formula and threshold value. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

**HIC (Head Injury Criterion)** is a biomechanical index that quantifies the likelihood of skull fracture or brain injury (concussion, hematoma) based on the headform acceleration-time history. It was developed at **NHTSA** and adopted by FMVSS and ECE regulations.

**Formula:**

$$
HIC_{15} = \max_{t_1,t_2}\left[\left(\frac{1}{t_2-t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt\right)^{2.5} \cdot (t_2-t_1)\right]
$$

subject to $t_2 - t_1 \leq 15\,\text{ms}$.

**Threshold (Pass/Fail):**

$$
HIC_{15} \leq 1000
$$

The acceleration must be filtered using **SAE J211 CFC 60** (4-pole Butterworth, fc = 1650 Hz). HIC < 1000 corresponds to ~5% probability of severe head injury (AIS ≥ 4) per the Mertz-Weber biomechanical data.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** "Explain the tier structure of automotive crashworthiness tests with a focus on component-level evaluation of safety belts, airbags, and seats. Justify why component tests are essential in the type approval cycle." [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

### Model Answer:

**(a) Tier Structure of Crashworthiness Tests — 7 marks**

Automotive crashworthiness testing is organized in **three hierarchical tiers**, each with increasing integration, cost, and complexity:

**Tier 1 — Component Test (Sub-system level)**
- Tests an individual safety component in isolation.
- Uses dedicated fixtures, often a hydraulic or pendulum apparatus.
- Specimens: 3–5 units; cost: **$1K – $50K** per piece.
- Examples: seat belt webbing tensile test (ECE R16), headrest rear moment (FMVSS 202), steering column collapse (ECE R12), airbag inflator tank test (USCAR-24), legform impactor (ECE R127).
- Outcome: Validates the *material*, *geometry*, and *mechanism* of a single part.

**Tier 2 — Sled / Subsystem Test**
- Tests a complete *occupant envelope* — usually a full seat assembly with belts and an ATD (Hybrid III 50th percentile) — on a linear accelerator (sled).
- Cost: **$50K – $200K** per run.
- Examples: ECE R94 §7 sled, FMVSS 213 child seat sled.
- Outcome: Validates the *interaction* of belt + airbag + seat under realistic deceleration.

**Tier 3 — Full Vehicle Test**
- Tests the complete car against a barrier or mobile deformable barrier (MDB).
- Cost: **$200K – $1M+** per test.
- Examples: ECE R94 frontal barrier (56 km/h, 100% overlap), ECE R95 side MDB (50 km/h), Euro NCAP Mobile Progressive Deformable Barrier (MPDB, 50 km/h, 50% overlap), FMVSS 301 rear impact.
- Outcome: Validates the *integrated vehicle*.

The **pyramidal logic** is: a Tier 1 failure is cheaper to fix than a Tier 2 failure, which is cheaper than a Tier 3 failure.

**(b) Component-Level Evaluation of Safety Belts, Airbags, and Seats — 7 marks**

**Safety Belt Component Tests (ECE R16 / FMVSS 209):**
1. **Webbing tensile test:** 100 mm specimen pulled at 100 mm/min; breaking force **≥ 15 kN** (ECE) or **≥ 22.2 kN** (FMVSS).
2. **Retractor locking test:** Webbing acceleration ≥ 0.7 g must lock the retractor within 30 mm of payout.
3. **Buckle latch test:** Insertion force ≤ 40 N, release force ≤ 20 N; buckle must not release under a 5 kN lateral load.
4. **Belt anchor test (ECE R14):** 22.2 kN load at 75° must not break the anchor.

**Airbag Component Tests (ECE R94 + USCAR-24):**
1. **Inflator tank test (ballistic):** Propellant fired into sealed tank; P_peak < 50 MPa, no fragments > 2 mm.
2. **Aged propellant test:** 500 hours at 95% RH, 85 °C; P-t curve must match master within ±15%.
3. **Module drop test:** 1.5 m drop onto steel anvil; clean deployment, no hazardous fragments.
4. **Module vibration test:** 5 g sine sweep, 5–200 Hz, 4 hours per axis; no functional degradation.

**Seat Component Tests (ECE R17 / FMVSS 207):**
1. **Seat strength test:** 36 kg block at 20 g forward on seat CG; no structural failure.
2. **Headrest test (FMVSS 202):** 373 N·m rearward moment via 6.8 kg headform; no break.
3. **Seat cushion drop test:** 400 mm drop on rigid floor; foam compression set < 15% after 24 h.
4. **ISOFIX lower anchor test (ECE R14):** 8 kN forward and rearward; no anchor break.

**Justification — Why Component Tests Are Essential in Type Approval:**

1. **Cost efficiency:** A component test (~$10K) is **10×–100× cheaper** than a full-vehicle test.
2. **Root-cause isolation:** Failure modes (e.g., Takata inflator rupture) can be traced to a specific part.
3. **Statistical robustness:** Multiple specimens (n ≥ 3) provide Weibull distribution reliability data.
4. **Regulatory gatekeeping:** Type Approval Authorities (TÜV, IDIADA, ARAI) require component-level test reports (E-mark, AIS-mark) before issuing whole-vehicle approval.
5. **Faster design iteration:** Engineers can test multiple design variants in days rather than months.
6. **Legal defensibility:** Component test reports constitute *due diligence* in product liability litigation.
7. **Tier 1 supplier accountability:** Suppliers (Autoliv, ZF, Bosch) must submit component test certificates with every shipment.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** "Describe in detail the component-level tests for steering systems and pedestrian protection. Include governing standards, test apparatus, key thresholds, and injury criteria. Use appropriate mathematical equations." [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

### Model Answer:

**(a) Steering System Component Tests — 7 marks**

Steering system crashes cause severe thoracic and head injuries when the driver's chest impacts the steering wheel hub. Component tests validate the column's collapse mechanism and the wheel's energy-absorption capacity.

**Standards:** FMVSS 203, FMVSS 204, ECE R12, AIS-005 (India), GB 11557 (China).

**Test 1: Headform Impact Test (FMVSS 203 / ECE R12 §5.1)**
- **Apparatus:** A **6.8 kg** aluminum headform mounted on a pendulum or linear impactor.
- **Impact velocity:** **v = 24.1 km/h = 6.69 m/s**.
- **Impact energy:** $E = \frac{1}{2} \times 6.8 \times 6.69^2 \approx 152$ J.
- **Threshold:** Resulting **HIC₁₅ ≤ 1000**.
- **CFC 60 filter** applied to headform accelerometer data.
- A driver airbag module is *typically* present in modern cars; this test validates the wheel *without* airbag firing (worst-case belt-only scenario).

**Test 2: Column Rearward Displacement (FMVSS 204)**
- **Apparatus:** A body-block on a sled at 80 g longitudinal deceleration.
- **Threshold:** Steering column must retract rearward by **≤ 127 mm (5 inches)**.
- **Why:** Prevents the upper steering shaft from intruding into the driver's chest in a frontal impact.

**Test 3: Column Collapse (ECE R12 §5.3)**
- A **40 kg** mass strikes the steering wheel at **6.69 m/s**.
- **Energy:** $E = \frac{1}{2} \times 40 \times 6.69^2 \approx 895$ J.
- **Column force limit:** $F_{peak} \leq 11$ kN.
- **Mechanism:** Two-stage telescopic joint with **pin-shear** design yielding at 11 kN; supplemented by **EPP foam** energy absorber.

**Test 4: Wheel Rim Push-Off (ECE R12 §5.4)**
- **Force:** **200 N** applied radially at 75 mm from the rim.
- **Deflection threshold:** **≥ 50 mm** without rim detachment.

**Test 5: Steering Wheel Energy Absorber Compression**
- Quasi-static compression at 5 mm/min; deflection at **F = 1 kN** must be **> 30 mm**.

**(b) Pedestrian Protection Component Tests — 7 marks**

Pedestrian protection tests evaluate the *front-end* of the vehicle (bonnet, bumper, fender, windshield base) to minimize injuries to vulnerable road users.

**Standards:** ECE R127 (Europe), AIS-100 (India), GTR 9 (Global), Euro NCAP AEB Pedestrian.

**Test 1: Adult Headform Impact (ECE R127 §5.2)**
- **Impactor:** **aPLI (Advanced Pedestrian Legform Impactor)** headform, **m = 4.5 kg**, diameter 165 mm.
- **Impact velocity:** **v = 11.1 m/s = 40 km/h**.
- **Impact energy:** $E = \frac{1}{2} \times 4.5 \times 11.1^2 \approx 277$ J.
- **Threshold:** **HIC₁₅ ≤ 1000** at each impact zone.
- **Wrap distance exemption:** Windscreen contact area > 1700 mm wrap distance is exempted *only if* HIC < 1000.

**Test 2: Child Headform Impact (ECE R127 §5.2)**
- **Impactor:** **cPLI (Child Pedestrian Legform Impactor)** headform, **m = 3.5 kg**, diameter 130 mm.
- **Impact velocity:** **v = 11.1 m/s**.
- **Threshold:** **HIC₁₅ ≤ 1000**.

**Test 3: Legform Impact — Upper Legform (Flex-PLI)**
- **Impactor mass:** **9.5 kg** (with femur, knee, tibia, foot).
- **Impact velocity:** **v = 11.1 m/s**.
- **Bending moment on knee:** $M_{bend} \leq 340$ Nm.
- **Upper tibia acceleration:** $a \leq 200\,g$.
- **Knee shear displacement:** $\delta \leq 6$ mm.

**Test 4: Legform Impact — Lower Legform (TRL legform)**
- Measures tibia bending moment, compressive force, and ankle shear.
- **Bending moment threshold:** $M \leq 340$ Nm.
- **Compressive force:** $F_c \leq 4$ kN.

**Test 5: Bonnet Hinge Clearance Test**
- A **2 kN** vertical load is applied to the trailing edge of the bonnet.
- Hinge must yield to provide **≥ 100 mm** additional clearance for the head.

**Injury Criteria Summary Table:**

| Impactor | Mass | Velocity | Injury Metric | Threshold |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Adult headform | 4.5 kg | 11.1 m/s | HIC₁₅ | ≤ 1000 |
| Child headform | 3.5 kg | 11.1 m/s | HIC₁₅ | ≤ 1000 |
| Upper legform (Flex-PLI) | 9.5 kg | 11.1 m/s | M_bend, a, δ | 340 Nm, 200 g, 6 mm |
| Lower legform (TRL) | 13.4 kg | 11.1 m/s | M_bend, F_comp | 340 Nm, 4 kN |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students frequently lose marks on the following points in this 14-mark question:
>
> 1. **Forgetting the filtering standard (CFC class).** Always state *SAE J211 CFC 60* for headform and *CFC 180* for chest data. **–1 mark if omitted.**
> 2. **Confusing HIC₁₅ vs HIC₃₆.** HIC₁₅ (15 ms window) is used in FMVSS 208/203/201 and ECE R94; HIC₃₆ (36 ms) is used in legacy FMVSS 208. **–0.5 mark if mixed.**
> 3. **Quoting wrong mass/velocity for legform.** The Flex-PLI is **9.5 kg**, not 13.4 kg. The 13.4 kg is the older TRL legform. **–1 mark.**
> 4. **Omitting the wrap distance exemption rule for the windscreen.** This is a common KTU trick question. **–0.5 mark.**
> 5. **Forgetting the 22.2 kN threshold for FMVSS seat belt anchor test (R14).** Many students write 15 kN (the ECE threshold). **–1 mark.**
> 6. **Not mentioning SAE J211 filtering or injury criteria (HIC formula).** The examiner will deduct **at least 2 marks** if the numerical part of a 14-mark question is missing.
> 7. **Mixing up steering column peak force (11 kN ECE R12) and energy (895 J).** Always state both. **–1 mark.**
> 8. **Writing "pendulum test" without specifying the mass, length, and release angle.** Examiners want the apparatus detail. **–1 mark.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Card — Read This Twice Before the Exam**

### A. Core Concepts (Tier 1 Knowledge)
- ✅ Crashworthiness = managed energy dissipation + survival space.
- ✅ Component test = single-part lab test, prerequisite for sled and full-vehicle test.
- ✅ Three-tier pyramid: **Component → Sled → Full Vehicle**.
- ✅ Cost ratio: 1 : 10 : 100.

### B. Key Standards (Must-Memorize Mapping)
- ✅ **Seat belt:** ECE R16 / FMVSS 209 / AIS-005.
- ✅ **Belt anchor:** ECE R14 / FMVSS 210.
- ✅ **Airbag:** ECE R94 (frontal) / R95 (side) / R129 (child) / USCAR-24.
- ✅ **Seat:** ECE R17 / FMVSS 207.
- ✅ **Headrest:** ECE R25 / FMVSS 202.
- ✅ **Steering:** ECE R12 / FMVSS 203, 204.
- ✅ **Bumper:** ECE R42 / FMVSS 215.
- ✅ **Fuel tank:** ECE R34 / FMVSS 301.
- ✅ **Pedestrian:** ECE R127 / AIS-100 / GTR 9.
- ✅ **ISOFIX:** ECE R14 / R44 / R145 / FMVSS 225.
- ✅ **EV battery:** UN R100 / AIS-156 / GTR 20.
- ✅ **Door:** FMVSS 206 / ECE R11.

### C. Key Thresholds (Memorize)
- ✅ **HIC₁₅ ≤ 1000** (15 ms window).
- ✅ **HIC₃₆ ≤ 1000** (36 ms window).
- ✅ **Nij ≤ 1.0** (neck injury).
- ✅ **ThCC ≤ 63 mm** (Hybrid III chest deflection).
- ✅ **Femur force ≤ 10 kN**.
- ✅ **Webbing break ≥ 15 kN** (ECE) / **≥ 22.2 kN** (FMVSS).
- ✅ **Belt anchor 22.2 kN** at 75°.
- ✅ **ISOFIX anchor 8 kN** F/R.
- ✅ **Steering column F_peak ≤ 11 kN**.
- ✅ **Bumper energy 678 J** (1100 kg × 4 km/h).
- ✅ **Pedestrian impact 11.1 m/s = 40 km/h**.
- ✅ **Battery 50 g / 6 ms** shock.

### D. Injury Criteria (Memorize the Formula)
- ✅ **HIC formula** with 2.5 power and 15 ms window.
- ✅ **Nij** = F_z/F_zc + M_y/M_yc ≤ 1.0.
- ✅ **Viscous criterion V\*C** = V(t) × C(t).
- ✅ **Weibull injury probability** $P = 1 - \exp(-(x/x_{63\%})^\beta)$.

### E. Filtering Standard (Memorize)
- ✅ **SAE J211 / ISO 6487** channel frequency classes.
- ✅ **CFC 60** for head acceleration (fc = 1650 Hz).
- ✅ **CFC 180** for chest acceleration (fc = 300 Hz).
- ✅ **CFC 600** for bone force (fc = 1000 Hz).

### F. ATDs (Memorize the Family)
- ✅ **Hybrid III 50th Male** — 78 kg, 1.75 m.
- ✅ **Hybrid III 5th Female** — 49 kg.
- ✅ **Hybrid III 95th Male** — 100 kg.
- ✅ **THOR-50M / THOR-5F** — modern biofidelic.
- ✅ **WorldSID** — side impact.
- ✅ **ES-2 / ES-2re / SID-IIs** — Euro NCAP side.
- ✅ **Q-dummies** — pediatric.
- ✅ **aPLI / Flex-PLI / cPLI** — pedestrian.

### G. Test Apparatus (Memorize the Types)
- ✅ Drop tower (gravitational).
- ✅ Pendulum (angular release).
- ✅ Sled (linear accelerator, hydraulic).
- ✅ Hydraulic press (quasi-static).
- ✅ Ballistic tank (inflator).
- ✅ Climatic chamber (environmental).
- ✅ High-speed camera (≥ 1000 fps, often 10,000 fps).

### H. Engineering Constants
- ✅ **g = 9.80665 m/s²** (use 9.81 for exam).
- ✅ **Speed conversions:** 1 km/h = 0.2778 m/s; 1 mph = 0.4470 m/s.
- ✅ **Energy conversion:** 1 cal = 4.184 J.

### I. Exam Strategy
- ✅ Always state the **regulation number** when describing a test.
- ✅ Always state the **threshold value** (not just "passes").
- ✅ Always show the **units** (N, kN, J, m/s, mm).
- ✅ Always state the **CFC filter class** for acceleration data.
- ✅ Use **$\vert$ and \vert$ LaTeX vertical bars for absolute value** — not pipe characters inside markdown tables.

### J. Common Confusions to Avoid
- ❌ HIC ≤ 1000 *not* 100.
- ❌ FMVSS 209 webbing = 22.2 kN *not* 15 kN.
- ❌ ECE R12 F_peak = 11 kN *not* 5 kN.
- ❌ Pedestrian velocity = 11.1 m/s (40 km/h) *not* 15 m/s.
- ❌ Battery shock = 50 g/6 ms *not* 30 g.
- ❌ ISOFIX = 8 kN *not* 22.2 kN (which is for adult belt anchor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 1 Notes — Component Crashworthiness Tests**
> **Total topics covered:** 12 major component categories, 25+ test standards, 6 derivations, 5 Mermaid diagrams, 2 full 14-mark model answers, 2 short-answer Q&As, 1 examiner pitfall callout, 1 comprehensive recap checklist.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sled tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sled tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **sled test** (also called a *sled impact test* or *crash sled test*) is a controlled, reproducible laboratory method used in automotive engineering to evaluate the performance of **occupant restraint systems** — such as seat belts, airbags, seats, head restraints, steering columns, and child restraint systems (CRS) — by accelerating or decelerating a test sled carrying a **biofidelic Anthropomorphic Test Device (ATD)** along a single rigid axis, in a manner that reproduces the **acceleration–time pulse** of a real vehicle crash, without destroying a production vehicle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **ECE Regulation R16 (Rev. 9)**, **R17 (Rev. 5)**, **R25 (Rev. 3)**, and corresponding **FMVSS 210/207/202a** and **AIS-008 / AIS-010 / AIS-072 (India)**, the sled test is the **statutorily accepted surrogate** for full vehicle crashworthiness certification of sub-system components where the component is the only variable under evaluation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU 2024 Vocabulary:**
> - **Crash pulse** – the time-history of the occupant compartment's deceleration during impact.
> - **ΔV (velocity change)** – the integral of the crash pulse, equal to the change in vehicle velocity.
> - **Pulse replication fidelity** – the degree to which the sled pulse matches a target full-vehicle pulse.
> - **Biofidelity** – the geometric and biomechanical similarity between an ATD and a human occupant.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are on a **high-speed train that suddenly stops** — your body continues forward at the original speed while the seat and floor come to rest beneath you. The train stopped, but you experienced the crash. A sled test is essentially a **"reverse crash"**:

- Instead of the **car hitting a wall**, the **wall (the deceleration brake) hits the car (the sled) at a controlled rate**.
- The car does not deform; the wall does the absorbing.
- Inside the car, the **dummy behaves exactly as a human would** in a real crash because the acceleration profile it feels is identical to that of a real impact.

> A sled test is like a **flight simulator for crashes** — the sled is the simulator, the dummy is the pilot, and the crash pulse is the flight profile.

### 1.3 Why Sled Tests Are Conducted

- **Cost reduction** – A full vehicle crash costs ₹15–80 lakhs; a sled test costs ₹2–8 lakhs.
- **Reproducibility** – Same pulse can be repeated 50–100 times for design iteration.
- **Component isolation** – Only the seat, belt, airbag, or CRS varies; the chassis pulse is constant.
- **Reduced development time** – Enables parametric sweeps (belt pretensioner timing, airbag vent size, seat foam density, etc.).
- **Regulatory acceptance** – Globally harmonized standards permit sled-based certification for sub-systems.

### 1.4 Key Test Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **ΔV (velocity change)**
- **Peak acceleration (g_max)**
- **Pulse duration (ms)**
- **Acceleration Severity Index (ASI)**
- **Head Injury Criterion (HIC, HIC₁₅, HIC₃₆)**
- **Neck Injury Criterion (Nij, NIC)**
- **Thoracic Trauma Index (TTI)**
- **Femur Compressive Force (F_c)**
- **Channel Frequency Class (CFC 60, CFC 180, CFC 1000)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

A sled test executes the following logical sequence:

1. **Target pulse selection** – A reference crash pulse (e.g., 56 km/h frontal rigid barrier per **FMVSS 208**, or 50 km/h per **ECE R94**) is defined by the standard.
2. **Sled payload preparation** – The seat/belt/airbag assembly is mounted on the sled; the **Hybrid III 50th percentile male dummy** (mass ≈ 77.7 kg) is positioned with standardized posture.
3. **Sled propulsion** – The sled is accelerated to a pre-determined impact velocity by one of:
   - **Hydraulic actuator (longest stroke, lowest peak g)**
   - **Pneumatic / nitrogen rocket (high peak g, short stroke)**
   - **Linear synchronous electric motor (most repeatable, environmentally clean)**
   - **Rubber bungee / falling weight (low-cost, R&D only)**
4. **Deceleration phase** – The sled is arrested by a **water-brake, hydraulic decelerator, or crushable honeycomb** arranged to reproduce the target crash pulse.
5. **Data acquisition** – Onboard triaxial accelerometers (sled), load cells (restraints), and dummy-mounted transducers (head, neck, chest, femur) record data at **≥10 kHz** per **SAE J211 / ISO 6487** Channel Frequency Class (CFC).
6. **Post-test analysis** – Injury criteria are computed and compared to regulatory limits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pulse matching tolerance** as per **SAE J2189** and **ECE R94 Annex 6**: the sled pulse must lie within a corridor of **±10% of the target's velocity and acceleration** between 5% and 95% of the pulse duration.

### 2.2 The Crash Pulse — Why It Matters

The **crash pulse** is the single most important variable in a sled test. It dictates:
- The **forward displacement** of the unrestrained occupant,
- The **belt loading** experienced,
- The **airbag deployment timing window**,
- The **biomechanical injury outcome**.

A pulse is characterized by three parameters:

| Parameter | Definition | Typical Range (Frontal) |
|-----------|------------|--------------------------|
| **Peak acceleration** | Maximum instantaneous g | 20 – 35 g |
| **Pulse duration** | Time from 5% to 95% of ΔV | 60 – 120 ms |
| **ΔV (velocity change)** | Area under pulse | 40 – 70 km/h equivalent |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains every formula a KTU PEMUT526 student is expected to know for Module 1. Memorize units and the CFC (Channel Frequency Class) filter specification.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Standard / Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Velocity change (ΔV)** | $\Delta V = \int_{0}^{t_p} a(t)\,dt$ | $a(t)$ in m/s², $t_p$ in s, $\Delta V$ in m/s | SAE J2189 |
| 2 | **Average acceleration (Ā)** | $\bar{A} = \dfrac{1}{t_2-t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt$ | Filtered with CFC 60 | ISO 6487 |
| 3 | **Average velocity (V̄)** | $\bar{V} = \dfrac{1}{t_2-t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} v(t)\,dt$ | $v(t)=\int a\,dt$ filtered with CFC 180 | Wood & Lim (1980) |
| 4 | **Acceleration Severity Index (ASI)** | $\text{ASI}(t) = \left[\left(\dfrac{\bar{A}(t)}{A^*}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{\bar{V}(t)}{V^*}\right)^{2}\right]^{1/2}$ | $A^* = 12$ m/s², $V^* = 7$ m/s (ISO 2631-5) | ISO 2631-5 |
| 5 | **Head Injury Criterion (HIC)** | $\text{HIC} = \max_{t_1,t_2}\left[(t_2-t_1)\left(\dfrac{1}{t_2-t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt\right)^{2.5}\right]$ | $a(t)$ in $g$, $t_2-t_1 \le 36$ ms for HIC₃₆ | FMVSS 208 / ECE R94 |
| 6 | **HIC₁₅ (15 ms interval)** | Same as HIC with $t_2-t_1 \le 15$ ms | – | FMVSS 208 (2000+) |
| 7 | **Neck Injury Criterion (NIC)** | $\text{NIC} = 0.2\cdot a_{\text{rel}} + v_{\text{rel}}^{2}$ | $a_{\text{rel}}$ in m/s², $v_{\text{rel}}$ in m/s | Boström et al. (1996) |
| 8 | **Nij (NISS)** | $N_{ij} = \dfrac{F_{\text{axial}}}{F_{\text{int}}} + \dfrac{M_{\text{OC}}}{M_{\text{int}}}$ | All forces in kN, moments in Nm | ECE R16 / FMVSS 208 |
| 9 | **3-ms clip acceleration** | $a_{\text{clip}} = \max\left[\dfrac{1}{\Delta t}\int_{t-\Delta t}^{t+\Delta t} a\,dt\right]$ with $\Delta t = 1.5$ ms | In $g$ | ECE R94 (a_3ms ≤ 60 g) |
| 10 | **Injury Risk Probability** | $P(\text{AIS}\ge k) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-\left(\beta_0+\beta_1 x\right)}}$ | $x$ = HIC, $N_{ij}$, etc. | ISO/TR 12349 |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEM R&D** – Vehicle manufacturers use sled tests to validate > 80% of occupant protection designs before a single full-vehicle crash.
- **Supplier validation** – Tier-1 suppliers (Autoliv, ZF, Joyson, etc.) certify seat belts, airbag inflators, and steering columns via sled tests.
- **Regulation & homologation** – Type approval under **AIS-008 (M1 category)**, **AIS-010**, **ECE R16/17/25/44/129** relies on sled data.
- **Insurance & NCAP** – Euro NCAP, GNCAP, and IIHS use sled-tested component data in their star-rating algorithms.
- **Forensic reconstruction** – Inverse sled tests with Injury Risk Curves are used in accident reconstruction to estimate occupant injury from a known ΔV.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Velocity Change (ΔV) from a Crash Pulse

The sled (and the vehicle it represents) undergoes a **time-varying deceleration** $a(t)$. The change in velocity is the **time integral of acceleration** by Newton's second law in its kinematic form.

$$
v(t) = v_0 + \int_{0}^{t} a(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

When the sled comes to rest at time $t_p$, the final velocity is zero ($v(t_p)=0$). Therefore the **change in velocity** is:

$$
\Delta V = v_0 - 0 = v_0
$$

Equating the two expressions for $v_0$:

$$
\Delta V = -\int_{0}^{t_p} a(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

In crashworthiness, $a(t)$ is conventionally taken as **positive** (deceleration magnitude), so:

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta V = \int_{0}^{t_p} a(t)\,dt\;}
$$

**Discretization for sampled data** (sampled at frequency $f_s$):

$$
\Delta V \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i\,\Delta t,\quad \Delta t = \dfrac{1}{f_s},\quad N = t_p\cdot f_s
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign convention in the literature: many standards (e.g., **ECE R94**) treat $a$ as deceleration (positive when the vehicle slows), hence the equation above is written without the minus sign.

### 3.2 Derivation: Head Injury Criterion (HIC)

The HIC is derived from the principle that **brain injury correlates with the rate of change of momentum** of the head over a short interval, which is captured by the **average acceleration** raised to the 2.5 power.

**Step 1 — Define the resultant head acceleration.** At the head CG of the ATD, the magnitude of the 3D acceleration vector is:

$$
a_{\text{res}}(t) = \sqrt{a_x^2(t) + a_y^2(t) + a_z^2(t)}
$$

This is filtered through a **CFC 1000** filter (–3 dB cutoff at 1000 Hz) per **SAE J211**.

**Step 2 — Form the interval average.** Over a candidate time window $[t_1, t_2]$:

$$
\bar{a} = \dfrac{1}{t_2-t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a_{\text{res}}(t)\,dt
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Gadd-Severin weighting exponent.** The original work by **Gadd (1961)** and **Versace (1971)** showed that injury severity scales approximately with the 2.5 power of acceleration, and is also proportional to the time over which the acceleration is sustained. Thus:

$$
\text{HIC} = (t_2-t_1)\cdot \bar{a}^{2.5}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximize over all admissible intervals.** The criterion selects the worst-case window:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{HIC} = \max_{t_1,t_2}\left[(t_2-t_1)\left(\dfrac{1}{t_2-t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a_{\text{res}}(t)\,dt\right)^{2.5}\right]\;}
$$

subject to $0 \le t_2 - t_1 \le \Delta t_{\max}$, where:

- $\Delta t_{\max} = 36$ ms for **HIC₃₆** (legacy),
- $\Delta t_{\max} = 15$ ms for **HIC₁₅** (modern, FMVSS 208 from 2000).

**Step 5 — Interpretation.** The HIC value maps to injury risk as per the **ISO/TR 12349** risk curves. A common regulatory limit:

$$
\text{HIC}_{36} \le 1000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \approx 16\%\ \text{probability of AIS}\ge 4
$$

$$
\text{HIC}_{15} \le 700 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \approx 5\%\ \text{probability of AIS}\ge 4
$$

**Numerical example (for understanding):** Suppose the head acceleration over a 15 ms window is constant at 80 g.

$$
\bar{a} = 80\,g
$$

$$
\text{HIC}_{15} = (0.015\,\text{s})\cdot (80)^{2.5}
$$

$$
80^{2.5} = 80^{2}\cdot 80^{0.5} = 6400 \times 8.944 = 57243.5
$$

$$
\text{HIC}_{15} = 0.015 \times 57243.5 = 858.6
$$

This **exceeds the HIC₁₅ limit of 700**, so the restraint system fails this injury metric.

### 3.3 Derivation: Acceleration Severity Index (ASI)

The ASI is a **single scalar that quantifies the overall severity of a crash pulse** for the entire occupant population, combining both **acceleration** and **velocity** effects.

**Step 1 — Define the time-windowed average acceleration.** Using a moving window of duration $t_w$ (typically 50 ms or the full pulse length), the average acceleration at time $t_0$ is:

$$
\bar{A}(t_0) = \dfrac{1}{t_w}\int_{t_0-t_w/2}^{t_0+t_w/2} a(t)\,dt
$$

This is filtered with **CFC 60** (–3 dB cutoff at 60 Hz) to remove high-frequency noise.

**Step 2 — Define the corresponding average velocity.** The velocity is first obtained by integration:

$$
v(t) = \int_{0}^{t} a(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

and is filtered with **CFC 180**. Then:

$$
\bar{V}(t_0) = \dfrac{1}{t_w}\int_{t_0-t_w/2}^{t_0+t_w/2} v(t)\,dt
$$

**Step 3 — Form the ASI function.** Normalize each by its limit value and combine via a Minkowski-type distance:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{ASI}(t_0) = \left[\left(\dfrac{\bar{A}(t_0)}{A^*}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{\bar{V}(t_0)}{V^*}\right)^{2}\right]^{1/2}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Reference (limit) values per ISO 2631-5:**

$$
A^* = 12\ \text{m/s}^2 \approx 1.22\,g,\qquad V^* = 7\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Global severity.** The maximum ASI over the pulse is the global severity:

$$
\text{ASI}_{\max} = \max_{t_0} \text{ASI}(t_0)
$$

with severity bands:

$$
\text{ASI} < 0.5 \rightarrow \text{low};\quad 0.5\le \text{ASI} < 1.0 \rightarrow \text{medium};\quad \text{ASI} \ge 1.0 \rightarrow \text{high}
$$

**Numerical example:** Let $\bar{A}=9$ m/s² and $\bar{V}=5$ m/s.

$$
\text{ASI} = \sqrt{(9/12)^2 + (5/7)^2} = \sqrt{0.5625 + 0.5102} = \sqrt{1.0727} = 1.036
$$

This pulse is **high-severity** (ASI > 1.0).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pulses vs. Injury Modes

| Pulse Shape | Peak g | Duration | ΔV | HIC risk | Belt load risk | Submarining risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Square (ideal)** | 25 g | 80 ms | 56 km/h | High | High | Medium |
| **3-2-1 pulse** | 30 / 20 / 10 g | 30+30+30 ms | 56 km/h | Medium | High | Low |
| **Triangular** | 50 g | 60 ms | 42 km/h | Very High | Medium | Low |
| **Half-sine** | 35 g | 75 ms | 50 km/h | High | High | Medium |
| **ECE R94 reference** | ~28 g | ~95 ms | 65 km/h | High | High | Medium |

### 3.5 Sample Worked Problem (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A sled test produces the following acceleration-time samples (filtered, CFC 60). Sampling rate: 1000 Hz.

| t (ms) | a (g) | t (ms) | a (g) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 60 | 30 |
| 20 | 10 | 80 | 20 |
| 40 | 25 | 100 | 5 |

**Find:** (i) ΔV in km/h, (ii) average acceleration over 0–100 ms, (iii) ASI using the full pulse.

**Solution:**

(i) Using the trapezoidal rule with $\Delta t = 0.02$ s:

$$
\Delta V = \int a\,dt \approx \sum \frac{1}{2}(a_i+a_{i+1})\Delta t
$$

$$
\Delta V = \frac{0.02}{2}\cdot[(0+10)+(10+25)+(25+30)+(30+20)+(20+5)]
$$

$$
\Delta V = 0.01 \cdot [10 + 35 + 55 + 50 + 25] = 0.01 \times 175 = 1.75\ \text{m/s}
$$

Converting:

$$
\Delta V = 1.75 \times 3.6 = 6.3\ \text{km/h}
$$

(ii) Average acceleration:

$$
\bar{A} = \dfrac{1}{0.1}\int_{0}^{0.1} a(t)\,dt = \dfrac{1.75}{0.1} = 17.5\ \text{m/s}^2 \approx 1.785\,g
$$

(iii) ASI calculation (using full pulse as window):

$$
\text{ASI} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{17.5}{12}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{1.75}{7}\right)^2}
$$

$$
\text{ASI} = \sqrt{(1.458)^2 + (0.25)^2} = \sqrt{2.126 + 0.0625} = \sqrt{2.189} = 1.479
$$

**Interpretation:** ASI = 1.479 → **high-severity** crash; the restraint system must handle peak chest loading and potential head contact.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define a sled test. List four major international standards that mandate sled testing for automotive restraint systems.

**Model Answer:**

A **sled test** is a controlled laboratory procedure in which a test sled carrying a biofidelic anthropomorphic test device (ATD) is accelerated or decelerated along a single rigid axis to reproduce the crash pulse of a real vehicle impact, in order to evaluate the performance of occupant restraint systems without destroying a production vehicle.

Four major international standards mandating sled tests:

1. **ECE Regulation R16** – Safety belt anchorages and assemblies.
2. **ECE Regulation R17** – Strength of seats and their anchorages.
3. **ECE Regulation R25** – Head restraints.
4. **ECE Regulation R44 / R129** – Child restraint systems (CRS).
5. *Additional:* **FMVSS 210**, **AIS-008** (India).

*(Any four listed standards earn full credit.)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Explain the term "crash pulse" and state why pulse replication fidelity is critical in a sled test.

**Model Answer:**

The **crash pulse** is the time-history of the deceleration (or acceleration) experienced at the occupant compartment of a vehicle during an impact, typically expressed as $a(t)$ in $g$ versus time in milliseconds, integrated to give the **velocity change (ΔV)**.

**Pulse replication fidelity** is critical because:

- The injury outcome of an occupant depends on the **shape, peak, and duration** of the pulse — not merely the ΔV.
- Two sled tests with identical ΔV but different pulse shapes can produce **vastly different HIC, NIC, and chest deflection** values.
- Per **SAE J2189** and **ECE R94 Annex 6**, the sled-generated pulse must lie within a **±10% corridor** of the reference vehicle pulse between 5% and 95% of the pulse duration, ensuring regulatory equivalence to a full vehicle crash.
- Poor fidelity leads to **non-conservative certification** of restraint systems, defeating the safety purpose of the test.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (per KTU ESE 2024):** *Answer either Option A or Option B. Each carries 14 marks split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

#### **Option A (14 Marks)**

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Modified)]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **propulsion systems** used in modern sled test facilities. Compare **hydraulic, pneumatic (rocket), and linear electric motor** drives with respect to peak acceleration capability, stroke length, repeatability, environmental impact, and cost.

**(b)** Derive the **Head Injury Criterion (HIC)** mathematically starting from the definition of resultant head acceleration, and discuss its **limitations** with reference to biofidelity of the dummy, age-related tolerance, and rotational kinematics.

**Model Solution for (a):**

The propulsion subsystem accelerates the sled to the pre-impact velocity. The three dominant technologies are:

**1. Hydraulic Actuator (Servo-Hydraulic Ram)**

- **Principle:** High-pressure oil (typically 210 bar) drives a double-acting piston attached to the sled.
- **Stroke:** 1.5 – 5 m (longest of the three).
- **Peak g:** 5 – 25 g.
- **Repeatability:** Excellent (CFC-class pulse shaping achievable).
- **Cost:** High (₹2 – 5 crore installed).
- **Environmental impact:** Moderate (oil leaks, hydraulic fluid disposal).
- **Use case:** Low- to mid-severity tests, far-side occupant, and long-pulse rear-impact evaluations.

**2. Pneumatic / Nitrogen Rocket (Cold Gas Accelerator)**

- **Principle:** Rapid release of compressed nitrogen (180 – 300 bar) into a cylinder drives the sled piston.
- **Stroke:** 0.5 – 1.5 m.
- **Peak g:** 20 – 80 g (highest peak).
- **Repeatability:** Good (within 5% with pre-warmed gas).
- **Cost:** Medium (₹80 lakh – 1.5 crore).
- **Environmental impact:** Low (nitrogen is inert).
- **Use case:** High-g frontal and side-impact tests, airbag deployment validation.

**3. Linear Synchronous Electric Motor (LSM)**

- **Principle:** A linear motor stator (mounted on the track) propels a permanent-magnet sled carrier.
- **Stroke:** 0.3 – 3 m (variable).
- **Peak g:** 10 – 50 g.
- **Repeatability:** Outstanding (closed-loop digital control, <1% deviation).
- **Cost:** Highest (₹5 – 10 crore).
- **Environmental impact:** Very low (no consumables).
- **Use case:** R&D with high iteration rate, NCAP protocols, child restraint testing.

**Comparison summary table** *(internal answer; not on paper)*: LSM > Hydraulic > Rocket for repeatability; Rocket > LSM > Hydraulic for peak g; Hydraulic > LSM > Rocket for stroke.

**Model Solution for (b):**

The HIC derivation proceeds in five logical steps as shown in **Section 3.2** of these notes:

1. **Resultant head acceleration** at the dummy's head CG: $a_{\text{res}}(t) = \sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2}$, filtered with **CFC 1000**.

2. **Interval average:** $\bar{a} = \dfrac{1}{t_2-t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a_{\text{res}}(t)\,dt$.

3. **Gadd-Versace weighting:** injury correlates with $\bar{a}^{2.5}$ multiplied by interval length.

4. **Closed-form HIC expression:**

$$
\text{HIC} = \max_{t_1,t_2}\left[(t_2-t_1)\left(\dfrac{1}{t_2-t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a_{\text{res}}(t)\,dt\right)^{2.5}\right]
$$

with $t_2 - t_1 \le 36$ ms (HIC₃₆) or 15 ms (HIC₁₅).

5. **Maximization** is performed numerically by sliding the window over the full head-acceleration time history.

**Limitations of HIC:**

- **Linear translation only:** HIC is computed from linear acceleration; it **ignores rotational acceleration** of the brain, which is increasingly recognized as the cause of diffuse axonal injury (DAI).
- **Adult male biofidelity only:** The criterion is calibrated on Hybrid III 50th percentile male (77.7 kg) data. **Females, children, and elderly** have different injury tolerances.
- **No directional sensitivity:** A pure frontal acceleration of 80 g and a pure lateral acceleration of 80 g give the same HIC, but injury risk differs markedly.
- **Time-invariant:** HIC assumes a single interval of maximum loading; **multiple sub-injurious peaks** can cumulatively cause injury but are not summed.
- **No contact-location data:** A forehead impact and a rear-of-skull impact can have the same HIC but different injury outcomes (coup vs. contrecoup).
- **Filtering artifacts:** CFC 1000 filtering can attenuate very short transients associated with skull fracture.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — How students lose marks on (b):**
> - Writing the HIC formula **without specifying the CFC filter** (–1 mark).
> - Confusing HIC₃₆ with HIC₁₅ intervals (–1 mark).
> - Failing to mention the **2.5 exponent originates from Gadd (1961) and Versace (1971)** (–0.5 mark).
> - Omitting **rotational kinematics** as a limitation (–1 mark).
> - Writing HIC as a single number without the **max operator** (–1 mark).

---

#### **Option B (14 Marks)**

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Modified)]**

**(a)** A sled test is to replicate a 56 km/h frontal rigid barrier crash. The target pulse has a peak acceleration of **28 g** sustained for **60 ms** (square pulse approximation). Compute the **ΔV** of the test, the **average acceleration Ā**, the **average velocity V̄**, and the **Acceleration Severity Index (ASI)**. Comment on the severity classification.

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **ECE regulations** and **FMVSS** frameworks governing occupant protection testing. Discuss the role of **biofidelity** in ATD selection and explain why the **Hybrid III dummy is the global standard** for frontal sled tests.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Given:**
- Target peak acceleration $a = 28\,g = 28 \times 9.81 = 274.68$ m/s².
- Pulse duration $t_p = 60$ ms $= 0.060$ s.
- Pulse shape: square (constant $a$ over duration).

**Step 1 — Compute ΔV:**

$$
\Delta V = \int_{0}^{t_p} a(t)\,dt = a\cdot t_p
$$

$$
\Delta V = 274.68 \times 0.060 = 16.48\ \text{m/s}
$$

Converting to km/h:

$$
\Delta V = 16.48 \times 3.6 = 59.33\ \text{km/h}
$$

> Note: The target was 56 km/h; the slight overshoot is **expected** because the simple square-pulse approximation over-estimates ΔV; the real FMVSS 208 pulse has a ramp-up/ramp-down that reduces effective ΔV.

**Step 2 — Compute average acceleration Ā:**

$$
\bar{A} = \dfrac{1}{t_p}\int_{0}^{t_p} a(t)\,dt = \dfrac{a\cdot t_p}{t_p} = a = 274.68\ \text{m/s}^2
$$

For a square pulse, $\bar{A}$ equals the peak.

**Step 3 — Compute average velocity V̄:**

The velocity profile is triangular (linear ramp from 0 to peak at the pulse start, then linear ramp back to 0 at the pulse end):

$$
v(t) = a\cdot t\quad\text{for }0\le t\le t_p
$$

$$
\bar{V} = \dfrac{1}{t_p}\int_{0}^{t_p} a\cdot t\,dt = \dfrac{a\cdot t_p}{2}
$$

$$
\bar{V} = \dfrac{274.68 \times 0.060}{2} = 8.24\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute ASI:**

$$
\text{ASI} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\bar{A}}{A^*}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\bar{V}}{V^*}\right)^2}
$$

with $A^* = 12$ m/s², $V^* = 7$ m/s.

$$
\text{ASI} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{274.68}{12}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{8.24}{7}\right)^2}
$$

$$
\text{ASI} = \sqrt{(22.89)^2 + (1.177)^2} = \sqrt{523.95 + 1.385} = \sqrt{525.34} = 22.92
$$

**Severity classification:** ASI = 22.92 ≫ 1.0 → **extreme severity**, consistent with a 56 km/h frontal barrier crash. Such a pulse is life-threatening without a properly tuned restraint system.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Comparison of ECE vs. FMVSS frameworks:**

| Aspect | ECE (UN/ECE WP.29) | FMVSS (U.S. NHTSA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Legal basis** | UN Treaty (1958 Agreement) | U.S. Federal Law (49 USC §301) |
| **Geographical scope** | Europe + 50+ countries (including India via AIS adoption) | United States |
| **Type approval** | Type approval with self-certification | Self-certification by manufacturer |
| **Frontal test speed** | 56 km/h offset deformable barrier (ECE R94) | 56 km/h full-width rigid barrier (FMVSS 208) |
| **Sled-only sub-system tests** | Yes (R16, R17, R25, R44, R129) | Yes (FMVSS 207, 210, 213) |
| **Dummy** | Hybrid III 50th male, Hybrid III 5th female, Q-series children | Hybrid III 50th male, Hybrid III 5th female |
| **Injury criteria limits** | HIC₃₆ ≤ 1000, chest deflection ≤ 50 mm, femur ≤ 10 kN | HIC₁₅ ≤ 700, chest deflection ≤ 63 mm, femur ≤ 10 kN |
| **Pulse validation** | ±10% corridor (ECE R94 Annex 6) | ±10% corridor (per NHTSA test procedure) |
| **Airbag out-of-position** | Not mandatory in ECE | Required in FMVSS 208 (advanced airbag rule) |
| **Update pace** | Slower, consensus-based | Faster, NHTSA rulemaking |
| **In India** | Adapted as **AIS** by ARAI | Rarely adopted directly |

**Biofidelity and ATD Selection:**

**Biofidelity** is the **degree to which an ATD mimics the geometric, inertial, and biomechanical response of a human** under load. It is quantified by:

- **Geometric biofidelity** – external dimensions matching the human (H-point, hip pivot, spine curvature, limb lengths).
- **Kinematic biofidelity** – joint ranges of motion matching the human.
- **Force–deflection biofidelity** – the force vs. deflection response of dummy flesh, bone, and joints matching cadaver and PMHS (post-mortem human subject) data.

**Why Hybrid III is the global standard for frontal sled tests:**

1. **Comprehensive instrumentation** – 12+ channels including head, neck, chest, pelvis, femur, tibia, and feet transducers.
2. **Repeatability** – Coefficient of variation < 5% across dummy population; critical for regulatory compliance.
3. **Calibration maturity** – Documented in **SAE J2856** and **ISO 7866**, with detailed calibration fixtures.
4. **Validation against PMHS** – Frontal and oblique impact corridors established by **Mertz (1984)**, **Eppinger (1984)**, and **Kuppa (2001)**.
5. **Global regulatory acceptance** – Both ECE R94 and FMVSS 208 explicitly list Hybrid III as the only acceptable frontal ATD.
6. **Industry tooling** – Suppliers, instrumentation, and dummy repair infrastructure exist worldwide.

**Limitations of Hybrid III:**

- **No biofidelic thorax–shoulder interaction** (relevant for belted and airbag interaction).
- **Linear-only spine** (cannot reproduce human S-curvature during extension).
- **No muscle response simulation** (passive ATD only).
- **Limited biofidelity for small females and elderly** (THOR-5F, THOR-M, and WorldSID are emerging alternatives).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — How students lose marks on (a):**
> - Forgetting to convert **28 g to m/s²** before integration (–1 mark).
> - Reporting ΔV in m/s but **failing to convert to km/h** to match the 56 km/h target (–0.5 mark).
> - Using peak $a$ instead of **average $a$** in the ASI formula (–1 mark).
> - Computing the average velocity as the **peak velocity** instead of $\frac{1}{2}\times v_{\text{peak}}$ for a triangular profile (–1 mark).
> - Misclassifying ASI = 22.92 as "low severity" (–1 mark).
>
> **Pitfall for (b):**
> - Listing ECE and FMVSS as **identical frameworks** — they are not; the offset vs. full-width barrier difference is critical.
> - Failing to mention that **AIS (India)** adapts ECE, not FMVSS.
> - Stating that Hybrid III is the **only** ATD — THOR-5F and Q-dummies are accepted for side and child tests respectively.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Recap — KTU PEMUT526 Module 1, Topic: Sled Tests**

- **Definition:** Sled test = a **single-axis, accelerated/decelerated test platform** replicating a real vehicle crash pulse to evaluate **restraint sub-systems** (belt, airbag, seat, headrest, CRS).
- **Why used:** Cost (1/10th of full vehicle), repeatability, parametric sweep capability, regulatory acceptance for sub-systems.
- **Pulse parameters:** peak $g$, duration (ms), **ΔV** (m/s or km/h), shape (square, half-sine, 3-2-1, triangular, ECE R94 reference).
- **Standard corridors:** **±10%** of target pulse per **SAE J2189 / ECE R94 Annex 6**.
- **Mandatory standards:** **ECE R16, R17, R25, R44, R129; FMVSS 207, 208, 210, 213; AIS-008, AIS-010, AIS-072** (India).
- **ATD:** **Hybrid III 50th male (77.7 kg)** — global standard for frontal tests; **THOR-5F, WorldSID, Q-series** for other applications.
- **Filters (SAE J211 / ISO 6487):** **CFC 60** (sled acceleration), **CFC 180** (velocity), **CFC 1000** (head).
- **Injury criteria to remember:** **HIC₃₆ ≤ 1000**, **HIC₁₅ ≤ 700**, **3-ms clip ≤ 60 g** (ECE R94), **chest deflection ≤ 50 mm** (ECE), **femur ≤ 10 kN**, **Nij ≤ 1.0** (each axis).
- **Key formulas (memorize verbatim):**
  - $\Delta V = \int a\,dt$
  - $\text{ASI} = \sqrt{(\bar{A}/A^*)^2 + (\bar{V}/V^*)^2}$
  - $\text{HIC} = \max_{t_1,t_2}\left[(t_2-t_1)\left(\dfrac{1}{t_2-t_1}\int a\,dt\right)^{2.5}\right]$
  - $\text{NIC} = 0.2\,a_{\text{rel}} + v_{\text{rel}}^2$
  - $N_{ij} = F_{\text{axial}}/F_{\text{int}} + M_{\text{OC}}/M_{\text{int}}$
- **Propulsion comparison:** **Hydraulic** (long stroke, low g, high cost) | **Pneumatic rocket** (short stroke, very high g, medium cost) | **Linear electric motor** (variable, highest repeatability, highest cost).
- **Pulse shaping brake types:** **Water brake, hydraulic decelerator, crushable honeycomb, friction brake**.
- **Severity bands (ASI):** < 0.5 low | 0.5 – 1.0 medium | > 1.0 high.
- **Why HIC is imperfect:** ignores rotational acceleration, biofidelic only for adult males, single-interval averaging, no directional sensitivity, no contact-location weighting.
- **KTU 2024 hot topics:** pulse corridor validation, ASI calculation in numerical problems, comparison ECE vs. FMVSS vs. AIS, dummy biofidelity, and HIC derivation with all assumptions.
- **Sampling rule:** **≥ 10 kHz** acquisition rate, with **CFC filtering** per **SAE J211**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Full-scale barrier impact tests and common safety standards for type approval of passenger cars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Full-scale barrier impact tests and common safety standards for type approval of passenger cars

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Full-Scale Barrier Impact Test** is a destructive, physically executed crashworthiness evaluation procedure mandated by regulatory authorities wherein a complete production-intent passenger car (or its specific body-in-white structure) is propelled at a precisely defined velocity and trajectory into a rigid, semi-rigid, or deformable barrier (or vice versa, using a moving barrier) to objectively measure occupant survivability, structural integrity, and injury biomechanics for the purpose of **Type Approval** certification.

**Type Approval** (in the context of KTU PEMUT526 and global automotive homologation) refers to the formal administrative process by which a national or regional authority (e.g., Ministry of Road Transport and Highways, Government of India; or UNECE via the 1958 Agreement) certifies that a vehicle prototype satisfies all applicable safety, emission, and anti-theft technical regulations before serial production and market release is legally permitted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Type approval is a *whole-vehicle* certification philosophy. A car cannot be legally sold in India (after a defined date) unless it carries a homologation certificate proving compliance with the relevant Automotive Industry Standards (AIS) and corresponding ECE (United Nations Economic Commission for Europe) regulations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a school examination where the final exam is a single 3-hour test. Type approval is the equivalent of that final exam, but for cars. Before a manufacturer (like Maruti Suzuki or Tata Motors) can sell a new car model in India or Europe, the "final exam" must be passed. The examiner (the regulatory authority) requires the manufacturer to literally *crash* the car into a wall in a laboratory to see if the car passes the safety test.

The "barrier" is essentially a strong, instrumented wall, and the crash is highly choreographed — the speed, the angle, the weight of the car, even the temperature of the dummies (representing human occupants) inside are precisely specified. Sensors inside the dummies measure forces on the head, neck, chest, and legs. If the forces are too high (meaning a real human would be severely injured or killed), the car **fails the test** and cannot be type-approved for sale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics of the "Final Exam":**
> - **HIC (Head Injury Criterion)** — must be < **1000** (this is the magic global number).
> - **Chest Deflection** — must be < **50 mm**.
> - **Neck Injury (NIC)** — must be below threshold.
> - **Femur (Thigh) Compression Force** — must be < **10 kN**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following are absolute, globally accepted constants used during full-scale barrier impact tests and type approval:

- **Frontal Impact Test Velocity:** **56 km/h** (or 64 km/h for offset deformable barrier per Euro NCAP/ECE R94 variant conditions).
- **Side Impact Moving Barrier Velocity:** **50 km/h** (ECE R95) with a **950 kg** deformable mobile barrier.
- **Rear Impact Test Velocity:** **80 km/h** equivalent (for whiplash via sled test under ECE R17).
- **Anthropometric Test Device (Dummy) Masses:** Hybrid III 50th percentile male = **77.7 kg**; 5th percentile female = **49.5 kg**; 6-year-old child = **22.3 kg**; 18-month-old infant = **11.2 kg**.
- **HIC (Head Injury Criterion) Limit:** **1000** (for adults, 36 ms sliding window).
- **Chest Severity Index (CSI) Limit:** **1.0** (dimensionless).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Why and How of Full-Scale Barrier Tests

### 2.1.1 Why Full-Scale Tests Are Required (The Engineering Logic)

1. **Component-level testing is insufficient.** A seatbelt may pass a tensile pull test at 25 kN, but under real crash dynamics, a 25 kN peak force occurs in **< 80 ms**. Component tests cannot capture the multi-axial, time-dependent loading environment.
2. **Structural load paths are nonlinear.** A modern car has a programmed crumple zone. Only a full-vehicle test reveals whether the *intended* crash energy is dissipated as designed.
3. **Occupant biomechanics depend on kinematics.** A dummy's flailing motion (submarining, head slap, knee punch-through) is determined by the entire vehicle-pulse environment — not individual parts.
4. **Regulatory harmonization demands identical protocols.** ECE R94 frontal impact test, for instance, is reproduced identically in 60+ countries.

### 2.1.2 The Core Engineering How — Step Logic

1. **Vehicle Preparation:** Car is weighed (unloaded + fuel tank filled to 90% + dummy mass placement), tires inflated, brakes released, windows closed, gear in neutral.
2. **Dummy Instrumentation:** Hybrid III 50th percentile driver and front passenger dummies installed with instrumented head, neck, chest, femurs, tibiae.
3. **Towing System:** A tow cable with quick-release mechanism propels the car.
4. **Barrier Engagement:** At the defined velocity, the car strikes the barrier.
5. **Data Acquisition:** High-speed cameras (typically 1000–2000 fps) and onboard dummy sensors record kinematics at > 10 kHz.
6. **Post-Crash Analysis:** Force-time and acceleration-time curves are filtered (SAE CFC 60 / CFC 180 channels) and injury metrics calculated.

## 2.2 The Four Principal Test Modalities

| # | Test Type | Standard Reference | Barrier Type | Velocity | Primary Injury Risk Addressed |
|---|-----------|---------------------|--------------|----------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Full Frontal Rigid Barrier | ECE R16 / ECE R94 (100% overlap) | Rigid wall, full-width | 56 km/h | Head, chest, femur, tibia |
| 2 | Frontal Offset Deformable Barrier (ODB) | ECE R94 / Euro NCAP | Aluminum honeycomb, 40% overlap | 64 km/h | Far-side occupant, lower extremities |
| 3 | Side Moving Deformable Barrier (MDB) | ECE R95 / FMVSS 214 | 950 kg deformable, 90° | 50 km/h | Thorax, pelvis, head (lateral) |
| 4 | Rear Impact / Whiplash | ECE R17 / FMVSS 202a | Sled-based, no full vehicle | Pulse equivalent to 80 km/h | Cervical spine (neck) |
| 5 | Roof Crush (Strength) | FMVSS 216 / ECE R66 | Hydraulic platen | Quasi-static 3x vehicle weight | Roof intrusion, ejection |

## 2.3 Common Safety Standards for Type Approval of Passenger Cars

The following globally authoritative regulations govern type approval of passenger cars. Note that India has adopted most of these through **AIS (Automotive Industry Standards)** under CMVR (Central Motor Vehicles Rules).

### 2.3.1 United Nations / ECE (UNECE) Regulations (1958 Agreement)

| ECE Reg. | Title (Abbreviated) | Application |
|----------|---------------------|-------------|
| **ECE R12** | Steering Wheel Impact Protection | Interior head impact on steering wheel |
| **ECE R14** | Seat Belt Anchorages | Strength of seat belt mounting points |
| **ECE R16** | Safety Belts and Restraint Systems | Performance of seat belts |
| **ECE R17** | Seats, Anchorages, and Head Restraints | Strength + Anti-Whiplash |
| **ECE R21** | Interior Fittings | Dashboard / protrusions |
| **ECE R29** | Protection of Occupants of Cab of Commercial Vehicles | Commercial cab (referenced contextually) |
| **ECE R32** | Behavior of Structure in Rear-end Collision | Rear structural integrity |
| **ECE R34** | Prevention of Fire Risks | Fuel leakage / electrical fire |
| **ECE R94** | Frontal Collision Protection (Occupants) | Full Frontal Crash |
| **ECE R95** | Lateral Collision Protection (Occupants) | Side Impact Crash |
| **ECE R66** | Superstructure Strength of Large Passenger Vehicles | Bus/coach rollover (context) |
| **ECE R100** | Electric Powertrain Safety | EV-specific |

### 2.3.2 US Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standards (FMVSS)

| FMVSS | Title |
|-------|-------|
| **FMVSS 208** | Occupant Crash Protection (Frontal) |
| **FMVSS 214** | Side Impact Protection |
| **FMVSS 216** | Roof Crush Resistance |
| **FMVSS 219** | Windshield Zone Intrusion (active) |
| **FMVSS 301** | Fuel System Integrity |
| **FMVSS 202a** | Head Restraints (Rear-impact whiplash) |
| **FMVSS 226** | Ejection Mitigation (rollover) |

### 2.3.3 India — Automotive Industry Standards (AIS)

| AIS | Equivalent To | Subject |
|-----|---------------|---------|
| **AIS 098** | ECE R94 | Frontal Impact |
| **AIS 099** | ECE R95 | Side Impact |
| **AIS 014** | ECE R14 | Seat Belt Anchorages |
| **AIS 015** | ECE R16 | Seat Belts |
| **AIS 017** | ECE R17 | Seats & Head Restraints |
| **AIS 096** | ECE R95 variant | Side Pole Impact (additional) |
| **AIS 156** | — | EV Battery Safety (India specific) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Regional Convergence:** India has aligned with ECE (UN) regulations rather than the US FMVSS system, although FMVSS is studied in Module 1 for global comparative context (KTU syllabus often contrasts ECE vs FMVSS).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Description | Units |
|---------|-----------|-------------|-------|
| **Head Injury Criterion (HIC)** | $HIC = \left( t_2 - t_1 \right) \left[ \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt \right]^{2.5}$ | Max value over 36 ms window must be < 1000 | $a$ in g, $t$ in s |
| **3 ms Peak Head Acceleration** | $a_{peak} \le 80\,g$ | Head injury threshold | $g$ |
| **Neck Injury Criterion (NIC)** | $NIC = 0.2 \cdot a_{rel} + F_{z,rel} \cdot l_{eff}$ | Where $a_{rel}$ is relative head/upper-neck acceleration, $F_{z,rel}$ is axial force, $l_{eff}$ is effective moment arm | Various |
| **Chest Compression (Deflection)** | $\delta_{chest} \le 50\,mm$ | Sternum displacement limit | mm |
| **Chest Severity Index (CSI)** | $CSI = \frac{a_{max}}{a_{crit}} + \frac{\delta_{max}}{\delta_{crit}} \le 1.0$ | Combined acceleration + deflection severity | dimensionless |
| **Femur Compressive Force** | $F_{femur} \le 10\,kN$ | Each femur individually | kN |
| **Tibia Index (TI)** | $TI = \frac{M_y}{M_{y,crit}} + \frac{F_{z}}{F_{z,crit}} \le 1.0$ | Bending moment + axial force | dimensionless |
| **Barrier Equivalent Velocity (BEV)** | $v_{BEV} = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{crush}}{\mu_{crush} \cdot A_{contact}}}$ | For assessing the on-road impact severity that a test replicates | m/s |
| **Kinetic Energy at Impact** | $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Crash energy dissipated | Joules |
| **Average Deceleration (CFC60)** | $a_{avg} = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}$ | Filtered per SAE Channel Frequency Class 60 | $g$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU TIP — The Pipe Symbol Rule:** In KTU exam answer sheets, when writing injury criteria equations on paper, **never** use the ASCII pipe `\vert` to denote "less than or equal to." Always use the standard $\le$ symbol. In the formula sheet above, the `$\le$` notation is used for clarity; this is the same format KTU examiners expect in your answer booklets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Head Injury Criterion (HIC)

The HIC is the most globally recognized injury metric for crashworthiness. The following exhaustive derivation explains its origin, meaning, and computational procedure as expected in KTU 14-mark Part B questions.

### Step 1 — The Underlying Physical Reality

During a frontal crash, the head experiences a rapidly changing acceleration pulse. The brain (a viscoelastic mass suspended in cerebrospinal fluid) lags behind the skull's motion. The *relative* motion between skull and brain causes:
- Subdural hematoma (from stretching of bridging veins)
- Diffuse axonal injury (from shear strain in white matter)
- Cerebral contusions (from impact against inner skull)

Wayne State University Tolerance Curve (1960s) empirically established that the **acceleration-time product** (rather than acceleration alone) is the most predictive single variable for skull-brain injury severity.

### Step 2 — The Mathematical Foundation (Ridella–Eppinger Formulation, 1980s)

The HIC is defined using the resultant head acceleration magnitude $a(t)$ measured at the dummy's head CG. The acceleration is filtered (CFC 1000, i.e., 1000 Hz cutoff) and expressed in $g$-units.

$$
HIC = \left( t_2 - t_1 \right) \left[ \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt \right]^{2.5}
$$

where:
- $a(t)$ = resultant head acceleration as a function of time, in units of $g$.
- $t_1, t_2$ = two arbitrary time points (in seconds) that **maximize** the HIC value.
- The interval $\Delta t = t_2 - t_1$ must satisfy the constraint $0 \le \Delta t \le 36\,ms$ (per the **HIC36** convention adopted by FMVSS 208, ECE R94, and AIS 098).
- A stricter version, **HIC15**, restricts the interval to $\le 15\,ms$ (used in some racing and military standards).

### Step 3 — Step-by-Step Computational Procedure (Examiner-Expected)

**Given:** A driver-side dummy head acceleration trace during a 56 km/h full-frontal rigid barrier test. The relevant portion of the trace (3 ms resolution) is:

| Time $t$ (ms) | Acceleration $a(t)$ ($g$) |
|---------------|---------------------------|
| 0 | 0 |
| 3 | 8 |
| 6 | 18 |
| 9 | 32 |
| 12 | 41 |
| 15 | 38 |
| 18 | 29 |
| 21 | 22 |
| 24 | 14 |
| 27 | 9 |
| 30 | 5 |
| 33 | 2 |
| 36 | 0 |

**Step 3a:** Compute the average acceleration over the interval $[0, 36\,ms]$ using the trapezoidal rule:

$$
\bar{a} = \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt
$$

Discretized:

$$
\bar{a} = \frac{\Delta t}{2} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} (a_i + a_{i+1}) \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta t}
$$

where $\Delta t = 3\,ms = 0.003\,s$.

$$
\sum a_i \cdot \Delta t = (0 + 8 + 18 + 32 + 41 + 38 + 29 + 22 + 14 + 9 + 5 + 2 + 0) \cdot 0.003
$$

$$
= 218 \cdot 0.003 = 0.654\,g\cdot s
$$

Thus:

$$
\bar{a} = \frac{0.654}{0.036} = 18.17\,g
$$

**Step 3b:** Substitute into the HIC expression with $\Delta t = 0.036\,s$:

$$
HIC = (0.036) \cdot (18.17)^{2.5}
$$

Compute the power:

$$
18.17^2 = 330.15
$$

$$
18.17^{0.5} = \sqrt{18.17} = 4.263
$$

$$
18.17^{2.5} = 330.15 \cdot 4.263 = 1407.6
$$

Therefore:

$$
HIC = 0.036 \cdot 1407.6 = 50.67
$$

**Step 3c:** Since $HIC = 50.67 \ll 1000$, the head injury risk is **acceptable**. The vehicle passes the head injury criterion for this particular dummy in this particular test.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Logic Check:** Always state the **pass/fail threshold explicitly** (HIC < 1000 for adults; HIC < 700 is a common OEM internal target for safety margin). Mentioning "the value is below 1000" without citing the regulatory source will cost you 1 mark in a 14-mark Part B answer.

### Step 4 — Why the Exponent 2.5? (Physical Justification)

The exponent of 2.5 emerges from dimensional analysis and empirical correlation of cadaver test data. It reflects the non-linear scaling of brain injury severity with applied impulse — small increases in acceleration or duration lead to disproportionate increases in injury probability. This is consistent with the **Weibull injury risk curve** that the HIC implicitly approximates.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Crash Energy and Barrier Equivalent Velocity

A 1400 kg passenger car strikes a rigid barrier at 56 km/h. Compute the crash kinetic energy and the equivalent on-road crash speed (assuming a typical car-to-car overlap crash where 60% of the kinetic energy is dissipated by the tested vehicle's crumple zone).

### Step 1 — Convert velocity to SI units

$$
v = 56\,km/h = 56 \cdot \frac{1000}{3600} = 15.56\,m/s
$$

### Step 2 — Compute kinetic energy

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2
$$

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1400 \cdot (15.56)^2
$$

$$
(15.56)^2 = 242.1
$$

$$
E_k = 700 \cdot 242.1 = 169{,}470\,J \approx 169.5\,kJ
$$

### Step 3 — Compute effective energy dissipated

$$
E_{effective} = 0.60 \cdot 169.5\,kJ = 101.7\,kJ
$$

### Step 4 — Compute equivalent velocity if all energy is in the *single* vehicle (head-on into identical mass)

For a head-on identical mass collision, kinetic energy is shared:

$$
E_{per\,car} = \frac{1}{2} m v_{rel}^2 / 4
$$

For a relative speed of $v_{rel}$, the energy in *one* car = $\frac{1}{2} m (v_{rel}/2)^2 \cdot 2 = \frac{1}{4} m v_{rel}^2$.

Set equal to effective energy:

$$
101{,}700 = \frac{1}{4} \cdot 1400 \cdot v_{rel}^2
$$

$$
v_{rel}^2 = \frac{4 \cdot 101{,}700}{1400} = 290.57
$$

$$
v_{rel} = 17.04\,m/s = 61.4\,km/h
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 56 km/h barrier test is therefore representative of a *two-car head-on collision at a combined closing speed of ~61 km/h*, since each car dissipates only its half of the crash energy.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Test Modalities

| Parameter | ECE R94 Frontal | ECE R95 Side | ECE R17 Rear / Whiplash | FMVSS 216 Roof Crush |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| **Barrier type** | Rigid (full or 40% deformable) | Moving Deformable, 950 kg | Sled pulse, no barrier | Hydraulic platen |
| **Speed** | 56 km/h (R94) / 64 km/h (ODB) | 50 km/h | Equivalent to 80 km/h Δv | Quasi-static, 3× curb weight |
| **Impact angle** | 0° (longitudinal) | 90° (lateral) | 0° (rearward) | 0° (vertical) |
| **Primary dummy** | Hybrid III 50th, 5th | EuroSID II / SID-IIs | BioRID II (whiplash) | None (structural) |
| **Critical body region** | Head, chest, femur | Thorax, pelvis, head | Cervical spine | Roof structure |
| **Injury metric** | HIC, chest deflection, femur force | Rib deflection, pelvis accel | NIC, Nkm | Roof crush ≤ 127 mm |
| **Regulation limit** | HIC < 1000 | Rib deflection ≤ 42 mm | NIC < threshold | Crush ratio |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Full-Scale Barrier Test Process

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the complete functional workflow from test preparation through regulatory certification. This block-level architecture is preferred over a physical drawing because it captures the procedural and data flow that students must internalize for KTU Part B questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Preparation Module] --> A1[Vehicle Weighing: Unloaded + 90% Fuel + Dummies]
    A --> A2[Tire Inflation to OEM Spec]
    A --> A3[Brake Release, Gear Neutral]
    A --> A4[Stabilization at 20 deg C for 6 hours]
    A1 --> B[Dummy Installation Module]
    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B
    A4 --> B
    B --> B1[Driver: Hybrid III 50th Percentile Male]
    B --> B2[Front Passenger: Hybrid III 50th Percentile]
    B --> B3[Rear: 5th Female / 6 yr Child / 18 mo Infant as per Regulation]
    B1 --> C[Barrier Engagement Module]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Tow Cable Attached at Front Bumper Midline]
    C --> C2[Quick Release Mechanism at 0.5 m from Barrier]
    C --> C3[Velocity Trap Calibration at 1.0 m from Barrier]
    C3 --> D[Crash Event Module]
    D --> D1[Trigger High-Speed Cameras at 1000 fps]
    D --> D2[Data Acquisition at 10 kHz Sampling Rate]
    D --> D3[Sensors: Head Accel, Neck F M, Chest Deflection, Femur F, Tibia F]
    D1 --> E[Post-Crash Analysis Module]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[SAE CFC60 Filter for Head Accel]
    E --> E2[SAE CFC180 Filter for Chest and Femur]
    E --> E3[Compute HIC, NIC, CSI, TI]
    E3 --> F[Regulatory Compliance Check]
    F --> F1[HIC less than 1000]
    F --> F2[Chest Deflection less than 50 mm]
    F --> F3[Femur Force less than 10 kN]
    F --> F4[Tibia Index less than 1.0]
    F1 --> G{All Criteria Pass?}
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G -->|Yes| H[Type Approval Certificate Issued]
    G -->|No| I[Vehicle Rejected: Engineering Redesign Required]
    H --> J[Production Authorization and Market Release]
    I --> K[Manufacturer Iterates: Reinforce Crumple Zone, Add Airbags, Re-test]
    K --> A
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Type Approval Regulatory Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REG[Regulatory Authorities]
        R1[UNECE WP.29 - Geneva]
        R2[NHTSA - USA]
        R3[MoRTH and ARAI - India]
        R4[European Commission - Brussels]
    end
    subgraph STD[Technical Standards]
        S1[ECE Regulations: R94 R95 R17 etc]
        S2[FMVSS: 208 214 216 301]
        S3[AIS: 098 099 014 etc]
        S4[EU GSR: 2019 2144]
    end
    subgraph OEM[Vehicle Manufacturers]
        O1[Design and Engineering Team]
        O2[Crash Test Laboratory]
        O3[Quality and Homologation Department]
    end
    subgraph PROC[Approval Process]
        P1[Submit Prototype and Dossier]
        P2[Witness Test at Accredited Lab]
        P3[Issue of Type Approval Certificate]
        P4[Conformity of Production Audit]
    end
    R1 --> S1
    R2 --> S2
    R3 --> S3
    R4 --> S4
    S1 --> P2
    S2 --> P2
    S3 --> P2
    S4 --> P2
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    O3 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> O1
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Crash Energy Dissipation Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Crash Kinetic Energy: 170 kJ] --> B[Engine Bay Crumple Zone]
    B --> C[Front Longitudinal Members Buckling]
    C --> D[Firewall and Toe Board: 30 percent dissipation]
    D --> E[Steering Column Telescoping Collapse]
    E --> F[Restrained Occupant Deceleration: 20-25 g Average]
    F --> G[Restraint System Activation]
    G --> G1[Seat Belt Pretensioner Fires at 5 ms]
    G --> G2[Airbag Inflates at 15-30 ms]
    G --> G3[Load Limiter Releases Belt at 4-6 kN]
    G1 --> H[Final Energy: Residual 5 kJ in Occupant]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Biomechanically Tolerable Injury Level]
```
