# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEMUT526]`
**Define the term "Type Approval" as applied to passenger cars. Name the authority that issues type approval in India.**

**Model Answer:**
Type approval is the formal regulatory certification procedure whereby a national authority verifies that a vehicle prototype satisfies all applicable safety, emission, and anti-theft technical regulations before granting permission for mass production and market release.

In India, type approval for passenger cars is issued by the **Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH)** through testing and certification executed by **ARAI (Automotive Research Association of India)** and other designated test agencies such as iCAT (International Centre for Automotive Technology).

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Students often write "RTO" — this is **wrong**. RTOs issue *registration* certificates, not type approval. The distinction is critical for full marks.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`
**List any three internationally recognized full-scale barrier impact tests used in passenger car type approval, with their standard reference numbers.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Test Name | Standard Reference |
|---|-----------|--------------------|
| 1 | Full Frontal Rigid Barrier Test | **ECE R94** (adopted in India as **AIS 098**) |
| 2 | Side Moving Deformable Barrier Test | **ECE R95** (adopted as **AIS 099**) |
| 3 | Rear-end / Whiplash Sled Test | **ECE R17** (adopted as **AIS 017**) |
| 4 | US Frontal Occupant Protection | **FMVSS 208** |
| 5 | Side Impact Protection (US) | **FMVSS 214** |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common mark-loss areas in full-scale barrier test questions:
> 1. **Confusing ECE R94 with FMVSS 208** — these have *different* test velocities and barrier types.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** in numerical problems.
> 3. **Omitting the dummy type** (Hybrid III 50th vs 5th female) in test descriptions.
> 4. **Writing "AIS 94" instead of "AIS 098"** — AIS numbering does not match ECE numbering.
> 5. **Not stating the HIC threshold (1000)** explicitly.
> 6. **Citing fictitious "AIS 2020" standards** that do not exist; always verify the AIS number.

---

### PART B — Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]`

**(a) Describe in detail the Full Frontal Rigid Barrier Impact Test as specified in ECE R94. Include the test setup, dummies used, vehicle preparation, and the injury criteria evaluated. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Test Setup:**
- A complete production-intent passenger car (kerb mass + 90% fuel tank fill + dummy masses) is propelled, engine forward, into a rigid concrete block barrier.
- The barrier is vertical, faced with plywood (19 mm) backed by concrete of at least 70 MPa strength.
- Impact velocity: **56 km/h** (or 15.56 m/s), measured at impact trigger point ≤ 1 m from barrier.
- Impact angle: 0° (longitudinal, perpendicular to barrier face).

**2. Dummies Used:**
- Driver seat: **Hybrid III 50th percentile adult male** (77.7 kg, 1750 mm height).
- Front passenger seat: **Hybrid III 50th percentile adult male**.
- Rear seats (per AIS 098 amendments): out-of-position child dummies where required.

**3. Vehicle Preparation:**
- Gear in neutral, brakes released, parking brake released.
- Tires inflated to OEM specification.
- All doors closed, windows up, steering wheel centered.
- Stabilized at 20 ± 4 °C for at least 6 hours.
- Steering wheel positioned to "least protective" design position per OEM.
- Seats adjusted to design riding position.

**4. Instrumentation:**
- Head: tri-axial accelerometers at head CG (CFC 1000 filtered).
- Neck: upper and lower load cells measuring Fz, Fx, My.
- Chest: 3-element chest potentiometer for sternum deflection.
- Femur: axial load cell on each femur.
- Tibia: upper and lower load cells (Fx, Fz, My).

**5. Injury Criteria Evaluated:**

| Criterion | Symbol | Limit |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Head Injury Criterion | HIC36 | ≤ 1000 |
| Peak Head Acceleration (3 ms) | $a_{peak}$ | ≤ 80 g |
| Neck Injury Criterion | NIC | ≤ threshold |
| Chest Compression (deflection) | $\delta$ | ≤ 50 mm |
| Chest Acceleration (3 ms) | $a_{chest}$ | ≤ 60 g |
| Femur Compressive Force | $F_{femur}$ | ≤ 10 kN |
| Tibia Index | TI | ≤ 1.0 |

**6. Post-Test:**
Vehicle must maintain survival space, doors must be openable without tools (post-crash escape requirement), no fuel leakage > prescribed limits (per ECE R34 / AIS).

**(b) Compute the Head Injury Criterion (HIC) for the following head acceleration data recorded during a 56 km/h frontal barrier test. The data is sampled at 1 ms intervals and the relevant interval is from t = 10 ms to t = 25 ms. Data: a(10) = 5g, a(11) = 12g, a(12) = 22g, a(13) = 35g, a(14) = 42g, a(15) = 45g, a(16) = 40g, a(17) = 30g, a(18) = 22g, a(19) = 15g, a(20) = 10g, a(21) = 6g, a(22) = 3g, a(23) = 1g, a(24) = 0g, a(25) = 0g. State whether the head injury risk is acceptable per ECE R94. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the test interval and parameters.**

Given interval: $t_1 = 10\,ms = 0.010\,s$, $t_2 = 25\,ms = 0.025\,s$.

$\Delta t = t_2 - t_1 = 15\,ms = 0.015\,s$.

Note: $\Delta t = 15\,ms < 36\,ms$, so the **HIC15** convention applies (and is also HIC36-compatible since the interval is shorter).

**Step 2: Compute the average acceleration $\bar{a}$ over the interval using the trapezoidal rule.**

The integral $\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt$ is approximated as:

$$
\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt \approx \sum_{i=10}^{24} \frac{a_i + a_{i+1}}{2} \cdot \Delta t_{sample}
$$

where $\Delta t_{sample} = 1\,ms = 0.001\,s$.

Sum the accelerations:

$$
\sum a_i = 5 + 12 + 22 + 35 + 42 + 45 + 40 + 30 + 22 + 15 + 10 + 6 + 3 + 1 + 0 + 0 = 288\,g
$$

Number of trapezoidal sub-intervals = 15 (from index 10 to index 25, i.e., 16 points → 15 intervals).

Trapezoidal integral:

$$
\int_{10}^{25} a(t)\,dt = \frac{\Delta t_{sample}}{2} \cdot (a_{10} + 2a_{11} + 2a_{12} + \cdots + 2a_{24} + a_{25})
$$

Compute the weighted sum:

$$
a_{10} + a_{25} = 5 + 0 = 5
$$

$$
2 \cdot (a_{11} + a_{12} + \cdots + a_{24}) = 2 \cdot (12 + 22 + 35 + 42 + 45 + 40 + 30 + 22 + 15 + 10 + 6 + 3 + 1 + 0)
$$

$$
= 2 \cdot 283 = 566
$$

$$
\text{Total} = 5 + 566 = 571
$$

$$
\int_{10}^{25} a(t)\,dt = \frac{0.001}{2} \cdot 571 = 0.2855\,g \cdot s
$$

**Step 3: Compute the average acceleration.**

$$
\bar{a} = \frac{0.2855}{0.015} = 19.03\,g
$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the HIC equation.**

$$
HIC = (0.015) \cdot (19.03)^{2.5}
$$

Compute the power:

$$
19.03^2 = 362.14
$$

$$
19.03^{0.5} = \sqrt{19.03} = 4.362
$$

$$
19.03^{2.5} = 362.14 \cdot 4.362 = 1579.4
$$

Therefore:

$$
HIC = 0.015 \cdot 1579.4 = 23.69
$$

**Step 5: Conclude on pass/fail.**

Since $HIC = 23.69 \ll 1000$, the head injury risk is **acceptable** per ECE R94. In fact, the margin is exceptionally large (HIC < 1000 implies < 5% probability of AIS 4+ head injury per the Prasad-Mertz injury risk curve).

---

### PART B — Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, Internal Choice]`

**(a) Compare the ECE R94 Full Frontal Barrier Test with the FMVSS 208 Frontal Occupant Protection Test. Highlight the differences in barrier type, velocity, dummy instrumentation, and injury criteria. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | ECE R94 (Europe / India) | FMVSS 208 (USA) |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------|
| **Barrier type** | Rigid concrete wall, 100% overlap (and 40% offset deformable variant) | Rigid flat wall, full-width (100% overlap) |
| **Test velocity** | 56 km/h | 32–40 km/h (unbelted tests) + 48 km/h (belted test) |
| **Dummies** | Hybrid III 50th percentile | Hybrid III 50th percentile (also 5th female) |
| **Injury criteria** | HIC < 1000, chest deflection < 50 mm, femur < 10 kN | HIC < 1000, chest deflection < 63 mm, femur < 10 kN (less strict) |
| **Belted test** | Optional, but increasingly required | Required for compliance |
| **Offset test** | Yes (ODB at 64 km/h) | None originally (added later as part of NCAP) |
| **Fuel system integrity** | Per ECE R34 (linked) | FMVSS 301 (linked) |
| **Adopted in India as** | AIS 098 | Not directly adopted |
| **Philosophy** | Comprehensive occupant + structural | Tiered (belted vs unbelted) |

**Key Differences:**

1. **Velocity:** ECE R94 tests at higher velocity (56 km/h) than the original FMVSS 208 (which historically tested at 30 mph = 48.3 km/h for belted, lower for unbelted).
2. **Dummy instrumentation:** ECE requires more channels of chest deflection measurement (3-element vs 2-element in older FMVSS).
3. **Injury limits:** FMVSS 208 has historically permitted higher chest deflection (≤ 63 mm) than ECE R94 (≤ 50 mm), making ECE more stringent.
4. **Offset Deformable Barrier (ODB):** This is a distinctive ECE R94 / Euro NCAP innovation; the US system is still primarily full-width rigid barrier. ODB replicates a partial-overlap real-world crash and stresses the lower extremity and far-side occupant more aggressively.
5. **India's Position:** India has adopted ECE-aligned AIS 098 (frontal) and AIS 099 (side), not the US FMVSS system.

**(b) Explain in detail the Side Moving Deformable Barrier (MDB) test as specified in ECE R95. Include the moving barrier mass, velocity, dummies used, and the specific injury criteria for thorax, abdomen, and pelvis. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Test Setup:**

- A complete passenger car is stationary.
- A **Moving Deformable Barrier (MDB)** of total mass **950 ± 20 kg** strikes the driver's side (or passenger side, as required) of the stationary car perpendicularly.
- The MDB consists of an aluminum honeycomb deformable face mounted on a rigid trolley, with the face shaped to represent the front-end geometry of a typical car (bumper and bonnet profile).
- Impact velocity: **50 km/h** (13.89 m/s).
- The MDB's centerline must align with the R-point of the driver seat (or as per the test protocol's specific alignment).
- Impact direction: lateral (90° to vehicle's longitudinal axis).

**2. Dummies Used:**

- Driver seat: **EuroSID II** (European Side Impact Dummy, 50th percentile male).
- Optional rear-seat dummies as per the regulation.

**3. Injury Criteria Evaluated (ECE R95 / AIS 099):**

| Body Region | Criterion | Limit |
|-------------|-----------|-------|
| Head | HIC36 | ≤ 1000 |
| Head | Peak lateral acceleration (3 ms) | ≤ 80 g |
| Thorax (Rib) | Rib deflection (each rib, summed) | ≤ 42 mm total for upper ribs (typically 3 ribs measured) |
| Thorax | Chest acceleration (3 ms) | ≤ 60 g lateral |
| Abdomen | Abdominal force | ≤ 2.5 kN (sum of 3 abdominal load cells) |
| Pelvis | Pubic symphysis force | ≤ 6.0 kN |

**4. Post-Test Structural Requirements:**

- Survival space must be maintained for the driver.
- Side door must be openable (one-handed test).
- No intrusion of intruding components (e.g., B-pillar) into occupant survival space.
- Fuel system integrity (per ECE R34 / AIS 122).

**5. Engineering Significance:**

- The MDB test replicates a typical intersection "T-bone" crash, which is one of the most lethal real-world crash modes due to limited crush space on the side of a car (only one door + B-pillar separates occupant from impact).
- Modern cars counter this with side-curtain airbags, side-torso airbags, reinforced B-pillars, and load-spreading door beams.
- India's AIS 099 adoption has driven the proliferation of side airbags even in budget cars (Maruti, Tata, Hyundai).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU PEMUT526, Module 1**

- **Type Approval** is issued by MoRTH (India), tested at ARAI/iCAT; UNECE WP.29 issues global ECE regulations.
- The **1958 UN Agreement** underpins global ECE regulation mutual recognition among 60+ countries including India.
- The **1958 Agreement** is distinct from the **1998 Global Technical Regulations (GTRs)** agreement.
- A **Full-Scale Barrier Test** is a destructive, instrumented, regulatory crash test on a complete vehicle.
- **Three primary test modalities:** Frontal (ECE R94), Side (ECE R95), Rear/Whiplash (ECE R17).
- **ECE R94** uses a **56 km/h** full-frontal rigid barrier test (or 64 km/h ODB in Euro NCAP).
- **ECE R95** uses a **50 km/h**, **950 kg** Moving Deformable Barrier striking the side.
- **HIC threshold = 1000** (HIC36) is the global benchmark for head injury.
- **Chest Deflection threshold = 50 mm** (ECE) vs **63 mm** (FMVSS 208).
- **Femur Compression Force threshold = 10 kN**.
- **Tibia Index threshold = 1.0** (dimensionless combination of moment and force).
- **Hybrid III 50th percentile** is the standard adult male dummy (77.7 kg).
- **EuroSID II** is the side-impact-specific dummy.
- **BioRID II** is the whiplash dummy (rear impact).
- **SAE CFC 60** and **CFC 180** are the standard filter classes for injury calculation channels.
- **India has harmonized with ECE**, not FMVSS: AIS 098 = ECE R94; AIS 099 = ECE R95.
- **FMVSS 208** is the US frontal crash standard; **FMVSS 214** is the US side impact standard; **FMVSS 216** is the US roof crush standard.
- **A 56 km/h rigid barrier test** is energetically equivalent to a ~**61 km/h** two-car head-on crash.
- **Fuel system integrity** must hold post-crash (no leakage > limits per ECE R34 / AIS 122).
- **Post-crash door operability** is a *structural* test, not an *injury* test, and is critical for rescue.
- **Anthropometric percentile** matters: 50th male is the design baseline; 5th female and 95th male are tested in modern protocols for inclusivity.
- **Type approval is vehicle-type-specific**: a new variant (different engine, different bumper) requires *variant* or *extension* approval.
- **Conformity of Production (CoP)** audits continue *after* type approval — a certificate is not a one-time pass.

topics.title: Environmental standards: noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Environmental standards: noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Environmental Noise Standards (Automotive Context)?

**Environmental noise standards** in the automotive domain are a set of legally enforceable, internationally harmonized technical regulations that **limit the maximum permissible sound emission** from road vehicles (passenger cars, motorcycles, heavy commercial vehicles) under standardized test conditions. The objective is to:

- Protect human auditory health and community well-being.
- Reduce traffic noise pollution in urban and residential zones.
- Standardize measurement procedures globally so that vehicles tested in one country can be homologated in another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMUT526):**
> *"Environmental noise standards specify the maximum allowable sound pressure level emitted by motor vehicles, measured under strictly controlled drive-by and stationary test conditions, expressed in dB(A) referenced to 20 µPa."*

The governing international framework is primarily:
- **UNECE Regulation No. 51** (R51) – *Uniform provisions concerning the approval of motor vehicles having at least four wheels with regard to their noise emissions.*
- **UNECE Regulation No. 41** (R41) – For motorcycles.
- **ECE R28 / R51 / R138** – For audible warning devices and replacement silencing systems.
- **ISO 362-1:2022** – *Measurement of noise emitted by accelerating road vehicles.*
- **ISO 5130** – Stationary noise measurement.
- **AIS-018 / CMVR (India)** – Indian adaptation under Central Motor Vehicle Rules.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Shouting Neighbor" Problem

Imagine you live in an apartment building. Everyone is allowed to *speak* (run their car), but the building management has set a rule: **nobody may speak louder than 65 dB(A) at the boundary wall** (the microphone position). 

To make this rule enforceable, the management must:
1. **Define a measurement scale** (decibels with A-weighting – because human ears don't hear all frequencies equally).
2. **Standardize the test scenario** (drive-by at 50 km/h with engine at Wide Open Throttle from a defined gear ratio).
3. **Define the test environment** (a flat, open test site of ISO 10844 class with specific temperature/humidity).
4. **Set the limit value** (e.g., 74 dB(A) for passenger cars under R51 Rev.3).

The vehicle manufacturer is the "shouting neighbor" and the standard is the building rule. The car must "stay quiet enough" to be allowed on the road.

## 1.3 Core Physical Quantities (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Sound Pressure (p)** – Fluctuating pressure component superimposed on atmospheric pressure; measured in **Pascals (Pa)**.
- **Reference Sound Pressure (p₀) = 20 µPa (0.00002 Pa)** – Threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz.
- **Sound Pressure Level (Lp or SPL)** – Logarithmic measure expressed in **decibels (dB)**.
- **A-weighted SPL (LpA or dB(A))** – SPL filtered to mimic the human ear's response at moderate levels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why decibels?**
> Human hearing spans from 20 µPa (rustling leaves) to 200 Pa (jet engine at 1 m) — a ratio of 10,000,000 : 1. A linear scale is impractical. The decibel is a **logarithmic ratio** that compresses this huge range into manageable numbers (0 dB to 140 dB).

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of an Automotive Noise Standard

Every standard, whether R51, ISO 362, or AIS-018, defines the same three things:

1. **The Measurement Method** – Microphone position, vehicle speed, gear selection, throttle condition.
2. **The Test Environment** – ISO 10844 test track specification (surface, dimensions, weather).
3. **The Limit Value** – A numerical cap (e.g., 68 dB(A), 72 dB(A), 74 dB(A)) depending on vehicle category.

These three pillars will be the recurring theme throughout the following sections.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Decibel — Mathematical Foundation

The human ear responds **logarithmically** to sound pressure. A tenfold increase in sound pressure is perceived as a *doubling* of loudness. To capture this, we use a base-10 logarithm multiplied by 20 (because sound *intensity* is proportional to pressure *squared*).

### 2.1.1 Sound Pressure Level (SPL)

$$L_p = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right) \;\; \text{[dB]}$$

where:
- $L_p$ = sound pressure level in dB
- $p$ = RMS sound pressure in Pa
- $p_0 = 20 \,\mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa (reference)

### 2.1.2 Sound Intensity Level (L_I)

$$L_I = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) \;\; \text{[dB]}$$

where $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² (reference intensity).

### 2.1.3 Sound Power Level (L_W) — Used for Stationary Noise (ISO 5130)

$$L_W = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right) \;\; \text{[dB]}$$

where $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W (reference power).

### 2.1.4 Conversion Between SPL and SIL

Since $I \propto p^2$, the relationship between $L_I$ and $L_p$ is:

$$L_I = L_p - 10 \cdot \log_{10}(\rho \cdot c)$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of air (≈ 1.2 kg/m³)
- $c$ = speed of sound in air (≈ 343 m/s at 20 °C)
- $\rho c \approx 411.6 \; \text{kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}$ → $10 \log_{10}(411.6) \approx 26.14$ dB

So under free-field conditions:

$$L_I \approx L_p - 0.14 \;\; \text{dB (essentially } L_I \approx L_p\text{)}$$

## 2.2 A-Weighting — Mimicking the Human Ear

The human auditory system is **not equally sensitive** to all frequencies. At low loudness levels, the ear is most sensitive around 2–5 kHz and much less sensitive at very low (< 100 Hz) and very high (> 10 kHz) frequencies.

**A-weighting** is a standardized filter (IEC 61672) that adjusts raw SPL readings to match this 40-phon equal-loudness contour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Automotive noise limits are **always quoted in dB(A)**, never in raw dB. The A-weighting correction $A(f)$ is added to the SPL at each frequency band before summing.

The A-weighted SPL is computed as:

$$L_{pA} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( \sum_{i} 10^{(L_{pi} + A_i)/10} \right) \;\; \text{[dB(A)]}$$

where $L_{pi}$ is the SPL in the $i$-th octave/one-third-octave band and $A_i$ is the A-weighting correction (in dB) for that band.

### 2.2.1 Key A-Weighting Correction Values (Selected Octave Bands)

| Frequency (Hz) | A-Weighting $A(f)$ (dB) |
|---|---|
| 63 | -26.2 |
| 125 | -16.1 |
| 250 | -8.6 |
| 500 | -3.2 |
| 1000 | 0.0 (reference) |
| 2000 | +1.2 |
| 4000 | +1.0 |
| 8000 | -1.1 |

A **63 Hz** tone that produces 80 dB SPL contributes only **80 − 26.2 = 53.8 dB(A)** to the overall A-weighted level. This is why low-frequency engine rumble is partially "discounted" by the standard.

## 2.3 Combining Sound Levels (Energy Addition Rule)

When two independent sound sources operate simultaneously, their **sound pressures do not add directly** — but their **sound intensities do**. This gives the energy summation rule:

$$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( 10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10} \right) \;\; \text{[dB]}$$

**Quick approximate rules** (useful in numerical problems):
- Two **identical** sources: $L_{\text{total}} = L_1 + 3$ dB
- Two sources differing by **≥ 10 dB**: $L_{\text{total}} \approx L_{\text{louder}} + 0.5$ dB (the quieter one is negligible)
- Two sources differing by **3 dB**: $L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{louder}} + 1.8$ dB
- Two sources differing by **6 dB**: $L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{louder}} + 1.0$ dB

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Relation | Formula | Units | Remarks / KTU Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( p / p_0 \right)$ | dB | $p_0 = 20 \,\mu\text{Pa}$ |
| 2 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( I / I_0 \right)$ | dB | $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² |
| 3 | Sound Power Level | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( W / W_0 \right)$ | dB | $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W |
| 4 | A-weighted SPL | $L_{pA} = 10 \log_{10}\!\sum_i 10^{(L_{pi}+A_i)/10}$ | dB(A) | Automotive limits use this |
| 5 | Energy Addition | $L_\Sigma = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10}\right)$ | dB | Two-source rule |
| 6 | Doubling distance (-6 dB law) | $L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}\!\left( r_2 / r_1 \right)$ | dB | Free-field point source |
| 7 | Number of identical sources | $L_N = L_1 + 10 \log_{10}\!\left( N \right)$ | dB | $N$ identical incoherent sources |
| 8 | Approx. 3-dB rule | $L_\Sigma \approx L_1 + 3$ for $L_1 = L_2$ | dB | Use in numericals |
| 9 | Reference pressures | $p_0 = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa; $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² | — | Constant — remember! |
| 10 | Speed of sound in air | $c \approx 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | m/s | $T$ in °C, used in $L_I \to L_p$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse the factor **20** in SPL with the factor **10** in SIL. The reason: $L_p$ uses **pressure (linear in amplitude)** and $L_I$ uses **intensity (quadratic in amplitude)**, so the prefactor changes from 20 to 10.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Type Approval & Homologation** – Before a vehicle is sold in the EU, India, Japan, etc., it must pass the drive-by noise test (R51). Failure means the vehicle cannot be type-approved.
2. **NVH Engineering** – Vehicle design teams use these standards as a **target specification**; they tune intake/exhaust silencing, engine timing, and tire selection to meet it.
3. **Traffic Noise Mapping** – Municipal authorities use standardized pass-by measurements to build noise maps (EU Environmental Noise Directive 2002/49/EC).
4. **Acoustic Vehicle Alerting Systems (AVAS)** – EVs are *too quiet*; R138 requires them to emit artificial sound at low speeds, also measured per the A-weighted decibel scale.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sound Pressure Level Formula

### Step 1 — Define the Goal

We want a unit that:
- Reflects the *physiological* perception of loudness.
- Spans the range $p = 20 \,\mu\text{Pa}$ to $p = 200$ Pa without overflow.

The Weber–Fechner law (and Stevens' power law) suggests a **logarithmic** relationship between stimulus and perception.

### Step 2 — Start with a Ratio

Define the dimensionless ratio $R = p / p_0$. If we simply took $R$ itself, the value at jet engine would be $10^7$ — unmanageable.

### Step 3 — Apply Base-10 Logarithm

The smallest audible change in loudness corresponds roughly to a **factor of $\sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$ in pressure**, or a factor of 10 in *intensity*. This is the origin of the **Bel** unit (1 Bel = factor of 10 in intensity).

$$L_{\text{Bel}} = \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{I}{I_0} \right)$$

### Step 4 — Convert Bel → decibel

One Bel is too large a step for practical use, so we divide into 10 smaller steps — hence **deci**-bel:

$$L_{\text{dB}} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{I}{I_0} \right)$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $I \propto p^2$ (Plane Wave Relation)

For a plane acoustic wave, the time-averaged intensity is:

$$I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}}{\rho c}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = \frac{p^2}{p_0^2}$$

### Step 6 — Simplify

\begin{aligned}
L_p &= 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{p^2}{p_0^2} \right) \\
    &= 10 \cdot 2 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{p}{p_0} \right) \\
    &= 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{p}{p_0} \right) \;\; \text{dB}
\end{aligned}

This is the canonical SPL formula.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Doubling-Distance (-6 dB) Rule

### Step 1 — Start with Inverse-Square Law

For a point source radiating uniformly into free space, the sound **intensity** at distance $r$ is:

$$I(r) = \frac{W}{4 \pi r^2}$$

### Step 2 — Form the Ratio at Two Distances

\begin{aligned}
\frac{I(r_2)}{I(r_1)} &= \frac{r_1^{\,2}}{r_2^{\,2}}
\end{aligned}

### Step 3 — Convert to Decibels

\begin{aligned}
\Delta L_I &= 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{I(r_2)}{I(r_1)} \right) \\
           &= 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{r_1}{r_2} \right)^2 \\
           &= 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{r_1}{r_2} \right) \;\; \text{dB}
\end{aligned}

### Step 4 — Specialize to Doubling

Set $r_2 = 2 r_1$:

\begin{aligned}
\Delta L_I &= 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{1}{2} \right) \\
           &= 20 \cdot (-0.30103) \\
           &= -6.02 \;\; \text{dB}
\end{aligned}

Hence **doubling the distance reduces SPL by ≈ 6 dB**. This is the **-6 dB law** used in noise propagation and microphone calibration.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Energy Addition

**Problem:** Two independent sound sources produce $L_1 = 80$ dB(A) and $L_2 = 77$ dB(A) at the microphone. Find the combined SPL.

### Step 1 — Convert Each dB to Linear Power Ratio

\begin{aligned}
\frac{I_1}{I_0} &= 10^{80/10} = 10^{8} = 1.00 \times 10^{8} \\
\frac{I_2}{I_0} &= 10^{77/10} = 10^{7.7} = 5.012 \times 10^{7}
\end{aligned}

### Step 2 — Add the Intensities (Energies)

\begin{aligned}
\frac{I_\Sigma}{I_0} &= 1.00 \times 10^{8} + 5.012 \times 10^{7} \\
                    &= 1.5012 \times 10^{8}
\end{aligned}

### Step 3 — Convert Back to dB

\begin{aligned}
L_\Sigma &= 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( 1.5012 \times 10^{8} \right) \\
         &= 10 \cdot (8 + \log_{10} 1.5012) \\
         &= 10 \cdot (8 + 0.1764) \\
         &= 10 \cdot 8.1764 \\
         &= 81.76 \;\; \text{dB(A)}
\end{aligned}

### Step 4 — Compare with the +3 dB Approximation

If both sources were equal (80 + 80), the result would be exactly 83 dB. With the 3-dB difference here, we get +1.76 dB above the louder source — consistent with the **+1.8 dB rule** in the cheat sheet.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — A-Weighting Application

**Problem:** A vehicle's exhaust noise is measured in octave bands. Compute the overall dB(A) from the following octave-band SPLs:

| Band (Hz) | SPL $L_{pi}$ (dB) | A-weighting $A_i$ (dB) |
|---|---|---|
| 125 | 78 | -16.1 |
| 250 | 80 | -8.6 |
| 500 | 75 | -3.2 |
| 1000 | 70 | 0.0 |
| 2000 | 65 | +1.2 |
| 4000 | 60 | +1.0 |

### Step 1 — Apply A-Weighting to Each Band

\begin{aligned}
L_{pA}(125) &= 78 + (-16.1) = 61.9 \;\; \text{dB(A)} \\
L_{pA}(250) &= 80 + (-8.6)  = 71.4 \;\; \text{dB(A)} \\
L_{pA}(500) &= 75 + (-3.2)  = 71.8 \;\; \text{dB(A)} \\
L_{pA}(1000) &= 70 + 0.0    = 70.0 \;\; \text{dB(A)} \\
L_{pA}(2000) &= 65 + 1.2    = 66.2 \;\; \text{dB(A)} \\
L_{pA}(4000) &= 60 + 1.0    = 61.0 \;\; \text{dB(A)}
\end{aligned}

### Step 2 — Convert to Linear Intensities

\begin{aligned}
10^{61.9/10} &= 1.549 \times 10^{6} \\
10^{71.4/10} &= 2.754 \times 10^{7} \\
10^{71.8/10} &= 3.020 \times 10^{7} \\
10^{70.0/10} &= 1.000 \times 10^{7} \\
10^{66.2/10} &= 4.169 \times 10^{6} \\
10^{61.0/10} &= 1.259 \times 10^{6}
\end{aligned}

### Step 3 — Sum the Intensities

\begin{aligned}
\sum &= (1.549 + 27.54 + 30.20 + 10.00 + 4.169 + 1.259) \times 10^{6} \\
    &= 74.717 \times 10^{6}
\end{aligned}

### Step 4 — Convert Back to dB(A)

\begin{aligned}
L_{pA,\text{total}} &= 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( 7.4717 \times 10^{7} \right) \\
                     &= 10 \cdot (7 + \log_{10} 7.4717) \\
                     &= 10 \cdot (7 + 0.8733) \\
                     &= 10 \cdot 7.8733 \\
                     &= 78.73 \;\; \text{dB(A)}
\end{aligned}

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Note that the **250 Hz and 500 Hz** bands dominate the total. The 125 Hz band, despite having a high raw SPL of 78 dB, contributes little because of the heavy -16.1 dB A-weighting. This is why exhaust systems are tuned in the **mid-frequency (250–500 Hz)** range for noise control.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Automotive Noise Standards

| Standard | Scope | Test Type | Microphone Position | Limit Example (PCar) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **UNECE R51 Rev.3** | M, N categories (≥ 4 wheels) | Drive-by (accelerating) | 7.5 m from path centerline | 68 dB(A) (Phase 1), 65 dB(A) (Phase 2) |
| **UNECE R41** | L category (motorcycles) | Drive-by | 7.5 m | 73–80 dB(A) (varies by engine capacity) |
| **ISO 362-1:2022** | Method reference for R51 | Drive-by, ASR-controlled | 7.5 m | (Method, not a limit) |
| **ISO 5130** | Stationary noise | Engine at idle, 2500, 3500 rpm | 0.5 m | (Method) |
| **AIS-018 (India)** | All motor vehicles | Stationary + drive-by | 5 m / 7.5 m | 80 dB(A) (older) → aligning to R51 |
| **UN R138** | AVAS for EVs | Stationary synthetic sound | 1–2 m | 50–75 dB(A) at low speed |
| **SAE J986** | US pass-by | Drive-by | 15 m (or 7.5 m) | 80 dB(A) at 15 m (legacy US standard) |

## 3.6 Sample Numerical — ISO 362 Drive-by (Simplified)

**Problem:** A passenger car is tested per R51. Microphone records peak SPL of **72.4 dB(A)** during the pass-by. The background noise is 58.2 dB(A) and the ambient temperature correction $K_T$ is **-0.6 dB**. Calculate the corrected test result.

### Step 1 — Apply Background Noise Correction

If background is $\geq 10$ dB below signal → no correction needed. Difference = 72.4 − 58.2 = 14.2 dB, so no correction (already > 10 dB).

### Step 2 — Apply Temperature Correction

\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{result}} &= L_{\text{measured}} + K_T \\
                  &= 72.4 + (-0.6) \\
                  &= 71.8 \;\; \text{dB(A)}
\end{aligned}

### Step 3 — Compare with Limit (R51 Phase 1: 68 dB(A))

$$71.8 \;\text{dB(A)} > 68 \;\text{dB(A)} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{FAIL — type approval denied}$$

The manufacturer must either re-engineer the exhaust silencer or add acoustic damping material.

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of the Automotive Noise Standardization System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete workflow from physical sound generation to type-approval certificate, covering all major entities and standards involved in noise regulation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sound Source: Engine, Exhaust, Tire, Aerodynamics] --> B[Acoustic Field Propagation in Air]
    B --> C[ISO 10844 Test Track Specification]
    C --> D[Microphone at 7.5 m, Height 1.2 m]
    D --> E[Signal Conditioning and A-Weighting Filter IEC 61672]
    E --> F[Data Acquisition SPL vs Time]
    F --> G{Test Type Selector}
    G --> G1[Drive-By Test ISO 362 R51]
    G --> G2[Stationary Test ISO 5130]
    G --> G3[AVAS Test R138 for EVs]
    G1 --> H[Apply Background Temperature and Wind Corrections]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Compute LAeq Peak Pass-By Level]
    I --> J{Compare with Limit Value}
    J --> J1[Pass Type Approval Granted]
    J --> J2[Fail Re-engineer NVH Components]
    J2 --> K1[Exhaust Silencer Tuning]
    J2 --> K2[Intake Manifold Resonator]
    J2 --> K3[Tire Selection and Wheel Arch Lining]
    J2 --> K4[Engine Mount Optimization]
    K1 --> A
    K2 --> A
    K3 --> A
    K4 --> A
    J1 --> L[Homologation Certificate and Market Access]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of the A-Weighting Filter Chain

This schematic shows how raw sound pressure is processed through the A-weighting network to produce the dB(A) value that all automotive standards require.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Microphone Transducer] --> A2[Preamplifier and ICP Supply]
    A2 --> A3[Octave or One-Third-Octave Band Filter Bank]
    A3 --> B1[63 Hz Band]
    A3 --> B2[125 Hz Band]
    A3 --> B3[250 Hz Band]
    A3 --> B4[500 Hz Band]
    A3 --> B5[1000 Hz Band]
    A3 --> B6[2000 Hz Band]
    A3 --> B7[4000 Hz Band]
    A3 --> B8[8000 Hz Band]
    B1 --> C1[Add -26.2 dB]
    B2 --> C2[Add -16.1 dB]
    B3 --> C3[Add -8.6 dB]
    B4 --> C4[Add -3.2 dB]
    B5 --> C5[Add 0.0 dB]
    B6 --> C6[Add +1.2 dB]
    B7 --> C7[Add +1.0 dB]
    B8 --> C8[Add -1.1 dB]
    C1 --> D[Energy Summation 10 log10 Sum 10 Li slash 10]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    C7 --> D
    C8 --> D
    D --> E[Final LAeq in dB A]

```

## 4.3 Functional Flow of the Drive-By Test (UNECE R51 / ISO 362-1)

This block diagram illustrates the vehicle-side and instrumentation-side workflow during a single drive-by pass.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Test Track: ISO 10844 Class Surface] --> P2[Vehicle Entry Gate AA']
    P2 --> P3[Acceleration Zone: Target Speed Reached]
    P3 --> P4[Line PP': 20 m Acceleration Entry]
    P4 --> P5[Line BB': 10 m Before Microphone]
    P5 --> P6[MIC Position: 7.5 m Lateral, 1.2 m Height]
    P6 --> P7[Vehicles at 50 km/h WOT in 3rd or Pre-selected Gear]
    P7 --> P8[Line CC': 10 m Past Microphone]
    P8 --> P9[Cut Throttle Coasting]
    P9 --> P10[Compute Lmax from LpA Time History]
    P10 --> P11{Repeat for Multiple Runs}
    P11 --> P12[Statistical Average: Top 3 of 4 Runs on Each Side]
    P12 --> P13[Final Reported Lurban in dB A]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** R51 specifies four test lines on the track — **AA'** (entry gate), **PP'** (start of acceleration), **BB'** (10 m before microphone), and **CC'** (10 m after microphone). The vehicle crosses **BB'–CC'** with the throttle fully open. The microphone at the lateral 7.5 m position records the **maximum A-weighted SPL** during this interval. The final reported value is the **arithmetic mean of the three highest readings** from a set of four valid runs on each side of the track.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Read Before Attempting!**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Students frequently write "dB" when the question demands "dB(A)". A 5–7 mark question can lose 2 marks just for this.
> 2. **Factor of 2 vs 20:** SPL uses **20 log**, SIL uses **10 log**. Mixing them gives wrong answers; examiners check this in the first line itself.
> 3. **Linear addition of dB:** Never add 80 dB + 70 dB and write 150 dB. This is the **#1 most common mistake** in KTU papers.
> 4. **A-weighting is a correction, not a multiplier:** $L_{pA} = L_p + A(f)$. Don't confuse with the energy summation formula.
> 5. **Reference value $p_0 = 20 \,\mu\text{Pa}$ must be quoted explicitly** in derivations; many students omit this and lose 1 mark.
> 6. **R51 vs R41 confusion:** Passenger cars (M1) → R51; Motorcycles (L3) → R41. Examiners ask this directly.
> 7. **Forgetting the A-weighting table values** at 63 Hz and 125 Hz — these are the most-tested bands.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEMUT526]
**"Define the term Sound Pressure Level and explain why the A-weighted decibel is used as the standard metric in automotive noise regulations." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Sound Pressure Level (SPL):** It is the logarithmic measure of the RMS sound pressure of a sound wave, expressed in decibels, referenced to the threshold of human hearing.

$$L_p = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right) \;\; \text{dB, with } p_0 = 20 \,\mu\text{Pa}$$

**Why A-weighted dB(A) is the automotive standard:**

(1) **Human hearing response is frequency-dependent.** The ear is most sensitive in the 2–5 kHz range and less sensitive at very low and very high frequencies. A-weighting (IEC 61672) mathematically approximates the 40-phon equal-loudness contour.

(2) **Regulatory comparability** — since different countries and different time periods used different filter networks (B, C, D), the international community adopted A-weighting as the common metric in UNECE R51, ISO 362, AIS-018, and SAE J986.

(3) **Correlation with perceived annoyance** — community noise studies show dB(A) correlates better with subjective annoyance than unweighted dB.

Hence, all automotive noise limits — e.g., the R51 limit of **68 dB(A)** for passenger cars — are quoted in dB(A), not in raw dB.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEMUT526]
**"State the governing international standard for measurement of noise emitted by accelerating road vehicles. List the three key test parameters specified in it." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Governing Standard:** **UNECE Regulation No. 51 (R51)** harmonized with **ISO 362-1:2022** *"Measurement of noise emitted by accelerating road vehicles — Engineering method"*.

**Three Key Test Parameters:**

(1) **Test Track:** ISO 10844-class surface — flat, dense asphalt, specific texture depth, with a propagation zone free from reflective surfaces for at least 50 m on either side of the path.

(2) **Microphone Position:** 7.5 m lateral distance from the centerline of the vehicle path, 1.2 m above the ground, free-field oriented (45° incidence).

(3) **Vehicle Operating Conditions:** Approach speed of **50 km/h** with **wide-open throttle (WOT)** from line **BB'** (10 m before microphone line), engine in **third gear or a pre-selected gear** giving a target engine speed between 75% of rated power speed and rated power speed, measured by the **maximum A-weighted SPL** between lines BB' and CC'.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, PEMUT526]**

**(a) Derive the expression for Sound Pressure Level starting from the definition of the decibel. (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Define the Bel:**
The Bel is defined as the base-10 logarithm of the ratio of two powers (or intensities):

$$L_{\text{Bel}} = \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{I}{I_0} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Convert Bel to decibel (1 Bel = 10 dB):**

$$L_{\text{dB}} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{I}{I_0} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the plane-wave relation** $I = p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2} / (\rho c)$:

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = \frac{p^2 / (\rho c)}{p_0^{\,2} / (\rho c)} = \frac{p^2}{p_0^{\,2}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify using log property $\log(x^2) = 2 \log x$:**

\begin{aligned}
L_p &= 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{p^2}{p_0^{\,2}} \right) \\
    &= 10 \cdot 2 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{p}{p_0} \right) \\
    &= 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{p}{p_0} \right) \;\; \text{dB}
\end{aligned}

**Step 5 — State the reference value:**

$$p_0 = 20 \,\mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{Pa (threshold of hearing at 1 kHz)}$$

**Physical interpretation:** A doubling of sound pressure increases SPL by 6 dB; a tenfold increase in pressure increases SPL by 20 dB.

---

**(b) Two identical machines operate simultaneously and each produces a sound pressure level of 78 dB(A). What is the combined SPL? If one of them is switched off, by how many decibels does the level drop? (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Combined SPL using energy addition (two equal sources):**

$$L_\Sigma = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( 10^{78/10} + 10^{78/10} \right) = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( 2 \cdot 10^{7.8} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Simplify using $\log(2) = 0.301$:**

\begin{aligned}
L_\Sigma &= 10 \cdot \left( \log_{10} 2 + 7.8 \right) \\
         &= 10 \cdot (0.301 + 7.8) \\
         &= 10 \cdot 8.101 \\
         &= 81.01 \;\; \text{dB(A)}
\end{aligned}

(Equivalently, by the +3 dB rule for two equal sources: $78 + 3 = 81$ dB(A).)

**Step 3 — Drop when one source is switched off:**

$$\Delta L = 81.01 - 78 = 3.01 \;\; \text{dB(A)}$$

**Answer:** Combined SPL = **81 dB(A)**; the level drops by approximately **3 dB(A)** when one machine is switched off.

**Engineering implication:** Turning off half the noise sources only reduces the level by 3 dB, which is **just barely perceptible** to the human ear. Substantial noise reduction requires reducing the **loudest single source**, not simply adding more silencing.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023, PEMUT526]**

**(a) Explain the A-weighting network. Why are automotive noise limits expressed in dB(A) and not in raw dB? Provide the A-weighting correction for the 125 Hz, 1000 Hz, and 4000 Hz octave bands. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**A-Weighting Definition:**
A-weighting is a standardized frequency-weighting filter defined in **IEC 61672-1** (and historically IEC 179) that modifies the measured SPL in each frequency band to emulate the sensitivity of the human ear at moderate sound levels (around 40 phons). It is mathematically derived from the **Fletcher–Munson equal-loudness contours** for pure tones.

**Why dB(A) and not raw dB in automotive standards:**

(1) **Biological relevance:** The human auditory system is **non-linear** with frequency. At low levels, low-frequency sounds must be much more intense than mid-frequency sounds to be perceived as equally loud. Raw dB treats all frequencies equally and therefore over-estimates the annoyance of low-frequency rumble (e.g., diesel clatter) and under-estimates the annoyance of high-frequency whine (e.g., tire squeal).

(2) **Regulatory harmonization:** All major automotive standards (UNECE R51, R41, R138; ISO 362; SAE J986; AIS-018) use dB(A) to ensure global comparability of test results.

(3) **Correlation with community annoyance:** Epidemiological studies on traffic noise (e.g., ISO/TS 15666 surveys) show dB(A) correlates strongly with subjective complaint indices.

**A-Weighting Corrections (from IEC 61672-1):**

| Octave Band Center Frequency (Hz) | A-Weighting Correction $A(f)$ (dB) |
|---|---|
| 125 | **−16.1** |
| 1000 | **0.0** (reference) |
| 4000 | **+1.0** |

**Conclusion:** A 1000 Hz tone is taken as the reference (no correction), a 125 Hz tone is heavily attenuated, and a 4000 Hz tone receives a small positive boost — reflecting the human ear's mid-to-high frequency emphasis at typical listening levels.

---

**(b) The 1/3-octave band SPL measurements of a vehicle's exhaust are as follows:**

| Freq. (Hz) | 100 | 200 | 500 | 1000 | 2000 | 4000 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SPL (dB) | 82 | 80 | 76 | 70 | 64 | 58 |

**A-weighting corrections: −19.1, −10.3, −3.2, 0, +1.2, +1.0 respectively. Compute the overall A-weighted SPL using the energy summation method. (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply A-weighting to each band:**

\begin{aligned}
L_{pA}(100)  &= 82 + (-19.1) = 62.9 \;\text{dB(A)} \\
L_{pA}(200)  &= 80 + (-10.3) = 69.7 \;\text{dB(A)} \\
L_{pA}(500)  &= 76 + (-3.2)  = 72.8 \;\text{dB(A)} \\
L_{pA}(1000) &= 70 + 0      = 70.0 \;\text{dB(A)} \\
L_{pA}(2000) &= 64 + 1.2    = 65.2 \;\text{dB(A)} \\
L_{pA}(4000) &= 58 + 1.0    = 59.0 \;\text{dB(A)}
\end{aligned}

**Step 2 — Convert each to linear power ratio (×10ᴸ/10):**

\begin{aligned}
10^{6.29} &= 1.95 \times 10^{6} \\
10^{6.97} &= 9.33 \times 10^{6} \\
10^{7.28} &= 1.91 \times 10^{7} \\
10^{7.00} &= 1.00 \times 10^{7} \\
10^{6.52} &= 3.31 \times 10^{6} \\
10^{5.90} &= 7.94 \times 10^{5}
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 — Sum the intensities:**

\begin{aligned}
\Sigma &= (1.95 + 9.33 + 19.1 + 10.0 + 3.31 + 0.794) \times 10^{6} \\
       &= 44.484 \times 10^{6}
\end{aligned}

**Step 4 — Convert back to dB(A):**

\begin{aligned}
L_{pA,\text{total}} &= 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( 4.4484 \times 10^{7} \right) \\
                     &= 10 \cdot (7 + \log_{10} 4.4484) \\
                     &= 10 \cdot (7 + 0.6483) \\
                     &= 10 \cdot 7.6483 \\
                     &= 76.48 \;\; \text{dB(A)}
\end{aligned}

**Answer:** Overall A-weighted SPL ≈ **76.5 dB(A)**.

**Interpretation:** The **500 Hz band** (72.8 dB(A) after weighting) is the dominant contributor. This is typical of exhaust noise in the 2nd–3rd harmonic range of a 4-cylinder engine at cruise. The 100 Hz and 4000 Hz contributions are minor due to large A-weighting corrections.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Environmental Standards: Noise**

### A. Core Definitions
- ☐ **SPL:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$, $p_0 = 20 \,\mu\text{Pa}$
- ☐ **SIL:** $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$, $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m²
- ☐ **PWL:** $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$, $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W
- ☐ **dB(A):** SPL with IEC 61672 A-weighting applied

### B. Key Standards
- ☐ **R51 (UNECE):** Drive-by noise for M, N vehicles — limit **68 dB(A)** (Phase 1)
- ☐ **R41 (UNECE):** Drive-by noise for L-category (motorcycles)
- ☐ **R138 (UNECE):** AVAS for electric/hybrid vehicles
- ☐ **ISO 362-1:2022:** Engineering method for accelerating pass-by
- ☐ **ISO 5130:** Stationary noise measurement
- ☐ **ISO 10844:** Test track specification
- ☐ **AIS-018 (India):** Indian equivalent under CMVR
- ☐ **SAE J986:** US pass-by at 15 m

### C. Critical Numbers to Memorize
- ☐ $p_0 = 20 \,\mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa
- ☐ $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m²
- ☐ Microphone distance: **7.5 m lateral, 1.2 m height** (R51)
- ☐ Approach speed: **50 km/h** (R51)
- ☐ PCar limit: **68 dB(A)** (R51 Phase 1), tightening to **65 dB(A)** (Phase 2)
- ☐ A-weighting at 1 kHz = **0 dB** (reference)
- ☐ Doubling distance → **−6 dB** (free field)

### D. Quick Rules of Thumb
- ☐ Two equal sources → **+3 dB**
- ☐ Two sources differing by 10 dB → louder dominates (≈ +0.5 dB)
- ☐ Tenfold increase in pressure → **+20 dB**
- ☐ Hundredfold increase in intensity → **+20 dB**

### E. Common Mistake Traps
- ☐ Never add dB linearly (80 dB + 80 dB ≠ 160 dB; it equals 83 dB)
- ☐ Use **20 log** for SPL, **10 log** for SIL/PWL
- ☐ Always specify A-weighting when reporting automotive noise
- ☐ R51 is for cars (4+ wheels); R41 is for motorcycles
- ☐ A-weighting **reduces** low frequencies; it does not boost them

### F. Energy Summation Sequence (for Exam)
1. Convert each dB to linear via $10^{L/10}$
2. Add the linear values
3. Convert back to dB via $10 \log_{10}(\Sigma)$

### G. Vocabulary Quick-Glance
- ☐ **Lurban:** R51 final reported value (mean of top 3 of 4 runs)
- ☐ **WOT:** Wide Open Throttle
- ☐ **AVAS:** Acoustic Vehicle Alerting System (for quiet EVs)
- ☐ **NVH:** Noise, Vibration, Harshness (the engineering discipline)
- ☐ **Pass-by:** Drive-by noise test
- ☐ **Homologation:** Legal type-approval certification

> **End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: Environmental Standards: Noise**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Emission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automotive Emission (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> Automotive emission refers to the **unwanted by-products of the combustion process** in internal combustion (IC) engines and other evaporative processes in a motor vehicle, which are released into the atmosphere through the exhaust system, crankcase, or fuel system. These emissions are regulated stringently under national and international standards (e.g., **Bharat Stage (BS)** in India, **EURO norms** in Europe, **EPA Tier norms** in the USA) to mitigate environmental degradation and protect human health.

The three primary regulated pollutant families from automobiles are:
1. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** – Product of *incomplete combustion* of hydrocarbon fuels.
2. **Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)** – Fuel that escapes the combustion zone unburnt or partially burnt.
3. **Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx)** – Formed at *high in-cylinder temperatures* (>1500°C) from N₂ and O₂.
4. **Particulate Matter (PM)** – Primarily a **diesel engine** concern; soot from rich combustion zones.

> [!NOTE]
> **Greenhouse Gases (GHG):** CO₂, CH₄, and N₂O are also regulated under **Corporate Average Fuel Economy (CAFE)** and **CO₂ emission (g/km)** standards, though they are not directly toxic at low concentrations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Dirty Chimney Analogy" 🏭**
> Imagine your car engine is a factory chimney. You feed it a mixture of **air (mostly Nitrogen + Oxygen)** and **fuel (hydrocarbons)**. The ideal outcome would be pure **H₂O (water vapour)** and **CO₂ (carbon dioxide)**. However, the combustion is rarely perfect. Some fuel doesn't get a chance to burn (HC), some burns but doesn't get enough oxygen (CO), and at the extreme heat inside the chamber, nitrogen from the air itself reacts with oxygen to form NOx. The "dirt" coming out of this chimney is what the government wants to control using **emission standards** — like setting a maximum allowed "dirtiness level" for every kilometre the car travels.

**Real-World Snapshot:**
- A typical petrol car emits ~**120 g/km of CO₂** (BS-VI limit is 130 g/km).
- A heavy-duty diesel truck can emit **~5 g/kWh of NOx** under the **ESC (European Stationary Cycle)** — a metric defined by EURO VI.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Regulations

| **Metric Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Typical Unit** |
|-------------------|-------------|------------------|
| `g/km` | Mass of pollutant per kilometre driven | grams/kilometre |
| `g/kWh` | Brake-specific emission (engine dyno) | grams/kilowatt-hour |
| `% vol` | Volumetric concentration (e.g., CO% in exhaust) | percentage |
| `ppm` | Parts per million (HC, NOx) | ppm |
| `m⁻¹` | Smoke opacity (Hartridge / Bosch) | inverse metre |
| `BSFC` | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | g/kWh |
| **BSCO** | Brake Specific CO emission | **g/kWh** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Formation Mechanisms of Major Pollutants

#### 2.1.1 Carbon Monoxide (CO) Formation
- **Source:** Incomplete oxidation of fuel carbon due to **local rich air-fuel mixtures** (insufficient O₂).
- **Chemistry:** $2C + O_2 \rightarrow 2CO$ (instead of $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$).
- **Governing factor:** **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)**. Rich mixtures (AFR < 14.7 for petrol) → High CO. Lean mixtures → Low CO.
- **Why it matters:** CO binds to haemoglobin **200 times more effectively than O₂**, causing asphyxiation.

#### 2.1.2 Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC) Formation
- **Sources:**
  1. *Quench layer* — fuel trapped in the thin (~0.05 mm) layer near cylinder walls where temperature is too low to sustain flame.
  2. *Crevices* — fuel trapped in piston-ring grooves, head-gasket crevices.
  3. *Wall quenching* and *adsorption/desorption* of fuel in oil film.
  4. *Combustion chamber deposits* re-releasing fuel.
- **Equation (typical HC species):** $C_xH_y$ (e.g., Methane CH₄, Benzene C₆H₆ — both carcinogenic).

#### 2.1.3 Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx) Formation
- **Three primary mechanisms:**
  1. **Thermal (Zeldovich) NO** — Dominant at T > 1500°C. $N_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2NO \rightarrow NO_2$.
  2. **Fuel-bound NO** — From nitrogen content in fuel (diesel).
  3. **Prompt (Fenimore) NO** — Hydrocarbon radicals react with N₂ in the flame front.
- **Zeldovich Equations:**
$$O + N_2 \xrightarrow{k_1} NO + N$$
$$N + O_2 \xrightarrow{k_2} NO + O$$
$$N + OH \xrightarrow{k_3} NO + H$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The NO₂/NOx Ratio:** In modern EURO VI / BS-VI, both **NO** and **NO₂** are measured separately using a **Chemiluminescent Analyzer (CLA)**, since NO₂ is **~10× more toxic** photochemically.

#### 2.1.4 Particulate Matter (PM) Formation (Diesel)
- **Stages:**
  1. **Nucleation mode (0.005–0.05 µm):** Heavy HC condensation.
  2. **Accumulation mode (0.05–0.5 µm):** Soot aggregates + adsorbed HC.
  3. **Coarse mode (>0.5 µm):** Ash, wear debris.
- **Why diesels emit more PM:** Heterogeneous (non-premixed) combustion → local rich zones → soot.

### 2.2 Diesel Smoke – A Special Sub-Topic
Smoke is the **visible component of diesel PM**. It is measured by **opacity** (the fraction of light blocked by exhaust smoke).

> [!NOTE]
> **Smoke Number (SN) vs. Opacity (N):**
> $$N = 1 - (I/I_0) \times 100\%$$
> where $I$ = transmitted light intensity, $I_0$ = incident light intensity. Smoke Number is related to **Bosch number** (filter-paper blackness, 0–10 scale) and **Hartridge units** (scale 0–100).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Variables / Units** |
|-------------|-------------|------------------------|
| $BSCO = \frac{\dot{m}_{CO}}{\dot{W}_{brake}} = \frac{m_{CO}}{V_{fuel} \cdot \rho_{fuel}} \cdot \frac{1}{P_{brake}/3600}$ | Brake Specific CO emission | $m_{CO}$ in g, $P_{brake}$ in kW, $V_{fuel}$ in L |
| $NO_x = K \cdot e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT_{flame}}}$ | Arrhenius form of Zeldovich NO | $E_a \approx 320$ kJ/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K |
| $SN = (1 - R_d/10) \times 100$ | Smoke Number from Bosch darkness $R_d$ | dimensionless, 0–100 |
| $E_{g/km} = \frac{m_{pollutant}}{d_{test}}$ | Emission per km (driving cycle) | grams, km |
| $E_{g/kWh} = E_{g/km} \cdot \frac{1}{BSFC \cdot \rho_f}$ | Conversion: g/km ↔ g/kWh | $BSFC$ in kg/kWh, $\rho_f$ in kg/L |
| $BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P_{brake}}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | g/kWh |
| $\eta_{comb} = 1 - \frac{[CO] + [HC]}{[CO_2] + [CO] + [HC]}$ | Combustion efficiency (Orsat analysis) | ratio, 0–1 |
| $AFR_{stoich} = \frac{34.56}{1}$ (petrol) | Stoichiometric AFR | 14.56 ± 0.2 (typical) |
| $\dot{V}_{exh} = \dot{V}_{air} + \dot{V}_{fuel} - \dot{V}_{CO_2\_abs}$ | Exhaust volumetric flow | m³/s |
| $N = 1 - e^{-k \cdot L}$ | Beer-Lambert for opacity (k = extinction coeff.) | $L$ = optical path, m |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the conversion factor:** To convert g/km to g/kWh for a passenger car: multiply by **~30** (because 1 L fuel ≈ 0.74 kg ≈ 9.5 kWh; typical test car consumes ~6 L/100 km → 0.06 L/km).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban air-quality planning** (CPCB in India, EPA in USA) — sets ambient air quality standards.
- **Vehicle homologation** — A new car model must pass the **Type Approval** test before sale.
- **OBD (On-Board Diagnostics)** — Detects emission-system malfunctions (e.g., catalyst efficiency, EVAP leak).
- **Carbon-credit trading** — EU's **CO₂ pool**, India's **CO₂ emission targets for OEMs**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Between g/km and g/kWh

This is a **favourite KTU question**. Given a vehicle tested over a **NEDC cycle** giving emission in **g/km** and fuel consumption in **L/100km**, derive equivalent **g/kWh** for engine certification.

**Given:**
- Emission in driving cycle: $E_{g/km}$ (g/km) = 0.50 g/km (HC, for example)
- Fuel consumption: $FC$ (L/100 km) = 5.0 L/100 km
- Fuel density: $\rho_f$ = 0.745 kg/L (diesel)
- Lower Heating Value (LHV) of fuel: $LHV$ = 42.5 MJ/kg
- Engine efficiency at cycle-average point: $\eta_b$ = 0.32 (32%)

**Step 1 — Convert fuel consumption to mass per km:**

$$FC_{kg/km} = \frac{FC \cdot \rho_f}{100}$$

$$FC_{kg/km} = \frac{5.0 \cdot 0.745}{100} = 0.03725 \text{ kg/km}$$

**Step 2 — Convert mass-flow of fuel to chemical energy per km:**

The energy released per km of travel by burning the fuel:

$$E_{fuel/km} = FC_{kg/km} \cdot LHV$$

$$E_{fuel/km} = 0.03725 \text{ kg/km} \cdot 42.5 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg}$$

$$E_{fuel/km} = 1.583 \times 10^6 \text{ J/km}$$

**Step 3 — Convert energy per km to brake-work per km using engine efficiency:**

The brake work delivered at the crankshaft per km:

$$W_{brake/km} = E_{fuel/km} \cdot \eta_b$$

$$W_{brake/km} = 1.583 \times 10^6 \text{ J/km} \cdot 0.32$$

$$W_{brake/km} = 5.066 \times 10^5 \text{ J/km}$$

**Step 4 — Convert J/km to kWh/km (1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J):**

$$W_{brake/km} = \frac{5.066 \times 10^5}{3.6 \times 10^6} \text{ kWh/km}$$

$$W_{brake/km} = 0.1407 \text{ kWh/km}$$

**Step 5 — Convert g/km emission to g/kWh emission:**

$$E_{g/kWh} = \frac{E_{g/km}}{W_{brake/km}}$$

$$E_{g/kWh} = \frac{0.50}{0.1407} = 3.553 \text{ g/kWh}$$

**Final Answer:** The engine-equivalent HC emission is approximately **3.55 g/kWh**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Magic Factor" Shortcut:** For an average passenger car with $\eta_b \approx 0.32$, $\rho_f \approx 0.745$ kg/L, and $LHV \approx 42.5$ MJ/kg, the conversion from g/km to g/kWh can be approximated by:
> $$\boxed{E_{g/kWh} \approx \frac{E_{g/km}}{FC_{L/100} \cdot 0.9255}}$$
> where the constant $0.9255$ aggregates $\rho_f \cdot LHV \cdot \eta_b / (3.6 \times 10^6 \cdot 100)$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Smoke Opacity from Light Transmission

**Given:** A photometer measures incident intensity $I_0 = 1000$ lux and transmitted intensity $I = 250$ lux through exhaust smoke of optical path $L = 0.5$ m.

**Step 1 — Calculate opacity N:**

$$N = \left(1 - \frac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100\%$$

$$N = \left(1 - \frac{250}{1000}\right) \times 100\% = 75\%$$

**Step 2 — Convert opacity to extinction coefficient k (Beer-Lambert Law):**

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-kL} \Rightarrow k = -\frac{1}{L} \ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$$

$$k = -\frac{1}{0.5} \ln(0.25) = \frac{1.386}{0.5} = 2.772 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Bosch Smoke Number (BSN) using empirical correlation:**

For typical diesel exhaust, the Hartridge-to-Bosch approximation is:

$$BSN = 10 \cdot (1 - 0.01 \cdot N) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{k}{6}\right)^{-0.5}$$

$$BSN = 10 \cdot (1 - 0.75) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{2.772}{6}\right)^{-0.5}$$

$$BSN = 10 \cdot 0.25 \cdot (1.462)^{-0.5} = 2.5 \cdot 0.827 = 2.07$$

**Final Answer:** Opacity = **75%**, Extinction coefficient = **2.77 m⁻¹**, Bosch Smoke Number ≈ **2.07**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Combustion Efficiency from Orsat Analysis

**Given (Orsat apparatus readings):** CO₂ = 12%, O₂ = 4%, CO = 2%, HC ≈ 0% (neglected).

**Formula:**

$$\eta_{comb} = 1 - \frac{[CO] + [HC]}{[CO_2] + [CO] + [HC]}$$

**Substitution:**

$$\eta_{comb} = 1 - \frac{2 + 0}{12 + 2 + 0} = 1 - \frac{2}{14} = 1 - 0.1429 = 0.8571$$

**Final Answer:** Combustion efficiency = **85.71%**.

### 3.4 KTU Numerical Problem Set (Practice)

**Q1:** A petrol car emits 60 g/km of CO over the NEDC cycle. Fuel consumption is 6.5 L/100 km. Using $\rho_f = 0.745$ kg/L, LHV = 44 MJ/kg, $\eta_b = 0.30$, find the CO emission in **g/kWh**.

**Solution (apply the shortcut formula):**

$$E_{g/kWh} = \frac{60}{6.5 \cdot 0.9255} = \frac{60}{6.016} = 9.97 \text{ g/kWh}$$

**Q2:** During a full-load smoke test, the Hartridge reading was 55 units. Estimate the **opacity (%)** and the **light transmittance (I/I₀)**.

**Solution:** Hartridge scale 0–100 is directly opacity in %. So **N = 55%**, and $I/I_0 = 1 - 0.55 = 0.45$.

**Q3:** A diesel engine produces 0.85 g/kWh of PM at rated power 250 kW. If the vehicle runs 8 hours/day for 300 days/year, calculate **annual PM emission in kg**.

**Solution:**

$$m_{PM/yr} = 0.85 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/kWh} \cdot 250 \text{ kW} \cdot 8 \cdot 300$$

$$m_{PM/yr} = 0.00085 \cdot 250 \cdot 2400 = 510 \text{ kg/year}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Engine Combustion → Pollutant Formation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Air Induction] --> B[In-Cylinder Mixing]
    F[Fuel Injection] --> B
    B --> C{Combustion Quality}
    C -->|Incomplete| D[CO Formation]
    C -->|Wall Quench| E[HC Formation]
    C -->|High T 1500C| G[NOx Formation Zeldovich]
    C -->|Rich Local Zones| H[PM Soot]
    D --> I[Exhaust Manifold]
    E --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[After-Treatment]
    J --> K[TWC / SCR / DPF / LNT]
    K --> L[Tailpipe Emission]
    L --> M[Atmosphere]
    J --> N[EGR Recirculation]
    N --> B
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Emission Control System Architecture (BS-VI Compliant Diesel)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Engine Cylinder] --> EXM[Exhaust Manifold]
    EXM --> DOC[Diesel Oxidation Catalyst DOC]
    DOC --> DPF[Diesel Particulate Filter DPF]
    DPF --> SCR[Selective Catalytic Reduction SCR]
    SCR --> ASC[Ammonia Slip Catalyst ASC]
    ASC --> TAIL[Tailpipe]
    subgraph DEFSystem
        ADBLUE[AdBlue Urea Tank] --> INJ[Dosage Injector]
        INJ --> SCR
    end
    subgraph EGRLoop
        EXM --> EGRC[EGR Cooler]
        EGRC --> EGRV[EGR Valve]
        EGRV --> ENG
    end
    subgraph OBDSystem
        O2S[Lambda / NOx Sensor] --> SCR
        O2S --> ECU[ECU]
        ECU --> INJ
        ECU --> EGRV
    end
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Emission Measurement Setup (Engine Dynamometer)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FUELF[Flow Meter Fuel] --> ENG[Engine Under Test]
    AIRF[Flow Meter Air] --> ENG
    ENG --> DYN[Eddy Current Dynamometer]
    DYN --> LOAD[Load Cell]
    ENG --> EXH[Raw Exhaust]
    EXH --> CVS[Constant Volume Sampler CVS]
    CVS --> BAG[Background Bag]
    CVS --> DIL[Dilution Tunnel]
    DIL --> FILTER[PM Filter - Teflon]
    DIL --> FTIR[FTIR Analyzer NOx, HC, CO]
    DIL --> NDIR[NDIR Analyzer CO2 CO]
    DIL --> HCLD[Heated Chemiluminescent Detector NOx]
    FILTER --> WEIGH[Microbalance PM mass]
    FTIR --> DAQ[Data Acquisition]
    NDIR --> DAQ
    HCLD --> DAQ
    WEIGH --> DAQ
    DAQ --> PC[Reporting as g per kWh]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Decision Tree — Test Cycle Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Choose Emission Test Cycle] --> Q1{Region of Sale}
    Q1 -->|India| IND[BS-VI uses WLTP India]
    Q1 -->|Europe| EU[EURO VI uses WHTC WHSC]
    Q1 -->|USA| US[FTP-75 + US06 + SC03]
    Q1 -->|Japan| JP[JC08 Mode]
    IND --> Q2{Vehicle Class}
    Q2 -->|M1 N1| M1[IDC Indian Driving Cycle]
    Q2 -->|M2 M3 N2 N3| HDE[ISC Heavy Diesel]
    EU --> Q3{Engine Type}
    Q3 -->|HD Diesel| HDE2[WHSC Steady + WHTC Transient]
    Q3 -->|LD Diesel| Q4[WLTP Class 1 2 3a 3b]
    US --> Q5{Passenger Car}
    Q5 -->|Yes| FTP[FTP-75 Bag 1 2 3]
    Q5 -->|No| HDEUS[EPA Heavy-Duty Transient]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for Exams:** When asked to "draw a schematic of the emission measurement system," use **Figure 4.3** (CVS) as the template. KTU expects at least **6 labeled blocks** in such a question to award full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Automotive Emission. List the four major regulated pollutants from IC engines.**

**Model Answer:**
Automotive emission refers to the unwanted by-products of combustion and fuel evaporation released from motor vehicles into the atmosphere.
The four major regulated pollutants are:
1. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**
2. **Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)**
3. **Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx)**
4. **Particulate Matter (PM)**

*(Plus regulated GHGs like CO₂.)*
**(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Explain the Zeldovich mechanism of NOx formation in SI engines.**

**Model Answer:**
The Zeldovich mechanism, also called **thermal NO formation**, is dominant in SI engines where peak flame temperatures exceed 1500°C. The three reactions are:

$$O + N_2 \rightarrow NO + N$$
$$N + O_2 \rightarrow NO + O$$
$$N + OH \rightarrow NO + H$$

The rate is **exponentially sensitive** to temperature (Arrhenius form): the NO formation rate roughly **doubles for every 50°C rise** above 1500°C. Hence, lowering peak flame temperature via EGR, retarded timing, or water injection reduces NOx.
**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Full-Model Question

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — 14-Mark Model Question

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the formation mechanisms of **CO, HC, and NOx** in a Spark Ignition (SI) engine. Discuss how each pollutant varies with **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)** and **ignition timing**. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Carbon Monoxide (CO):** Formed by *incomplete combustion* when local AFR is **rich** (Φ > 1). The dissociation equilibrium $2CO \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + C$ shifts left at high temperature and low O₂ partial pressure. With **lean mixtures (Φ < 1)**, CO drops sharply because excess O₂ oxidises CO to CO₂. **(2 marks)**

**Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC):** Originate from (i) the **quench layer** near cold cylinder walls where flame cannot propagate, (ii) **crevice volumes** (piston ring land, head gasket), and (iii) **oil film absorption/desorption**. HC varies non-monotonically with AFR — minimum at slightly rich (Φ ≈ 1.05). **(2 marks)**

**NOx:** Formed via the **Zeldovich mechanism** at peak flame temperatures > 1500°C. Strongly dependent on (i) **peak temperature** — NOx drops 80% if peak T is reduced by 100°C, (ii) **residence time at high T**, and (iii) **O₂ concentration**. Hence NOx peaks at slightly lean mixture (Φ ≈ 0.95) where O₂ is available but T is still high. **(2 marks)**

**Effect of Ignition Timing:**
- **Advanced timing** → higher peak T → **NOx ↑**, **HC ↓** (better combustion).
- **Retarded timing** → lower peak T → **NOx ↓**, **HC ↑**, **CO ↑**, **BSFC ↑** (efficiency loss). **(1 mark)**

---

**Q. (b)** A passenger car emits **0.45 g/km of HC** and **0.060 kg/km of CO₂ equivalent** during a NEDC test. Fuel consumption is **5.8 L/100 km**. Compute the engine-specific HC emission in **g/kWh**. Use $\rho_f = 0.745$ kg/L, LHV = 44 MJ/kg, $\eta_b = 0.30$. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Mass of fuel per km:**

$$m_f = \frac{5.8 \times 0.745}{100} = 0.04321 \text{ kg/km}$$

**Step 2 — Energy from fuel per km:**

$$E_{fuel} = 0.04321 \times 44 \times 10^6 = 1.901 \times 10^6 \text{ J/km}$$

**Step 3 — Brake work per km:**

$$W_{brake} = 1.901 \times 10^6 \times 0.30 = 5.703 \times 10^5 \text{ J/km}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kWh:**

$$W_{brake} = \frac{5.703 \times 10^5}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 0.1584 \text{ kWh/km}$$

**Step 5 — HC in g/kWh:**

$$HC_{g/kWh} = \frac{0.45}{0.1584} = 2.841 \text{ g/kWh}$$

**Final Answer:** HC emission = **2.84 g/kWh** (engine-equivalent).
**(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Constant Volume Sampling (CVS)** system used for measuring automotive emissions. State the role of the **dilution tunnel** and **Teflon filter** in PM measurement. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram of CVS System:** Refer to **Figure 4.3** of Section 4. **(2 marks)**

**Working Principle:**
The CVS system draws a *fixed volume* of diluted exhaust (exhaust + filtered ambient air) through a sampling system, regardless of the engine's exhaust flow rate. The pollutant concentration is then measured in the **diluted stream**, and the total mass emission is calculated by multiplying the concentration (ppm or g/m³) by the **total diluted volume** drawn over the test cycle. **(2 marks)**

**Role of Dilution Tunnel:**
- It cools and dilutes the raw exhaust (typically 5–10× dilution) to **prevent condensation of heavy HC and water** before the PM filter.
- It simulates **atmospheric dispersion** and prevents artefacts in particle nucleation/aggregation.
- It enables PM collection at temperatures similar to ambient tailpipe conditions. **(1.5 marks)**

**Role of Teflon Filter:**
- The filter (47 mm diameter, 2 µm pore size) collects PM from a known volume of diluted exhaust.
- After sampling, the filter is **conditioned** (24 h at 22°C, 50% RH) and weighed on a **microbalance** (1 µg resolution).
- Mass of PM = (Filter weight after − Filter weight before) → gives PM in **mg/km** or **g/kWh**. **(1.5 marks)**

---

**Q. (b)** A diesel engine emits **4.5 g/kWh of NOx** and **0.05 g/kWh of PM** under the **WHTC (World Harmonised Transient Cycle)**. The engine delivers an average power of **180 kW** over a vehicle duty cycle of **150,000 km/year**. The vehicle averages **35 km/L**. Find:
(i) the annual fuel consumption in kg and litres.
(ii) the annual mass of NOx and PM emitted (in kg), assuming the same average specific emission in g/kWh. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Annual fuel consumption (volume):**

$$V_{fuel} = \frac{150{,}000}{35} = 4285.7 \text{ L/year}$$

**Step 2 — Annual fuel consumption (mass), with $\rho_f = 0.835$ kg/L:**

$$m_{fuel} = 4285.7 \times 0.835 = 3578.6 \text{ kg/year}$$

**Step 3 — Brake energy delivered per year:**

$$E_{brake} = P_{avg} \cdot t_{operating}$$

We need the operating hours. Using BSFC = 220 g/kWh (typical for modern diesel):

$$t = \frac{m_{fuel}/1000}{BSFC_{kg/kWh} \cdot P_{avg}} = \frac{3578.6/1000}{0.220 \cdot 180} = 0.0903 \text{ h}$$

*Wait — this is per km, so multiply by 150,000 km:*

$$t_{year} = 0.0903 \times 150{,}000 = 13{,}548 \text{ h/year}$$

**Step 4 — Annual brake energy:**

$$E_{brake} = 180 \text{ kW} \times 13{,}548 \text{ h} = 2.439 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 5 — Annual NOx:**

$$m_{NOx} = 4.5 \times 2.439 \times 10^6 = 10.97 \times 10^6 \text{ g} = 10{,}974 \text{ kg/year}$$

**Step 6 — Annual PM:**

$$m_{PM} = 0.05 \times 2.439 \times 10^6 = 1.22 \times 10^5 \text{ g} = 121.9 \text{ kg/year}$$

**Final Answer:** Fuel = **3579 kg/year** (4286 L/year); NOx = **10,974 kg/year**; PM = **121.9 kg/year**.
**(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions** (g ↔ kg, L ↔ m³, kJ ↔ kWh). Examiners will deduct **0.5–1 mark** for missing a single conversion step.
> 2. **Confusing g/km (chassis dyno) with g/kWh (engine dyno)**. Always state clearly which test you are modelling.
> 3. **Skipping the dilution-tunnel role** in CVS — the *cooling effect* is the key phrase examiners expect.
> 4. **Writing Zeldovich as "one equation"** — it is a *set of three* reactions. Writing only one loses 1 mark.
> 5. **In numerical answers, NOT boxing the final value** — examiners scan for the boxed answer; an unboxed answer may lose partial credit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — 60-Second Scan**

- ✅ **Four regulated pollutants:** CO, HC, NOx, PM (+ CO₂ as GHG).
- ✅ **CO** → rich mixture, incomplete combustion, AFR < 14.7.
- ✅ **HC** → wall quench, crevice, oil-film adsorption, deposits.
- ✅ **NOx** → Zeldovich (3 equations), T > 1500°C, exponential with T.
- ✅ **PM** → diesel-specific, soot + condensed HC, three size modes.
- ✅ **Zeldovich 3 equations:** O+N₂→NO+N; N+O₂→NO+O; N+OH→NO+H.
- ✅ **Test cycles:** NEDC, WLTP (Europe); FTP-75 (US); IDC (India); WHTC, WHSC (HD engines).
- ✅ **BSFC units:** g/kWh; **Emission units:** g/km (LD), g/kWh (HD).
- ✅ **Conversion shortcut:** $E_{g/kWh} \approx E_{g/km} / (FC_{L/100} \cdot 0.9255)$.
- ✅ **Smoke:** Opacity N (%) = $(1 - I/I_0) \times 100$; Bosch 0–10; Hartridge 0–100.
- ✅ **CVS role:** Constant volume of *diluted* exhaust sampled; dilution tunnel cools exhaust to prevent artefact PM.
- ✅ **Orsat analysis:** CO₂, O₂, CO measured; combustion efficiency = $1 - ([CO]+[HC])/([CO_2]+[CO]+[HC])$.
- ✅ **BS-VI / EURO VI emission limits (LD petrol):** CO = 1.0 g/km; HC+NOx = 0.16 g/km; NOx = 0.06 g/km; PM = 0.0045 g/km.
- ✅ **BS-VI (HD diesel, WHTC):** CO = 4.0 g/kWh; NOx = 0.46 g/kWh; PM = 0.01 g/kWh; NH₃ = 10 ppm.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric AFR petrol ≈ 14.56**, diesel ≈ 14.5; **λ = 1** at stoichiometric.
- ✅ **EGR** lowers peak T → reduces NOx; **SCR with AdBlue** converts NOx to N₂ + H₂O.
- ✅ **TWC (Three-Way Catalyst)** is for petrol: reduces CO, HC, and NOx simultaneously at λ ≈ 1.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel consumption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fuel consumption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fuel Consumption (FC)** is a quantitative performance and regulatory parameter that defines the volume (or mass) of fuel consumed by an internal combustion engine (ICE) or a complete vehicle to traverse a specified unit distance, or alternatively, the fuel consumed per unit of useful work or time, evaluated under strictly standardized test conditions prescribed by statutory bodies (ECE, EPA, BIS/AIS, ISO, SAE).

In regulatory language, the term is inseparable from **CO₂ emissions**, because the carbon content of the fuel dictates that any fuel burned produces a stoichiometrically fixed mass of CO₂, making **fuel consumption the single most regulated operational metric** of a road vehicle in the modern automotive sector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 – Syllabus Definition Snapshot**
> *Fuel consumption* = Quantity of fuel used per unit distance, expressed in **L/100 km** (Europe, India, most of Asia), **MPG** (US, UK) or **g/km of CO₂** (regulatory, type-approval).

### 1.2 Common Units of Fuel Consumption

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit | Region / Standard |
|----------|--------|---------------|-------------------|
| Volumetric FC | FC | **L/100 km** | EU, India (BIS/AIS), China |
| Distance per fuel | DPF | **km/L** | India (consumer), Japan |
| Fuel economy | FE | **MPG (US)** | USA (EPA) |
| Fuel economy | FE | **MPG (Imp.)** | UK, older Commonwealth |
| CO₂ specific emission | eCO₂ | **g CO₂/km** | EU, ECE R101, UN/ECE |
| Brake specific FC | BSFC | **g/kWh** | Engine dynamometer, ISO 3046 |
| Specific FC | SFC | **kg/kWh** | Industrial, marine (ISO 3046-1) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Identity**: $\dfrac{1}{\text{L/100 km}} = \dfrac{1}{100} \times \dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{L}}$. The two forms are reciprocals. Always invert before comparing values.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you drive a car from Kochi to Trivandrum (≈ 200 km). The fuel gauge drops from full to almost empty, and you refuel **38 litres** of petrol at the end of the trip.

Your **fuel consumption** is therefore:

$$\text{FC} = \frac{38 \text{ L}}{200 \text{ km}} \times 100 = 19 \text{ L/100 km}$$

Intuitively, **fuel consumption is the “fuel-thirstiness” of a car** — how much petrol the vehicle "drinks" for every 100 km it walks. Two cars starting the same trip will finish with different "drink levels" in their tank. The one with the higher L/100 km value is thirstier. Regulatory authorities don't test a car on real highways — instead they roll it onto a **chassis dynamometer** (a treadmill for cars) and follow a **standardized drive cycle** so that the "thirstiness" is measured in a way every manufacturer in the world can be compared on the **same scale**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why standardize the test?** A sporty driver and a careful driver will record very different fuel consumption on the same car. By forcing every car through an identical, robot-driven "robot driver" (the drive cycle), the *vehicle* is the only variable, making comparison fair and legally defensible.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Regulatory Chain: Why Fuel Consumption is Measured

A modern passenger vehicle's **type-approval certificate** (issued by the testing agency such as ARAI in India, TÜV in Germany, or EPA in the USA) **must contain** a declared fuel consumption value. This declared value:

1. Determines **CO₂-based road tax** in many jurisdictions (EU, Singapore, India under discussion).
2. Is used for **fleet-average CO₂ compliance** (e.g., 95 g CO₂/km fleet target for cars in EU 2025 phase-in).
3. Drives the **fuel economy label** displayed on the windshield at dealerships.
4. Forms the basis for **manufacturer penalties** if targets are missed.

### 2.2 Three Pillars of a Fuel Consumption Measurement

For *any* standard (ECE, EPA, AIS, ISO, SAE), the measurement rests on three pillars:

1. **The Drive Cycle** — A prescribed speed-vs-time profile (urban, extra-urban, combined).
2. **The Test Procedure** — Vehicle preconditioning, sampling method (CVS – Constant Volume Sampling), ambient conditions (T, P, humidity).
3. **The Calculation Formula** — How the raw fuel mass/volume is converted to the published metric.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Measurement (Chassis Dynamometer Method)

| Step | Action | Engineering "Why" |
|------|--------|---------------------|
| 1 | Place vehicle on a **chassis dynamometer** (single-roll or twin-roll) | Reproduces road-load resistance in a lab |
| 2 | Pre-condition the vehicle (soak at 20–30 °C, tire pressure set) | Removes start-up variability |
| 3 | Run the prescribed **drive cycle** (speed trace followed by robot driver) | Standardises the *driving* demand |
| 4 | Capture exhaust via **Constant Volume Sampling (CVS)** system | Allows bag collection of all exhaust gases |
| 5 | Measure total fuel consumed $\dot{m}_f$ (or volume $\dot{V}_f$) over cycle | Direct measurement of thirstiness |
| 6 | Compute FC, CO₂ mass, and pollutant emissions | Publishable, legally defensible value |

### 2.4 The Test Cycles (Regulatory Comparators)

| Standard / Region | Drive Cycle | Duration | Max Speed | Distance |
|-------------------|-----------|----------|-----------|----------|
| **ECE R101** (Europe, India) | NEDC (New European Driving Cycle) — 4× ECE-15 + 1× EUDC | 1180 s | 120 km/h | 11.007 km |
| **ECE R101 / EU 2017** | **WLTP** (World-harmonized Light vehicles Test Procedure) — Low / Medium / High / Extra-High | 1800 s | 131 km/h | 23.266 km |
| **India – AIS 144** | **MIDC** (Modified Indian Driving Cycle) | 1916 s | 90 km/h | ~10.57 km |
| **USA – EPA** | FTP-75 (Federal Test Procedure) | 1877 s | 91.3 km/h | 17.77 km |
| **USA – EPA** | HWFET (Highway) | 765 s | 96.4 km/h | 16.45 km |
| **USA – EPA** | US06 (Aggressive / High speed) | 596 s | 129.2 km/h | 12.89 km |
| **Japan** | JC08 | 1204 s | 81.6 km/h | 8.17 km |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot-Question**: "Why did India adopt the MIDC instead of NEDC?"
> *Answer skeleton*: Indian traffic is dominated by low-speed, stop-and-go, highly congested urban driving with lower average speeds than Europe. MIDC's profile (peak 90 km/h, average ≈ 32 km/h) better represents Indian road realities. AIS 144 (based on SIAM/ARAI trials) implements the MIDC.

### 2.5 Carbon Balance Method (Indirect FC from Exhaust)

Because modern multi-cylinder engines cannot measure fuel consumption directly in real time, regulations also allow a **Carbon-Balance Method** (per **SAE J1346** and **ISO 16183**). The idea:

*Measure the CO₂ in the exhaust → back-calculate the fuel mass burned.*

The mass of CO₂ emitted equals the carbon content of the fuel burned:

$$m_{\text{CO}_2} = m_{\text{fuel}} \times w_C \times \frac{M_{\text{CO}_2}}{M_C}$$

Solving for fuel mass:
