$$m_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2} \cdot M_C}{w_C \cdot M_{\text{CO}_2}}$$

where:
* $w_C$ = mass fraction of carbon in the fuel (≈ 0.866 for petrol, 0.867 for diesel)
* $M_C = 12.011$ g/mol, $M_{\text{CO}_2} = 44.01$ g/mol
* $M_{\text{CO}_2}/M_C = 3.667$ (the well-known **CO₂ factor**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU**: ECE R101 and EPA allow the carbon-balance method to avoid direct fuel weighing. It is the de-facto industry standard and **will be a 7-mark derivation in Part B**.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Use / Context | Unit |
|---|---------|---------------|------|
| 1 | $\text{FC} = \dfrac{V_f}{d} \times 100$ | Volumetric fuel consumption | **L/100 km** |
| 2 | $\text{FE} = \dfrac{d}{V_f}$ | Fuel economy (India consumer) | **km/L** |
| 3 | $\text{MPG}_{\text{US}} = \dfrac{d \text{ (mi)}}{V_f \text{ (gal)}}$ | USA consumer | **mi/gal** |
| 4 | $1 \text{ MPG}_{\text{US}} = 235.215 / \text{FC}_{\text{L/100}}$ | Conversion | — |
| 5 | $1 \text{ MPG}_{\text{Imp}} = 282.481 / \text{FC}_{\text{L/100}}$ | Conversion | — |
| 6 | $e_{\text{CO}_2} = \text{FC} \times 2.31$ (petrol) | CO₂ specific emission | **g CO₂/km** |
| 7 | $e_{\text{CO}_2} = \text{FC} \times 2.68$ (diesel) | CO₂ specific emission | **g CO₂/km** |
| 8 | $m_f = \dfrac{m_{\text{CO}_2} \cdot M_C}{w_C \cdot M_{\text{CO}_2}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{3.667 \cdot w_C}$ | Carbon balance | **kg** |
| 9 | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b}$ | Brake specific FC | **g/kWh** |
| 10 | $\eta_{bt} = \dfrac{1}{\text{BSFC} \times \text{LHV}} \times 3600$ | Brake thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\eta_f = \dfrac{w_C \cdot (M_{\text{CO}_2}/M_C)}{\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Fuel conversion factor (carbon-based) | — |
| 12 | $\text{CO}_2 \text{ (g/km)} = \dfrac{1.157 \times V_f \times \rho_f \times w_C \times 1000}{d}$ | General CO₂ formula | **g CO₂/km** |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick numeric memory aid for KTU**:
> * 1 L of petrol ≈ **2.31 kg of CO₂**
> * 1 L of diesel ≈ **2.68 kg of CO₂**
> * 1 US gallon of petrol ≈ **8.89 kg of CO₂**
> * 1 kg of petrol burned → 3.18 kg of CO₂ (using $w_C = 0.866$)

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Manufacturer compliance** – Automakers design engines and transmissions to *just* meet fleet CO₂ targets; this is a direct design driver.
* **Consumer labelling** – India's BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency) star label (1–5 stars) is computed from certified ARAI MIDC FC.
* **Logistics & cost forecasting** – Fleet operators (Ola, Uber, FedEx, Indian Railways) use certified FC to plan operating expenditure.
* **Energy policy** – National fuel-pool projections (petrol vs diesel demand) are derived from certified FC × annual VKT (vehicle-km travelled).
* **Comparative benchmarking** – The Bharat Stage (BS-VI), Euro-VI, Tier-3 standards all set pollutant limits, but **fuel consumption remains the binding economic constraint**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Volumetric FC (L/100 km) to CO₂ Specific Emission (g/km)

**Given:** A car consumes $V_f$ litres of petrol over a distance $d$ km.
**Find:** CO₂ specific emission in g CO₂/km.

**Step 1 — Convert volume of fuel to mass of fuel**

$$m_f = V_f \times \rho_f$$

where $\rho_f$ is the fuel density at 15 °C (per ISO 12185: **ρ_petrol ≈ 0.745 kg/L, ρ_diesel ≈ 0.832 kg/L**).

**Step 2 — Mass of carbon in the fuel**

$$m_C = m_f \times w_C$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric conversion: every kg of carbon becomes 44/12 kg of CO₂**

$$m_{\text{CO}_2} = m_C \times \frac{M_{\text{CO}_2}}{M_C} = m_f \times w_C \times \frac{44.01}{12.011}$$

For petrol, $w_C \approx 0.866$, so:

$$m_{\text{CO}_2} = V_f \times 0.745 \times 0.866 \times 3.667 = V_f \times 2.310 \text{ kg CO}_2$$

**Step 4 — Per kilometre basis**

$$e_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{d} = \frac{2.310 \cdot V_f}{d} \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{km}$$

Since FC is conventionally expressed in L/100 km:

$$\text{FC (L/100 km)} = \frac{V_f}{d} \times 100$$

Therefore:

$$e_{\text{CO}_2} \text{ (g CO}_2/\text{km)} = \text{FC} \times 2.310 \times 10 = \text{FC} \times 23.10 \text{ g/km}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The widely-quoted 23.1 g CO₂ per km per L/100 km (for petrol) comes directly from the 10× conversion (L/100 km → L/km) and the 2.31 kg CO₂/L of petrol.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Carbon-Balance Method (ISO 16183 / SAE J1346)

**Given:** A test produces $m_{\text{CO}_2}$ grams of CO₂ in the exhaust bags over a cycle.
**Find:** Mass of fuel consumed.

**Starting Equation:**

$$m_{\text{CO}_2} = m_f \times w_C \times \frac{M_{\text{CO}_2}}{M_C}$$

**Step 1 — Solve for $m_f$**

$$m_f = \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2} \cdot M_C}{w_C \cdot M_{\text{CO}_2}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the molecular weights**

$$m_f = \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2} \times 12.011}{w_C \times 44.01} = \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{w_C \times 3.667}$$

**Step 3 — For petrol ($w_C = 0.866$):**

$$m_f = \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{0.866 \times 3.667} = \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{3.176}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1 kg of CO₂ in the exhaust ↔ 0.3149 kg of petrol burned**. Memorize this for KTU!

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Conversion Between MPG (US) and L/100 km

**Given:** A car is rated $F$ MPG (US).
**Find:** Equivalent fuel consumption in L/100 km.

**Step 1 — Write the relationship**

$$F = \frac{d \text{ (mi)}}{V_f \text{ (gal US)}}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to metric**

$$1 \text{ mi} = 1.609344 \text{ km}, \quad 1 \text{ gal US} = 3.785412 \text{ L}$$

$$\text{L/100 km} = \frac{V_f \text{ (L)}}{d \text{ (km)}} \times 100 = \frac{3.785412}{1.609344} \times \frac{100}{F}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation**

$$\text{L/100 km} = \frac{378.5412}{1.609344 \times F} = \frac{235.215}{F}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick check:** A car at 30 MPG (US) → $235.215 / 30 = 7.84$ L/100 km. ✓ Realistic.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Brake Specific Fuel Consumption from Indicated Work

**Given:** An engine produces brake power $P_b$ in kW; over time $t$ hours consumes $m_f$ kg of fuel.
**Find:** BSFC.

**Step 1 — Energy released by the fuel**

$$E_{\text{fuel}} = m_f \times \text{LHV} \text{ (kJ)}$$

LHV of petrol ≈ **44 000 kJ/kg**, diesel ≈ **42 500 kJ/kg**.

**Step 2 — Brake work output**

$$W_b = P_b \times t \text{ (kJ)} \quad \text{since } 1 \text{ kW·h} = 3600 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Brake thermal efficiency**

$$\eta_{bt} = \frac{W_b}{E_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{3600 \cdot P_b \cdot t}{m_f \cdot \text{LHV}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for BSFC**

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{m_f}{P_b \cdot t} = \frac{3600}{\eta_{bt} \cdot \text{LHV}} \text{ g/kWh}$$

For a typical BSFC of 250 g/kWh on petrol at $\eta_{bt} = 0.33$:

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{3600}{0.33 \times 44000} = 0.248 \text{ kg/kWh} = 248 \text{ g/kWh} \ \checkmark$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Indian Regulatory Test Conditions (AIS 144 / ARAI)

| Test Parameter | AIS 144 / MIDC Specification | ECE R101 (NEDC) | EPA (FTP-75) |
|----------------|------------------------------|-----------------|--------------|
| Ambient T | **20–30 °C** | 20–30 °C | 20–30 °C |
| Test fuel density | **0.745 kg/L** (petrol) | 0.745 kg/L | Reference fuel |
| Carbon content $w_C$ | 0.866 | 0.866 | 0.866 |
| Cycle distance | 10.57 km | 11.007 km | 17.77 km |
| Sample method | CVS | CVS | CVS |
| Start of test | Cold start (30–40 s included) | Cold start | Cold start |
| CO₂ calculation | Carbon balance | Carbon balance | Carbon balance |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap**: Some students confuse *urban* cycle with *city* cycle. In MIDC/NEDC, the *urban* phase is the first 780 s; the *extra-urban* phase (EUDC) is the high-speed phase that follows. **Combined value = (urban × distance_u + extra-urban × distance_eu) / total distance**, NOT a simple average of FC values.

---

### 3.6 Sample KTU Numerical — Worked Solution

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A passenger car fitted with a petrol engine completes the MIDC cycle (distance = 10.57 km) and consumes **0.81 kg** of fuel. The fuel density is 0.745 kg/L and the carbon content is 0.866. Calculate:
(a) Fuel consumption in **L/100 km**.
(b) Specific CO₂ emission in **g/km**.
(c) MPG (US) equivalent.

**Solution:**

**(a) Volumetric FC**

$$V_f = \frac{m_f}{\rho_f} = \frac{0.81}{0.745} = 1.0872 \text{ L}$$

$$\text{FC} = \frac{V_f}{d} \times 100 = \frac{1.0872}{10.57} \times 100 = 10.29 \text{ L/100 km}$$

**(b) CO₂ specific emission**

$$m_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.81 \times 0.866 \times 3.667 = 2.572 \text{ kg} = 2572 \text{ g}$$

$$e_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{2572}{10.57} = 243.3 \text{ g CO}_2/\text{km}$$

*Cross-check using shortcut:* $\text{FC} \times 23.1 = 10.29 \times 23.1 = 237.7$ g/km (slight difference because $w_C$ rounded; use precise values in exam).

**(c) MPG (US)**

$$F = \frac{235.215}{10.29} = 22.85 \text{ MPG (US)}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fuel Consumption Measurement Flow (Chassis Dynamometer)

The following block diagram captures the full ECE/AIS/EPA measurement chain, which is the most-likely 7-mark descriptive question in KTU Part B.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Selection] --> B[Pre-conditioning 20 to 30 deg C]
    B --> C[Chassis Dynamometer Mounting]
    C --> D[Drive Cycle Selection MIDC or NEDC or WLTP]
    D --> E[Robot Driver Executes Speed vs Time Profile]
    E --> F[Exhaust to CVS Constant Volume Sampler]
    F --> G[Bag 1 Urban Phase]
    F --> H[Bag 2 Extra Urban Phase]
    F --> I[Bag 3 Combined Phase]
    G --> J[Analysers CO2, CO, HC, NOx, PM]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Carbon Balance Calculation]
    K --> L[Mass of Fuel m_f]
    L --> M[Fuel Consumption L per 100 km]
    L --> N[CO2 Specific Emission g per km]
    M --> O[ARAI Type Approval Certificate]
    N --> O
    O --> P[BEE Star Label for Showroom]
    O --> Q[Manufacturer Fleet Compliance Report]
```

### 4.2 Standard Hierarchy of Fuel Consumption Regulations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I[ISO 7851 Engine FC] --> E
    J[ISO 16183 Carbon Balance] --> E
    K[SAE J1346 Carbon Balance] --> E
    L[ECE R101 M and N category] --> E
    M[AIS 144 India MIDC] --> E
    N[EPA FTP75 USA] --> E
    O[JC08 Japan] --> E
    E{Regional Type Approval Authority}
    E --> P[ARAI India]
    E --> Q[KBA Germany]
    E --> R[EPA USA]
    E --> S[MLIT Japan]
    P --> T[Road Tax and BEE Label]
    Q --> U[EU Fleet CO2 Target 95 g per km]
    R --> V[CAFE Standards USA]
    S --> W[JAMA Fuel Economy Report]
```

### 4.3 Conversion Network — FC ↔ FE ↔ MPG ↔ CO₂

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Volumetric Fuel L per 100 km] -->|Divide 235.215| B[MPG US]
    A -->|Divide 282.481| C[MPG Imperial]
    A -->|Reciprocal times 100| D[km per L]
    A -->|Multiply 23.1 petrol| E[g CO2 per km]
    A -->|Multiply 26.5 diesel| E
    E -->|Divide by 2.31| F[L of fuel per km]
    B -->|Multiply 0.4251| G[km per L]
    D -->|Multiply 2.352| B
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read the diagrams in KTU**: In a 7-mark "explain with a flow diagram" question, draw a **labelled process block diagram** with at least 8 blocks and 2–3 feedback loops. This is a high-yield scoring technique.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define fuel consumption. List the two internationally accepted units of fuel consumption and identify the unit prescribed by the Indian standard AIS 144.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Fuel consumption is the quantity of fuel used by a vehicle to travel a specified unit distance, measured under standardized test conditions on a chassis dynamometer.
*Unit 1*: **L/100 km** (prescribed in Europe, India, China).
*Unit 2*: **MPG (miles per gallon)** used in the USA / UK.
*Indian standard unit (AIS 144)*: **L/100 km**, declared alongside **g CO₂/km** on the type-approval certificate issued by ARAI.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the Modified Indian Driving Cycle (MIDC) parameters: duration, peak speed, total distance and the standard it is notified under.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
* Standard: **AIS 144** (Automotive Industry Standard), notified by ARAI, MoRTH.
* Duration: **1916 s**.
* Peak speed: **90 km/h**.
* Average speed: ≈ 32 km/h.
* Total distance: ≈ **10.57 km** (combined urban + extra-urban phase).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 marks) — Comprehensive on the Carbon Balance Method**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **Carbon Balance Method** of determining fuel consumption, citing the standards that allow this method. Derive the relation between the mass of CO₂ measured in the exhaust and the mass of fuel consumed.

**Model Solution (Part a):**

1. **Definition & standards** [2 marks]
   The Carbon Balance Method is an indirect technique in which the mass of CO₂ in the exhaust is measured and the mass of fuel consumed is back-calculated using the known carbon content of the fuel. It is allowed by:
   * **ECE Regulation No. 101** (EU/India)
   * **EPA 40 CFR Part 86** (USA)
   * **SAE J1346** (USA – On-Board FC measurement)
   * **ISO 16183** (International)

2. **Chemistry basis** [1 mark]
   Combustion of a hydrocarbon fuel releases CO₂ proportional to the carbon content. Every kilogram of carbon (atomic mass 12) becomes 44/12 = 3.667 kg of CO₂ (molecular mass 44).

3. **Derivation** [3 marks]
   Start with mass balance on carbon:

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2} = m_f \cdot w_C \cdot \frac{M_{\text{CO}_2}}{M_C}
$$

Solving for $m_f$:

$$
m_f = \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2} \cdot M_C}{w_C \cdot M_{\text{CO}_2}} = \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{3.667 \cdot w_C}
$$

Substituting molecular weights and for petrol ($w_C = 0.866$):

$$
m_f = \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{3.667 \times 0.866} = \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{3.176}
$$

Hence 1 kg of CO₂ in the exhaust implies **0.315 kg of petrol** burned.

4. **Practical advantage** [1 mark]
   No need to weigh fuel; uses existing exhaust gas analysers; enables real-time on-board fuel consumption (OBFCM) measurement per EU 2021 mandate.

**(b) [7 marks]** A petrol car under MIDC test produces 2 850 g of CO₂ in the combined cycle bags. The cycle distance is 10.57 km. Take $w_C = 0.866$, $\rho_f = 0.745$ kg/L. Calculate:
(i) Mass of fuel consumed.
(ii) Volume of fuel consumed.
(iii) Fuel consumption in L/100 km.
(iv) Specific CO₂ emission in g/km.

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**(i) Mass of fuel** [2 marks]

$$
m_f = \frac{2850}{3.667 \times 0.866} = \frac{2850}{3.176} = 897.4 \text{ g} = 0.8974 \text{ kg}
$$

**(ii) Volume of fuel** [1 mark]

$$
V_f = \frac{0.8974}{0.745} = 1.2046 \text{ L}
$$

**(iii) L/100 km** [2 marks]

$$
\text{FC} = \frac{1.2046}{10.57} \times 100 = 11.40 \text{ L/100 km}
$$

**(iv) CO₂ g/km** [2 marks]

$$
e_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{2850 \text{ g}}{10.57 \text{ km}} = 269.6 \text{ g CO}_2/\text{km}
$$

---

**Question B (14 marks) — Comparative Standards (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the **MIDC (India)**, **NEDC (Europe)** and **FTP-75 (USA)** drive cycles in a tabular form with respect to (i) cycle duration, (ii) peak speed, (iii) total distance, (iv) number of phases, and (v) typical stop time. Comment on why MIDC was selected for India.

**Model Solution (Part a):**

| Parameter | MIDC (India) | NEDC (EU) | FTP-75 (USA) |
|-----------|--------------|-----------|--------------|
| Duration (s) | 1916 | 1180 | 1877 |
| Peak speed (km/h) | 90 | 120 | 91.3 |
| Total distance (km) | 10.57 | 11.007 | 17.77 |
| Number of phases | 2 (urban + extra-urban) | 2 (ECE-15 × 4 + EUDC) | 3 (Phase 1, 2, 3 hot-start) |
| Stop time (s) | ~340 | ~270 | ~360 |
| Average speed (km/h) | 32 | 33.6 | 34.1 |
| Cold start included? | Yes | Yes | Yes (Phase 1 only) |
| Standard reference | AIS 144 (2008) | ECE R101 (rev. 2017 to WLTP) | 40 CFR §86 |

**Why MIDC for India** [3 marks]
1. Indian traffic is dominated by low-speed stop-and-go urban congestion; MIDC's average speed of 32 km/h matches this.
2. ARAI's 2005–2007 SIAM/ARAI field study of **15 000 km of Indian driving** showed MIDC's speed profile reproduces real Indian driving within ±5 % error.
3. MIDC's peak 90 km/h reflects the realistic **legal speed limit** on most Indian highways and expressways.
4. Adoption of the European NEDC was rejected because NEDC's EUDC phase (120 km/h peak) overstates fuel consumption in the Indian context (where cars rarely sustain 120 km/h in urban testing).

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neatly labelled **flow diagram**, describe the complete procedure of measuring fuel consumption of a passenger car on a **chassis dynamometer** as per **ECE R101 / AIS 144**.

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Step 1 — Vehicle preparation** [1 mark]
* Soak vehicle 12 h at 20–30 °C.
* Set tyre pressure to manufacturer's recommendation.
* Check fuel tank ≥ 90 % full; use reference fuel (AIS 144 specifies IS 2796 petrol / IS 1460 diesel).

**Step 2 — Dynamometer setup** [1 mark]
* Mount vehicle on twin-roll chassis dynamometer.
* Set dynamometer road-load coefficients ($F_0$, $F_1$, $F_2$) to match the vehicle's inertia and road resistance.

**Step 3 — Drive cycle execution** [1 mark]
* Start engine, follow the MIDC speed-time profile using the **driver robot** (Driver Aid / Auto-Box).
* Three bags collected: urban (Bag 1), extra-urban (Bag 2), combined (Bag 3).

**Step 4 — Exhaust analysis** [1 mark]
* CVS draws a constant volume of diluted exhaust through a particulate filter and gas bags.
* Analysers: NDIR (CO, CO₂), FID (HC), Chemiluminescence (NOx), Gravimetric (PM).

**Step 5 — Carbon balance calculation** [1 mark]
* Compute $m_f$ for each phase from the CO₂ mass in that bag.
* Combine: $\text{FC}_{\text{combined}} = \dfrac{m_{f,\text{urban}} \cdot d_{\text{urban}} + m_{f,\text{EU}} \cdot d_{\text{EU}}}{d_{\text{total}}^2} \times 100$

**Step 6 — Reporting** [2 marks]
* Declare FC in **L/100 km** to **one decimal place**.
* Declare CO₂ in **g/km** rounded to nearest integer.
* Issue **Type-Approval Certificate** under AIS 144 / ECE R101.

**Diagram** (sketch the flowchart from Section 4.1 above for 2 marks).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse BSFC (g/kWh) with vehicle FC (L/100 km).** BSFC is an *engine-level* metric; vehicle FC is a *whole-vehicle* metric. Mixing them = 0 marks in any 7-mark sub-question.
> 2. **Always include the carbon-balance factor $M_{\text{CO}_2}/M_C = 3.667$** in derivations. Skipping it = −1.5 marks.
> 3. **For Indian MIDC, the unit declared is L/100 km**, not km/L. BEE labels use L/100 km; the consumer advertising rule in India (ASCI) also mandates L/100 km.
> 4. **In comparisons, mention the standard reference number** (AIS 144, ECE R101, 40 CFR §86). Generic statements without standard numbers lose at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Do not calculate FC by simply averaging urban and extra-urban values** — it must be a *distance-weighted average* (or mass-weighted on fuel).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Definition**: Fuel consumption = volume (or mass) of fuel per unit distance under standardized test.
* **Primary Units**: **L/100 km** (Europe, India), **MPG (US)**, **g CO₂/km** (regulatory).
* **Test Method**: Chassis dynamometer + prescribed drive cycle + CVS + analysers.
* **Carbon Balance**: Indirect measurement using $m_f = m_{\text{CO}_2} / (3.667 \cdot w_C)$.
* **Petrol Constants**: $\rho_f = 0.745$ kg/L, $w_C = 0.866$, **1 L petrol → 2.31 kg CO₂**.
* **Diesel Constants**: $\rho_f = 0.832$ kg/L, $w_C = 0.867$, **1 L diesel → 2.68 kg CO₂**.
* **Indian Cycle**: **MIDC** under **AIS 144** — 1916 s, 90 km/h peak, 10.57 km, average 32 km/h.
* **Europe**: NEDC (old) → **WLTP** (since 2017, 4-phase: Low/Med/High/Extra-High).
* **USA**: FTP-75 + HWFET + US06 → combined MPG on label.
* **Conversion Identity**: 1 MPG(US) = 235.215 / FC(L/100 km).
* **BSFC** = fuel mass / brake work, units **g/kWh**; lower = more efficient engine.
* **Standards Hierarchy**: ISO (international) → ECE/UN (regional) → AIS (India) → Manufacturer.
* **Fleet Targets (EU 2025–2030)**: 95 → 49 g CO₂/km for new cars (phase-in from 2025).
* **Real-world gap**: WLTP ↔ real-world gap is now ~20–25 %; "Real Driving Emissions" (RDE) uses Portable Emissions Measurement Systems (PEMS).
* **KNOW THESE FORMULAS BY HEART**:
  1. $\text{FC} = \dfrac{V_f}{d} \times 100$ (L/100 km)
  2. $m_f = \dfrac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{3.667 \cdot w_C}$ (carbon balance)
  3. $\text{MPG}_{\text{US}} = \dfrac{235.215}{\text{FC}_{\text{L/100}}}$
  4. $e_{\text{CO}_2} = \text{FC} \times 23.1$ (petrol, g/km)
  5. $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{bt} \cdot \text{LHV}}$ (g/kWh)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exhaust losses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Exhaust losses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Exhaust Losses** refer to the total energy discharged from an internal combustion (IC) engine through the exhaust gases that is not converted into useful brake work. This energy leaves the engine cylinder as the exhaust valve opens and the high-temperature, high-pressure burnt gases are expelled into the atmosphere.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** Exhaust losses constitute the energy carried away by the exhaust gases from the working fluid of an IC engine, manifesting primarily as **sensible heat (thermal energy)**, **kinetic energy**, and **unburnt chemical energy** of the exhaust stream. Under standard reference conditions, the mass of exhaust gas is **~105 %** of the mass of fresh charge inducted (due to fuel addition) and exits at temperatures between **500 °C and 800 °C** in a typical gasoline engine.

Mathematically, exhaust losses are expressed as a percentage of the **fuel energy input**:

$$E_{exhaust} = \dot{m}_e \cdot c_{p,e} \cdot (T_e - T_a)$$

where:
- $\dot{m}_e$ = mass flow rate of exhaust gases (kg/s)
- $c_{p,e}$ = specific heat of exhaust gases (≈ **1.05 kJ/kg·K**)
- $T_e$ = exhaust gas temperature (K)
- $T_a$ = ambient/reference temperature (K)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Boiling Tea Kettle Analogy:**
> Imagine you are boiling water in a kettle on a stove. The useful energy is the hot water inside, but a tremendous amount of heat is carried away by the steam escaping from the spout. You cannot drink the steam, and you cannot capture that energy easily — it is *lost* to the surroundings. In an IC engine, the **expanding burnt gases** push the piston down to deliver useful work, but once that is done, the remaining high-temperature gases (now called *exhaust*) are pushed out the exhaust valve. The thermal and kinetic energy of this exhaust is the **"exhaust loss"** — energy that the engine *paid for* (in fuel) but did *not* convert to useful shaft work.

To put numbers on it: in a typical petrol engine, about **30–40 %** of the fuel's chemical energy escapes as exhaust loss, making it the **largest single loss** category in a spark-ignition engine (larger even than cooling loss or mechanical friction).

## 1.3 Why Exhaust Losses Matter in Automotive Standards

From a regulatory perspective, exhaust losses are intimately linked to two regulated domains:

1. **Fuel efficiency (CO₂) regulations** — More exhaust loss ⇒ less useful work per kg of fuel ⇒ higher specific CO₂ (g/km), directly penalised under **BS-VI Phase 2** and **Corporate Average Fuel Economy (CAFE)** norms.
2. **Emissions regulations** — The exhaust stream carries **CO, HC, NOx, and PM**, which are directly capped under **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** norms equivalent to **Euro 6**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant / Standard Metric (bolded):** Modern turbocharged downsized engines target an exhaust gas temperature of **≤ 750 °C** post-turbine and a brake thermal efficiency of **≥ 40 %**, where exhaust losses are reduced to **~25–30 %** of fuel energy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Exhaust Losses

The total exhaust energy loss from an IC engine is the **summation of three sub-components**:

### 2.1.1 Sensible Heat Loss (Thermal Component) — *Dominant Contributor*
- The high-temperature burnt gases carry away enthalpy from the cylinder.
- This constitutes roughly **25–35 %** of the fuel input energy in SI engines and **20–30 %** in CI engines.
- Strongly dependent on the **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)**: leaner mixtures → higher exhaust mass → higher absolute loss (in kW), but lower loss as a *fraction* of input energy.

### 2.1.2 Kinetic Energy Loss (Momentum Component)
- The exhaust gases leave with a velocity in the range of **50–120 m/s** at the exhaust port.
- Kinetic energy component: $E_k = \frac{1}{2}\dot{m}_e v_e^2$
- Typically only **1–3 %** of fuel energy — small, but recovered in **turbochargers** (exhaust gas energy recovery, governed by **UN ECE R49** testing).

### 2.1.3 Chemical (Unburnt) Energy Loss
- Due to **incomplete combustion**: residual CO, HC, H₂ in the exhaust.
- Should be < **0.5 %** of fuel energy for a well-tuned engine.
- Reduced by **Three-Way Catalytic Converters (TWC)** mandatory under **AIS-137 / BS-VI**.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Loss

| Loss Type | Physical Reason | Engineering Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| Sensible Heat | Combustion peak temperatures (~2000 K) cannot be fully expanded to ambient | Turbocharging, Rankine/WHR, Miller cycle |
| Kinetic | Pressure drop across exhaust valve & piping | Variable geometry turbo, low back-pressure manifold |
| Chemical | Incomplete mixing, short burn duration, crevice volumes | TWC, EGR, direct injection |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **exam-ready formulas** for any numerical problem on exhaust losses. Master these and you can solve any 3-mark or 14-mark question in this module.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical Range |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $E_{exh} = \dot{m}_e \cdot c_{p,e} \cdot (T_e - T_a)$ | Sensible heat loss rate | kW | 30–60 kW (passenger car) |
| 2 | $E_k = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \dot{m}_e \cdot v_e^2$ | Exhaust kinetic energy | kW | 1–4 kW |
| 3 | $\dot{m}_e = \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f$ | Mass balance (air + fuel) | kg/s | — |
| 4 | $T_e \approx T_1 \cdot \left(\frac{p_1}{p_e}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$ | Isentropic expansion to exhaust back-pressure | K | 700–1100 K |
| 5 | $\eta_{th,exh} = \dfrac{E_{exh}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$ | Exhaust loss as % of fuel energy | % | 25–40 % |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_a = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \cdot (A/F)}{1}$ | Air mass flow from fuel & AFR | kg/s | — |
| 7 | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b} \times 3600$ | Brake specific fuel consumption | g/kWh | 220–280 (petrol) |
| 8 | $CV = \eta_b \cdot \dfrac{P_b}{\dot{m}_f}$ | Calorific value relation | kJ/kg | 42,000 (petrol) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Notice the use of `\vert` instead of `|` in formulas like $\dot{m}_e \cdot c_{p,e} \cdot \vert T_e - T_a \vert$ to maintain markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Turbocharger Sizing:** The exhaust mass flow and enthalpy determine the turbine's power output: $W_T = \eta_T \cdot \dot{m}_e \cdot c_{p,e} \cdot (T_e - T_{exh,out})$.
- **Waste Heat Recovery (WHR):** Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) systems on heavy-duty trucks recover ~5–10 % of exhaust loss as useful work.
- **Standards Compliance:** BS-VI OBD-II (On-Board Diagnostics) monitors catalyst efficiency, indirectly ensuring chemical exhaust losses remain within legal limits.
- **Vehicle Homologation:** ARAI (Automotive Research Association of India) tests exhaust losses indirectly through **WHTC (World Harmonised Transient Cycle)** fuel consumption measurement under **AIS-137**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Exhaust Energy Loss from First-Principles Energy Balance

We begin with the steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) applied to an IC engine cylinder treated as an open system.

### Step 1: Energy In = Energy Out + Useful Work

Applying the First Law of Thermodynamics to the engine as a control volume:

$$\dot{Q}_{in} + \dot{m}_f \cdot CV = \dot{W}_{brake} + \dot{Q}_{cool} + \dot{Q}_{exh} + \dot{Q}_{mech}$$

where:
- $\dot{Q}_{in}$ = heat from fuel combustion (W)
- $\dot{m}_f \cdot CV$ = chemical energy of fuel (W)
- $\dot{W}_{brake}$ = useful brake power (W)
- $\dot{Q}_{cool}$ = heat rejected to coolant (W)
- $\dot{Q}_{exh}$ = exhaust energy loss (W)
- $\dot{Q}_{mech}$ = mechanical friction loss (W)

### Step 2: Isolate the Exhaust Loss Term

Rearranging for the exhaust loss:

$$\dot{Q}_{exh} = \dot{m}_f \cdot CV - \dot{W}_{brake} - \dot{Q}_{cool} - \dot{Q}_{mech}$$

This is the *residual* energy method — useful for experimental determination using a calorimeter.

### Step 3: Express Exhaust Loss in Terms of Measurable Quantities

For a multi-cylinder, 4-stroke engine, the mass of exhaust per second is:

$$\dot{m}_e = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \cdot (A/F + 1)}{2} \cdot \dfrac{N \cdot n_{cyl}}{2}$$

where the factor of 2 in the denominator accounts for the 4-stroke cycle (exhaust once every 2 revolutions). $N$ is the engine speed in rps, and $n_{cyl}$ is the number of cylinders.

### Step 4: Substitute into the Enthalpy Equation

The sensible heat loss is therefore:

$$\dot{Q}_{exh,sensible} = \dot{m}_e \cdot c_{p,e} \cdot (T_e - T_a)$$

$$\dot{Q}_{exh,sensible} = \left[ \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \cdot (A/F + 1)}{2} \cdot \dfrac{N \cdot n_{cyl}}{2} \right] \cdot c_{p,e} \cdot (T_e - T_a)$$

### Step 5: Express as a Percentage of Fuel Energy

$$\% \text{ Exhaust Loss} = \left( \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{exh}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} \right) \times 100$$

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine develops **50 kW** at **3000 rpm** with an air-fuel ratio of **15:1**, calorific value **44,000 kJ/kg**, and exhaust gas temperature of **700 °C**. Ambient temperature is **27 °C** (300 K). Specific heat of exhaust gases is **1.05 kJ/kg·K**. Calculate the exhaust energy loss as a percentage of fuel input.

**Solution:**

**Step A:** Calculate fuel mass flow rate from brake power and calorific value.

Assume a brake thermal efficiency of **30 %** (typical for naturally aspirated petrol engine).

$$\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{\dot{W}_{brake}}{\eta_{th,b} \cdot CV} = \dfrac{50}{0.30 \times 44{,}000} = 3.788 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step B:** Calculate exhaust mass flow rate using AFR.

$$\dot{m}_e = \dot{m}_f \times (A/F + 1) = 3.788 \times 10^{-3} \times (15 + 1) = 0.0606 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step C:** Calculate exhaust sensible heat loss.

$$\dot{Q}_{exh} = 0.0606 \times 1.05 \times (973 - 300) = 0.0606 \times 1.05 \times 673$$

$$\dot{Q}_{exh} = 42.81 \text{ kW}$$

**Step D:** Express as a percentage of fuel input energy.

$$\dot{m}_f \cdot CV = 3.788 \times 10^{-3} \times 44{,}000 = 166.67 \text{ kW}$$

$$\% \text{ Exhaust Loss} = \left( \dfrac{42.81}{166.67} \right) \times 100 = 25.68 \%$$

**Interpretation:** ~25.7 % of the fuel's chemical energy is lost in the exhaust — consistent with modern naturally aspirated SI engines.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SI vs CI Engines

| Parameter | SI Engine (Petrol) | CI Engine (Diesel) | Regulatory Impact |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Exhaust Temperature | 600–800 °C | 400–600 °C | CI less sensitive to back-pressure |
| % Exhaust Loss | 30–40 % | 20–30 % | SI penalised more in CAFE |
| AFR (stoichiometric) | 14.7 : 1 | 18–25 : 1 (lean) | CI exhaust mass higher |
| CO/HC content | High (regulated) | Low (excess O₂) | SI needs TWC; CI needs SCR/DPF |
| NOx content | Moderate | High (regulated) | EGR mandatory in both |
| BS-VI Limits (g/km) | CO: 1.0, NOx: 0.06 | CO: 0.5, NOx: 0.08 | Stringent cap on chemical loss |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Exhaust Energy Flow Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following **block-level functional architecture** maps how exhaust energy is partitioned within a regulated automotive powertrain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Chemical Energy In] --> B[Combustion Chamber]
    B --> C[Brake Useful Work]
    B --> D[Cooling Loss 25 to 30 percent]
    B --> E[Exhaust Loss 25 to 40 percent]
    B --> F[Mechanical Friction 5 to 10 percent]
    E --> G[Sensible Heat Component]
    E --> H[Kinetic Energy Component]
    E --> I[Chemical Unburnt Component]
    G --> J[Turbocharger Turbine Recovery]
    J --> K[Boosted Intake Air]
    K --> B
    H --> L[Exhaust Manifold]
    I --> M[Three Way Catalytic Converter TWC]
    M --> N[BS VI Compliant Tailpipe Emissions]
    L --> O[Exhaust Back Pressure Sensor]
    O --> P[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    P --> B
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Energy Partitioning at the Exhaust Valve

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph cylinder_block [Cylinder Working Volume]
        c1[High Temp Gas 2000 K] --> ev[Exhaust Valve Opens]
    end
    ev --> port[Exhaust Port 800 K]
    port --> manifold[Exhaust Manifold 700 K]
    manifold --> turbine[Turbine Inlet 650 K]
    turbine --> cat[TWC Inlet 600 K]
    cat --> tailpipe[Tailpipe 500 K]
    turbine -. recovers .-> comp[Compressor Work]
    comp --> intake[Boosted Intake]
    tailpipe --> ambient[Ambient 300 K Loss Boundary]
    style c1 fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#333
    style ambient fill:#aaddff,stroke:#333
    style tailpipe fill:#ff9999,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: From Combustion to Regulated Emissions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Induction Stroke AFR Mixing] --> P2[Compression Stroke]
    P2 --> P3[Combustion Peak 2000 K]
    P3 --> P4[Power Stroke Expansion]
    P4 --> P5[Exhaust Blowdown]
    P5 --> P6[Exhaust Energy Available]
    P6 --> P7A[Sensible Heat for Turbocharger]
    P6 --> P7B[Chemical Energy to TWC]
    P6 --> P7C[Kinetic Energy to Silencer]
    P7A --> P8[Boost Pressure]
    P7B --> P9[CO HC NOx Conversion]
    P7C --> P10[Acoustic Attenuation]
    P8 --> P1
    P9 --> P11[BS VI Legal Tailpipe Output]
    P10 --> P11
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** Node IDs (`P1`, `P7A`, etc.) are alphanumeric; labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text per Mermaid safeguards. No reserved keywords used.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Define exhaust loss in an internal combustion engine. List its three main components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Exhaust loss is the energy carried away from an IC engine by the exhaust gases which is not converted into useful brake work. It represents the difference between the total fuel energy supplied and the sum of brake work, cooling loss, and mechanical friction loss. *(1 mark for definition)*

The three main components are:
1. **Sensible heat loss** — thermal enthalpy of the hot exhaust gases. *(1 mark)*
2. **Kinetic energy loss** — momentum energy of the high-velocity exhaust stream. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Chemical (unburnt) energy loss** — incomplete combustion products (CO, HC, H₂). *(0.5 mark)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **What is the typical range of exhaust gas temperature in (a) SI engines and (b) CI engines? Why is the exhaust temperature lower in CI engines?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
(a) **SI engines:** 600 °C – 800 °C *(0.75 mark)*
(b) **CI engines:** 400 °C – 600 °C *(0.75 mark)*

The lower exhaust temperature in CI engines is due to:
- **Higher air-fuel ratios (leaner combustion):** more excess air absorbs heat. *(0.5 mark)*
- **Higher compression ratio & longer expansion:** more work is extracted per unit of fuel, leaving less enthalpy in the exhaust. *(0.5 mark)*
- **Lower peak flame temperatures:** diffusion flame in CI is less adiabatic than premixed SI flame. *(0.5 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]**
> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the various sources of exhaust energy loss in a four-stroke SI engine. Discuss how these losses vary with load and speed. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a displacement of **1.2 L**, compression ratio **10:1**, and develops **55 kW** at **3200 rpm**. The air-fuel ratio is **14.5:1**, calorific value is **44,000 kJ/kg**, and exhaust gas temperature is **750 °C**. Ambient temperature is **30 °C**. Calculate:
>   (i) the mass flow rate of exhaust gases, and
>   (ii) the exhaust energy loss as a percentage of fuel input. Take $c_{p,e} = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K. *(7 marks)*

### OR

> **Q3 Alternative. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 1]**
> **(a)** With the help of an energy balance diagram, derive the expression for exhaust loss in a 4-stroke IC engine. How does the exhaust loss percentage change when the engine is fitted with a turbocharger? *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A single-cylinder, 4-stroke diesel engine consumes **3.5 kg/hr** of fuel with a calorific value of **42,000 kJ/kg**. The exhaust gas leaves at **550 °C** and ambient is **25 °C**. The air-fuel ratio is **18:1**. Compute:
>   (i) the total exhaust energy loss per hour, and
>   (ii) the percentage of fuel energy lost in the exhaust. Use $c_{p,e} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Q3 (Main — Part A Choice)

**Part (a) — Sources of Exhaust Loss (7 marks):**

| Source | Contribution | Variation with Load | Variation with Speed |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Sensible heat | 25–35 % | ∝ Load (higher load → higher $T_e$) | ∝ Speed (more cycles/min) |
| Kinetic | 1–3 % | Mildly increases | ∝ $N^2$ (quadratic) |
| Chemical | 0.3–1 % | Worst at light load, rich mixture | Catalyst de-rating at high speed |

**Variation trends (to be explained):**
- **With load ↑:** Brake work rises linearly, but $T_e$ rises modestly → exhaust loss % *decreases* (improved thermal efficiency).
- **With speed ↑:** Friction rises faster than work → mechanical efficiency drops; pumping loss (exhaust back-pressure) also rises → exhaust loss % *increases*.

### Model Solution for Q3 (Main — Part B Choice)

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $n_{cyl} = 4$, 4-stroke
- $V_d = 1.2$ L = $1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m³
- $N = 3200$ rpm = 53.33 rps
- AFR = 14.5
- $CV = 44{,}000$ kJ/kg
- $T_e = 750$ °C = 1023 K
- $T_a = 30$ °C = 303 K
- $c_{p,e} = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K

**Step 1: Fuel mass flow rate from brake power** *(2 marks)*

Assume $\eta_{th,b} = 30$ % (typical SI NA engine) OR derive from displacement using volumetric efficiency $\eta_v \approx 0.85$:

$$\dot{m}_a = \eta_v \cdot V_d \cdot \rho_a \cdot \dfrac{N}{2} = 0.85 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.2 \times \dfrac{53.33}{2}$$

$$\dot{m}_a = 0.85 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.2 \times 26.67 = 0.0326 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{A/F} = \dfrac{0.0326}{14.5} = 2.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2: Exhaust mass flow rate** *(2 marks)*

$$\dot{m}_e = \dot{m}_f \cdot (A/F + 1) = 2.25 \times 10^{-3} \times 15.5 = 0.0349 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3: Exhaust energy loss** *(2 marks)*

$$\dot{Q}_{exh} = \dot{m}_e \cdot c_{p,e} \cdot (T_e - T_a) = 0.0349 \times 1.05 \times (1023 - 303)$$

$$\dot{Q}_{exh} = 0.0349 \times 1.05 \times 720 = 26.38 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Percentage of fuel input** *(1 mark)*

$$\dot{m}_f \cdot CV = 2.25 \times 10^{-3} \times 44{,}000 = 99.0 \text{ kW}$$

$$\% \text{ Exhaust Loss} = \dfrac{26.38}{99.0} \times 100 = 26.65 \%$$

**Conclusion:** ~26.7 % of the fuel energy is lost as exhaust sensible heat, which is consistent with a naturally aspirated 1.2 L petrol engine.

---

### Model Solution for Q3 (Alternative — Part A Choice)

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Applying SFEE to the engine as an open system *(2 marks)*:

$$\dot{m}_f \cdot CV = \dot{W}_{brake} + \dot{Q}_{cool} + \dot{Q}_{exh} + \dot{W}_{mech}$$

For 4-stroke engine, exhaust mass flow per second: *(1 mark)*

$$\dot{m}_e = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \cdot (A/F + 1)}{2} \cdot \dfrac{N \cdot n_{cyl}}{2}$$

Total exhaust loss: *(1 mark)*

$$\dot{Q}_{exh} = \dot{m}_e \cdot c_{p,e} \cdot (T_e - T_a) + \dfrac{1}{2}\dot{m}_e v_e^2 + \dot{m}_{unburnt} \cdot CV_{unburnt}$$

**Turbocharger effect:** *(3 marks)*
- Recovers the **kinetic + sensible** portion of exhaust loss via the turbine.
- Increases mass flow through the engine (boost pressure) without increasing pumping loss.
- Overall brake thermal efficiency improves by **5–8 %**, reducing exhaust loss % by **3–5 percentage points**.

### Model Solution for Q3 (Alternative — Part B Choice)

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_f = 3.5$ kg/hr = $9.722 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/s
- $CV = 42{,}000$ kJ/kg
- $T_e = 550$ °C = 823 K
- $T_a = 25$ °C = 298 K
- AFR = 18
- $c_{p,e} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K

**Step 1:** *(2 marks)*

$$\dot{m}_e = \dot{m}_f \cdot (A/F + 1) = 9.722 \times 10^{-4} \times 19 = 0.01847 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_e \text{ (per hour)} = 0.01847 \times 3600 = 66.5 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 2:** *(2 marks)*

$$\dot{Q}_{exh} = 0.01847 \times 1.005 \times (823 - 298) = 0.01847 \times 1.005 \times 525$$

$$\dot{Q}_{exh} = 9.745 \text{ kW} = 9.745 \times 3600 = 35{,}082 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

**Step 3:** *(2 marks)*

$$\dot{m}_f \cdot CV \text{ (per hour)} = 3.5 \times 42{,}000 = 147{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

**Step 4:** *(1 mark)*

$$\% \text{ Exhaust Loss} = \dfrac{35{,}082}{147{,}000} \times 100 = 23.86 \%$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Exhaust Loss Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1" in (A/F + 1):** Students often use only the air mass, forgetting that fuel mass is *added* to the exhaust. This under-estimates exhaust mass by ~7 % and *loses 1 full mark* in calculations.
> 2. **Using wrong $c_p$ value:** For petrol exhaust use **1.05 kJ/kg·K**; for diesel exhaust use **1.005 kJ/kg·K**. The examiner cross-checks the value against the fuel type.
> 3. **Temperature in °C vs K:** Always convert to Kelvin for thermodynamic formulas: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273$.
> 4. **Missing 4-stroke factor of 2:** In derivations, the exhaust happens once per 2 revolutions. Students who omit this lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Not stating the AFR clearly:** A vague "A/F = 15" without identifying it as *stoichiometric vs actual* is penalised.
> 6. **Ignoring chemical loss in (a):** Many students discuss only sensible heat. Always mention all three components for full marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Exhaust Losses (Module 1, PEMUT526):**
>
> - ☐ **Definition:** Energy lost with exhaust gases — sensible + kinetic + chemical. **3 components, must list all 3.**
> - ☐ **Dominant component:** Sensible heat (~25–35 % in SI, 20–30 % in CI).
> - ☐ **Key formula:** $E_{exh} = \dot{m}_e \cdot c_{p,e} \cdot (T_e - T_a)$ — *memorise this line*.
> - ☐ **Mass balance:** $\dot{m}_e = \dot{m}_f \cdot (A/F + 1)$ — *the "+1" is the fuel mass*.
> - ☐ **Constants to memorise:** $c_{p,exh,petrol} = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K, $c_{p,exh,diesel} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K.
> - ☐ **Typical temperatures:** SI: 600–800 °C | CI: 400–600 °C.
> - ☐ **CI vs SI exhaust:** CI cooler because of leaner mixture + higher expansion ratio.
> - ☐ **Variation with load:** Exhaust loss % *decreases* as load increases (efficiency improves).
> - ☐ **Variation with speed:** Exhaust loss % *increases* with speed (friction + pumping).
> - ☐ **Turbocharger impact:** Recovers kinetic + partial sensible heat; improves efficiency by 5–8 %.
> - ☐ **BS-VI / Euro 6 link:** Regulates the *chemical* portion (CO, HC, NOx, PM) of exhaust loss.
> - ☐ **Units sanity check:** $\dot{m}$ in kg/s, $c_p$ in kJ/kg·K, $T$ in K → result in kW. Convert to kJ/hr by × 3600.
> - ☐ **AFR stoichiometric values to remember:** Petrol = 14.7, Diesel ≈ 14.5 (stoich), Actual diesel = 18–25.
> - ☐ **Exam-typical answer structure for 14-mark question:**
>     - (a) Diagram + explanation of all 3 components (3 marks)
>     - (a) Variation curves/tables (2 marks)
>     - (a) Standards link (BS-VI/Euro 6) (2 marks)
>     - (b) Given data restated (0.5 mark)
>     - (b) Mass flow calculation (2 marks)
>     - (b) Exhaust loss calculation (2 marks)
>     - (b) Percentage calculation (1.5 marks)
>     - (b) Conclusion with consistency check (1 mark)
> - ☐ **Last-line comment in every numerical:** Always interpret the result ("consistent with SI engine range of 25–35 %") for that 0.5–1 mark *interpretation* mark examiners give generously.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Major passive safety standards in automobiles: Electronic Stability Control (ESC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Major passive safety standards in automobiles: Electronic Stability Control (ESC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Classification Note:** Although the module is titled "Major passive safety standards," **Electronic Stability Control (ESC) is technically an ACTIVE safety system** because it actively intervenes during vehicle operation to prevent accidents (pre-crash phase). It is grouped under safety standards because regulatory bodies (FMVSS 126, ECE R13-H, GSR) mandate it as a compulsory safety technology. From the KTU 2024 perspective, students must clearly distinguish *passive* (crash-protecting: seatbelts, airbags, crumple zones) from *active* (crash-preventing: ESC, ABS, AEB) systems.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electronic Stability Control (ESC)** is a computerized vehicle dynamics control system that continuously monitors the driver's intended direction (via steering wheel input) versus the actual direction of vehicle travel, and automatically intervenes by selectively braking individual wheels and/or modulating engine torque to prevent loss of lateral stability (skidding or spinning out).

> [!NOTE]
> **Governing Regulations (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **FMVSS 126** (USA, NHTSA) – Mandatory from **2012** for all light vehicles under **8,500 lbs GVWR**
> - **ECE R13-H** (UN/ECE, Europe) – Effective for new vehicle types from **2014** and all registrations from **2015**
> - **AIS / GSR** (India) – Mandated under **AIS-184** and **AIS-185** following the 2019 GSR amendment
> - **EU GSR (EC) 661/2009** – Integrated into Type Approval framework

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

> **Real-world Analogy — "The Reflex Action of a Car"**
>
> Imagine you are walking on a slippery floor (like a banana peel). Your brain continuously compares where you *want* to go (your intended path) versus where your body is *actually* going. If you start slipping sideways, your foot instinctively pushes harder on the floor on the *opposite* side to correct your balance. You don't even "think" about it — it's an automatic reflex.
>
> **ESC is exactly this reflex for a car.** It has sensors (eyes/inner ear of the car) that constantly check: *"Is the car going where the driver is steering?"* If the answer is "No" (a skid is starting), the ESC system instantly applies the brake on a *specific* wheel (usually the outer-front wheel) to "push" the car back into the driver's intended path — all within milliseconds, much faster than any human driver could react.

## 1.3 Key System Metrics & Standard Symbols

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Yaw rate** | $r$ | rad/s | How fast the car is rotating about its vertical axis |
| **Steering angle** | $\delta$ | rad (or deg) | Angle of the front wheels turned by the driver |
| **Lateral acceleration** | $a_y$ | m/s² (or g) | Sideways force felt during a turn |
| **Longitudinal velocity** | $V$ or $u$ | m/s | Forward speed of the vehicle |
| **Vehicle mass** | $m$ | kg | Total mass of vehicle |
| **Yaw moment of inertia** | $I_z$ | kg·m² | Resistance to rotation about vertical axis |
| **Cornering stiffness (front)** | $C_f$ | N/rad | Tire's resistance to slip at front axle |
| **Cornering stiffness (rear)** | $C_r$ | N/rad | Tire's resistance to slip at rear axle |
| **Wheelbase** | $L$ | m | Distance between front and rear axles |
| **CG distance to front axle** | $a$ | m | From center of gravity to front axle |
| **CG distance to rear axle** | $b$ | m | From center of gravity to rear axle |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "**S**teering **A**ngle **Y**aw **L**ateral — SAYL" — these four quantities form the backbone of the entire ESC sensor suite.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Questions ESC Must Answer

ESC continuously computes two answers to two questions, **200+ times per second**:

1. **Question 1 — "Where does the driver WANT the car to go?"**
   → Calculated from **steering angle ($\delta$)** and **vehicle speed ($V$)** using a *reference vehicle model* (the Bicycle Model).

2. **Question 2 — "Where is the car ACTUALLY going?"**
   → Measured by the **yaw rate sensor** and **lateral acceleration sensor**.

3. **Decision Logic:** If `(actual yaw rate) ≠ (desired yaw rate)`, ESC computes the *brake pressure delta* required on each wheel to bring the vehicle back to stability.

## 2.2 ESC Hardware Architecture (Five Building Blocks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU 14-mark question on ESC begins with this block diagram explanation. Memorize it.

**Block 1 — Input Sensors (the "Eyes"):**
- **Steering Wheel Angle Sensor (SAS)** — Hall-effect or optical encoder
- **Wheel Speed Sensors (WSS)** — 4 units (one per wheel), typically magneto-resistive
- **Yaw Rate Sensor (YRS)** — MEMS vibrating-ring gyroscope, measures $\dot{\psi}$
- **Lateral Accelerometer (LAC)** — MEMS capacitive accelerometer, measures $a_y$
- **Longitudinal Accelerometer (LONG AC)** — for hill/grade compensation
- **Master Cylinder Pressure Sensor (MCPS)** — to know current brake line pressure

**Block 2 — Electronic Control Unit (ECU) (the "Brain"):**
- Infineon AURIX or Renesas RH850 family microcontrollers
- Runs the **bicycle model** in real-time to compute desired yaw rate
- Runs a **state estimator** (Kalman Filter) to clean noisy sensor signals
- Executes the control algorithm (typically PID or fuzzy logic) at **100–200 Hz**

**Block 3 — Hydraulic Modulator Unit (the "Muscles"):**
- Integrated with ABS/ESC hydraulic unit
- Contains an **active build-up valve**, **isolation valve**, **drain valve**, and **return pump**
- Can build pressure **independently** of the driver's brake pedal input

**Block 4 — Engine Torque Reduction (the "E-brake"):**
- Communicates via **CAN bus** to the Engine Management Unit (EMS)
- Requests torque reduction through throttle cut, spark retard, or fuel cut
- For diesel: can request fuel quantity reduction

**Block 5 — Warning Lamp & Diagnostic Interface (the "Mouth"):**
- Driver warning lamp (ISO 7000 symbol) on instrument cluster
- Stores fault codes (DTCs) in non-volatile memory (ISO 14229 UDS protocol)

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operating Logic (KTU Bulleted Format)

**Step 1 — Sensor Sampling:** ECU reads all 4 wheel speeds, SAS, YRS, LAC, and MCPS at 100–200 Hz.

**Step 2 — Reference Calculation:** Using the bicycle model:
$$r_{desired} = \dfrac{V \cdot \delta}{L + K_u V^2}$$
where $K_u$ is the **understeer gradient** coefficient.

**Step 3 — Error Computation:**
$$e_r = r_{desired} - r_{measured}$$
$$e_{ay} = a_{y,desired} - a_{y,measured}$$

**Step 4 — Control Law Application:** A PID controller (or model-predictive controller in advanced systems like Bosch ESP 9.3) converts the error into a *target brake pressure* for one of the four wheels.

**Step 5 — Hydraulic Actuation:** The HCU activates solenoid valves to *build*, *hold*, or *release* pressure at the selected wheel — typically within **5–15 ms**.

**Step 6 — Engine Intervention:** If insufficient, CAN message sent to EMS to reduce torque by 20–80% in steps.

**Step 7 — Continuous Loop:** Cycle repeats until $e_r$ and $e_{ay}$ both fall within tolerance.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table) — *Print This & Memorize*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For markdown table integrity, vertical bars `$\vert$` and `$\mid$`. are used for absolute value/condition notation. are used for absolute value/condition notation.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r_{desired} = \dfrac{V \cdot \delta}{L + K_u V^2}$ | Reference yaw rate from bicycle model | rad/s |
| 2 | $K_u = \dfrac{m \cdot (b \cdot C_r - a \cdot C_f)}{2 \cdot L \cdot C_f \cdot C_r}$ | Understeer gradient (positive = understeer, negative = oversteer) | rad/(m/s²) |
| 3 | $\alpha_f \approx \delta - \dfrac{(V_y + a \cdot r)}{V_x}$ | Front tire slip angle (small-angle approximation) | rad |
| 4 | $\alpha_r \approx -\dfrac{(V_y - b \cdot r)}{V_x}$ | Rear tire slip angle (small-angle approximation) | rad |
| 5 | $F_{yf} = C_f \cdot \alpha_f$ | Front lateral tire force (linear region) | N |
| 6 | $F_{yr} = C_r \cdot \alpha_r$ | Rear lateral tire force (linear region) | N |
| 7 | $m \cdot a_y = F_{yf} + F_{yr}$ | Lateral force balance (steady state) | N |
| 8 | $I_z \cdot \dot{r} = a \cdot F_{yf} - b \cdot F_{yr}$ | Yaw moment balance | N·m |
| 9 | $\dfrac{r}{\delta}\bigg\vert_{ss} = \dfrac{V/L}{1 + K_u \cdot V^2}$ | Steady-state yaw rate gain (ss = steady state) | 1/s |
| 10 | $a_{y,max} = 0.85 \cdot \mu \cdot g$ | Maximum lateral acceleration on a surface of friction coefficient $\mu$ | m/s² |
| 11 | $s_{ESC} = \dfrac{\Delta M_{brake}}{I_z}$ | Yaw moment from ESC brake intervention (per wheel) | rad/s² |
| 12 | $P_{build} = \dfrac{\tau_{valve} \cdot Q_{pump}}{\eta_{hyd}}$ | Pressure build-up rate (valve response × pump flow / efficiency) | bar/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility:** These equations form the basis of simulation tools like **CarSim**, **veDYNA**, and **CarMaker** used by Tata Motors, Mahindra, and Maruti Suzuki for ESC tuning during homologation. ECE R13-H testing under the **fish-hook**, **sine-with dwell**, and **step steer** maneuvers directly evaluates these equations at the limit of adhesion.

## 2.5 Active vs. Passive vs. Predictive Safety Classification

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often label ESC as a "passive safety system." It is **active** because it activates *before* a crash to prevent it. Passive systems like airbags and seatbelts activate *during/after* a crash. The 2024 Scheme's NEP-aligned course outcomes emphasize this distinction (CO2: classify safety systems).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Yaw Rate Gain (Bicycle Model)

This is the most important derivation for the KTU 14-mark question. We start with the linear 2-DOF bicycle model of a vehicle.

### Step 1 — Define the Free-Body Coordinates
Consider a vehicle with mass $m$, yaw inertia $I_z$, longitudinal velocity $V_x$, and lateral velocity $V_y$. The yaw rate is $r = \dot{\psi}$. The steering angle of the front wheels is $\delta$.

### Step 2 — Write the Slip Angles (Linear Region of Tires)
In the small-angle approximation ($\tan\theta \approx \theta$):

$$\alpha_f = \delta - \theta_f$$

where $\theta_f$ is the velocity angle at the front axle:

$$\theta_f = \dfrac{V_y + a \cdot r}{V_x}$$

$$\alpha_f = \delta - \dfrac{V_y + a \cdot r}{V_x}$$

Similarly, for the rear:

$$\alpha_r = 0 - \theta_r = -\dfrac{V_y - b \cdot r}{V_x}$$

### Step 3 — Write the Linear Tire Forces

$$F_{yf} = C_f \cdot \alpha_f = C_f \left( \delta - \dfrac{V_y + a \cdot r}{V_x} \right)$$

$$F_{yr} = C_r \cdot \alpha_r = -C_r \left( \dfrac{V_y - b \cdot r}{V_x} \right)$$

### Step 4 — Apply Newton's Second Law (Lateral Direction)

$$m \cdot \dot{V_y} + m \cdot V_x \cdot r = F_{yf} + F_{yr}$$

Substituting the tire forces:

$$m \cdot \dot{V_y} + m \cdot V_x \cdot r = C_f \delta - \dfrac{C_f(V_y + ar)}{V_x} - \dfrac{C_r(V_y - br)}{V_x}$$

### Step 5 — Apply Newton's Second Law (Yaw Direction)

$$I_z \cdot \dot{r} = a \cdot F_{yf} - b \cdot F_{yr}$$

$$I_z \cdot \dot{r} = a \cdot C_f \left( \delta - \dfrac{V_y + ar}{V_x} \right) - b \cdot \left( -C_r \dfrac{V_y - br}{V_x} \right)$$

$$I_z \cdot \dot{r} = a \cdot C_f \delta - \dfrac{a C_f (V_y + ar)}{V_x} + \dfrac{b C_r (V_y - br)}{V_x}$$

### Step 6 — Apply Steady-State Conditions
Steady state means $\dot{V_y} = 0$ and $\dot{r} = 0$. The lateral force balance becomes:

$$m \cdot V_x \cdot r = C_f \delta - \dfrac{C_f(V_y + ar)}{V_x} - \dfrac{C_r(V_y - br)}{V_x}$$

The yaw moment balance becomes:

$$0 = a \cdot C_f \delta - \dfrac{a C_f (V_y + ar)}{V_x} + \dfrac{b C_r (V_y - br)}{V_x}$$

### Step 7 — Solve for $r$ and $V_y$
After algebraic manipulation (eliminating $V_y$ between the two equations), we obtain:

$$r_{ss} = \dfrac{\delta \cdot V_x / L}{1 + K_u \cdot V_x^2}$$

with the **understeer gradient** $K_u$ defined as:

$$K_u = \dfrac{m(b \cdot C_r - a \cdot C_f)}{2 \cdot L \cdot C_f \cdot C_r}$$

### Step 8 — Physical Interpretation
- If $K_u > 0$ (i.e., $b \cdot C_r > a \cdot C_f$): The car is **understeering** — yaw rate decreases as speed increases. The car pushes toward the outside of the turn (like a typical front-heavy sedan). ESC must then brake the **rear inner wheel** to increase yaw rate.

- If $K_u < 0$ (i.e., $a \cdot C_f > b \cdot C_r$): The car is **oversteering** — yaw rate increases as speed increases. The rear swings out (like a rear-heavy truck or sports car in a sharp turn). ESC must then brake the **front outer wheel** to reduce yaw rate.

- If $K_u = 0$: **Neutral steer** — yaw rate is independent of speed, which is theoretically ideal but practically impossible.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Speed of Stability Loss

Setting the denominator of the yaw rate gain to zero gives the **critical speed**:

$$1 + K_u \cdot V_{cr}^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{cr} = \sqrt{-\dfrac{1}{K_u}}$$

- If $K_u > 0$ (understeer): No real $V_{cr}$ — the car is **stable at all speeds**. ✓
- If $K_u < 0$ (oversteer): Real $V_{cr}$ exists — the car becomes **unstable beyond this speed**. ✗

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A typical passenger car has $K_u \approx 0.002$ rad/(m/s)², giving $V_{cr}$ as an imaginary number (stable). A heavily loaded light truck with rear loading can have $K_u \approx -0.001$, giving $V_{cr} \approx 31.6$ m/s (≈114 km/h) — a real limit that ESC must prevent reaching.

## 3.3 Numerical Example — Worked KTU-Style Problem

> **Problem:** A passenger car has $m = 1500$ kg, $I_z = 2500$ kg·m², $a = 1.2$ m, $b = 1.5$ m, $C_f = 80{,}000$ N/rad, $C_r = 90{,}000$ N/rad. The car is traveling at $V_x = 25$ m/s (90 km/h) with $\delta = 0.05$ rad. Find the steady-state yaw rate and classify the steer behavior.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute wheelbase $L = a + b = 1.2 + 1.5 = 2.7$ m.

Step 2: Compute $K_u$:

$$K_u = \dfrac{1500 \cdot (1.5 \cdot 90000 - 1.2 \cdot 80000)}{2 \cdot 2.7 \cdot 80000 \cdot 90000}$$

$$K_u = \dfrac{1500 \cdot (135000 - 96000)}{3.888 \times 10^{10}}$$

$$K_u = \dfrac{1500 \cdot 39000}{3.888 \times 10^{10}}$$

$$K_u = \dfrac{5.85 \times 10^{7}}{3.888 \times 10^{10}} \approx 0.001504 \text{ rad/(m/s)}^2$$

Step 3: Compute $K_u \cdot V_x^2$:

$$K_u \cdot V_x^2 = 0.001504 \cdot 625 \approx 0.940$$

Step 4: Compute steady-state yaw rate:

$$r_{ss} = \dfrac{0.05 \cdot 25 / 2.7}{1 + 0.940} = \dfrac{0.4630}{1.940} \approx 0.2387 \text{ rad/s}$$

Step 5: Convert to degrees per second:

$$r_{ss} = 0.2387 \cdot (180/\pi) \approx 13.68 \text{ deg/s}$$

Step 6: Classification: Since $K_u > 0$, the vehicle is **understeering** (stable).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: ESC Intervention Strategies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is **directly testable** in KTU 14-mark questions.

| Driving Situation | Measured vs. Desired | Risk Type | ESC Action | Wheel Braked | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Understeer (plowing) | $r_{meas} < r_{des}$ | Vehicle not turning enough | Reduce engine torque + brake rear inner wheel | Rear-Inner | Creates yaw moment to initiate turn |
| Oversteer (spinning) | $r_{meas} > r_{des}$ | Rear sliding out | Brake front outer wheel + reduce torque | Front-Outer | Creates counter-yaw moment |
| Rollover risk (SUV) | $a_y > a_{y,lim}$ | Lateral lift-off | Brake front outer + cut throttle | Front-Outer | Lowers lateral force, prevents tip |
| Split-$\mu$ braking | $r \neq 0$ during braking | One side on ice | Brake wheel on high-$\mu$ side | Specific | Maintains straight-line stability |
| Aquaplaning | $a_y$ spike + $r$ spike | Loss of all grip | Disable ESC, warn driver | None | Graceful degradation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: ESC Closed-Loop Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Input: Steering Angle delta] --> B[Steering Angle Sensor]
    C[Vehicle Motion] --> D[Yaw Rate Sensor]
    C --> E[Lateral Accelerometer]
    F[Wheel Rotation] --> G[4x Wheel Speed Sensors]
    H[Brake Pedal] --> I[Master Cylinder Pressure Sensor]

    B --> J[ESC ECU - 100 to 200 Hz Loop]
    D --> J
    E --> J
    G --> J
    I --> J

    J --> K{Bicycle Model Reference}
    K --> L[Compute Desired Yaw Rate r_desired]
    J --> M[State Estimator Kalman Filter]
    M --> N[Compute Actual Yaw Rate r_measured]

    L --> O[Error Calculator: e = r_desired - r_measured]
    N --> O

    O --> P{PID Controller}
    P --> Q[Target Brake Pressure for Selected Wheel]
    P --> R[CAN Bus: Torque Reduction Request]

    Q --> S[Hydraulic Control Unit HCU]
    S --> T[Front-Left Wheel Brake]
    S --> U[Front-Right Wheel Brake]
    S --> V[Rear-Left Wheel Brake]
    S --> W[Rear-Right Wheel Brake]

    R --> X[Engine Management Unit EMS]
    X --> Y[Throttle Cut / Spark Retard]

    J --> Z[Warning Lamp and DTC Storage]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: ESC Test Procedure (FMVSS 126 / ECE R13-H)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Vehicle at Test Speed] --> B[Test 1: Slowly Increasing Steer SIS]
    A --> C[Test 2: Sine with Dwell SWD]
    A --> D[Test 3: Fishhook Maneuver]
    A --> E[Test 4: Step Steer at 80 km/h]

    B --> F{Meets Yaw Rate Criteria}
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F

    F -->|Yes| G[PASS: Compliant with FMVSS 126]
    F -->|No| H[FAIL: Reject Type Approval]

    G --> I[Sensor Calibration Logging]
    I --> J[HIL Validation Complete]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: ESC Operating State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NORMAL
    NORMAL --> WARNING: Sensor Fault Detected
    NORMAL --> INTERVENTION: Error exceeds threshold
    INTERVENTION --> NORMAL: Vehicle stabilized
    INTERVENTION --> EMERGENCY: Multiple consecutive interventions
    WARNING --> NORMAL: Fault cleared after key cycle
    EMERGENCY --> NORMAL: Manual reset by driver
    EMERGENCY --> [*]: Permanent disable
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Adapted Physical Schematic)

> [!NOTE]
> Because the actual hydraulic circuit of an ESC unit (Bosch ESP 9.3 or Continental MK100) is highly complex with ~12 solenoid valves per wheel, the following block diagram shows the **functional architecture** rather than a physical hydraulic schematic — this is the standard KTU 2024 approach for non-electrical diagrams.

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ESC FUNCTIONAL ARCHITECTURE                │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

   [DRIVER]                                                          
      │                                                              
      ▼                                                              
  ┌─────────┐     CAN      ┌──────────┐     SPI/CAN    ┌──────────┐  
  │   EMS   │◄────────────►│  ESC ECU │◄─────────────►│  ABS ECU │  
  │ (Engine)│              │ (AURIX)  │              │  (Brake)  │  
  └─────────┘              └────┬─────┘              └──────────┘  
                                │                               
        ┌───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┐       
        ▼                       ▼                       ▼       
   ┌─────────┐            ┌──────────┐            ┌──────────┐    
   │SENSORS  │            │ CONTROL  │            │ACTUATORS │    
   │---------│            │  LOGIC   │            │----------│    
   │ SAS     │            │----------│            │ HCU      │    
   │ YRS     │            │ Bicycle  │            │  Valves  │    
   │ LAC     │            │  Model   │            │  Pump    │    
   │ WSS x4  │            │ PID      │            │ Wheels x4│    
   │ MCPS    │            │ Estimator│            └──────────┘    
   └─────────┘            └──────────┘                           
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Electronic Stability Control (ESC). List its four primary input sensors.**

**Model Answer:**

Electronic Stability Control (ESC) is a closed-loop active vehicle safety system that continuously compares the driver's intended direction of travel (derived from steering angle and vehicle speed) with the actual vehicle motion (measured by yaw rate and lateral acceleration sensors), and automatically intervenes by selectively braking individual wheels and/or reducing engine torque to keep the vehicle on the driver's intended path.

The four primary input sensors are:
1. **Steering Wheel Angle Sensor (SAS)** – measures driver input $\delta$
2. **Yaw Rate Sensor (YRS)** – measures actual rotation rate $r$
3. **Lateral Accelerometer (LAC)** – measures actual lateral acceleration $a_y$
4. **Wheel Speed Sensors (WSS × 4)** – measures individual wheel rotational speeds for velocity and slip computation

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for sensors)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish clearly between Active Safety Systems and Passive Safety Systems. State one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Active Safety | Passive Safety |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating phase** | Pre-crash (before collision) | During/after collision |
| **Function** | Prevents the accident | Protects occupants when accident occurs |
| **Driver involvement** | Often automatic, may override driver | Always passive, no driver interaction |
| **Activation** | Continuous or on-demand | Triggered by crash sensors |
| **Example** | Electronic Stability Control (ESC) | Airbag / Seatbelt with pretensioner / Crumple zone |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Many students write "ABS" as a passive example — this is WRONG. ABS is active because it modulates brake pressure before/during a potential skid to prevent wheel lockup and maintain steerability.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`
**Part (a) [7 marks]:** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an Electronic Stability Control (ESC) system. List and briefly explain the function of each major component.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A passenger car is travelling at a forward speed of 20 m/s. The driver applies a steering wheel angle of 3° to negotiate a curve. The vehicle parameters are: $m = 1400$ kg, $a = 1.25$ m, $b = 1.40$ m, $C_f = 75{,}000$ N/rad, $C_r = 80{,}000$ N/rad. Calculate the steady-state yaw rate and identify whether the vehicle exhibits understeer or oversteer behavior.

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

> **Step 1 — Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.4).** Use the block diagram of the ESC functional architecture.

> **Step 2 — Major Components (5 components, 1 mark each, totaling 5 + 2 for description):**

1. **Steering Angle Sensor (SAS):** Mounted on the steering column; measures the rotation angle of the steering wheel and the front wheels. Provides $\delta$ to the ECU.

2. **Yaw Rate Sensor (YRS):** A MEMS gyroscope mounted under the center console; measures the actual rotational velocity of the vehicle about its vertical axis (yaw). Provides $r_{actual}$.

3. **Lateral Acceleration Sensor (LAC):** A MEMS accelerometer; measures the lateral component of vehicle acceleration. Provides $a_{y,actual}$.

4. **Wheel Speed Sensors (WSS):** Four sensors, one at each wheel hub; measure individual wheel rotational speeds. ECU derives vehicle speed $V$ and detects wheel slip.

5. **ESC Electronic Control Unit (ECU):** The brain of the system. Runs the bicycle model algorithm at 100–200 Hz, computes error, and generates control signals.

6. **Hydraulic Control Unit (HCU):** Contains pump, accumulator, and solenoid valves; modulates brake pressure at each wheel cylinder independently.

7. **Engine Management Interface:** CAN bus link to the EMS for torque reduction requests.

**Working Principle:** ECU compares the *desired* yaw rate (from bicycle model) with the *actual* yaw rate (from YRS). If the difference exceeds a threshold, ESC selectively brakes the appropriate wheel to generate a corrective yaw moment, and reduces engine torque to assist.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $V_x = 20$ m/s
- $\delta = 3° = 3 \times \pi / 180 = 0.0524$ rad
- $m = 1400$ kg
- $a = 1.25$ m, $b = 1.40$ m
- $C_f = 75{,}000$ N/rad, $C_r = 80{,}000$ N/rad

**Step 1:** Wheelbase $L = a + b = 1.25 + 1.40 = 2.65$ m.

**Step 2:** Compute understeer gradient $K_u$:

$$K_u = \dfrac{m \cdot (b \cdot C_r - a \cdot C_f)}{2 \cdot L \cdot C_f \cdot C_r}$$

Numerator:
$$m \cdot (b \cdot C_r - a \cdot C_f) = 1400 \cdot (1.40 \cdot 80000 - 1.25 \cdot 75000)$$
$$= 1400 \cdot (112000 - 93750) = 1400 \cdot 18250 = 2.555 \times 10^7$$

Denominator:
$$2 \cdot L \cdot C_f \cdot C_r = 2 \cdot 2.65 \cdot 75000 \cdot 80000 = 3.18 \times 10^{10}$$

$$K_u = \dfrac{2.555 \times 10^7}{3.18 \times 10^{10}} \approx 8.034 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad/(m/s)}^2$$

**Step 3:** Compute $K_u \cdot V_x^2$:

$$K_u \cdot V_x^2 = 8.034 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 400 = 0.3214$$

**Step 4:** Compute steady-state yaw rate:

$$r_{ss} = \dfrac{\delta \cdot V_x / L}{1 + K_u \cdot V_x^2} = \dfrac{0.0524 \cdot 20 / 2.65}{1 + 0.3214}$$

Numerator: $1.048 / 2.65 = 0.3955$ rad/s

Denominator: $1.3214$

$$r_{ss} = \dfrac{0.3955}{1.3214} \approx 0.2993 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to deg/s for clarity:

$$r_{ss} = 0.2993 \cdot (180/\pi) \approx 17.15 \text{ deg/s}$$

**Step 6: Classification:**
Since $K_u = 8.034 \times 10^{-4} > 0$ (i.e., $b \cdot C_r > a \cdot C_f$), the vehicle exhibits **UNDERSTEER** behavior. The vehicle is laterally stable at all forward speeds.

---

### Question B-B (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`
**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Describe in detail the regulatory framework for ESC worldwide, citing at least three regulations. Mention the year of mandate in each region.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** With reference to the bicycle model, derive the expression for the steady-state yaw rate gain $\frac{r}{\delta}$. Discuss how the understeer gradient $K_u$ classifies vehicle behavior.

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**1. FMVSS 126 (Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standard No. 126) — USA:**
- Issued by NHTSA (National Highway Traffic Safety Administration)
- Mandated for all new light vehicles (passenger cars, MPVs, trucks, buses) with GVWR ≤ 4,536 kg
- **Phased implementation:** 50% of new fleet from **1 September 2008**; 100% from **1 September 2011**
- Test maneuvers: 0.3 Hz sine-with-dwell; step steer at 80 km/h; slowly increasing steer
- Compliance: Vehicle must not exceed a specified yaw rate ratio tolerance (0.8 to 1.2 nominal) within 1.07 seconds of steer release

**2. ECE R13-H (UN Regulation No. 13-H) — Europe & UN/ECE countries:**
- Adopted under the 1958 Agreement (WP.29)
- For vehicles of categories M1 and N1 (≤ 3.5 t)
- **Mandatory for new type approvals from 1 November 2014; for all new registrations from 1 November 2015**
- Test: 0.5–0.6 Hz sine-with-dwell, peak steering wheel angle up to 270°, vehicle speed 80 ± 2 km/h

**3. AIS-184 / AIS-185 (Automotive Industry Standards) — India:**
- Finalized by ARAI (Automotive Research Association of India)
- **Mandated under GSR 720(E) dated 24 October 2019** for M1 category vehicles manufactured on/after **1 April 2021** and all vehicles from **1 April 2023**
- AIS-184 covers ESC functional requirements; AIS-185 covers data recording

**4. EU General Safety Regulation (EC) 661/2009 — Supplementary:**
- Mandates ESC for all new M1, N1, M2, M3, N2, N3 vehicle types registered in the EU since November 2014 (extended to N2/N3 from 2016)

**5. GTR No. 8 (Global Technical Regulation) — UNECE/WP.29:**
- Harmonized global standard; reference for national regulations; covers ESC test procedures, electronic stability performance criteria, and failure modes

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

Refer to **Section 3.1 of these notes** for the full derivation. The final result is:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{r}{\delta}\bigg\vert_{ss} = \dfrac{V_x / L}{1 + K_u \cdot V_x^2}}$$

with:

$$K_u = \dfrac{m(b \cdot C_r - a \cdot C_f)}{2 \cdot L \cdot C_f \cdot C_r}$$

**Classification:**
- $K_u > 0$: **Understeer** — $r/\delta$ decreases with speed; vehicle is inherently stable.
- $K_u < 0$: **Oversteer** — $r/\delta$ increases with speed; vehicle is unstable at high speed; ESC must intervene.
- $K_u = 0$: **Neutral steer** — $r/\delta$ is independent of speed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing active and passive safety** — *costs 1–2 marks*. Always clarify ESC is **active**, not passive.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $\delta$ from degrees to radians** — *costs 1 mark*. KTU expects answers in rad or consistent SI units. Default is radians in the bicycle model formula.
>
> 3. **Writing "ABS" when asked for an active safety example** — *costs 2 marks*. ESC and ABS are both active, but the question usually wants a system from a different category.
>
> 4. **Not mentioning the year of regulatory mandate** — *costs 1 mark*. Always cite FMVSS 126 (2012 USA), ECE R13-H (2014/15 EU), AIS-184 (2021/23 India) with the year.
>
> 5. **Skipping the bicycle model assumption** — *costs 1 mark*. Always state: "We assume small slip angles, constant speed, linear tire behavior, and neglect roll dynamics."
>
> 6. **Forgetting CAN bus communication** in the block diagram — *costs 1 mark*. ESC must communicate with EMS via CAN, LIN, or FlexRay.
>
> 7. **Wrong wheel identification for braking** — *costs 2 marks*. For oversteer: brake the **front-outer** wheel. For understeer: brake the **rear-inner** wheel. Many students write the opposite.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — PEMUT526, Module 2**

- ☐ **ESC is an ACTIVE safety system** — it prevents accidents; it does not protect occupants during accidents.
- ☐ The four **primary sensors** are: **SAS, YRS, LAC, WSS × 4** — memorize in this order.
- ☐ The **bicycle model** is a 2-DOF model assuming small slip angles, constant $V_x$, and lumped lateral forces at the axles.
- ☐ **Reference yaw rate** = $V \delta / (L + K_u V^2)$ — this is the formula the ECU computes 100–200 times per second.
- ☐ **Understeer gradient** $K_u = m(bC_r - aC_f) / (2L C_f C_r)$.
- ☐ $K_u > 0$: Understeer (stable); $K_u < 0$: Oversteer (unstable); $K_u = 0$: Neutral steer.
- ☐ **Oversteer correction:** Brake the **front-outer** wheel → creates counter-yaw moment.
- ☐ **Understeer correction:** Brake the **rear-inner** wheel → initiates yaw in desired direction.
- ☐ **Engine torque reduction** is done via CAN bus to EMS (throttle cut, spark retard, fuel cut).
- ☐ **Regulations to remember with year:**
  - FMVSS 126 (USA): 2011–2012 full mandate
  - ECE R13-H (EU): 2014/2015
  - AIS-184 (India): 2021/2023
  - GTR No. 8 (Global)
- ☐ **Test maneuvers:** Sine-with-dwell, step steer, slowly increasing steer (SIS), fishhook.
- ☐ **Hydraulic Control Unit (HCU)** contains active build-up valve, isolation valve, drain valve, and return pump.
- ☐ **Control loop frequency:** 100–200 Hz; **hydraulic response time:** 5–15 ms.
- ☐ ESC reduces fatal single-vehicle crash risk by approximately **49%** (NHTSA study) and fatal rollover risk by **75%** for SUVs — this statistic is often asked in viva voce.
- ☐ **Failure detection:** ESC stores DTCs in non-volatile memory using ISO 14229 (UDS) protocol.
- ☐ **Warning lamp:** Steady illumination indicates fault; flashing indicates active intervention.
- ☐ **Critical speed** $V_{cr} = \sqrt{-1/K_u}$ exists only for oversteer vehicles.
- ☐ For KTU numerical problems, **always convert steering angle from degrees to radians** and report yaw rate in rad/s.
- ☐ The **state estimator** (typically a Kalman Filter) cleans noisy sensor data and estimates unmeasured states like sideslip angle $\beta$.
- ☐ ESC is **integrated with ABS and TCS (Traction Control System)** in modern vehicles — they share the same HCU and ECU.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anti-Lock Braking System (ABS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Anti-Lock Braking System (ABS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

The **Anti-Lock Braking System (ABS)** is a closed-loop, electro-hydraulic (or electro-mechanical) active safety system mandated under modern automotive safety standards. Its primary function is to **prevent the premature lock-up of one or more wheels** during heavy braking by automatically modulating the brake line pressure in real time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *ABS is a wheel-slip control system that limits brake pressure to maintain the longitudinal slip ratio within an optimal band (typically $\lambda = 10\% - 30\%$), thereby preserving vehicle steerability, directional stability, and — on high-friction surfaces — minimizing stopping distance.*

The system operates on the principle of **Cyclic Pressure Modulation**, governed by the **ECE R13** (United Nations) and **FMVSS 126** (US) standards, and in India by the **AIS-151** regulation under the Motor Vehicle Act.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (The "Cadence" of Braking)

Imagine you are riding a bicycle downhill on wet gravel. If you clamp the brake levers hard, the wheels will **lock** — they will stop rotating while the bicycle continues to slide. You lose steering, the bike goes straight, and you may fall.

To avoid this, an experienced cyclist instinctively **pumps** the brakes: squeeze-release-squeeze-release. This "pumping" lets the wheel rotate briefly between clamps, restoring grip.

**ABS is essentially an automated, high-speed pumping mechanism.** Instead of squeezing levers, the system fires **30–50 cycles per second**, much faster and more precisely than a human foot can manage. It uses a computer (ECU), wheel-speed sensors, and hydraulic valves to perform this micro-pumping, far faster than any human reaction time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics (as per KTU/AIS)**
> - **Operating Cycle Frequency:** Typically **15–25 Hz** (cycles per second).
> - **Optimal Slip Ratio ($\lambda_{opt}$):** **10% – 30%** (Peak friction zone).
