| Luminous Efficacy | $\eta = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$ | **lm/W** | Efficiency of light source (H4 ≈ 18 lm/W, LED ≈ 100 lm/W) |
| Trigonometric Test Point | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{y}{25\,\text{m}}$ | **degrees** | Conversion of screen coordinates to angles |
| Color Temperature | $T_c$ | **K (Kelvin)** | Halogen ≈ 3200 K, Xenon ≈ 4200 K, LED ≈ 5000–6500 K |
| CIE Chromaticity | $(x, y)$ | dimensionless | Defines color of emitted light |
| Solid Angle of a Cone | $\Omega = 2\pi(1 - \cos\theta)$ | **sr** | Used in flux-to-intensity conversions |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Type Approval:** Before a vehicle model can be sold in a market, every lamp must undergo standardized photometric, color, and durability tests at accredited test houses (ARAI in India, TÜV in Germany, UTAC in France).
- **Crash Safety:** Headlamps must not shatter into dangerous fragments in a pedestrian impact (hence polycarbonate lenses with hard coatings).
- **Glare Control:** ECE R112 specifies that the passing beam must not exceed **625 cd** in Zone III (the glare zone for oncoming drivers).
- **Power Management:** Modern vehicles use CAN-bus-controlled LED lamps with PWM dimming to comply with both DRL and position lamp functions using a single LED module.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Photometric Test Point Geometry

**Problem Setup:**
A headlamp is mounted on a test goniophotometer and aimed at a vertical screen placed at a standard test distance of **$d = 25$ m**. A test point on the screen is located at a vertical distance **$y$** (in meters) above the H–V axis. Derive the expression for the angular position **$\theta$** of this test point as seen from the headlamp.

**Step 1 — Define the Geometry:**

The reference axis of the lamp (H–V axis) intersects the screen perpendicularly. Any test point P on the screen is offset from this axis by a perpendicular distance **$y$**. The line from the lamp to point P makes an angle **$\theta$** with the H–V axis.

**Step 2 — Construct the Right Triangle:**

In the right triangle formed by the lamp, the H–V axis intersection, and point P:
- Opposite side to angle $\theta$ = $y$
- Adjacent side to angle $\theta$ = $d = 25$ m

**Step 3 — Apply Trigonometry:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\theta &= \dfrac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} \\
\tan\theta &= \dfrac{y}{d} \\
\tan\theta &= \dfrac{y}{25\,\text{m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\theta$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \arctan\left(\dfrac{y}{25}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Example Numerical Evaluation:**

If a test point is at $y = 0.5$ m above the H–V axis on the 25 m screen:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\theta &= \dfrac{0.5}{25} = 0.02 \\
\theta &= \arctan(0.02) \\
\theta &= 1.1459^{\circ} \approx 1.15^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the basis for the "Test Point Grid" in ECE R112 / AIS-010, where screen coordinates in meters are converted to angular positions in degrees to define the zones (Zone I, II, III) of the passing beam.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Inverse Square Law for Photometric Testing

**Problem Setup:**
A headlamp produces a luminous intensity of **$I = 20{,}000$ cd** along the H–V axis. The lamp is moved from a test distance of **$d_1 = 10$ m** to the standard distance of **$d_2 = 25$ m**. Derive the ratio of illuminance at these two distances.

**Step 1 — Write the Inverse Square Law:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \dfrac{I}{d^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Illuminance at $d_1 = 10$ m:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= \dfrac{20{,}000\,\text{cd}}{(10\,\text{m})^{2}} \\
E_1 &= \dfrac{20{,}000}{100} \\
E_1 &= 200\,\text{lx}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Illuminance at $d_2 = 25$ m:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_2 &= \dfrac{20{,}000\,\text{cd}}{(25\,\text{m})^{2}} \\
E_2 &= \dfrac{20{,}000}{625} \\
E_2 &= 32\,\text{lx}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Form the Ratio $E_1 / E_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{E_1}{E_2} &= \dfrac{I / d_1^{2}}{I / d_2^{2}} \\
\dfrac{E_1}{E_2} &= \dfrac{d_2^{2}}{d_1^{2}} \\
\dfrac{E_1}{E_2} &= \left(\dfrac{25}{10}\right)^{2} \\
\dfrac{E_1}{E_2} &= 6.25
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This shows that the closer the test screen, the higher the measured illuminance for the same source intensity. Standardization at 25 m ensures globally comparable headlamp test results.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Standards Mapping for Major Lamp Categories

| Lamp Category | Indian Standard (AIS) | European Standard (ECE) | US Standard (FMVSS) | Light Color | Min. Photometric Intensity |
|---------------|----------------------|------------------------|---------------------|-------------|----------------------------|
| Headlamp (Passing Beam) | AIS-010 | ECE R112 | FMVSS 108 | White | Zone I: ≥ 320 cd (varies by point) |
| Headlamp (Driving Beam) | AIS-010 | ECE R112 | FMVSS 108 | White | Reference point HV: ≥ 16,200 cd |
| Front Position Lamp | AIS-008 | ECE R7 (R148 cat. 1a) | FMVSS 108 | White | ≥ 4 cd along axis |
| Rear Position Lamp | AIS-008 | ECE R7 (R148 cat. R1) | FMVSS 108 | Red | ≥ 4 cd along axis |
| Stop Lamp (S1) | AIS-008 | ECE R7 (R148 cat. S1) | FMVSS 108 | Red | ≥ 60 cd along axis |
| Direction Indicator | AIS-008 | ECE R6 (R148 cat. 1, 2) | FMVSS 108 | Amber | ≥ 175 cd (front) |
| Front Fog Lamp | AIS-008 | ECE R19 | FMVSS 108 | White / Yellow | Reference point: ≥ 10,000 cd |
| Rear Fog Lamp | AIS-008 | ECE R38 | FMVSS 108 | Red | ≥ 150 cd along axis |
| Reversing Lamp | AIS-008 | ECE R23 | FMVSS 108 | White | ≥ 800 cd along axis |
| DRL | AIS-008 | ECE R87 | FMVSS 108 | White | ≥ 400 cd (reference point) |
| Retro-Reflector (Rear) | AIS-008 | ECE R3 | FMVSS 108 | Red | CIL ≥ 60 mcd/lux |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Headlamp Type Approval Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Manufacturer] --> B[Lamp Supplier / OEM]
    B --> C1[Photometric Test at 25 m]
    B --> C2[Colorimetric Test CIE 1931]
    B --> C3[Durability and Heat Test]
    B --> C4[Mechanical and Vibration Test]
    C1 --> D[Compliance Report Generation]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E{Compliance with AIS-010 and ECE R112}
    E -- Yes --> F[ARAI Test Certificate]
    E -- No --> B
    F --> G[CMVR Type Approval]
    G --> H[Vehicle Homologation by MoRTH]
    H --> I[Market Release in India]
```

**Block Explanation:**
- **A → B:** Vehicle OEM sources lamps from supplier.
- **C1–C4:** Four mandatory test categories per AIS-010.
- **D → E:** Compliance is evaluated against pass/fail criteria in the standard.
- **F → G → H → I:** Certification → Type Approval → Homologation → Sales.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Passing Beam Photometric Test Grid (ECE R112 / AIS-010)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Lamp[Headlamp Under Test]
        L1[Light Source H4 or LED]
        L2[Reflector or Projection Lens]
        L3[Lens and Bezel Assembly]
        L1 --> L2 --> L3
    end

    Lamp -- Reference Axis H-V --> Screen[Vertical Test Screen at 25 m]

    subgraph Zones[Test Point Zones on Screen]
        Z1[Zone I Hot Spot above H-V]
        Z2[Zone II Right Side Illumination]
        Z3[Zone III Glare Zone for Oncoming Traffic]
        Z4[Zone IV Short Range 25 m ahead]
    end

    Screen --> Z1
    Screen --> Z2
    Screen --> Z3
    Screen --> Z4

    Z3 -- Must be LESS THAN 625 cd --> Result[Pass Compliance]
    Z1 -- Must be MORE THAN 320 cd --> Result
    Z2 -- Must be MORE THAN Specified --> Result
```

**Block Explanation:**
- The lamp projects its beam onto the **25 m screen**.
- The screen is divided into **four photometric zones**.
- **Zone III** is the glare control zone — maximum intensity is limited to **625 cd** to prevent blinding oncoming drivers.
- **Zone I** is the hot spot directly ahead — minimum intensity **320 cd** ensures road visibility.

---

### 4.3 Functional Flow: Adaptive Front-Lighting System (AFS) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Steering Angle Sensor] --> ECU[AFS Control Unit ECU]
    S2[Vehicle Speed Sensor] --> ECU
    S3[Ambient Light Sensor] --> ECU
    S4[GPS and Map Data Optional] --> ECU
    ECU --> M1[Horizontal Swivel Motor for Left Headlamp]
    ECU --> M2[Horizontal Swivel Motor for Right Headlamp]
    ECU --> D1[DRL Intensity Adjustment]
    ECU --> D2[Cornering Lamp Activation]
    M1 --> L1[Left Headlamp Beam]
    M2 --> L2[Right Headlamp Beam]
```

**Block Explanation:**
- Multiple sensors feed data to the **AFS ECU**.
- The ECU actuates **swivel motors** to rotate the headlamp modules up to **±15° horizontally**.
- This provides improved illumination in curves, enhancing night-time safety.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define the term "Reference Axis (H–V axis)" of a headlamp as per AIS-010 / ECE R112.**

**Model Answer:**
The reference axis, also called the **H–V axis**, is a unique axis of the headlamp defined by the manufacturer and used as the origin (0,0) in the photometric test grid. It is established by the intersection of the horizontal plane (H-plane, passing through the lamp's reference center) and the vertical plane (V-plane). All photometric test points are specified as angular offsets from this axis. The illuminance of the headlamp is measured on a vertical screen placed at a distance of **25 m** along the H–V axis.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2023] State the photometric test distance and the color of light for a rear fog lamp as per AIS-008.**

**Model Answer:**
As per **AIS-008** (harmonized with **ECE R38**), a rear fog lamp must:
- Be tested at a photometric distance of **25 m**.
- Emit light of color **Red** (CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinates within the red boundary).
- Have a minimum luminous intensity of **150 cd** along the reference axis.
- Be installed such that the reference axis is parallel to the road surface in the loaded vehicle condition.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the classification of automotive lamps as per AIS-008 / AIS-010, with examples of light-signalling devices and their corresponding ECE regulations.**

**Model Solution:**

Automotive lamps are classified into the following functional categories:

1. **Illumination Lamps (Forward Lighting):**
   - **Driving Beam (High Beam):** Long-range, used on open roads. Standard: **ECE R112 / AIS-010**.
   - **Passing Beam (Low Beam):** Asymmetric, glare-controlled. Standard: **ECE R112 / AIS-010**.

2. **Light-Signalling Devices:**
   - **Front and Rear Position Lamps:** Indicate vehicle presence. Standard: **ECE R7 (R148) / AIS-008**.
   - **Stop Lamps:** Red, activated during braking. Standard: **ECE R7 (R148) / AIS-008**.
   - **Direction Indicators:** Amber, flashing. Standard: **ECE R6 (R148) / AIS-008**.
   - **Hazard Warning:** All indicators flash simultaneously.

3. **Special Purpose Lamps:**
   - **Front Fog Lamps:** **ECE R19 / AIS-008**.
   - **Rear Fog Lamps:** **ECE R38 / AIS-008**.
   - **Reversing Lamps:** **ECE R23 / AIS-008**.
   - **Daytime Running Lamps (DRL):** **ECE R87 / AIS-008**.
   - **Adaptive Front-Lighting Systems (AFS):** **ECE R123 / AIS-010**.

4. **Retro-Reflectors (Passive):** **ECE R3 (rear), R27 (front) / AIS-008**.

Each category has specific photometric intensity limits, color requirements (CIE 1931), installation geometric visibility angles, and test procedures.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] With the help of a neat sketch, describe the photometric test procedure for a passing beam headlamp. Mention the function of Zones I, II, and III.**

**Model Solution:**

**Test Procedure:**

1. The headlamp is mounted on a **goniophotometer** such that the H–V axis is perpendicular to a vertical test screen.
2. The screen is placed at a standardized test distance of **25 m**.
3. The lamp is supplied with **13.2 V ± 0.1 V** for a 12 V H4 halogen bulb.
4. A photo-detector (lux meter) is moved across the test screen to measure illuminance at each predefined test point.
5. Luminous intensity $I$ is calculated using the inverse square law: $I = E \cdot d^{2}$, where $d = 25$ m.

**Test Point Zones (as per ECE R112 / AIS-010):**

- **Zone I (Hot Spot Zone):** Located slightly below and to the right of the H–V axis on the screen. **Purpose:** Ensures adequate illumination of the road 50–75 m ahead. **Minimum intensity:** ~320 cd at the reference point.
- **Zone II (Right-Side Road Zone):** Spread to the right of the H–V axis. **Purpose:** Illuminates the road shoulder, road signs, and pedestrians on the right. **Minimum intensity:** specified at each test point.
- **Zone III (Glare Zone):** Located above and to the left of the H–V axis. **Purpose:** Restricts light that could dazzle oncoming drivers. **Maximum allowed intensity:** **625 cd**.

(Refer to the **Passing Beam Photometric Test Grid** block diagram in Section 4 for a visual mapping.)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the color specifications for automotive lamps as per AIS-008 / ECE regulations. Use the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram concept in your answer.**

**Model Solution:**

The color of light emitted by automotive lamps is specified using the **CIE 1931 (x, y) chromaticity diagram**, which maps all visible colors on a two-dimensional plot. The standard defines **color boundary vertices** for each lamp function. Light emitted must fall *within* the polygon defined by these vertices.

**Standard Color Requirements (AIS-008 / ECE):**

| Lamp Function | Color | CIE Boundary Example (approx.) |
|---------------|-------|-------------------------------|
| Headlamp (Passing/Driving) | White | $0.310 \le x \le 0.500$ |
| Front Position Lamp | White | Same as headlamp |
| Direction Indicator | Amber | Yellow boundary: $y \le 0.429$, etc. |
| Front Fog Lamp | White or Selective Yellow | Selective yellow boundary defined |
| Stop Lamp | Red | $y \le 0.335$, $x \le 0.300$ etc. |
| Rear Fog Lamp | Red | Same as stop lamp |
| Reversing Lamp | White | Same as headlamp |

**White light** is defined by a polygon on the chromaticity diagram bounded by chromaticity coordinates specified in the regulation. **Selective yellow** is used historically for fog lamps because yellow light scatters less in fog.

**Why color standards matter:** A driver relies on the color of lamps to instantly recognize a vehicle's behavior — *red* = danger/stop, *amber* = caution/turn, *white* = forward motion. Any deviation can cause confusion and accidents.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] A passing beam headlamp has a measured luminous intensity of 20,000 cd along the H–V axis. Calculate the illuminance on the road at (i) 25 m and (ii) 100 m. Comment on the result from a safety point of view.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $I = 20{,}000$ cd along the H–V axis.

**(i) Illuminance at $d = 25$ m:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= \dfrac{I}{d_1^{2}} \\
E_1 &= \dfrac{20{,}000\,\text{cd}}{(25\,\text{m})^{2}} \\
E_1 &= \dfrac{20{,}000}{625} \\
E_1 &= 32\,\text{lx}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Illuminance at $d = 100$ m:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_2 &= \dfrac{I}{d_2^{2}} \\
E_2 &= \dfrac{20{,}000\,\text{cd}}{(100\,\text{m})^{2}} \\
E_2 &= \dfrac{20{,}000}{10{,}000} \\
E_2 &= 2\,\text{lx}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{E_1}{E_2} &= \left(\dfrac{100}{25}\right)^{2} = 16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment (Safety Viewpoint):**
At **25 m**, the illuminance is **32 lx**, which is well above the safe visibility threshold for driving (~10–20 lx). However, at **100 m**, the illuminance drops to just **2 lx** — only **6.25%** of the value at 25 m. This demonstrates why high-beam (driving beam) headlamps with intensities of **50,000–100,000 cd** are required for safe high-speed night driving on highways. The inverse square law also explains why passing beams are adequate for urban driving (slower speeds, shorter stopping distances) but inadequate for highway speeds.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse AIS with ECE regulation numbers** — AIS standards are Indian, but they are *harmonized* with ECE R-numbers. Writing "AIS R112" is a common error; it is either "AIS-010" or "ECE R112".
> 2. **Always specify the photometric test distance (25 m)** in answers involving headlamp intensity. Marks are deducted if this is omitted.
> 3. **Color names are regulated** — do not write "yellow" instead of "**selective yellow**" or "amber" for direction indicators.
> 4. **Zone III is the GLARE zone** — many students incorrectly state the maximum intensity limit. The correct maximum is **625 cd**.
> 5. **Luminous intensity is in candelas (cd), NOT lumens (lm)** — flux and intensity are different quantities; flux is total light, intensity is directional light.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Lamps = Lighting + Light-Signalling + Retro-Reflectors** in regulatory terms.
- **Standard Photometric Test Distance = 25 m** for headlamps and signal lamps.
- **Inverse Square Law** governs illuminance: $E = I / d^{2}$.
- **AIS-008** governs installation of lamps; **AIS-010** governs performance of headlamps.
- **ECE Regulations** (R3, R6, R7, R19, R23, R38, R87, R112, R123) are the global benchmarks harmonized with AIS.
- **CMVR (Central Motor Vehicles Rules)** of India mandates compliance with AIS for type approval.
- **CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram** is the basis for color specifications.
- **Zone III maximum intensity = 625 cd** (anti-glare) for passing beam.
- **Reference axis (H–V axis)** is the origin (0,0) for all photometric measurements.
- **Lamp categories:** Driving beam, Passing beam, Position, Stop, Direction Indicator, Fog, Reversing, DRL, AFS, Retro-Reflector.
- **Color code (memory aid):** *White* = forward; *Red* = rear/brake; *Amber* = direction; *Selective Yellow* = front fog (optional).
- **Key governing bodies:** MoRTH (India), UNECE (Europe), NHTSA (USA — FMVSS 108), SAE (industry standards).
- **Test voltage for 12 V halogen bulbs = 13.2 V ± 0.1 V** (regulated test condition, not battery voltage).
- **Service life of H4 bulb ≥ 500 hours** at test voltage — a regulated durability criterion.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signalling devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Signalling devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Signalling Devices (Automotive):** Lamps, optical assemblies, audible emitters, and reflectors fitted to a motor vehicle whose primary function is to **convey information, intent, or hazard warnings** to other road users and pedestrians, rather than to illuminate the road ahead for the driver. They are governed by the principle of *retroreflection* (for passive devices) and *selective spectral emission* (for active devices) under prescribed angular and photometric tolerances defined in **AIS-008**, **AIS-010**, and **ECE R48 / R148**.

In KTU parlance, signalling devices form a sub-class under the wider umbrella of **Vehicle Lighting and Light-Signalling Devices (VLLSD)** as classified under **CMVR Rule 102–110** (Central Motor Vehicles Rules, India) and harmonized with **UNECE Regulations** through AIS-008 (Installation Requirements) and AIS-010 (Photometric Requirements).

## 1.2 Classification of Signalling Devices

The KTU 2024 syllabus (PEMUT526, Module 2) categorises signalling devices into two broad families:

### A. Active (Light-Emitting) Signalling Devices
These produce luminous flux when energised by the vehicle's electrical system.

1. **Direction-Indicator Lamps (Turn Signals)** — front, rear, side (Category 1, 2, 5, 6 per ECE R6/R48)
2. **Position Lamps** — front (white/amber), rear (red)
3. **Stop (Brake) Lamps** — S1 (single), S2 (supplementary on HCPV/LCV)
4. **Rear-Fog Lamps** — single red, ECE R38
5. **Reversing Lamps** — white, automatic engagement
6. **Hazard Warning Lamps** — simultaneous operation of all turn signals
7. **Side-Marker Lamps** — amber (front), red (rear)
8. **Daytime Running Lamps (DRL)** — automatic, white
9. **End-Outline Marker Lamps** — for LCVs/HCVs
10. **Tell-Tale/Indicator Lamps** — dashboard signals
11. **Audible Signalling Device (Horn)** — electro-mechanical or air-operated

### B. Passive Signalling Devices
These reflect incident light from external sources.

1. **Rear-Registration-Plate Illumination Lamp**
2. **Retro-Reflectors (Rear, Side, Front)**
3. **Retro-Reflective Markings** (for HCVs — ECE R104 contour markings)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Distinction (Examiner Favourite):** A *lamp* is a *light-emitting* device (active), while a *retro-reflector* is a *light-reflecting* device (passive). A common KTU Part-A question asks students to differentiate these two — the **light source** is the key discriminator.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Road Conversation"

Imagine a vehicle on a busy road as a person walking through a crowded market. Just as we use **hand gestures** (turn signal), **words** (horn), **facial expressions** (brake light glow), and **safety vests** (retro-reflectors) to communicate, a vehicle uses these devices to "talk" to other road users. 

- **Turn signals** = "I am turning left/right, please give me way."
- **Brake lights** = "I am slowing down — do not crash into me."
- **Horn** = "Attention! Danger / I am here."
- **Retro-reflectors** = Your glowing badge at night that catches someone else's torch.

This **road language** is standardised globally so a driver from Kerala can safely interpret signals from a vehicle registered in Munich or Michigan.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Signalling Device Standardisation

Every signalling device under KTU/CMVR/AIS-008 must satisfy three measurable pillars:

| Pillar | What it Governs | Example Standard |
|--------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Chromaticity** (Color) | Wavelength / CIE 1931 coordinates | AIS-010 (Rev 1), ECE R48 |
| **Photometry** (Intensity) | Candela distribution in prescribed angles | AIS-010, ECE R7, R6, R23 |
| **Geometry** (Position) | Mounting height, spacing, visibility angle | AIS-008, ECE R48, FMVSS 108 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values You Must Memorise:**
> - Turn signal flash rate: **60 to 120 flashes per minute** (preferred **90 ± 30 fpm**)
> - Brake light colour: **Red** (CIE x ≥ 0.645, y ≤ 0.335)
> - Turn signal colour: **Amber** (CIE y > 0.398, y < 0.429, x > 0.549)
> - Reversing lamp colour: **White** (selective yellow allowed in some jurisdictions)
> - Rear fog lamp: **Red**, **only one** permitted, mounted on/off centre
> - Horn fundamental frequency: typically **~110 Hz to ~5000 Hz** range, city horn ~ **2 kHz**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principles of Major Signalling Devices

### 2.1.1 Turn Indicator (Direction Signal Lamp)

**Working Principle:** A tungsten-halogen or LED cluster emits amber light through a Fresnel-patterned amber lens (or a colourless lens with amber LED chips). A flasher relay (thermal or electronic) cycles the circuit at **60–120 fpm**. Modern systems use **CAN-bus controlled electronic flasher units** that monitor current draw and indicate bulb failure to the BCM (Body Control Module).

- **Thermal Flasher:** A bimetallic strip heats and bends, breaking the circuit; cools, springs back, completes the circuit. Frequency depends on current.
- **Electronic Flasher:** Uses an RC-timing 555-timer or microcontroller PWM, frequency independent of load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 60–120 fpm?** This range corresponds to the human eye's critical flicker fusion (CFF) boundary. Below 60 fpm, the eye perceives individual flashes; above 120 fpm, the flashes merge and direction intent is lost. **AIS-008 specifies 90 ± 30 fpm** as the operational sweet spot.

### 2.1.2 Stop Lamp (Brake Light)

Energised by a brake-pedal-actuated pressure switch or pedal-position sensor. The light intensity is graded as:
- **S1** — standard stop lamp, single intensity
- **S2** — supplementary high-mounted stop lamp (HMSL, mandatory in India for LMVs manufactured after April 2018, AIS-008)

Photometrically, stop lamps must deliver **60–185 cd** along the reference axis (H–V), with a minimum of **0.3 cd** at the extreme angular points.

### 2.1.3 Rear-Fog Lamp

A high-intensity red lamp activated in fog/rain to mark the vehicle's rear contour. The driver-illuminated switch must be **non-latching with a tell-tale**. Only **one** is permitted in most jurisdictions (ECE R38) to avoid confusion with brake lights.

### 2.1.4 Reversing (Back-Up) Lamp

Activates only when:
1. The reverse gear is engaged, AND
2. (For some standards) the ignition is ON, AND
3. The device is wired to illuminate automatically.

White light, **minimum 300 cd** along the H–V axis (AIS-010).

### 2.1.5 Auditory Signalling Device (Horn)

Electromechanical horn operating on a **solenoid-driven diaphragm**. The diaphragm vibrates at the **resonant frequency** of the trumpet/horn chamber, producing a fundamental tone and several harmonics. AIS-018 (or ECE R28) governs permissible sound pressure levels:
- **A-weighted SPL: 93–112 dB at 2 m** from the source
- Single-frequency dominance is prohibited; **multi-tone** design encouraged for safety.

### 2.1.6 Retro-Reflectors

Optical device that returns incident light along a near-parallel path to the source. The three common types are:

1. **Cube-corner (prismatic) reflectors** — three mutually perpendicular faces; high efficiency.
2. **Lenticular (glass-bead) reflectors** — spherical beads; lower cost.
3. **Micro-prismatic sheeting** — millions of cube corners; for contour markings (ECE R104).

The Coefficient of Luminous Intensity (CIL) is the key metric, measured in **millicandela per lux (mcd/lx)**.

## 2.2 The Three-Layer Regulatory Hierarchy

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  UNECE Regulations (WP.29) — Global Geneva Treaty    │
│  R6, R7, R23, R38, R48, R104, R148                   │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  AIS (Automotive Industry Standards) — India         │
│  AIS-008 (Installation), AIS-010 (Photometric)       │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  CMVR (Central Motor Vehicles Rules) — Statutory     │
│  Rule 102, 105, 110, 110A, Schedule VI               │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

AIS-008 and AIS-010 are **type-approval** standards. Compliance with these allows a vehicle model to be issued an ARAI/ICAT certificate, which is mandatory for registration under **CMVR Rule 126**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas for Part-A and Part-B calculations.**

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Unit | Numerical Range (Typical) |
|---|-----------|---------------------|------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Luminous Intensity | $I = \dfrac{\Phi}{\omega}$ where $\Phi$ is luminous flux, $\omega$ is solid angle | Candela (cd) | Stop lamp: 60–185 cd |
| 2 | Illuminance at a point | $E = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$ (inverse-square law) | Lux (lx) | 1 lx = 1 lm/m² |
| 3 | Solid angle subtended | $\omega = \dfrac{A \cos\theta}{d^2}$ | Steradian (sr) | Hemisphere = $2\pi$ sr |
| 4 | Photometric range | $d_{vis} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{lamp}}{E_{threshold}}}$ | metres (m) | Threshold $E_{th} \approx 0.2$–$1$ lx |
| 5 | Flash frequency | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$, where $T$ = period of one on-off cycle | Hz or fpm | 60–120 fpm (1–2 Hz) |
| 6 | Coeff. of Luminous Intensity (CIL) | $R = \dfrac{I_{reflected}}{E_{incident}}$ | mcd/lx | Rear reflector: 300–1500 mcd/lx |
| 7 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right)$, $p_0 = 20\,\mu Pa$ | dB | Horn: 93–112 dB(A) |
| 8 | Power of horn acoustic source | $P_{ac} = \eta \cdot P_{elec}$ | Watt (W) | $\eta$ = 1–5% |
| 9 | Duty cycle of flasher | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on}+T_{off}} \times 100\%$ | % | 30–70% |
| 10 | CIE 1931 chromaticity drift | $\Delta = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2+(y_2-y_1)^2}$ | (dimensionless) | Tolerance $\leq 0.012$ |

## 2.4 Photometric Visibility — Engineering Utility

The **inverse-square law** is the fundamental governing equation for the reach of any signalling lamp at night. For a stop lamp emitting $I = 100\,\text{cd}$ on the H–V axis, the illuminance received at a following driver's eye (assume $d = 30\,\text{m}$):

$$E = \frac{100 \cdot \cos 0^\circ}{(30)^2} = \frac{100}{900} \approx 0.111\;\text{lx}$$

A driver's eye can reliably perceive a red light at ~0.2 lx, so this lamp is at the **edge of perceptibility**. This is precisely why **AIS-010 mandates S2 (supplementary high-mount) lamps** and a minimum H–V intensity of **60 cd** — to double the safety margin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Photometric engineers use this calculation to decide the **lumen output** of LED replacement bulbs for tail lamps, ensuring that the optical stack (reflector + lens) delivers the AIS-mandated cd distribution without glare to following drivers.

## 2.5 The Colour Box — Chromaticity Coordinates

The **CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram** partitions the visible spectrum into colour zones prescribed by AIS-010. Key zones (memorise these):

- **Red (Stop, Rear-Fog, Position-Rear):** Boundary: $y \leq 0.335$
- **Amber (Turn, Side-Marker-Front, Position-Front-when-not-white):** Inside the trapezoid $0.398 \leq y \leq 0.429$, $0.549 \leq x \leq 0.610$
- **White (Reversing, DRL, Front-Position):** Inside the trapezoid $0.310 \leq x \leq 0.500$, $y \leq x + 0.15$
- **Selective Yellow (Fog-Front in Europe):** Boundary: $0.382 \leq y \leq 0.435$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** A turn signal **must not** be red — this is a CMVR-AIS-008 violation that attracts heavy fines. The reverse is also true: brake lights must not be amber.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Flasher Frequency from RC Time Constant

**Objective:** Derive the flash frequency of an *electronic* flasher unit used in turn-signal circuits.

### Step 1 — Identify the timing element
A 555-timer astable multivibrator drives the turn-signal relay coil. The capacitor $C$ charges through $R_1$ and discharges through $R_2$.

### Step 2 — Charging time
The capacitor charges from $\dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$ to $\dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3}$:

$$t_{charge} = 0.693 \,(R_1 + R_2)\,C$$

### Step 3 — Discharging time
The capacitor discharges from $\dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ to $\dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$:

$$t_{discharge} = 0.693 \,R_2\,C$$

### Step 4 — Total period

$$T = t_{charge} + t_{discharge} = 0.693 \,(R_1 + 2R_2)\,C$$

### Step 5 — Frequency

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2)\,C}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to flashes per minute (fpm)

$$f_{fpm} = 60 \,f = \frac{86.4}{(R_1 + 2R_2)\,C}$$

### Step 7 — Numerical example
Given $R_1 = 4.7\,k\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\,k\Omega$, $C = 10\,\mu F$:

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(4700 + 2 \cdot 10000)\cdot 10 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(24700) \cdot 10^{-5}} = \frac{1.44}{0.247} = 5.83\,\text{Hz}$$

$$f_{fpm} = 60 \times 5.83 = 350\,\text{fpm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** 350 fpm is **far above** the AIS-008 limit of 120 fpm. Thus, this RC combination is non-compliant — the designer must increase $C$ or $R_2$ to bring the frequency into the **60–120 fpm band**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Visibility Range of a Signalling Lamp

**Objective:** Compute the maximum distance at which a turn-signal lamp remains perceptible.

### Step 1 — Inverse-square illuminance at the observer's eye

$$E_{eye} = \frac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

### Step 2 — Threshold illuminance for human perception
For an amber point source against a dark night background:

$$E_{th} \approx 0.2\,\text{lx} \quad \text{(AIS recommendation)}$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange for distance

$$d_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{E_{th}}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute on-axis values ($\cos\theta = 1$)
For a typical turn-signal lamp with $I = 400\,cd$:

$$d_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{400}{0.2}} = \sqrt{2000} = 44.7\,\text{m}$$

### Step 5 — Realistic accounting
At off-axis angles ($\pm 15^\circ$ horizontal), the intensity drops to ~$0.5 \cdot I$ due to lens design. So:

$$d_{off-axis} = \sqrt{\frac{200}{0.2}} = \sqrt{1000} = 31.6\,\text{m}$$

### Step 6 — Conclusion
The lamp is visible at **~32 m on a dark night** — consistent with AIS-008 requirements of **~30 m minimum visibility distance** for front turn signals at on-axis.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Horn Acoustic Power & SPL at Distance

**Objective:** Estimate the sound pressure level (SPL) at 7 m from a vehicle horn.

### Step 1 — Reference SPL definition
$$L_{p,d} = L_{p,2m} - 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{d}{d_{ref}}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $d = 7\,m$, $d_{ref} = 2\,m$

$$L_{p,7m} = L_{p,2m} - 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{7}{2}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Compute the log term

$$20 \log_{10}(3.5) = 20 \times 0.5441 = 10.88\,\text{dB}$$

### Step 4 — Apply to a horn with $L_{p,2m} = 105\,dB(A)$ (AIS-018 limit)

$$L_{p,7m} = 105 - 10.88 = 94.12\,\text{dB(A)}$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation
At 7 m, the horn still produces **~94 dB(A)** — well above the ~85 dB auditory "warning" threshold and just below the 95 dB pain threshold. This satisfies the AIS-018 audible safety requirement.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Retro-Reflector CIL & Visibility

**Objective:** Compute the visibility of a rear retro-reflector under low-beam headlamp illumination.

### Step 1 — Define CIL

$$R = \frac{I_{ref}}{E_{inc}}$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange

$$I_{ref} = R \cdot E_{inc}$$

### Step 3 — Compute incident illuminance from a headlamp at $d = 10\,m$ on axis
A typical low-beam H7 bulb delivers $I \approx 30000\,cd$ on the H–V axis:

$$E_{inc} = \frac{30000}{(10)^2} = 300\,\text{lx}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute CIL = $500\,mcd/lx = 0.5\,cd/lx$

$$I_{ref} = 0.5 \times 300 = 150\,\text{cd}$$

### Step 5 — Observer at $d_2 = 50\,m$ behind the vehicle

$$E_{eye} = \frac{150}{(50)^2} = 0.06\,\text{lx}$$

### Step 6 — Conclude
The reflected intensity yields $0.06\,lx$ at the following driver's eye — at the **edge of perception**. This is why AIS-010 mandates a minimum CIL of **300 mcd/lx** at 0° observation angle, and why modern vehicles add **LED signalling lamps** that are *active* (not passive) for redundancy.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Device-to-Standard Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Cross-Reference Table (Examiner's Favourite)**

| Signalling Device | CMVR Rule | Indian Standard | ECE Equivalent | FMVSS |
|-------------------|-----------|-----------------|----------------|-------|
| Turn Indicator (Front/Rear/Side) | Rule 102(2) | AIS-008, AIS-009 | ECE R6, R48 | FMVSS 108 |
| Position Lamp (Front/Rear) | Rule 102(1) | AIS-008, AIS-010 | ECE R7, R48 | FMVSS 108 |
| Stop Lamp | Rule 103 | AIS-008, AIS-010 | ECE R7, R48 | FMVSS 108 |
| Rear-Fog Lamp | Rule 104 | AIS-008, AIS-010 | ECE R38, R48 | FMVSS 108 |
| Reversing Lamp | Rule 105 | AIS-008, AIS-010 | ECE R23, R48 | FMVSS 108 |
| Retro-Reflector | Rule 106 | AIS-008, AIS-010 | ECE R3, R150 | FMVSS 108 |
| Hazard Warning | Rule 108 | AIS-008, AIS-009 | ECE R48 | FMVSS 108 |
| Side-Marker Lamp | Rule 110 | AIS-008, AIS-010 | ECE R91, R48 | FMVSS 108 |
| Daytime Running Lamp | Rule 110A | AIS-008, AIS-010 | ECE R87, R48 | FMVSS 108 |
| Horn (Auditory) | Rule 119 | AIS-018 | ECE R28 | FMVSS 109 |
| Retro-Reflective Marking (HCV) | Rule 110B | AIS-090 | ECE R104 | — |

## 3.6 Numerical Problem — Sample KTU 14-Mark Type

**Problem (excerpt):** A vehicle's stop lamp emits a luminous intensity of 90 cd along the reference axis. Compute (a) the illuminance at the eye of a driver following at 25 m directly behind, (b) the maximum visibility distance for a threshold of 0.3 lx, and (c) the minimum luminous intensity required to achieve 50 m visibility at the same threshold.

### Solution

**(a)** With $\theta = 0^\circ$, $\cos 0^\circ = 1$:

$$E = \frac{90}{(25)^2} = \frac{90}{625} = 0.144\,\text{lx}$$

**(b)** Rearranging $E_{th} = I / d_{max}^2$:

$$d_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{90}{0.3}} = \sqrt{300} = 17.32\,\text{m}$$

**(c)** Required intensity:

$$I = E_{th} \cdot d_{max}^2 = 0.3 \cdot (50)^2 = 750\,\text{cd}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Vehicle Signalling System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    driverInput[Driver Input Switches] --> bcm[Body Control Module BCM]
    canBus[CAN Bus Network] --> bcm
    bcm --> frontInd[Front Turn Indicator Lamp]
    bcm --> rearInd[Rear Turn Indicator Lamp]
    bcm --> sideInd[Side Repeater Lamp]
    bcm --> brakeLamp[Brake Stop Lamp S1]
    bcm --> brakelamp2[High-Mount Stop Lamp S2]
    bcm --> revLamp[Reversing Lamp]
    bcm --> fogLamp[Rear Fog Lamp]
    bcm --> drlLamp[Daytime Running Lamp]
    bcm --> hornDriver[Horn Relay Driver]
    bcm --> flasherUnit[Electronic Flasher Unit]
    flasherUnit --> frontInd
    flasherUnit --> rearInd
    flasherUnit --> sideInd
    hornDriver --> hornActuator[Horn Electromagnetic Actuator]
    hornActuator --> hornMembrane[Horn Diaphragm Chamber]
    sensorBrake[Brake Pedal Switch] --> bcm
    sensorReverse[Reverse Gear Switch] --> bcm
    sensorHazard[Hazard Switch] --> bcm
    battery[12V Battery] --> bcm
    tellTale[Dashboard Tell Tale Indicators] --> driverInput
```

## 4.2 Photometric Test Geometry (Type-Approval Bench)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    lamp[Signalling Lamp Under Test] --> screen[Vertical Screen at 10.7 m]
    screen --> goniophotometer[Goniophotometer Sensor]
    goniophotometer --> controller[Photometric Controller]
    controller --> pc[PC Data Acquisition]
    lamp --> powersupply[Stabilised DC Power Supply]
    powersupply --> lamp
    controller --> powersupply
    pc --> report[Luminous Intensity Report candela vs Angle]
```

## 4.3 Signalling Device — Type-Approval Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Vehicle Manufacturer] --> proto[Prototype Build]
    proto --> arai[Submit to ARAI or ICAT]
    arai --> photTest[Photometric Test AIS-010]
    arai --> chromTest[Chromaticity Test AIS-010]
    arai --> geomTest[Geometric Installation Test AIS-008]
    arai --> envTest[Environmental Vibration Test]
    photTest --> decision{All Tests Pass?}
    chromTest --> decision
    geomTest --> decision
    envTest --> decision
    decision --> yes[Yes]
    decision --> no[No]
    yes --> cert[Type Approval Certificate Issued]
    no --> rework[Design Re-iteration]
    rework --> proto
    cert --> cmvr[CMVR Compliance Check Rule 126]
    cmvr --> prod[Series Production Allowed]
```

## 4.4 Acoustic Signal Generation Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    driverPress[Driver Presses Horn Pad] --> relay[Steering Column Slip Ring]
    relay --> coil[Solenoid Coil Energised]
    coil --> armature[Armature Pulls Down]
    armature --> contact[Contact Breaks]
    contact --> coil
    armature --> membrane[Diaphragm Vibrates]
    membrane --> chamber[Horn Trumpet Chamber]
    chamber --> resonance[Resonance Amplification 2 kHz]
    resonance --> air[Acoustic Wave to Ambient Air]
```

## 4.5 Signalling Device — Hierarchical Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Signalling Devices]
    root --> active[Active Light Emitting]
    root --> passive[Passive Light Reflecting]
    root --> audible[Audible]
    active --> a1[Turn Indicator]
    active --> a2[Brake Lamp]
    active --> a3[Rear Fog Lamp]
    active --> a4[Reversing Lamp]
    active --> a5[Position Lamp]
    active --> a6[DRL]
    active --> a7[Hazard Lamp]
    active --> a8[Side Marker]
    active --> a9[Tell Tale Indicator]
    passive --> p1[Rear Retro Reflector]
    passive --> p2[Front Retro Reflector]
    passive --> p3[Side Retro Reflector]
    passive --> p4[Contour Marking ECE R104]
    audible --> au1[Electric Horn]
    audible --> au2[Air Horn HCV]
```

## 4.6 State Diagram — Flasher Unit Operation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Off
    Off --> Charging : Switch Closed
    Charging --> On : Capacitor reaches upper threshold
    On --> Discharging : Output toggles
    Discharging --> Off : Capacitor drops to lower threshold
    Off --> [*]
    Charging --> Fault : Current Out of Range
    On --> Fault : Current Out of Range
    Fault --> Indicator : Fast Flash 180 fpm
    Indicator --> Charging : Reset by Switch Off On
```

## 4.7 Geometric Mounting Diagram (Top View of Vehicle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Front Left Turn Lamp] --- F2[Front Right Turn Lamp]
    F1 --- F3[DRL Front Left]
    F2 --- F4[DRL Front Right]
    F1 --- F5[Front Position Lamp Left]
    F2 --- F6[Front Position Lamp Right]
    F5 --- S1[Side Marker Left Amber]
    F6 --- S2[Side Marker Right Amber]
    S1 --- SR1[Side Retro Reflector Left]
    S2 --- SR2[Side Retro Reflector Right]
    SR1 --- RR1[Rear Retro Reflector Left]
    SR2 --- RR2[Rear Retro Reflector Right]
    RR1 --- R1[Rear Left Turn Lamp]
    RR2 --- R2[Rear Right Turn Lamp]
    RR1 --- RL1[Rear Position Lamp Left Red]
    RR2 --- RL2[Rear Position Lamp Right Red]
    RL1 --- BL1[Brake Lamp S1 Left]
    RL2 --- BL2[Brake Lamp S1 Right]
    BL1 --- BL3[Brake Lamp S2 Centre High Mount]
    BL2 --- BL3
    RL1 --- RF1[Rear Fog Lamp Single]
    RL2 --- RV1[Reversing Lamp Single or Pair]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMUT526]**

**Q1. Differentiate between a retro-reflector and a signalling lamp in an automobile. State the relevant AIS/ECE standard that governs each.**

**Model Answer:**
A *signalling lamp* is an **active** device that emits light from an internal source (bulb/LED) when energised; governed by **AIS-008 (installation) and AIS-010 (photometric)**. A *retro-reflector* is a **passive** device that reflects incident light from an external source (e.g., another vehicle's headlamp) back along the same axis; governed by **AIS-008 and AIS-010** with specific CIL (Coefficient of Luminous Intensity) requirements per **ECE R3 / R150**. The key difference is the **internal light source** (lamp) versus **external light source** (reflector).

> **Examiner's Tip:** Always specify the **AIS number** — generic answers lose 1 mark.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper PEMUT526]**

**Q2. What is the permissible flash rate for a direction-indicator lamp as per AIS-008? Justify why this range is mandated.**

**Model Answer:**
Per **AIS-008**, the flash rate for a direction-indicator lamp is **60 to 120 flashes per minute**, with a preferred value of **90 ± 30 fpm**. This range corresponds to the human eye's *critical flicker fusion* (CFF) frequency, ensuring the driver perceives distinct, individual flashes (each communicating intent) without the flashing merging into a steady glow, which would defeat the purpose of the warning signal.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never write "AIS-008 governs turn signals" without also mentioning AIS-010** — installation and photometric are paired standards; missing one costs ~3 marks.
> 2. **Always quote CMVR Rule numbers** (102, 103, 104…) — Rule 126 alone is not enough.
> 3. **Photometric questions demand the formula $E = I/d^2$ explicitly** — not just the answer; the derivation is the mark-bearing content.
> 4. **CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinates must be quoted as a range** (e.g., $y \leq 0.335$ for red), not as a single number.
> 5. **For horn questions, the unit is dB(A)** — A-weighted; specifying dB without (A) is technically incorrect and loses a mark.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choose Either Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice-1]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the different types of automotive signalling devices with their colour specifications as per AIS-010. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle's front turn-signal lamp emits 350 cd on the reference axis. The driver of an approaching vehicle is at 15 m. Calculate the illuminance at the approaching driver's eye on the H–V axis. If the threshold for amber-light perception is 0.25 lx, compute the maximum visibility distance. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — Types and Colour Specifications

| Device | Colour (AIS-010 / ECE R48) | CIE 1931 Boundary |
|--------|------------------------------|--------------------|
| Turn Indicator (Front/Rear/Side) | **Amber** | $0.549 \le x \le 0.610$, $0.382 \le y \le 0.429$ |
| Position Lamp (Front) | **White** (or Amber if regulatory) | $0.310 \le x \le 0.500$, $y \le x + 0.15$ |
| Position Lamp (Rear) | **Red** | $y \le 0.335$ |
| Brake / Stop Lamp | **Red** | $y \le 0.335$ |
| Rear-Fog Lamp | **Red** | $y \le 0.335$ |
| Reversing Lamp | **White** | $0.310 \le x \le 0.500$ |
| DRL | **White** | $0.310 \le x \le 0.500$ |
| Side-Marker (Front) | **Amber** | Same as turn signal |
| Side-Marker (Rear) | **Red** | $y \le 0.335$ |
| Retro-Reflectors (Rear) | **Red** | $y \le 0.335$ |

#### Part (b) — Photometric Computation

**Step 1 — Illuminance at 15 m:**

$$E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^2} = \frac{350 \cdot \cos 0^\circ}{15^2} = \frac{350}{225} = 1.556\;\text{lx}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum visibility distance:**

$$d_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{I}{E_{th}}} = \sqrt{\frac{350}{0.25}} = \sqrt{1400} = 37.42\;\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
The lamp produces **1.556 lx at 15 m** (well above threshold), and remains perceptible up to **~37.4 m**, satisfying AIS-008 minimum visibility of 30 m for front turn indicators.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternate Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Choice-2]**

**Q. (a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the working of an electronic flasher unit used in turn-signal circuits. Derive the expression for its flash frequency. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle's stop lamp has a luminous intensity of 110 cd on the reference axis. Compute the maximum visibility distance if the threshold illuminance is 0.2 lx. If the lamp is replaced with an LED unit producing 240 cd, by what percentage does the visibility distance increase? **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — Electronic Flasher Working

The electronic flasher is a 555-timer-based astable multivibrator (see **Section 4.6** for state diagram).

**Step 1 — Working:**
When the driver engages the turn-signal stalk, the BCM supplies 12 V to the flasher. The 555 timer's internal flip-flop alternates the output between HIGH and LOW at a frequency determined by $R_1$, $R_2$, and $C$. The output drives a MOSFET that energises the indicator-lamp relay coil.

**Step 2 — Charge and discharge times:**

$$t_{charge} = 0.693(R_1 + R_2)C, \quad t_{discharge} = 0.693 \, R_2 C$$

**Step 3 — Period and frequency:**

$$T = 0.693(R_1 + 2R_2)C \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; f = \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2)C}$$

**Step 4 — Flashes per minute:**

$$f_{fpm} = \frac{86.4}{(R_1 + 2R_2)C}$$

**Step 5 — Bulb-failure detection:**
If a bulb blows, current drops. A sense resistor detects the drop and the microcontroller switches the relay to "fast-flash" mode (~180 fpm), alerting the driver.

#### Part (b) — Stop-Lamp Photometry

**Step 1 — Old lamp maximum distance:**

$$d_1 = \sqrt{\frac{110}{0.2}} = \sqrt{550} = 23.45\;\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — New LED-lamp maximum distance:**

$$d_2 = \sqrt{\frac{240}{0.2}} = \sqrt{1200} = 34.64\;\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage increase:**

$$\Delta\% = \frac{d_2 - d_1}{d_1} \times 100 = \frac{34.64 - 23.45}{23.45} \times 100$$

$$\Delta\% = \frac{11.19}{23.45} \times 100 = 47.72\%$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
The LED upgrade provides a **~47.7% increase in visibility distance**, with additional benefits of faster rise time (~10 ms vs ~150 ms for incandescent) — a critical safety factor for emergency braking scenarios.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Exam Day**

### 🔑 Definitions
- [ ] **Signalling Device** = a device that *conveys information* to other road users (not a headlamp that illuminates the road).
- [ ] **Active lamp** = contains its own light source (bulb/LED).
- [ ] **Passive reflector** = reflects light from an external source using cube-corner or glass-bead optics.
- [ ] **CIL** = Coefficient of Luminous Intensity, units **mcd/lx**.

### 🔑 Numerical Values
- [ ] Turn-signal flash rate: **60–120 fpm** (preferred **90 ± 30 fpm**).
- [ ] Horn SPL: **93–112 dB(A)** at 2 m.
- [ ] Stop lamp intensity: **60–185 cd** on H–V axis.
- [ ] Rear-fog lamp: **single**, red, **on/off-centre**.
- [ ] Visibility distance for front turn signal: **≥ 30 m** (AIS-008).
- [ ] Reversing lamp: **white**, automatic on reverse-gear engagement.

### 🔑 Standard Cross-Reference
- [ ] **AIS-008** = Installation requirements.
- [ ] **AIS-010** = Photometric requirements.
- [ ] **AIS-018** = Horn / audible device.
- [ ] **CMVR Rule 102–110** = Statutory mapping for each device.
- [ ] **ECE R48** = Master installation regulation (Europe).
- [ ] **ECE R6, R7, R23, R38, R104** = Device-specific regulations.
- [ ] **FMVSS 108** = U.S. Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standard.

### 🔑 Colour Codes (CIE 1931)
- [ ] **Red** → $y \leq 0.335$
- [ ] **Amber** → trapezoid defined in **AIS-010**
- [ ] **White** → $0.310 \leq x \leq 0.500$, $y \leq x + 0.15$

### 🔑 Common Examiner Traps
- [ ] Brake lamp ≠ rear-fog lamp (single vs single but different switching).
- [ ] Turn signal can be amber, but **never red**.
- [ ] DRL auto-activates on ignition — *not* a substitute for headlamps.
- [ ] "Telltale" = driver-side dashboard indicator (not an external lamp).
- [ ] **"S2" stop lamp** = high-mount supplementary brake lamp, mandated separately from S1.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Pearl:** When asked "Explain AIS-008 vs AIS-010", always frame the answer as: *AIS-008 governs **where** and **how** the device is mounted; AIS-010 governs **what light** the device emits (intensity, colour, area).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Position of indicators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Position of indicators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Position of Indicators (Direction Signal Lamps):** The geometrically prescribed mounting locations, height ranges, lateral spacing, and angular visibility zones of front, rear, and side turn-signal lamps (direction indicators) on a motor vehicle, as mandated by **AIS-008** (Automotive Industry Standard 008, harmonized with **ECE R6** and **ECE R7**) and enforced under the **Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR), 1989**.

In technical regulatory language, the *position* of an indicator defines **where exactly** a lamp must be physically installed on a vehicle such that its **illuminating surface, reference center, and reference axis** satisfy the dimensional and angular envelope prescribed by the standard.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Lighthouse of the Road"

Think of your vehicle's turn indicators as the **"blinkers" or "lighthouses"** of the car. Imagine a lighthouse on a coastline: the regulator does not just say "put a lighthouse there" — it specifies the **height above sea level** (so ships can see over waves), the **distance from the cliff edge** (so it's safely away from erosion), and the **arc of light it must sweep** (so ships in every direction are warned).

In exactly the same way, automotive regulators (BIS / ARAI in India, UN-ECE in Europe) say:
- **HOW HIGH** the indicator must sit off the ground,
- **HOW FAR IN** from the vehicle's extreme outer edge,
- **HOW FAR APART** the left and right indicators must be, and
- **OVER WHAT ANGLE** it must remain visible to other drivers and pedestrians.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Decoded (AIS-008):**
> - **Illuminating Surface:** The effective light-emitting portion of the lens (parallel projection).
> - **Reference Centre:** The geometric centre of the illuminating surface.
> - **Reference Axis:** The line through the reference centre perpendicular to the illuminating surface.
> - **Extreme Outer Edge:** The plane parallel to the median longitudinal plane of the vehicle and touching its outermost lateral contour.

## 1.3 Why "Position" Is a Separate Topic in the Standard

Position is **distinct from** photometric (light intensity), color, or flashing-frequency requirements. A lamp that produces perfect amber light at the correct frequency is still **non-compliant** if it is mounted **too high, too low, too close together, or out of sight** of the following driver's eye-line. Position is therefore a **geometric compliance checkpoint** evaluated before the lamp is even switched on.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Classes of Indicator Position

The standard recognizes **three mounting regions** on a vehicle, each with its own envelope:

### 2.1.1 Front Position Indicators (Category 1, 1a, 1b)

| Parameter | Requirement | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Number** | Exactly **2** (one per side) | Symmetry is mandatory |
| **Height above ground** | **≥ 350 mm** and **≤ 1500 mm** | For M1/N1 passenger cars and light commercial |
| **Height above ground (off-road)** | **≤ 2300 mm** | M1G / N1G category only |
| **Distance from extreme outer edge** | **≤ 400 mm** | Reference centre to outer edge |
| **Inward spacing between pair** | **≥ 600 mm** | Edge-to-edge of illuminating surface |
| **Lateral symmetry** | Mandatory | Mirror image about longitudinal axis |

### 2.1.2 Rear Position Indicators (Category 2a, 2b)

| Parameter | Requirement | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Number** | Exactly **2** (one per side) | Symmetry is mandatory |
| **Height above ground** | **≥ 350 mm** and **≤ 1500 mm** | Same envelope as front |
| **Height above ground (off-road)** | **≤ 2300 mm** | M1G / N1G category |
| **Distance from extreme outer edge** | **≤ 400 mm** | Reference centre to outer edge |
| **Inward spacing between pair** | **≥ 600 mm** | Edge-to-edge of illuminating surface |
| **Pairing with stop lamp** | Independent | May be combined with brake lamp optically but not electrically |

### 2.1.3 Side Direction Indicators (Category 5, 6)

> [!NOTE]
> **Trigger Condition:** Mandatory when the vehicle length **> 6 metres** (typically buses and trucks) OR when front and rear indicators alone cannot fulfil the longitudinal visibility requirement of the lamp category.

| Parameter | Requirement | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Number** | At least **1 per side** (intermediate) | More permitted; must be evenly distributed |
| **Height above ground** | **≥ 350 mm** and **≤ 1500 mm** | Same as front/rear |
| **Longitudinal position** | Distributed over vehicle length | To ensure mid-vehicle visibility |
| **Spacing between side units** | Uniformly distributed | Avoids dark zones in mid-section |

## 2.2 Geometric Visibility — The "Cone of Sight"

Every position requirement ultimately serves the **visibility angle** $\theta$ from which a following or oncoming observer can see the indicator. The standard specifies the **solid angle within which the lamp must remain visible without obstruction.**

### 2.2.1 Angular Envelope (as per AIS-008 / ECE R6)

The horizontal and vertical visibility angles are measured **from the reference axis of the lamp outward:**

| Direction | Front Indicator | Rear Indicator | Side Indicator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal — Inward** | $\geq 45^\circ$ | $\geq 45^\circ$ | $\geq 45^\circ$ forward / rearward |
| **Horizontal — Outward** | $\geq 80^\circ$ | $\geq 80^\circ$ | $360^\circ$ around axis |
| **Vertical — Upward** | $\geq 15^\circ$ | $\geq 15^\circ$ | $\geq 15^\circ$ |
| **Vertical — Downward** | $\geq 15^\circ$ | $\geq 15^\circ$ | $\geq 15^\circ$ |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Position Compliance Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All position-related measurements are linear (mm) or angular (degrees). No complex algebraic derivations — but a few **inequality-based checks** dominate KTU problems.**

| # | Compliance Check | Formula / Inequality | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ground clearance of lamp | $h_{\min} \leq h \leq h_{\max}$ | mm |
| 2 | Edge-to-outer-body distance | $d_{\text{edge}} \leq 400$ | mm |
| 3 | Inter-lamp spacing (front or rear pair) | $s_{\text{inter}} \geq 600$ | mm |
| 4 | Horizontal half-angle visibility | $\alpha_{\text{out}} \geq 80^\circ,\ \alpha_{\text{in}} \geq 45^\circ$ | degrees |
| 5 | Vertical half-angle visibility | $\beta \geq 15^\circ$ (up & down) | degrees |
| 6 | Side-indicator trigger condition | $L_{\text{vehicle}} > 6000$ | mm |
| 7 | Symmetry about longitudinal plane | $\Delta x = 0$ (left vs. right) | mm |

**Where:**
- $h$ = height of reference centre above ground
- $d_{\text{edge}}$ = horizontal distance from reference centre to extreme outer edge of vehicle
- $s_{\text{inter}}$ = edge-to-edge spacing between left and right lamps
- $\alpha_{\text{out}}$ = outward horizontal half-angle from reference axis
- $\alpha_{\text{in}}$ = inward horizontal half-angle
- $\beta$ = vertical half-angle (above or below reference axis)
- $L_{\text{vehicle}}$ = overall vehicle length

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Crash Safety Integration:** Mounting between 350 mm and 1500 mm keeps the lamp out of the primary crumple zone of the bumper while staying within the sight-line of a car driver (typical eye-height ≈ 1100 mm).
2. **Aerodynamic & Styling Constraint:** The 1500 mm upper bound forces the lamp onto the body shell, preventing designers from relocating it into roof-mounted pods where it would be invisible to nearby traffic.
3. **Pedestrian & Cyclist Visibility:** The 80° outward angle ensures a vehicle turning at an intersection is visible to a pedestrian stepping off the kerb — even from a near-side approach.
4. **Type-Approval Homologation:** A vehicle manufactured in India (or imported) must undergo **AIS-008 testing at ARAI**, where a **protractor-based sighting test** confirms the angular envelope. Failure at this stage rejects the lot, irrespective of photometric performance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The 600 mm Pair-Spacing Rule

The minimum inter-lamp spacing of 600 mm is **not an arbitrary number**. It is derived from a simple geometric argument rooted in driver perception.

### Step 1 — Define the problem
A following driver needs to **visually resolve two distinct lamps** (left and right indicators) at a safe stopping distance. Human visual acuity requires a **minimum angular separation** $\delta$ of approximately **0.57°** (1/60th of a degree subtended) for a typical eye.

### Step 2 — Set up the geometry
Let the following vehicle be at a distance $D$ behind the leading vehicle. The two indicators of the leading vehicle are separated laterally by a distance $s$. The visual angle $\delta$ subtended at the following driver's eye is:

$$
\delta \;=\; 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{s/2}{D}\right) \;\approx\; \frac{s}{D} \quad \text{(radians, for small angles)}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the safety stopping distance
For a typical road scenario, consider $D$ as the perception-reaction plus braking distance for **60 km/h** on a dry road:
- Perception-reaction time: $t \approx 1.5\ \text{s}$
- Reaction distance: $d_r = v \cdot t = (16.67\ \text{m/s})(1.5\ \text{s}) = 25\ \text{m}$
- Braking distance @ 0.7 deceleration: $d_b = \dfrac{v^2}{2 a} = \dfrac{(16.67)^2}{2(7)} \approx 19.8\ \text{m}$
- Total: $D = d_r + d_b \approx 45\ \text{m}$

### Step 4 — Solve for $s$
Setting $\delta = 0.57^\circ = 0.00995$ rad:

$$
s \;=\; \delta \cdot D \;=\; (0.00995)(45{,}000\ \text{mm}) \;\approx\; 448\ \text{mm}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply regulatory safety factor
To account for **low-light contrast, rain, dirt on lens, and ageing**, the regulator adds a **safety factor of ~1.34**, rounding up to the nearest 50 mm:

$$
s_{\text{regulatory}} \;=\; 448 \times 1.34 \;\approx\; 600\ \text{mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **why AIS-008 mandates ≥ 600 mm** — it is the **acuity-limited minimum lateral spacing** that guarantees two-point resolution by a following driver at a 45 m safe stopping distance. Students should remember the **perception-reaction model** behind this number — examiners love it.

## 3.2 Derivation — Vertical Visibility Cone

A lamp mounted at height $h$ above the ground with the reference axis horizontal must remain visible over a vertical half-angle $\beta = 15^\circ$. Deriving the **required clear vertical envelope** above and below the lamp:

$$
\Delta h_{\text{above}} \;=\; (D_{\text{eye}} - h) \tan(\beta) \quad \text{(occlusion clearance above lamp)}
$$

$$
\Delta h_{\text{below}} \;=\; h \tan(\beta) \quad \text{(occlusion clearance below lamp)}
$$

### Worked Example — Bus-mounted rear indicator
Take $h = 1100\ \text{mm}$ (typical bus rear) and observer eye height $D_{\text{eye}} = 1250\ \text{mm}$ (typical car driver):

$$
\Delta h_{\text{above}} \;=\; (1250 - 1100) \tan(15^\circ) \;=\; 150 \times 0.2679 \;\approx\; 40.2\ \text{mm}
$$

$$
\Delta h_{\text{below}} \;=\; 1100 \times \tan(15^\circ) \;=\; 1100 \times 0.2679 \;\approx\; 294.7\ \text{mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The 15° vertical half-angle ensures the lamp remains visible to a driver whose eye-line is up to **40 mm above** the lamp or **295 mm below** the lamp, at the reference distance. The 1500 mm upper-bound on $h$ ensures the eye-line of a typical following driver (≈ 1100–1250 mm) is **always within this 30° vertical cone**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Position Compliance Scenarios

| Vehicle Class | Length (m) | Lamp Position Set | Mandatory? | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Motorcycle (L1, L2) | ≤ 4 | Front pair + Rear pair | Yes | Side optional |
| Passenger Car (M1) | 3.5 – 5.0 | Front pair + Rear pair | Yes | Side not required |
| Light Commercial (N1) | ≤ 6 | Front pair + Rear pair | Yes | Side not required |
| Bus / Truck (M2, M3, N2, N3) | > 6 | Front + Rear + **Side** | Yes | Side mandatory |
| Off-road (M1G, N1G) | Any | All three sets | Yes | Height relaxation to 2300 mm |
| Trailer (O) | > 6 | Rear pair + Side | Yes | Front absent |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Compliance Check (Standard Algorithm)

Use this **7-step checklist** to verify any vehicle's indicator position compliance — this is exactly what an ARAI inspector does:

1. **Count the lamps** per side (front, rear, side).
2. **Measure the height** $h$ of the reference centre above the ground.
3. **Verify** $350\ \text{mm} \leq h \leq 1500\ \text{mm}$ (or 2300 mm for off-road).
4. **Measure the distance** $d_{\text{edge}}$ from reference centre to extreme outer body edge.
5. **Verify** $d_{\text{edge}} \leq 400\ \text{mm}$.
6. **Measure the lateral spacing** $s$ between left and right lamps (edge-to-edge).
7. **Verify** $s \geq 600\ \text{mm}$ AND symmetry about the longitudinal plane.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always state the **reference centre** explicitly when giving a position. Marks are routinely lost when students quote lamp "edge" coordinates instead of the **reference centre**, because the standard unambiguously defines all measurements from the **reference centre**, not from the lens edge.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Indicator Position Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Vehicle Position Compliance Check] --> B[Front Indicator Pair]
    A --> C[Rear Indicator Pair]
    A --> D[Side Indicator Set]
    A --> E[Geometric Visibility Cone]

    B --> B1[Height Check 350 to 1500 mm]
    B --> B2[Edge to Outer Body less than 400 mm]
    B --> B3[Inter Lamp Spacing more than 600 mm]

    C --> C1[Height Check 350 to 1500 mm]
    C --> C2[Edge to Outer Body less than 400 mm]
    C --> C3[Inter Lamp Spacing more than 600 mm]

    D --> D1[Mandatory if Vehicle Length over 6 m]
    D --> D2[Uniform Longitudinal Distribution]
    D --> D3[Height Check 350 to 1500 mm]

    E --> E1[Horizontal Outward more than 80 deg]
    E --> E2[Horizontal Inward more than 45 deg]
    E --> E3[Vertical Cone more than 15 deg]
    E --> E4[Symmetry about Longitudinal Plane]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Top-Down Visibility Angle Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FrontOfVehicle
        direction LR
        L1[Left Front Lamp]
        AX1[Reference Axis]
        R1[Right Front Lamp]
        L1 --- AX1
        AX1 --- R1
    end

    subgraph RearOfVehicle
        direction LR
        L2[Left Rear Lamp]
        AX2[Reference Axis]
        R2[Right Rear Lamp]
        L2 --- AX2
        AX2 --- R2
    end

    subgraph SideOfVehicle
        direction TB
        S1[Left Side Lamp 1]
        S2[Left Side Lamp 2]
        S3[Right Side Lamp 1]
        S4[Right Side Lamp 2]
    end

    FrontOfVehicle --> SideOfVehicle
    SideOfVehicle --> RearOfVehicle
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Position Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[New Vehicle Design] --> Q1{Vehicle Length over 6 m?}
    Q1 -- No --> Path1[Front Pair plus Rear Pair only]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Path2[Front Pair plus Rear Pair plus Side Set]
    Path2 --> Q2{Vehicle Class M1G or N1G?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Path3[Allow Height Limit up to 2300 mm]
    Q2 -- No --> Path4[Height Limit 1500 mm Strict]
    Path1 --> Test[ARAI Type Approval Test]
    Path3 --> Test
    Path4 --> Test
    Test --> R{All Checks Pass?}
    R -- Yes --> Approve[Homologation Granted]
    R -- No --> Reject[Non Compliant Return to Design]
```

## 4.4 Functional Interpretation of the Diagrams

- **Architecture Diagram (4.1):** Maps the **three independent position requirements** (front / rear / side) and the **overarching visibility cone** requirement. Each block represents a **verifiable measurement** in the homologation protocol.
- **Top-Down Angle Map (4.2):** Illustrates the **physical layout** of the lamp arrays on the vehicle silhouette. Note that **reference axes** are always parallel to the longitudinal plane of the vehicle.
- **Decision Flow (4.3):** Encapsulates the **logical branching** an OEM engineer follows during design. Vehicle length is the **primary branching variable**; off-road classification is a **secondary relaxation** on the height envelope.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

### **Q1. Define the term "reference centre" as used in AIS-008 for the position of indicators. Why is it important to use this point for all position measurements?**

**Model Answer:**

The **reference centre** is the **geometric centre of the illuminating surface** of the indicator lamp, identified by the manufacturer and declared during type-approval.

It is used as the **universal reference point** for all position-related measurements (height above ground, distance from outer edge, lateral spacing) because:
1. The **lens housing may have irregular shapes, flanges, or bezels** that make lens-edge measurements ambiguous.
2. The illuminating surface has a **well-defined parallel projection** under the standard's measurement protocol, giving a **unique and reproducible centre**.
3. It **decouples** the geometric position requirement from cosmetic/trim design variations.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for justification]**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### **Q2. State the minimum height above the ground and the maximum distance from the extreme outer edge of the vehicle for a rear direction indicator as per AIS-008.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Minimum height above ground:** $h_{\min} = 350\ \text{mm}$
- **Maximum height above ground:** $h_{\max} = 1500\ \text{mm}$ (2300 mm for off-road M1G / N1G classes)
- **Maximum distance from extreme outer edge (reference centre to edge):** $d_{\text{edge}} \leq 400\ \text{mm}$

**[3 Marks: 1 mark per correct numerical value with units]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **position requirements for front direction indicators** of a passenger car (M1 category) as per **AIS-008**. Your answer must include the **height envelope, edge clearance, and inter-lamp spacing**, with units. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, describe the **geometric visibility cone** (horizontal and vertical angles) for a front turn-signal lamp. Justify why the outward horizontal angle is **larger (80°) than the inward angle (45°)**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

The **position of front direction indicators** for an M1 passenger car is governed by AIS-008 and is described by the following **three independent constraints:**

**1. Height envelope above ground (2 marks)**

The reference centre of each front indicator must lie within the band:
$$350\ \text{mm} \;\leq\; h_{\text{front}} \;\leq\; 1500\ \text{mm}$$

The lower bound (350 mm) prevents the lamp from being embedded in the bumper fascia, where it would be damaged in low-speed impacts and contaminated by road spray. The upper bound (1500 mm) keeps the lamp within the typical following-driver eye-line (≈ 1100–1250 mm), ensuring the lamp sits within the vertical visibility cone.

**2. Distance from extreme outer edge of the vehicle (2 marks)**

The reference centre of the lamp must be:
$$d_{\text{edge}} \;\leq\; 400\ \text{mm}$$
measured horizontally inboard from the **extreme outer edge** (i.e., the lateral plane tangent to the vehicle's outermost body contour). This guarantees the indicator is visible to an oncoming or overtaking observer without being obscured by the side of the vehicle.

**3. Inter-lamp spacing (2 marks)**

The two front indicators (left and right) must be separated by an edge-to-edge spacing of:
$$s_{\text{inter}} \;\geq\; 600\ \text{mm}$$

This guarantees **two-point visual resolution** by a following driver at a 45 m perception-reaction plus braking distance, as derived from the human visual acuity model.

**4. Symmetry (1 mark)**

The pair must be installed in **mirror symmetry** about the longitudinal median plane of the vehicle.

---

#### Model Solution — (b)

The **geometric visibility cone** of a front turn-signal lamp defines the **solid angle of unobstructed space** within which an observer must be able to see the illuminated lamp. Refer to the top-down angle map in Section 4.2.

**Horizontal angles (3 marks):**
- **Inward (towards the vehicle centreline):** $\alpha_{\text{in}} \geq 45^\circ$
- **Outward (towards the vehicle side):** $\alpha_{\text{out}} \geq 80^\circ$

**Vertical angles (2 marks):**
- **Upward (above the reference axis):** $\beta_{\text{up}} \geq 15^\circ$
- **Downward (below the reference axis):** $\beta_{\text{down}} \geq 15^\circ$

**Justification for asymmetric horizontal angles (2 marks):**
The **outward angle (80°) is larger than the inward angle (45°)** because the critical observer for a front indicator is the **oncoming driver and the pedestrian at the far-side kerb**, both of whom are located **outboard and forward** of the subject vehicle. The inward direction is partly blocked by the vehicle's own body and the driver of the subject vehicle, so a narrower 45° cone in that direction is sufficient to cover the relevant observers (a driver beside the vehicle in an adjacent lane).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently write the angles as "**45° outward and 80° inward**" — this is **inverted** and will cost **2 marks**. Always remember: **inward = small, outward = large**, because the *outward* direction faces the most important observers (oncoming traffic and pedestrians).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** A passenger car has its front direction indicators mounted at the following measured positions:
- **Height of reference centre above ground:** $h = 820\ \text{mm}$
- **Distance of reference centre from extreme outer edge:** $d_{\text{edge}} = 280\ \text{mm}$
- **Inter-lamp lateral spacing (edge-to-edge of illuminating surface):** $s = 940\ \text{mm}$

**Check whether the mounting positions are AIS-008 compliant. Show all inequality evaluations.** *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Under what **vehicle-class and length conditions** are **side direction indicators mandatory**? List the **two height constraints** and explain why a side indicator may be needed even if front and rear indicators are present. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a) — Quantitative Compliance Check

| Check | Standard Requirement | Measured Value | Inequality Test | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Minimum height | $h \geq 350\ \text{mm}$ | $h = 820\ \text{mm}$ | $820 \geq 350$ ✅ | **Pass** |
| Maximum height | $h \leq 1500\ \text{mm}$ | $h = 820\ \text{mm}$ | $820 \leq 1500$ ✅ | **Pass** |
| Edge clearance | $d_{\text{edge}} \leq 400\ \text{mm}$ | $d_{\text{edge}} = 280\ \text{mm}$ | $280 \leq 400$ ✅ | **Pass** |
| Inter-lamp spacing | $s \geq 600\ \text{mm}$ | $s = 940\ \text{mm}$ | $940 \geq 600$ ✅ | **Pass** |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** All four AIS-008 position constraints are satisfied. The mounting configuration is **COMPLIANT** for the M1 passenger car.

**Full marking breakdown:** 1.5 marks per correct inequality evaluation (×4 = 6 marks) + 1 mark for final conclusion.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) — Side Indicators

**Triggering conditions (3 marks):**

Side direction indicators (Category 5/6 per AIS-008) become **mandatory** when **either** of the following is true:

1. **Vehicle length $L > 6\ \text{m}$** (i.e., buses, long trucks, and articulated vehicles). This is a hard rule in AIS-008.
2. **Front and rear indicators alone cannot satisfy the longitudinal visibility requirement** — for example, when the front-to-rear distance is so large that an indicator at the rear is geometrically occluded from an oblique-side view by the vehicle's own bodywork.

**Height constraints (2 marks):**

The two height constraints are identical to the front/rear envelope:
- **Lower bound:** $h \geq 350\ \text{mm}$ (above ground)
- **Upper bound:** $h \leq 1500\ \text{mm}$ (above ground)

**Why side indicators may be needed despite front and rear indicators being present (2 marks):**

Consider a long bus where the front indicator is at the front bumper and the rear indicator is at the rear. An observer standing at the **mid-side of the bus** (e.g., another vehicle at a perpendicular intersection) cannot see the front or rear indicator because the bus's own body blocks the line of sight. The side indicators fill this **longitudinal visibility gap**, ensuring that turn signals are always visible from any lateral position around the vehicle's perimeter.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Quant Problems:**
> 1. **Always quote the limit and the measured value on the same line** as a comparison — e.g., "820 ≥ 350 ✓". Half-marks are lost when the conclusion is stated without the supporting inequality.
> 2. **Do not forget the 400 mm edge rule** — many students quote only the height. AIS-008 has **three independent** position checks (height, edge, spacing); each must be evaluated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Position of Indicators (AIS-008 / ECE R6)**

- ☐ **Three classes of position:** Front, Rear, Side — each has its **own envelope** of constraints.
- ☐ **Universal height band:** $350\ \text{mm} \leq h \leq 1500\ \text{mm}$ (off-road relaxation to 2300 mm).
- ☐ **Universal edge rule:** $d_{\text{edge}} \leq 400\ \text{mm}$ (reference centre to extreme outer edge).
- ☐ **Inter-lamp spacing rule:** $s_{\text{inter}} \geq 600\ \text{mm}$ (edge-to-edge of illuminating surface).
- ☐ **Side indicators are mandatory** when vehicle length $L > 6\ \text{m}$ (buses and long trucks).
- ☐ **Horizontal visibility:** 80° outward, 45° inward.
- ☐ **Vertical visibility:** 15° above and 15° below the reference axis.
- ☐ **All measurements are made from the reference centre**, never from the lens housing or trim edge.
- ☐ **The 600 mm spacing** is derived from human visual acuity (≈ 0.57° at a 45 m stopping distance) with a 1.34 safety factor.
- ☐ **Symmetry** about the longitudinal median plane of the vehicle is a **mandatory design constraint**.
- ☐ **AIS-008 is the Indian homologation standard**, harmonized with **ECE R6 (front, side, rear)** and **ECE R7** regulations.
- ☐ **Position failure = homologation failure**, irrespective of photometric (light intensity) or colorimetric performance.
- ☐ **Reference axis** is the line through the reference centre perpendicular to the illuminating surface — used as the datum for angular visibility checks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Headlight height
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Headlight height

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Headlight Height** is a precisely regulated geometric parameter of an automotive vehicle's front lighting installation, defined as the **vertical distance measured from the ground (or a defined horizontal reference plane) to the optical center (H-V reference point / luminous center) of the headlamp's effective light-emitting aperture**, evaluated under standard laden vehicle conditions.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, headlight height is governed by a tripartite regulatory framework:

- **AIS-008** (Automotive Industry Standard – Installation Requirements for Lighting and Light-Signalling Devices) for M, N, and L category vehicles in India.
- **ECE R48** (UN Regulation No. 48 – Uniform Provisions Concerning the Approval of Vehicles with Regard to the Installation of Lighting and Light-Signalling Devices).
- **FMVSS 108** (Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standard No. 108 – Lamps, Reflective Devices, and Associated Equipment) applicable in the US market.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology: H-V Reference Point**
> The **H-V reference point** is the orthogonal intersection of the **horizontal plane (H-plane)** passing through the focal center of the headlamp and the **vertical plane (V-plane)** passing through the same focal center, perpendicular to the H-plane. This is the *exact* point from which mounting height is officially measured for type-approval purposes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of headlight height regulation like the **standardized height of a lecturer's microphone in a classroom**:
- If the microphone is too **low**, the sound only reaches the front-row students (short-range visibility).
- If the microphone is too **high**, the sound disperses uselessly toward the ceiling (glare and wastage).
