# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EPA Light-Duty Certification Architecture

The following flowchart maps the **complete Tier 3 certification pathway** for a light-duty vehicle manufacturer, from initial data submission to final EPA Certificate of Conformity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Manufacturer Initiates Certification Process]
    node2[Select Representative Vehicle Class]
    node3[Perform FTP-75 Dynamometer Test]
    node4[Perform HWFET Highway Test]
    node5[Perform US06 Aggressive Driving Test]
    node6[Perform SC03 A/C Test]
    node7[Measure NMOG NOx PM CO Emissions]
    node8[Compute Fleet Average NMOG NOx]
    node9{Fleet Average within Tier 3 Limit 30 mg/mi?}
    node10[Generate Application Documents]
    node11[Submit to EPA Office of Transportation]
    node12{EPA Review Acceptable?}
    node13[Issue Certificate of Conformity]
    node14[Manufacturer Sells Vehicles in US]
    node15[In-Use Compliance Testing Random Audit]
    node16{Emissions Conform Throughout Useful Life?}
    node17[Recall and Remediation Campaign]
    node18[Levy Civil Penalties up to 44,557 per Vehicle]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node2 --> node4
    node2 --> node5
    node2 --> node6
    node3 --> node7
    node4 --> node7
    node5 --> node7
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9
    node9 -->|Yes| node10
    node9 -->|No| node1
    node10 --> node11
    node11 --> node12
    node12 -->|Approved| node13
    node12 -->|Rejected| node1
    node13 --> node14
    node14 --> node15
    node15 --> node16
    node16 -->|Yes| node14
    node16 -->|No| node17
    node17 --> node18
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**

The diagram has 18 distinct decision/action nodes. Key observations:

- **Parallel Test Execution (nodes 3, 4, 5, 6):** All four test cycles run on the same engine family but with different driving conditions; their results are combined (Bag 1+2+3 for FTP-75, separate HWFET, US06, SC03).
- **Iterative Refinement (node 9 → No → node1):** If the fleet average fails, the manufacturer can re-engineer the vehicle, add a clean model, or purchase ZEV credits — then restart certification.
- **Lifetime Accountability (node 15):** The EPA retains the right to recall a model for up to 15 years / 120,000 miles, even after production ends (e.g., the Volkswagen Dieselgate scandal of 2015 triggered recalls extending back to MY 2009).

## 4.2 EPA Heavy-Duty Engine Certification Block Architecture

For heavy-duty engines under 40 CFR Part 1036 and Part 1065, the certification architecture is fundamentally different from light-duty vehicles. HD engines are **engine-certified** (not vehicle-certified), and the dynamometer test cycles are **steady-state and transient** engine cycles.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Engine Production Start]
    node2[Select Engine Family based on displacement fuel and aftertreatment]
    node3[Install Engine on Dynamometer]
    node4[Run Cold Start WHTC Hot Start WHTC]
    node5[Run Steady State WHSC at 13 RPM Torque Modes]
    node6[Run RMC LSI Cycle Low Speed Idle]
    node7[Measure NOx PM NMOG CO THC]
    node8[Apply Deterioration Factors]
    node9{Emissions Meet Tier 4 Standards?}
    node10[File Application with EPA]
    node11[Certificate Issued]
    node12[Engine Installed in Vehicles]
    node13[Annual Production Line Testing]
    node14[In-Use Testing by EPA]
    node15[Useful Life 435000 mi or 10 Years]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node3 --> node5
    node3 --> node6
    node4 --> node7
    node5 --> node7
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9
    node9 -->|Yes| node10
    node9 -->|No| node2
    node10 --> node11
    node11 --> node12
    node12 --> node13
    node12 --> node14
    node13 --> node15
    node14 --> node15
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**

- **Engine Family (node 2):** EPA groups HD engines into "families" based on displacement, fuel type, combustion cycle, and aftertreatment. A single certificate covers thousands of engines.
- **Three Test Cycles (nodes 4, 5, 6):** The WHTC (World Harmonized Transient Cycle), WHSC (World Harmonized Steady-State Cycle), and the RMC/LSI (Reference Mode Cycle/Low Speed Idle).
- **Deterioration Factors (node 8):** Unlike light-duty testing, HD engine emissions are projected over the entire useful life using **Deterioration Factors (DF)**, which account for catalyst and DPF aging. The DF for NOx on a typical HD engine is ~1.1–1.3 (i.e., emissions at end of life are 10–30% higher than at start of life).

## 4.3 Emission Standard Evolution Timeline

This Gantt-style block diagram illustrates the **overlapping phase-in periods** of the four major EPA emission tiers, showing the regulatory transition and the **technology gap** between each tier.

```mermaid
gantt
    title EPA Emission Standard Phase-In Timeline
    dateFormat YYYY
    axisFormat %Y

    section Light Duty Tiers
    Tier 1 LDV Phase-In       :t1, 1994, 3y
    Tier 2 LDV Phase-In       :t2, after t1, 6y
    Tier 3 LDV Phase-In       :t3, after t2, 9y

    section Heavy Duty Tiers
    Tier 1 HD Phase-In        :th1, 1996, 1y
    Tier 2 HD Phase-In        :th2, 2004, 6y
    Tier 4 HD Phase-In        :th4, 2027, 6y

    section Fuel Sulfur
    Tier 2 Sulfur 30 ppm      :sf2, 2004, 2y
    Tier 3 Sulfur 10 ppm      :sf3, 2017, 1y
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**

- The Tier 3 LDV phase-in spans 9 years (2017–2025), which is unusually long, allowing manufacturers to manage product cycles and battery-electric vehicle rollouts.
- Tier 4 HD phase-in starts in 2027 (after a 2-year delay from the original 2025 target, due to industry pushback).
- The **30 → 10 ppm sulfur reduction** between 2004 and 2017 enabled the introduction of advanced NOx adsorber catalysts, which would otherwise be sulfur-poisoned.

## 4.4 EPA Penalty and Compliance Cascade

This block diagram shows the **enforcement cascade** — i.e., what happens when a manufacturer is found in non-compliance, using the **Volkswagen Dieselgate (2015–2017)** as a real-world template.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[EPA Identifies Non-Compliance via In-Use Test]
    node2[Issue Notice of Violation]
    node3[Manufacturer Submits Recall Plan]
    node4[EPA Evaluates Recall Adequacy]
    node5{Recall Approved?}
    node6[Manufacturer Performs Recall and Refund]
    node7[Manufacturer Files Appeal]
    node8[Federal Court Adjudication]
    node9[Consent Decree Negotiated]
    node10[Levy Civil Penalties per Violation]
    node11[Levy Criminal Penalties if Intentional]
    node12[Public Disclosure on EPA Website]
    node13[Stock Price Impact and Class Action Lawsuits]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 -->|Yes| node6
    node5 -->|No| node7
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9
    node6 --> node10
    node6 --> node12
    node9 --> node10
    node9 --> node11
    node12 --> node13
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**

- The Volkswagen case triggered **$30+ billion** in cumulative penalties, buybacks, and settlements — the largest automotive environmental penalty in history.
- A key procedural point: **Civil penalties** (Tier 1) are imposed even for unintentional violations; **Criminal penalties** (Tier 2) require evidence of willful intent, fraud, or concealment.
- The **"Notice of Violation"** (node 2) is the formal legal trigger; it is filed with the court of competent jurisdiction under 42 U.S.C. § 7524.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Tier 2 and Tier 3 emission standards for light-duty vehicles in the U.S., with specific reference to the emission limits and fuel sulfur content.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Tier 2 (MY 2004–2009) | Tier 3 (MY 2017–2025) |
|---|---|---|
| **Emission Metric** | NMOG + NOx (combined, Bin system) | NMOG + NOx (combined, fleet average) |
| **LDV/LDT1–2 Limit** | Bin 5 ≈ 0.07 g/mi (160 mg/mi NMOG+NOx) | 30 mg/mi NMOG+NOx (full useful life) |
| **PM Standard for Gasoline** | None | 3 mg/mi (new in Tier 3) |
| **Fuel Sulfur Cap** | 30 ppm | 10 ppm |
| **Useful Life** | 120,000 mi / 15 yr | 120,000 mi / 15 yr |
| **Harmonization** | Federal only | Harmonized with CARB LEV III |

**Key Distinction:** Tier 3 introduced a **PM standard for gasoline vehicles** (previously only required for diesels) and **reduced the gasoline sulfur cap to 10 ppm**, enabling the use of advanced NOx aftertreatment on both gasoline and diesel platforms. Tier 3's **30 mg/mi fleet average** represents a **~80% reduction** in NMOG+NOx from Tier 2 Bin 5.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain the role of the Corporate Average Fuel Economy (CAFE) program and state the formula for calculating the fleet average fuel economy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **CAFE program**, established by the **Energy Policy and Conservation Act (EPCA) of 1975**, sets mandatory fuel economy standards for passenger cars and light trucks sold in the United States. It is jointly administered by the **NHTSA (DOT)** and the **EPA**. The program's purpose is to **reduce national oil consumption** in response to the 1973 oil crisis and to **lower greenhouse gas emissions** from the transportation sector.

**Fleet Average CAFE Formula:**

$$
\text{CAFE}_{\text{fleet}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{V_i}{FE_i}}
$$

Where $V_i$ is the production volume of model $i$, $FE_i$ is the fuel economy (in mpg) of model $i$, and $N$ is the number of distinct models. The **harmonic mean** is used (rather than arithmetic mean) to ensure that low-fuel-economy models (e.g., heavy trucks) are penalized more heavily in the fleet average.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **Tier 3 emission standards** promulgated by the U.S. EPA for light-duty vehicles. Include in your answer:
- The phase-in period
- The fleet-average NMOG + NOx standard for LDV/LDT1–2
- The new PM standard for gasoline vehicles
- The gasoline sulfur cap
- The cold-start CO standard
- The useful life requirement

**(b) [7 marks]** A manufacturer produces 200,000 light-duty vehicles in MY 2024 across two model families:
- **Family X:** 80,000 units at CAFE = 35 mpg
- **Family Y:** 120,000 units at CAFE = 28 mpg
- The CAFE standard for MY 2024 is **31 mpg**. The penalty rate is the original $5.00/0.1 mpg per vehicle.
- **(i)** Compute the combined fleet average CAFE.
- **(ii)** Determine if the fleet is in compliance.
- **(iii)** If non-compliant, calculate the total penalty owed.

#### Model Solution — Question A, Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Phase-in period [1 mark]:**
Tier 3 was promulgated in **March 2014** and phased in from **MY 2017 to MY 2025**, with full compliance required for all light-duty vehicles and light-duty trucks by MY 2025.

**Step 2 — Fleet-average NMOG + NOx [1 mark]:**
For **LDV and LDT1–2 (GVWR ≤ 6000 lb)**, the fleet-average NMOG + NOx target is **30 mg/mi** by MY 2025, measured over the FTP-75 + SFTP (US06 + SC03) cycle.

**Step 3 — PM standard for gasoline [1 mark]:**
Tier 3 introduced a **PM standard of 3 mg/mi** for gasoline-powered light-duty vehicles, previously only required for diesel. This addressed non-exhaust PM and direct PM emissions from port fuel injection and gasoline direct injection engines.

**Step 4 — Gasoline sulfur cap [1 mark]:**
The sulfur content of gasoline was capped at **10 ppm** (down from Tier 2's 30 ppm), necessary to enable advanced aftertreatment systems.

**Step 5 — Cold-start CO [1 mark]:**
The cold-start CO standard for LDV was tightened to **1.0 g/mi** (Bag 1 of FTP-75, equivalent to the first 505 seconds of the cold-start transient phase).

**Step 6 — Useful life [1 mark]:**
The Tier 3 useful life is **120,000 miles (192,000 km) or 15 years**, whichever occurs first. An "intermediate useful life" of 50,000 mi is also used for NMOG+NOx standards during the phase-in.

**Step 7 — Summary and impact [1 mark]:**
Tier 3 reduced NMOG+NOx by ~80% from Tier 2 levels, introduced PM standards for gasoline, and harmonized the federal program with California LEV III, eliminating the dual-regulatory regime. Technology-forcing innovations included **lean NOx traps, SCR on light-duty diesels, and high-efficiency GPFs (Gasoline Particulate Filters)**.

#### Model Solution — Question A, Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute combined fleet CAFE [2 marks]:**

$$
\text{CAFE} = \frac{V_X + V_Y}{\frac{V_X}{FE_X} + \frac{V_Y}{FE_Y}}
$$

$$
\text{CAFE} = \frac{80{,}000 + 120{,}000}{\frac{80{,}000}{35} + \frac{120{,}000}{28}}
$$

Evaluate the denominator:

$$
\frac{80{,}000}{35} = 2285.71
$$

$$
\frac{120{,}000}{28} = 4285.71
$$

Sum of denominators:

$$
2285.71 + 4285.71 = 6571.42
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{CAFE} = \frac{200{,}000}{6571.42} = 30.43 \text{ mpg}
$$

> **Step 1 result:** $\text{CAFE} = 30.43 \text{ mpg}$.

**Step 2 — Compliance check [1 mark]:**

$$
\text{CAFE} = 30.43 \text{ mpg} \quad \text{vs} \quad S = 31 \text{ mpg}
$$

Since $30.43 < 31$, the fleet is **NOT in compliance** by 0.57 mpg.

> **Step 2 result:** The fleet falls short of the standard by 0.57 mpg.

**Step 3 — Compute penalty [4 marks]:**

Penalty rate: $5.00 per 0.1 mpg shortfall per vehicle.

Deficit in mpg:

$$
\Delta = 31 - 30.43 = 0.57 \text{ mpg}
$$

Number of 0.1-mpg increments:

$$
n = \frac{0.57}{0.1} = 5.7
$$

Penalty per vehicle:

$$
P_{\text{veh}} = \$5.00 \times 5.7 = \$28.50
$$

Total fleet penalty:

$$
P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{veh}} \times 200{,}000 = \$28.50 \times 200{,}000 = \$5{,}700{,}000
$$

> **Step 3 result:** Total penalty = **\$5,700,000**.

**Alternative (preferred) formulation using the statutory formula:**

$$
P = \$50 \times \left(\frac{1}{S} - \frac{1}{\text{CAFE}}\right) \times \sum V_i
$$

$$
\frac{1}{31} = 0.032258 \text{ gal/mi}, \quad \frac{1}{30.43} = 0.032862 \text{ gal/mi}
$$

$$
\Delta g = 0.032862 - 0.032258 = 0.000604 \text{ gal/mi}
$$

$$
P = \$50 \times 0.000604 \times 200{,}000 = \$6{,}040
$$

> **Note:** The two methods give slightly different answers due to rounding and the precise regulatory treatment of 0.1-mpg increments. The examiner's preferred method is the statutory one.

---

### Question B (14 marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **EPA heavy-duty engine certification procedure** for Tier 4 emission standards. In your answer, include:
- The relevant CFR citations
- The test cycles used (WHTC, WHSC, RMC)
- The emission limits (NOx, PM, NMOG, CO)
- The useful life for Class 8 HD engines
- The role of deterioration factors (DF)

**(b) [7 marks]** An engine dynamometer test on a heavy-duty diesel engine yields the following data over a 1-hour steady-state test:
- Engine speed: 1800 rpm
- Brake torque: 850 N·m
- NOx mass flow rate: 4.2 g/min
- CO mass flow rate: 1.05 g/min
- PM mass flow rate: 0.08 g/min
- **Compute:**
  - **(i)** The engine brake power in kW.
  - **(ii)** The brake-specific emissions (BSNOx, BSCO, BSPM) in g/kWh.
  - **(iii)** Apply a deterioration factor of 1.15 for NOx and 1.05 for PM. Compute the "end-of-life" BSNOx and BSPM.
  - **(iv)** State whether the engine meets Tier 4 limits.

#### Model Solution — Question B, Part (a) (7 marks)

**CFR citations [1 mark]:**
- **40 CFR Part 1036** — Heavy-Duty Engine Emission Standards.
- **40 CFR Part 1065** — Engine-Testing Procedures (engine dynamometer).
- **40 CFR Part 1037** — Heavy-Duty Vehicle Emission Standards (for complete vehicles, e.g., vocational vehicles).

**Test cycles [2 marks]:**
1. **WHTC (World Harmonized Transient Cycle):** Cold-start + hot-start transient, 1800 s total, simulates real-world driving.
2. **WHSC (World Harmonized Steady-State Cycle):** 13-mode steady-state test, used for certification and production-line testing.
3. **RMC (Reference Mode Cycle) + LSI (Low Speed Idle):** Low-load and idle modes for specific pollutant measurements.

**Emission limits (Tier 4, MY 2027–2032) [2 marks]:**
- **NOx: 0.035 g/bhp-hr** (~0.026 g/kWh)
- **PM: 0.005 g/bhp-hr** (~0.0037 g/kWh)
- **NMOG: 0.14 g/bhp-hr** (gasoline)
- **CO: 15.5 g/bhp-hr**

**Useful life [1 mark]:**
- Class 8 heavy heavy-duty engines (GVWR > 33,000 lb): **435,000 mi (700,000 km) or 10 years**.
- Class 6–7 medium heavy-duty engines: **270,000 mi (435,000 km) or 10 years**.

**Deterioration factors [1 mark]:**
Manufacturers must apply DFs to project emissions over the useful life. DF for NOx is typically **1.1–1.3**, for PM is **1.1–1.4**, and for CO is **1.1–1.2**. The DF is determined from an **aging test** (typically 1250–1500 hours of engine operation simulating full useful life).

#### Model Solution — Question B, Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute engine brake power [1 mark]:**

$$
P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60}
$$

Where $N$ = 1800 rpm and $T$ = 850 N·m:

$$
P = \frac{2 \pi \times 1800 \times 850}{60} = \frac{9{,}611{,}038}{60} = 160{,}184 \text{ W} = 160.18 \text{ kW}
$$

> **Step 1 result:** $P = 160.18 \text{ kW}$.

**Step 2 — Compute brake-specific emissions [2 marks]:**

Convert mass flow rates to g/s:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{NOx}} = 4.2/60 = 0.070 \text{ g/s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{CO}} = 1.05/60 = 0.0175 \text{ g/s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{PM}} = 0.08/60 = 0.00133 \text{ g/s}
$$

Compute BS emissions:

$$
\text{BSNOx} = \frac{0.070}{160.18} \times 3600 = 1.573 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

$$
\text{BSCO} = \frac{0.0175}{160.18} \times 3600 = 0.393 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

$$
\text{BSPM} = \frac{0.00133}{160.18} \times 3600 = 0.030 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

> **Step 2 result:** $\text{BSNOx} = 1.57 \text{ g/kWh}$, $\text{BSCO} = 0.39 \text{ g/kWh}$, $\text{BSPM} = 0.030 \text{ g/kWh}$.

**Step 3 — Apply deterioration factors [2 marks]:**

$$
\text{BSNOx}_{\text{EOL}} = 1.573 \times 1.15 = 1.809 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

$$
\text{BSPM}_{\text{EOL}} = 0.030 \times 1.05 = 0.0315 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

> **Step 3 result:** End-of-life $\text{BSNOx} = 1.81 \text{ g/kWh}$, $\text{BSPM} = 0.0315 \text{ g/kWh}$.

**Step 4 — Tier 4 compliance check [2 marks]:**

Convert Tier 4 limits to g/kWh:

$$
\text{Tier 4 NOx limit} = 0.035 \text{ g/bhp-hr} \times 1.34102 \text{ bhp/kW} = 0.0262 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

$$
\text{Tier 4 PM limit} = 0.005 \text{ g/bhp-hr} \times 1.34102 = 0.00373 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

Compare:

$$
1.81 \text{ g/kWh} \gg 0.0262 \text{ g/kWh} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NOx fails Tier 4 by } \sim 69\times
$$

$$
0.0315 \text{ g/kWh} \gg 0.00373 \text{ g/kWh} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{PM fails Tier 4 by } \sim 8.4\times
$$

> **Step 4 result:** The engine **DOES NOT MEET** Tier 4 standards for NOx or PM.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion (5-mark penalty zone):** The most common error is mixing **g/mi and g/km** or **g/bhp-hr and g/kWh**. Always verify the unit in the question stem and convert explicitly.
> 2. **CAFE vs GHG (3-mark penalty zone):** CAFE measures **fuel economy (mpg)**; GHG measures **CO₂ emissions (g/mi)**. They are related (lower fuel economy = higher CO₂) but are administered under different statutory authorities (EPCA vs CAA).
> 3. **Deterioration Factor Application (2-mark penalty zone):** For HD engines, BS emissions **must be multiplied by DF** before comparison to standards. Many students forget this and lose 2 marks per question.
> 4. **Tier 4 is Heavy-Duty only (2-mark penalty zone):** Students often confuse Tier 3 (LDV) with Tier 4 (HD). Tier 4 has **no light-duty equivalent** as of 2024.
> 5. **Penalty Calculation (3-mark penalty zone):** The $5.00/0.1 mpg is the **base EPCA rate**; in actual enforcement, NHTSA uses an **inflation-adjusted rate** that is several times higher. Always clarify which rate the question expects.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Quick-Reference Summary Checklist:**

- 📌 **EPA** was established in 1970 under the Nixon administration; statutory authority is the **Clean Air Act** (1970, amended 1990).
- 📌 **Tier 1** (1994) → **Tier 2** (2004) → **Tier 3** (2017, light-duty) → **Tier 4** (2027, heavy-duty).
- 📌 **Tier 3 LDV fleet-average NMOG+NOx = 30 mg/mi** by 2025; **Tier 4 HD NOx = 0.035 g/bhp-hr** by 2027.
- 📌 **Tier 3** introduced **PM standards for gasoline vehicles (3 mg/mi)** and a **gasoline sulfur cap of 10 ppm**.
- 📌 **CAFE** is administered by **NHTSA (DOT)** with EPA technical support; the fleet fuel economy is a **harmonic mean** to penalize low-mpg vehicles.
- 📌 **CAFE Penalty:** $P = \$5.00 \times 10 \times \left(\frac{1}{S} - \frac{1}{\text{CAFE}}\right) \times \sum V_i$.
- 📌 **GHG Standards** rely on the **2009 Endangerment Finding** (post-*Massachusetts v. EPA*).
- 📌 **Useful life:** LDV/LDT = 120,000 mi / 15 yr; Class 8 HD = 435,000 mi / 10 yr.
- 📌 **Test cycles for LDV:** FTP-75, HWFET, US06, SC03.
- 📌 **Test cycles for HD:** WHTC, WHSC, RMC.
- 📌 **ZEV credits** are primarily a CARB program (adopted by Section 177 states); EPA Tier 3 incorporates credit-based averaging.
- 📌 **CFR citations:** 40 CFR Part 86 + 1066 (LDV/LDT); Part 1036 + 1065 (HD engines); Part 1037 (HD vehicles).
- 📌 **Dieselgate precedent:** A single Notice of Violation can trigger **billions of dollars** in penalties, recalls, and litigation.
- 📌 **Conversion constants to memorize:** $1 \text{ mi} = 1.60934 \text{ km}$; $1 \text{ kW} = 1.34102 \text{ bhp}$; $1 \text{ g/kWh} = 0.7457 \text{ g/bhp-hr}$.
- 📌 **Civil penalty inflation-adjusted maximum:** $\approx \$44{,}557$ per vehicle per violation (2023).
- 📌 **Endangerment Finding (2009)** — six GHGs covered: CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, HFCs, PFCs, SF₆.

> **Final Note for KTU Candidates:** EPA standards questions are most often tested through **numerical problems on CAFE, BSNOx, and emission-mass calculations**, supplemented by **2–3 mark short-answer questions on Tier 3 / Tier 4 evolution and statutory authority**. Master the **harmonic mean CAFE formula** and the **BS emission calculation with DF** — they are the most likely 14-mark question format.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: California Air Resources Board (CARB) standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** California Air Resources Board (CARB) standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **California Air Resources Board (CARB)** is the "clean air agency" of the State of California, United States, established under the **California Health and Safety Code, Section 39500** in 1967. It is a regulatory body within the California Environmental Protection Agency (CalEPA) responsible for protecting public health from air pollution through setting emission standards for motor vehicles, fuels, and consumer products.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** CARB is significant in global automotive engineering because California is the only U.S. state permitted under Section 209 of the federal **Clean Air Act (CAA)** to set its own vehicle emission standards stricter than the federal EPA norms. Due to the size of the U.S. automotive market, manufacturers typically build "California-compliant" or "50-state" vehicles, effectively making CARB standards **de facto** national and international benchmarks.

The formal standards framework issued by CARB is encapsulated in the **California Code of Regulations (CCR), Title 13, Division 3, Chapter 1** (commonly called "Title 13" or "13 CCR"). The core emission categories monitored are:

- **NMOG (Non-Methane Organic Gases)** — measured in grams per mile (g/mi)
- **NOx (Oxides of Nitrogen)** — measured in g/mi
- **CO (Carbon Monoxide)** — measured in g/mi
- **PM (Particulate Matter)** — measured in mg/mi
- **Formaldehyde (HCHO)** — measured in mg/mi
- **GHG (Greenhouse Gases)** — measured in grams of CO₂-equivalent per mile (g CO₂e/mi)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a school principal who decides that **only students who score above 90%** can be called "Honor Roll." Other schools may have lower passing marks (60%), but because this principal's school is the most prestigious, the entire country starts measuring students by the "90% rule" — not because of law, but because everyone wants to be associated with the top standard.

**CARB is that principal.** California set its air-quality bar so high that the entire U.S. (and later countries like China, India, and the EU) adopted similar or derived rules. A car is said to be **"CARB-compliant"** if it meets the strictest emission rules in the world for its class.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Key Point:** When an automotive engineer designs an engine for global sale, they design to the **toughest** standard — which is almost always CARB. Designing to a lower standard (e.g., Euro 4) is therefore uneconomical. This is why **"50-state legal"** in the U.S. automotive world *means* CARB-compliant.

## 1.3 Historical Foundation

| Year | Milestone |
|------|-----------|
| **1967** | CARB officially established |
| **1970** | First tailpipe emission standards adopted (HC, CO) |
| **1975** | Catalytic converter mandate (driven by CARB) |
| **1990** | **LEV (Low Emission Vehicle) Program** initiated |
| **1998** | **ZEV (Zero Emission Vehicle) Mandate** introduced |
| **2004** | PZEV (Partial ZEV) category formalized |
| **2012** | **LEV III / GHG Phase 1** announced |
| **2017** | **Tier 3** criteria pollutant standards harmonized with EPA |
| **2026+** | **LEV IV / Advanced Clean Cars II (ACC II)** — 100% ZEV sales target |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Point:** The 1970 amendment to the U.S. Clean Air Act gave California the **waiver authority**. This is the constitutional/legal mechanism that makes CARB legally distinct from federal EPA. Without Section 209, CARB standards would not be enforceable.

## 1.4 CARB Emission Categories — The Vehicle Classification Matrix

CARB classifies every light-duty vehicle (LDV) into one of the following certification categories based on its tailpipe emissions over the **FTP-75 (Federal Test Procedure)** and **SFTP (Supplemental Federal Test Procedure)** drive cycles:

| Category | Abbreviation | NMOG + NOx (g/mi) | Real-World Equivalent |
|----------|--------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Zero Emission Vehicle | **ZEV** | **0.000** | Battery Electric, Fuel Cell |
| Enhanced Advanced Technology PZEV | **Enhanced AT PZEV** | ≤ 0.025 | Plug-in hybrids with SULEV tailpipe |
| Super Ultra Low Emission Vehicle | **SULEV** | ≤ 0.030 | Hybrids with advanced after-treatment |
| Ultra Low Emission Vehicle | **ULEV** | ≤ 0.070 | Modern gasoline direct injection |
| Low Emission Vehicle | **LEV** | ≤ 0.090 | Standard modern ICE |
| Transitional Low Emission Vehicle | **TLEV** | ≤ 0.125 | Older standards (legacy) |
| Ultra Low Emission 150 | **ULEV 150** | ≤ 0.150 | 150,000-mile durability class |
| Low Emission 150 | **LEV 150** | ≤ 0.180 | 150,000-mile durability class |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — NMOG + NOx:** The combined sum of Non-Methane Organic Gases and Oxides of Nitrogen. This is the **primary smog-formation metric** because hydrocarbons and NOx react under sunlight to form ground-level ozone (smog). CARB was the first regulator to combine these into a single metric, anticipating photochemical reaction physics.

## 1.5 The Three Pillars of CARB Regulation

1. **Criteria Pollutant Standards** (LEV I, II, III) — limits on NOx, NMOG, CO, PM
2. **Greenhouse Gas Standards** — CO₂, CH₄, N₂O (LEV III / Phase 1, 2)
3. **Zero Emission Vehicle (ZEV) Mandate** — requires automakers to sell a minimum % of ZEVs, redeemable through **ZEV credits**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The LEV I → LEV II → LEV III → LEV IV Evolution

### 2.1.1 LEV I (1994–2003) — The Foundation

LEV I introduced the **fleet-average NMOG concept**. Instead of every individual car needing to meet a single number, manufacturers were required to make the *average* of their entire sold fleet meet a target. This created flexibility while maintaining aggregate environmental benefit.

**Operational Logic:**
- Manufacturers' entire California fleet must average ≤ **0.25 g/mi NMOG** (sales-weighted)
- Sub-categories (TLEV, LEV, ULEV) provided credit/debit pathway
- Cars cleaner than LEV earned **emission credits**; dirtier ones debited

### 2.1.2 LEV II (2004–2015) — Tightening the Noose

LEV II reduced fleet-average NMOG to **0.035 g/mi** by 2010, and introduced:
- **SULEV-30** (NMOG + NOx ≤ 0.030 g/mi — gasoline parity with diesel particulate filters)
- **PZEV (Partial Zero Emission Vehicle)** — SULEV tailpipe + zero evaporative emissions + 150,000-mile warranty
- Formaldehyde limits of **≤ 5 mg/mi**

### 2.1.3 LEV III (2015–2025) — The Modern Era

LEV III, harmonized with the **EPA Tier 3** standard, set:
- **NMOG + NOx fleet average**: 30 mg/mi by 2025
- **PM (Particulate Matter)**: 3 mg/mi by 2025
- **CO**: 1.7 g/mi (LDV); 2.6 g/mi (LDT)
- **Formaldehyde (HCHO)**: 4 mg/mi
- **GHG Fleet Average**: 177 g CO₂e/mi by 2025

### 2.1.4 LEV IV / ACC II (2026–2035) — The Electrification Mandate

Under **Advanced Clean Cars II (ACC II)**, adopted August 25, 2022:
- **100% ZEV and PHEV sales** in California by **model year 2035**
- Year-over-year ZEV percentage ramp:
  - 2026: **35%**
  - 2027: **43%**
  - 2028: **51%**
  - 2029: **59%**
  - 2030: **68%**
  - 2031: **76%**
  - 2032: **84%**
  - 2033: **92%**
  - 2034: **100%**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** LEV IV is no longer just a "tailpipe" standard — it is a **technology-forcing regulation** that mandates *which powertrain type* can be sold. This represents a paradigm shift from emission-based to technology-based regulation.

## 2.2 ZEV Mandate Mechanics — The Credit System

The **ZEV credit system** is CARB's most innovative regulatory tool. Manufacturers earn credits by selling ZEVs and near-ZEVs, which they can use or trade.

### 2.2.1 ZEV Credit Earning Formula

$$\boxed{C_{ZEV} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (V_i \times f_i \times D_i \times A_i)}$$

Where:
- $C_{ZEV}$ = Total ZEV credits earned (in credit units)
- $V_i$ = Volume (units) of vehicle category $i$ sold
- $f_i$ = Vehicle category credit factor (ZEV = 1.0 minimum; advanced tech varies)
- $D_i$ = **Driving Range Factor** = $\dfrac{\text{Range (miles)}}{100}$ for BEVs/FCEVs
- $A_i$ = **Off-Cycle and A/C credit adjustment** (0 to 1.0+)

### 2.2.2 ZEV Credit Category Multipliers

| Vehicle Type | ZEV Credit Multiplier |
|--------------|----------------------|
| BEV / FCEV with ≥ 200 mi range | **1.0 + 0.01 per mile above 200** |
| PHEV (with ≥ 10 mi AER) | 0.2 to 1.0 (varies) |
| SULEV + PZEV (Conventional HEV) | 0.2 |
| Neighborhood Electric Vehicle (NEV) | 0.3 |

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Worked Credit Calculation:** Tesla Model 3 sold 10,000 units with 300-mile range:
> $C = 10{,}000 \times 1.0 \times (300/100) \times 1.0 = 10{,}000 \times 3.0 = 30{,}000$ credits
> Each credit is **worth $5,000** in CARB enforcement fines. Thus, $30{,}000 \times \$5{,}000 = \$150$ million in credit value.

### 2.2.3 Manufacturer ZEV Obligation

Each manufacturer must achieve a minimum ZEV credit percentage of their total California sales:

$$Z_{min}(y) = Z_{target}(y) \times 1.0$$

The **ZEV credit bank** allows credits to be:
- Banked forward up to **5 years**
- Traded between manufacturers
- Sold to other manufacturers at market rate

## 2.3 The Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Fleet Average Calculation

For LEV III GHG compliance, the fleet-average CO₂-equivalent emission is:

$$\overline{GHG} = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{m}(S_j \times V_j \times GHG_j \times CF_j)}{\sum_{j=1}^{m}(S_j \times V_j)}$$

Where:
- $S_j$ = Sales of model $j$ in California
- $V_j$ = Vehicle weight category adjustment
- $GHG_j$ = Model $j$ test-cycle CO₂e value (g/mi)
- $CF_j$ = Compliance flexibility factor (0.85 to 1.0, expires over time)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---------|-----------|---------------|
| **Fleet-Average NMOG + NOx** | $\overline{E} = \dfrac{\sum(V_i \cdot E_i)}{\sum V_i}$ | g/mi; sales-weighted |
| **ZEV Credits** | $C = V \times f \times \dfrac{R}{100} \times A$ | Credits; range $R$ in miles |
| **Penalty per Credit Deficit** | $P = 5{,}000 \times (C_{req} - C_{earned})$ | USD ($) |
| **NMOG + NOx Combined Limit (LEV III)** | $E_{max} = 30$ mg/mi (2025) | mg/mi (note: $1$ g = $1000$ mg) |
| **GHG Fleet Avg (LEV III 2025)** | $\overline{GHG} \le 177$ | g CO₂e/mi |
| **PM Limit (LEV III 2025)** | $PM \le 3$ | mg/mi |
| **HCHO Limit (LEV III)** | $HCHO \le 4$ | mg/mi |
| **CO Limit (LDV)** | $CO \le 1.7$ | g/mi |
| **NO₂ Fraction of NOx** | $f_{NO_2} \le 0.20$ | Dimensionless (for HD diesel) |
| **LEV III Test Fuel Sulfur** | $S \le 10$ | ppm (down from 80 ppm) |
| **Deterioration Factor (DF)** | $E_{compliance} = E_{test} \times DF$ | DF > 1.0 |
| **Useful Life (LEV III)** | $L = 150{,}000$ miles | Was 120,000 in LEV II |
| **Mid-Useful Life Standard** | $E_{mid} = 0.5 \times (E_{50k} + E_{150k})$ | 50k/150k mile interpolation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Constants:**
> - **Useful life**: 150,000 miles (LEV III)
> - **Emission warranty**: 150,000 miles for PZEV
> - **Penalty per ZEV credit shortfall**: $5,000
> - **Fleet average NMOG + NOx (2025)**: 30 mg/mi
> - **ACC II ZEV sales 2035**: 100%

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The CARB framework drives engineering decisions across:
1. **Engine design** — GDI with GPF (Gasoline Particulate Filter), high EGR tolerance, low-SAPS lubricants
2. **After-treatment** — Three-way catalyst (TWC) optimization, SCR for NOx, AMOx for NH₃ slip
3. **EV powertrain development** — Battery sizing for ZEV credit multiplier, range optimization
4. **Hybrid system calibration** — SULEV PHEV strategy development
5. **Fuel formulation** — California Phase 3 reformulated gasoline (CaRFG3) — 10 ppm sulfur, low RVP

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1: Fleet-Average NMOG + NOx Compliance

**Problem Statement:**
An OEM sells three models in California for model year 2024:
- Model A (Gasoline): 50,000 units @ 0.060 g/mi NMOG+NOx
- Model B (Hybrid, SULEV): 30,000 units @ 0.025 g/mi NMOG+NOx
- Model C (Diesel): 20,000 units @ 0.090 g/mi NMOG+NOx

Determine if the fleet is LEV III compliant (target = 0.030 g/mi for 2024).

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Define the fleet-average emission formula.

$$\overline{E} = \frac{\sum(V_i \cdot E_i)}{\sum V_i}$$

This is the **sales-weighted arithmetic mean** of NMOG+NOx emissions. The reason for sales-weighting (rather than simple averaging) is that high-volume sellers dominate the actual emissions impact on California's airshed.

**Step 2:** Compute the total volume of sales.

$$\sum V_i = 50{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 20{,}000 = 100{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the volume-weighted emission numerator.

$$\sum(V_i \cdot E_i) = (50{,}000)(0.060) + (30{,}000)(0.025) + (20{,}000)(0.090)$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate each term explicitly.

- Model A: $50{,}000 \times 0.060 = 3{,}000$ g/mi·units
- Model B: $30{,}000 \times 0.025 = 750$ g/mi·units
- Model C: $20{,}000 \times 0.090 = 1{,}800$ g/mi·units

**Step 5:** Sum the numerator.

$$\sum(V_i \cdot E_i) = 3{,}000 + 750 + 1{,}800 = 5{,}550 \text{ g/mi·units}$$

**Step 6:** Compute fleet average.

$$\overline{E} = \frac{5{,}550}{100{,}000} = 0.0555 \text{ g/mi}$$

**Step 7:** Convert to mg/mi (standard CARB unit).

$$\overline{E} = 0.0555 \text{ g/mi} \times 1{,}000 = 55.5 \text{ mg/mi}$$

**Step 8:** Compare to 2024 target.

$$\text{LE III 2024 target} = 0.030 \text{ g/mi} = 30 \text{ mg/mi}$$

**Step 9:** Conclusion.

$$\overline{E} = 55.5 \text{ mg/mi} \gt 30 \text{ mg/mi} \quad \therefore \textbf{NOT COMPLIANT}$$

The manufacturer must either (a) sell more ZEVs/ULEVs to dilute the average, (b) upgrade Model C diesel to SCR, or (c) purchase emission credits from another manufacturer.

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2: ZEV Credit Deficit Penalty

**Problem Statement:**
Manufacturer X sold 200,000 light-duty vehicles in California in 2026. Their ACC II obligation is 35% ZEV (i.e., 70,000 ZEV-equivalent vehicles). They only sold 25,000 BEVs and 8,000 PHEVs.

- BEV credit factor: 1.0
- BEV range: 280 miles → $D = 280/100 = 2.8$
- PHEV credit factor: 0.5
- PHEV range: 40 miles → $D = 40/100 = 0.4$
- Off-cycle adjustment factor $A = 1.0$ for both

Calculate credits earned, credits required, and penalty.

**Step 1:** Compute credits earned from BEVs.

$$C_{BEV} = 25{,}000 \times 1.0 \times 2.8 \times 1.0 = 70{,}000 \text{ credits}$$

**Step 2:** Compute credits earned from PHEVs.

$$C_{PHEV} = 8{,}000 \times 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 1.0 = 1{,}600 \text{ credits}$$

**Step 3:** Total credits earned.

$$C_{earned} = 70{,}000 + 1{,}600 = 71{,}600 \text{ credits}$$

**Step 4:** Credits required (35% of 200,000 = 70,000 ZEV units × 1.0 = 70,000 credits).

$$C_{required} = 70{,}000 \text{ credits}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate credit surplus/deficit.

$$\Delta C = 71{,}600 - 70{,}000 = +1{,}600 \text{ credits (surplus)}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to monetary value (or bankable credits).

$$\text{Value} = 1{,}600 \times \$5{,}000 = \$8{,}000{,}000$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even though the manufacturer fell short on the *count* of ZEVs (33,000 vs 70,000), the **range-weighted credit system** allows the BEVs' superior range to compensate. This is the genius of the ZEV credit system — it rewards long-range ZEVs that displace more ICE miles.

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3: Multi-Year Credit Banking

**Problem Statement:**
A manufacturer earned 5,000 ZEV credits in 2023 and used 3,000 credits in 2024. They earned 4,000 more in 2024. They need 7,000 credits in 2025.

**Step 1:** Track bank forward.

$$\text{Credits banked from 2023} = 5{,}000 - 3{,}000 = 2{,}000 \text{ (remaining)}$$

**Step 2:** Add 2024 earnings to bank.

$$\text{Bank at end of 2024} = 2{,}000 + 4{,}000 = 6{,}000 \text{ credits}$$

**Step 3:** Apply to 2025 obligation.

$$\text{2025 deficit} = 7{,}000 - 6{,}000 = 1{,}000 \text{ credit shortfall}$$

**Step 4:** Penalty exposure.

$$P = 1{,}000 \times \$5{,}000 = \$5{,}000{,}000$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Credits older than **5 years expire** under current CARB rules. A credit earned in 2020 must be used by 2025, else it is destroyed. Students often forget this in multi-year compliance scenarios.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Standard Comparison

| Parameter | EPA Tier 3 (2025) | CARB LEV III (2025) | Euro 6d (2025) | India BS-VI (2025) |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **NMOG + NOx (g/mi)** | 0.030 | 0.030 | 0.060 g/km | 0.068 g/km |
| **PM (mg/mi)** | 3.0 | 3.0 | 4.5 mg/km | 4.5 mg/km |
| **CO (g/mi)** | 1.7 | 1.7 | 1.0 g/km | 1.0 g/km |
| **GHG Fleet Avg** | 205 g CO₂e/mi | 177 g CO₂e/mi | 95 g CO₂/km | Not regulated |
| **ZEV Mandate** | None | 100% by 2035 | Phase-out 2035 (EU) | None (yet) |
| **Test Cycle** | FTP-75 | FTP-75 + SFTP | WLTP | WLTP-derived |
| **Useful Life (mi)** | 150,000 | 150,000 | 100,000 (5 yr) | 100,000 (5 yr) |
| **Penalty per Credit** | None (fine-based) | $5,000 | Varies by country | Varies |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
**Define CARB. What is the legal basis for California's authority to set vehicle emission standards stricter than the EPA?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The California Air Resources Board (CARB) is the clean air regulatory agency of California, established in 1967 under the California Health and Safety Code, Section 39500. The legal basis is **Section 209 of the U.S. Clean Air Act (CAA)**, which grants California a **waiver** to set its own vehicle emission standards, provided EPA approves the waiver. Other U.S. states may voluntarily adopt California standards under Section 177. (3 marks)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**List the four vehicle certification categories under CARB LEV III standards in order of decreasing cleanliness.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
From cleanest to dirtiest: **(1) ZEV (Zero Emission Vehicle) — 0 g/mi NMOG+NOx; (2) SULEV (Super Ultra Low Emission Vehicle) — ≤ 0.030 g/mi; (3) ULEV (Ultra Low Emission Vehicle) — ≤ 0.070 g/mi; (4) LEV (Low Emission Vehicle) — ≤ 0.090 g/mi.** Legacy TLEV (Transitional Low Emission Vehicle) ≤ 0.125 g/mi also exists. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**

**(a) Explain the evolution of CARB's Low Emission Vehicle (LEV) program from LEV I to LEV IV (ACC II). Highlight the key emission limits and the technological shift introduced at each stage. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**LEV I (1994–2003):** Introduced the **fleet-average NMOG concept**. Required manufacturers' California sales fleet to average ≤ 0.25 g/mi NMOG. Sub-categories: TLEV, LEV, ULEV. Engineered technologies: improved TWC, EGR, electronic fuel injection. (1.5 marks)

**LEV II (2004–2015):** Fleet-average NMOG target tightened to 0.035 g/mi. Introduced **SULEV (≤ 0.030 g/mi)** and **PZEV** with zero evaporative emissions. Formaldehyde limit 5 mg/mi. Technologies: advanced TWC with higher Pd/Rh loading, electronically controlled EGR, low-permeation fuel hoses, closed crankcase ventilation. (1.5 marks)

**LEV III (2015–2025):** Fleet-average NMOG+NOx target **30 mg/mi by 2025**. PM limit **3 mg/mi**, HCHO **4 mg/mi**, CO **1.7 g/mi**. GHG fleet average **177 g CO₂e/mi by 2025**. Useful life extended to **150,000 miles**. Harmonized with EPA Tier 3. Technologies: **Gasoline Particulate Filter (GPF)** for GDI engines, high-pressure EGR, advanced SCR for light-duty diesel, improved turbocharging, 48V mild-hybrid systems. (2 marks)

**LEV IV / Advanced Clean Cars II (2026–2035):** Paradigm shift from **emission-based** to **technology-based** regulation. ZEV sales mandate ramps from **35% in 2026** to **100% in 2035** (model year 2034 onward). Engineered technologies: full battery-electric platforms, fuel cell vehicles (FCEV), long-range PHEVs. Phase-out of pure-ICE sales. (2 marks)

**(b) With a suitable example, explain CARB's ZEV credit system, including the formula for credit calculation, the banking rules, and the penalty for non-compliance. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Conceptual Overview (1 mark):** The ZEV credit system is a market-based mechanism that obligates manufacturers to sell a minimum percentage of zero-emission vehicles in California. Credits are earned per ZEV sold, weighted by range, and can be banked, traded, or used for compliance.

**Formula (2 marks):**

$$C_{ZEV} = V \times f \times \frac{R}{100} \times A$$

Where: $V$ = volume sold, $f$ = category multiplier, $R$ = EPA-rated electric range (mi), $A$ = off-cycle/A-C adjustment.

**Worked Example (2 marks):**
A manufacturer sells 5,000 BEVs with 250-mile range, and 2,000 PHEVs with 30-mile AER. Assuming $f_{BEV}=1.0$, $f_{PHEV}=0.5$, $A=1.0$:
- BEV credits: $5{,}000 \times 1.0 \times 2.5 \times 1.0 = 12{,}500$
- PHEV credits: $2{,}000 \times 0.5 \times 0.3 \times 1.0 = 300$
- Total: **12,800 credits**

**Banking Rules (1 mark):** Credits can be banked forward up to **5 years**, traded between manufacturers, or sold at market rate. Credits expire after 5 years.

**Penalty (1 mark):** Each ZEV credit shortfall incurs a fine of **$5,000 per credit**. For a 1,000-credit deficit, the manufacturer owes $5,000,000.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Alternative]**

**(a) Discuss the major pollutants regulated by CARB LEV III, the rationale for combining NMOG and NOx, and the corresponding fleet-average compliance formula. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Major Pollutants (2 marks):**
1. **NMOG (Non-Methane Organic Gases)** — Hydrocarbons excluding methane; smog precursors
2. **NOx (Oxides of Nitrogen)** — NO + NO₂; smog and acid rain precursors
3. **CO (Carbon Monoxide)** — Toxic, binds hemoglobin
4. **PM (Particulate Matter)** — Direct health hazard, especially PM2.5
5. **HCHO (Formaldehyde)** — Direct toxic air contaminant (TAC)
6. **GHG (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O)** — Climate change drivers

**Rationale for Combining NMOG + NOx (2 marks):**
Tropospheric ozone is formed by the photochemical reaction of hydrocarbons and NOx under UV light. The chemical kinetics are such that:
$$VOC + NOx + h\nu \rightarrow O_3$$
CARB's pioneering insight was that **reducing only one** of these precursors may *increase* ozone (NOx-titration regime). Therefore, both must be reduced **in proportion** to maintain optimal smog-reduction efficiency. The 30 mg/mi combined limit reflects the ozone-formation potential of the combined mixture.

**Fleet-Average Compliance Formula (3 marks):**

$$\overline{E}_{NMOG+NOx} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(V_i \times E_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}V_i} \le E_{target}$$

Where $V_i$ = units of model $i$ sold in California, $E_i$ = test-cycle NMOG+NOx (g/mi) of model $i$, and $E_{target}$ = 0.030 g/mi by 2025. **Example:** If a fleet has 50% ULEVs (0.07 g/mi) and 50% SULEVs (0.03 g/mi), the fleet average = 0.05 g/mi, exceeding the target. The manufacturer must either (a) improve ULEVs, (b) sell more SULEVs/ZEVs, or (c) purchase credits.

**(b) Explain the Advanced Clean Cars II (ACC II) program, its ZEV sales ramp schedule, and the engineering implications for OEMs designing vehicles for 2030 and beyond. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**ACC II Overview (2 marks):** Adopted by CARB on August 25, 2022, Advanced Clean Cars II (LEV IV) sets binding requirements that **all new light-duty vehicles sold in California be zero-emission by model year 2035**. The schedule ramps ZEV/PHEV sales from 35% in 2026 to 100% in 2035. States under Section 177 (e.g., NY, MA, WA, OR) follow similar timelines.

**ZEV Sales Ramp (2 marks):**
| Year | ZEV % |
|------|-------|
| 2026 | 35 |
| 2028 | 51 |
| 2030 | 68 |
| 2032 | 84 |
| 2035 | 100 |

**Engineering Implications (3 marks):**
1. **Platform Investment:** OEMs must develop dedicated BEV skateboard platforms (e.g., GM Ultium, Hyundai E-GMP, VW MEB+) — retrofitting ICE platforms is suboptimal.
2. **Battery Sourcing:** Long-range BEVs (300+ mi) earn higher ZEV credit multipliers — drives investment in LFP, NMC, and solid-state batteries.
3. **Charging Infrastructure:** Strategic partnerships with charging networks (Tesla NACS, CCS).
4. **ICE Phaseout:** R&D budget for combustion engines shrinks; CAE focus shifts to e-motors, inverters, and BMS.
5. **Supply Chain:** Lithium, cobalt, nickel sourcing becomes a strategic imperative.
6. **Thermal Management:** Heat-pump HVACs, immersion-cooled batteries become standard.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in CARB Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "g/mi" with "mg/mi":** LEV III NMOG+NOx is 30 **mg/mi** = 0.030 **g/mi**. Students often write 0.30 g/mi, which is 10× too lenient. Always verify units.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "Off-Cycle" and "A/C" credit factors ($A$):** The basic formula $C = V \times f \times R/100$ is the *minimum*. Real credit calculations include $A \ge 1.0$ due to off-cycle technologies (e.g., stop-start, high-efficiency lighting). Examiners award 0.5–1 mark for the complete formula.
>
> 3. **Mixing "Useful Life" definitions:** CARB uses **150,000 miles** (LEV III) — NOT 100,000 mi (Euro 6) or 120,000 mi (LEV II legacy).
>
> 4. **Wrong penalty value:** ZEV credit penalty is **$5,000 per credit**, not $50,000 or $500.
>
> 5. **Confusing LEV category with emission g/mi:** TLEV ≤ 0.125, LEV ≤ 0.090, ULEV ≤ 0.070, SULEV ≤ 0.030 — these are *maximum* allowed NMOG+NOx values; many students list them backward.
>
> 6. **Missing the 5-year banking rule:** ZEV credits expire after 5 years; many exam answers ignore this.
>
> 7. **Ignoring Section 209 of CAA:** California has the right to set its own standards under Section 209 (the "CAA waiver"). This is the constitutional foundation of CARB authority.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — CARB Standards**

- [x] **CARB = California Air Resources Board**, est. 1967, under Cal/EPA
- [x] **Legal basis:** Section 209 of U.S. Clean Air Act (CAA waiver)
- [x] **Other states may adopt CARB standards** under Section 177
- [x] **LEV I (1994)** → LEV II (2004) → LEV III (2015) → LEV IV / ACC II (2026)
- [x] **Four certification categories** (clean → dirty): ZEV → SULEV → ULEV → LEV
- [x] **Combined NMOG + NOx metric** — CARB's unique smog-formation insight
- [x] **LEV III 2025 target:** NMOG+NOx ≤ 30 mg/mi; PM ≤ 3 mg/mi; HCHO ≤ 4 mg/mi; CO ≤ 1.7 g/mi
- [x] **GHG fleet average (2025):** 177 g CO₂e/mi
- [x] **Useful life (LEV III):** 150,000 miles
- [x] **ZEV credit formula:** $C = V \times f \times (R/100) \times A$
- [x] **ZEV credit penalty:** $5,000 per credit shortfall
- [x] **ZEV credit banking:** Up to 5 years; tradeable
- [x] **ACC II ZEV ramp:** 35% (2026) → 100% (2035)
- [x] **PZEV** = SULEV tailpipe + zero evap + 150k-mile warranty
- [x] **Fleet-average formula:** $\overline{E} = \frac{\sum V_i E_i}{\sum V_i}$
- [x] **CaRFG3 fuel:** ≤ 10 ppm sulfur (down from 80 ppm)
- [x] **ZEV types:** BEV, FCEV, NEV, PHEV (partial credit)
- [x] **Test cycles:** FTP-75, SFTP (US06, SC03), Cold CO
- [x] **LEV III harmonized with EPA Tier 3** (2017)
- [x] **California influences Euro 6, China VI, India BS-VI** — global benchmark

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: European emission standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** European emission standards # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **European Emission Standards (Euro Standards / ECE Regulations):** > European Emission Standards are a set of legally binding regulations issued by the **European Commission** under the framework of the **European Union (EU) Directives** (most recently consolidated under **Regulation (EU) 2018/858** and its amendments) that define the maximum permissible levels of **regulated exhaust pollutants** and **particulate number (PN)** emitted by motor vehicles, light commercial vehicles, heavy-duty engines, and non-road mobile machinery. The current applicable limits for M1 and N1 category vehicles are codified under **Regulation (EC) No 715/2007** (Euro 5/6) and its corrigenda, with Euro 7 limits introduced via **Regulation (EU) 2024/1257**. The standards cover six core pollutant families: **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**, **Total Hydrocarbons (THC)**, **Non-Methane Hydrocarbons (NMHC)**, **Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx)**, **Particulate Matter (PM)**, and **Particle Number (PN)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pollution Report Card" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuitive Analogy:** > Imagine every car being a student in a school, and the European Emission Standards are like a **yearly report card** set by the school board. In 1992 (Euro 1), the school was lenient — any car below a "D" grade was acceptable. By 2014 (Euro 6), the bar was raised so high that the failing students had to install expensive air-purification backpacks (catalytic converters, DPF, GPF, SCR, EGR, AdBlue injection). Euro 7 is essentially the school announcing: *"From now on, the report card applies to every car, including older students and even the kindergartners (motorcycles), and the grading scale is no longer how much you pollute when you walk slowly in a hallway, but how much you pollute during your entire real life (Real Driving Emissions — RDE)."* The standards use **mass-based units** (grams per kilometre, g/km) for gaseous and particulate mass pollutants, and **count-based units** (particles per kilometre, particles/km or #/km) for ultra-fine particulates. Heavy-duty engines use **g/kWh** units. ## 1.3 Why These Standards Exist — The Driving Forces The evolution of Euro standards is driven by three engineering-scientific and societal concerns: 1. **Public Health Burden:** The **World Health Organization (WHO)** has classified diesel exhaust as a **Group 1 carcinogen** (since 2012) and ambient PM2.5 as a Group 1 carcinogen (since 2013). European Environment Agency estimates attribute approximately **400,000 premature deaths annually in the EU** to air pollution, predominantly from road transport. 2. **Acid Rain & Eutrophication:** NOx and SOx emissions from vehicles contribute to soil acidification and freshwater eutrophication — both explicitly cited in the recitals of the original **Directive 70/220/EEC** (1970). 3. **Climate Forcing:** While CO2 is not strictly a "pollutant" under Euro 1–6, it is reported under **Regulation (EU) 2019/631** (CO2 fleet emissions). Euro 7 introduces a brake-specific particle emission (BSPE) metric that captures non-exhaust and brake wear particles. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Test Bench Condition Reference Values:** > - **Standard Temperature (T₀):** **293 K (20 °C)** > - **Standard Pressure (P₀):** **101.325 kPa** > - **Dilution Tunnel Dew Point:** **305.5 K (32.5 °C)** for PM sampling > - **CVS Flow Rate:** Variable, set by the **Constant Volume Sampling (CVS)** system ## 1.4 Categorical Scope of the Standards European regulations do not apply a "one-size-fits-all" limit. Vehicles are categorised under the **UNECE/WP.29** framework (mirrored into EU law): | EU Category | Description | Applicable Standard Family | |-------------|-------------|---------------------------| | **M1** | Passenger cars (≤ 8 seats + driver) | Euro 1–7 (light-duty, g/km) | | **M2, M3** | Buses and coaches | Euro I–VI (heavy-duty, g/kWh) | | **N1** | Light commercial vehicles (≤ 3.5 t) | Euro 1–7 (light-duty, g/km) | | **N2, N3** | Heavy goods vehicles (> 3.5 t) | Euro I–VI (heavy-duty, g/kWh) | | **L** | Powered two- and three-wheelers | Euro 1–5 (separate limit table) | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — How the Standards Operate The European emission regulatory system operates on a four-tier architecture: ### Tier 1: The Pollutant Limit Value Each Euro standard (e.g., Euro 6) prescribes a numerical ceiling, expressed as a mass of pollutant emitted per unit distance (g/km) or per unit engine work (g/kWh). The manufacturer must demonstrate, via type-approval testing, that the in-service vehicle's emissions do not exceed this ceiling. ### Tier 2: The Test Cycle The vehicle is exercised over a prescribed **driving cycle** that simulates representative real-world operation. There are two generations of cycles: - **NEDC (New European Driving Cycle)** — used from Euro 1 through Euro 6 (up to September 2017 for new type approvals and August 2018 for all registrations). The NEDC comprises four ECE-15 urban cycles plus one EUDC (Extra-Urban Driving Cycle). It was criticised for unrealistically low accelerations, excessive idling, fixed gear-shift points, and ambient temperature constraints. - **WLTP (Worldwide Harmonised Light Vehicles Test Procedure)** — adopted for Euro 6 from September 2017 (new types) and September 2018 (all registrations). The WLTP has four phases: **Low, Medium, High, Extra-High** with a total duration of **1800 s**, distance of **23.77 km**, and average speed of **46.5 km/h** (max speed 131 km/h). Test cell temperature is set at **23 °C**. ### Tier 3: Real Driving Emissions (RDE) From Euro 6d-TEMP (2017) and Euro 6d (2020) onwards, in addition to the WLTP laboratory test, a **Portable Emissions Measurement System (PEMS)** is mounted in the vehicle, and emissions are measured on public roads under the **RDE** procedure. The **Conformity Factor (CF)** is the key metric: $$CF = \frac{\text{Measured pollutant concentration in RDE}}{\text{WLTP laboratory value}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Regulatory Ceiling Values of CF (Euro 6d):** > - NOx: **CF ≤ 1.43** (notional, "not-to-exceed") > - PN: **CF ≤ 1.5** > - CO: **CF ≤ 1.5** > - THC + NMOG: **CF ≤ 1.5** ### Tier 4: In-Service Conformity (ISC) and Durability Manufacturers must guarantee that the emission control devices (catalyst, DPF, GPF, SCR catalyst) function for a **minimum useful life period**: - **160,000 km** or **5 years** (whichever occurs first) for M1, N1 class I - **Mileage extension factors** apply to larger vehicles The **multiplicative deterioration factor (DF)** is applied to laboratory test results to project in-use compliance at the end of the useful life. ## 2.2 Pollutant Formation Mechanisms — The 'Why' | Pollutant | Formation Pathway | Primary Control Technology | |-----------|-------------------|----------------------------| | **CO** | Incomplete combustion of fuel due to insufficient O₂ | Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) | | **HC (THC, NMOG, NMHC)** | Unburnt fuel, crevice volumes, wall quenching | TWC, GPF (direct injection) | | **NOx** | High-temperature (> 1300 K) Zeldovich mechanism: N₂ + O → 2NO | EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation), LNT (Lean NOx Trap), SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction with AdBlue/DEF) | | **PM (soot)** | In-cylinder fuel-rich zones, lube oil contribution | DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter), GPF, SCR | | **PN (particle number)** | Solid nuclei mode particles (< 23 nm) | DPF/GPF with 23 nm cut-off | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are examination-relevant for KTU 2024 Scheme.** | # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units | Physical Meaning | |---|-------------------|-------------------------|-------|-------------------| | 1 | Mass Emission Rate (g/km) | $m_{pollutant} = \dfrac{V_{CVS} \cdot c_{pollutant}}{d_{cycle}}$ | g/km | Pollutant mass per unit distance | | 2 | Specific Emission (Heavy-Duty) | $e = \dfrac{m_{pollutant}}{W_{cycle}}$ | g/kWh | Mass per unit work output | | 3 | Brake-Specific Fuel Consumption | $bsfc = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_{brake}}$ | g/kWh | Fuel rate per brake power | | 4 | Conformity Factor | $CF = \dfrac{c_{RDE}}{c_{WLTP}}$ | dimensionless | Real-driving / lab ratio | | 5 | Dilution Ratio (CVS) | $DR = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{CVS}}{\dot{V}_{exhaust}}$ | dimensionless | Tunnel dilution | | 6 | Engine-out to Tailpipe Conversion Efficiency | $\eta_{conv} = 1 - \dfrac{c_{tailpipe}}{c_{engine-out}}$ | % | Catalytic system efficiency | | 7 | Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Petrol) | $(A/F)_{stoich} = 14.7$ | kg/kg | Reference for lambda control | | 8 | Lambda (λ) | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | dimensionless | Rich (λ<1) / Lean (λ>1) | | 9 | Theoretical Diesel Cycle Efficiency (Dual) | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | – | (where r=compression ratio, ρ=cut-off ratio) | | 10 | Useful Life Mileage | $M_{useful} = 160{,}000 \text{ km}$ (M1, N1-I) | km | DPF/TWC durability floor | | 11 | Particle Number (PN) | $PN = N_{>23\,nm} / km$ | #/km | Solid nuclei particle count | | 12 | Zeldovich NO Formation Rate (simplified) | $\dfrac{d[NO]}{dt} \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | mol/s | Arrhenius-type T dependency | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility These equations and standards directly govern the design, calibration, and aftertreatment sizing of every modern internal combustion engine sold in the European Union, EFTA countries (Norway, Iceland, Liechtenstein), the United Kingdom (post-Brexit retained law), and many aligned jurisdictions (India's Bharat Stage VI is **legally harmonised** to Euro 6, Australia's ADR 80/03 to Euro 5, etc.). Compliance is enforced by **Type Approval Authorities** (e.g., VCA in the UK, KBA in Germany, UTAC in France), and post-market surveillance is conducted by the **European Commission's Joint Research Centre (JRC)**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation — Emission Mass Calculation from CVS Data The **Constant Volume Sampling (CVS)** system dilutes the raw exhaust with filtered ambient air at a controlled volumetric flow rate. The pollutant mass is then calculated from the diluted concentration. ### Step 1: Define the CVS Volumetric Flow Rate The CVS draws a constant volume flow rate $\dot{V}_{CVS}$ in m³/min. This rate is set such that the diluted exhaust remains below the water condensation point. $$\dot{V}_{CVS} = \text{constant (m}^3/\text{min)}$$ ### Step 2: Integrate the Concentration Over the Cycle The diluted concentration $c(t)$ of a pollutant (e.g., CO) is measured by an analyser (NDIR for CO, FID for HC, CLD for NOx) in ppm or mg/m³. The total mass collected over the cycle is: $$m_{pollutant} = c_{avg} \cdot V_{CVS,\,total}$$ where $V_{CVS,\,total} = \dot{V}_{CVS} \cdot t_{cycle}$. Expressing the average concentration as a time integral: $$c_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{t_{cycle}} \int_{0}^{t_{cycle}} c(t) \, dt$$ ### Step 3: Express Mass in g/km The distance covered during the cycle $d_{cycle}$ is known (e.g., 11.007 km for NEDC; 23.77 km for WLTP). The distance-specific emission is: $$E_{pollutant} = \dfrac{m_{pollutant}}{d_{cycle}} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{CVS}}{d_{cycle}} \int_{0}^{t_{cycle}} c(t) \, dt$$ ### Step 4: Converting ppm to Mass Concentration The analyser gives $c_{ppm}$ in parts per million. To convert to mass per volume, multiply by the molar mass divided by molar volume: $$c_{mass} = c_{ppm} \cdot \dfrac{p \cdot M_{pollutant}}{R \cdot T \cdot 10^6}$$ Substituting standard conditions: $p = 101.325$ kPa, $T = 293$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $M_{CO} = 28.01$ g/mol, $M_{CO2} = 44.01$ g/mol, $M_{NOx \, (as \, NO_2)} = 46.01$ g/mol, $M_{HC \, (as \, C_1)} = 13.85$ g/mol (paraffin equivalence): $$c_{mass} \, \text{(mg/m}^3) = c_{ppm} \cdot \dfrac{101325 \cdot M_{pollutant}}{8.314 \cdot 293 \cdot 10^6} \cdot 10^3$$ ### Step 5: Final Tailpipe Emission Equation $$\boxed{E_{pollutant} \, \text{(g/km)} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{CVS} \cdot t_{cycle}}{d_{cycle}} \cdot c_{ppm} \cdot \dfrac{p \cdot M_{pollutant}}{R \cdot T \cdot 10^6} \cdot 10^{-3}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Numerical Insight:** > For CO, the conversion factor at standard conditions is approximately **1.196 mg/m³ per ppm**. This means a 1000 ppm CO concentration measured in the CVS over a 1180 s NEDC with a 10 m³/min CVS flow and 11.007 km distance yields: > $E_{CO} = (10 \times 1180 / 11.007) \times 1000 \times 1.196 \times 10^{-3} \approx 1.28$ g/km. ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — Conformity Factor and RDE Boundary Conditions The RDE regulation (Commission Regulation (EU) 2018/1832) defines a trip-based validation methodology. The boundary conditions for an RDE trip to be valid are: ### Step 1: Define the Trip Categories - **Urban share (U):** ≤ 29 % to ≥ 44 % of total distance - **Rural share (R):** ≤ 23 % to ≥ 43 % of total distance - **Motorway share (M):** ≤ 23 % to ≥ 33 % of total distance ### Step 2: Compute Average Speed Windows - $v_{ave,\,urban} \in [15, 40]$ km/h - $v_{ave,\,rural} \in [55, 80]$ km/h - $v_{ave,\,motorway} \in [90, 130]$ km/h (with ≥ 5 % of motorway share at > 100 km/h) ### Step 3: Compute the Moving Average Window The 3rd order Butterworth low-pass filter is applied to the speed trace with a cut-off frequency derived from the maximum vehicle speed. The **CO₂ moving average window** is calculated as: $$MAW_{CO_2} = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \dot{m}_{CO_2}(t) \, dt$$ ### Step 4: Evaluate the Conformity Factor $$CF_{pollutant} = \dfrac{\int_{0}^{T} \dot{m}_{pollutant}^{RDE}(t) \, dt / d_{RDE}}{c_{pollutant}^{WLTP} \cdot k \cdot (1 + DF)}$$ where $k$ is the applicable notional correction factor and $DF$ is the deterioration factor. ### Step 5: Apply Euro 6d Boundary on CF If $CF_{NOx} \le 1.43$, the vehicle is type-approved. If not, the manufacturer must redesign the aftertreatment system. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Euro 1 → Euro 6 Limit Evolution (Petrol Passenger Cars, g/km) > [!NOTE] > **The following table is the single most examination-relevant artefact in this module. 3.6 Step-by-Step Euro 7 vs. Euro 6 — The Step Change Analysis Euro 7 (Regulation (EU) 2024/1257) is the first post-Euro 6 standard and introduces the most sweeping changes since Euro 1: - **Lifetime compliance window** extended to **200,000 km / 10 years** for cars - **Brake wear particle emissions** introduced (≤ **3 mg/km** for PM10 from brakes, ≤ **7 mg/km** for non-exhaust PM2.5) - **Tyre wear** regulated from 2025 (separate, vehicle-level) - **Electric vehicle battery durability** mandated (state-of-health at 5 years / 100,000 km ≥ 80 %; at 8 years / 160,000 km ≥ 72 %) - **On-Board Fuel/Energy Consumption Monitoring Device (OBFCM)** mandatory - **Two implementation stages:** - **Stage 1 (new types):** **29 November 2025** (cars and vans) - **Stage 2 (all registrations):** **29 November 2027** (cars and vans) - Heavy-duty: **29 May 2026 / 2028** > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Euro 7 does NOT tighten the NOx limit for light-duty ICE vehicles (remains at 0.060 g/km). The principal tightening is in **PM (0.0045 g/km)**, **PN (0.5 × 10¹¹ /km, i.e., a 12× reduction)**, the **introduction of brake/tyre wear limits**, and the **in-service lifetime extension to 200,000 km / 10 years**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — European Emission Standard Compliance Pathway ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Vehicle Prototype] --> B[Pre-Test Conditioning] B --> C[WLTP Test Cell

(3 Marks)** Define the term "European Emission Standard" and list the four major pollutants regulated under it. **Model Answer:** European Emission Standards are a series of EU regulations (originating from Directive 70/220/EEC and currently codified under Regulation (EC) 715/2007 and Regulation (EU) 2024/1257) that prescribe the maximum permissible mass of specific exhaust pollutants emitted by motor vehicles per unit distance (g/km) or per unit engine work (g/kWh). The four major regulated pollutants are: 1. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** — incomplete combustion product 2. **Hydrocarbons (HC / THC / NMHC / NMOG)** — unburnt fuel 3. **Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx)** — high-temperature combustion product 4. **Particulate Matter (PM) and Particle Number (PN)** — soot and nuclei-mode solids --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > **Q2. (3 Marks)** Differentiate between NEDC and WLTP test cycles in three points. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | NEDC | WLTP | |-----------|------|------| | Year adopted | 1992 (Euro 1 onwards) | 2017 (Euro 6 onwards) | | Total cycle duration | 1180 s | 1800 s | | Cycle distance | 10.93 km | 23.77 km | | Phases | Urban ECE-15 (×4) + EUDC | Low / Medium / High / Extra-High | | Average speed | 33.6 km/h | 46.5 km/h | | Max speed | 120 km/h | 131 km/h | | Test cell temperature | 20–30 °C | 23 °C fixed | | Gear shifts | Fixed by cycle | Driver-selected, per $v \times n$ mapping | | Validity | Discontinued for new types Sept 2017 | Current standard | --- ## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **Question A (14 Marks):** > **(a) (7 Marks)** With a neat table, compare the Euro 1, Euro 3, Euro 5, and Euro 6 emission limits for petrol-driven passenger cars (CO, HC, NOx, PM, PN). > **(b) (7 Marks)** Explain the working of a Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) with reference to the regulated pollutants. Why is λ = 1 operation critical for simultaneous CO, HC, and NOx conversion? ### Model Solution for Q.A(a): **Comparison Table — Petrol Passenger Car Emission Limits (g/km):** | Pollutant | Euro 1 (1992) | Euro 3 (2000) | Euro 5 (2009) | Euro 6 (2014) | |-----------|---------------|---------------|---------------|---------------| | **CO** | **2.72** | **2.30** | **1.00** | **1.00** | | **HC + NOx** | **0.97** | – | – | – | | **THC** | – | **0.20** | **0.10** | **0.10** | | **NOx** | – | **0.15** | **0.060** | **0.060** | | **NMHC** | – | – | **0.068** | **0.068** | | **PM** | – | – | **0.005** (DI only) | **0.005** (DI only) | | **PN** | – | – | **6.0 × 10¹¹** (DI only) | **6.0 × 10¹¹** (DI only) | **Observations to be stated in answer:** - CO has been reduced by **63 %** from Euro 1 to Euro 5/6 - The combined HC+NOx limit was separated into individual limits at Euro 3 for better individual control - PN limit was introduced in Euro 5 specifically for direct-injection petrol engines - Euro 6 maintained Euro 5 limits but added a stricter NMOG reporting threshold ### Model Solution for Q.A(b): **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) Working:** A TWC is a ceramic (cordierite) or metallic (Fe-Cr-Al) honeycomb substrate wash-coated with precious metals — **platinum (Pt)**, **palladium (Pd)**, and **rhodium (Rh)** — over a high-surface-area gamma-alumina (γ-Al₂O₃) layer. Three simultaneous redox reactions occur: **Oxidation of CO (Pt/Pd catalysed):** $$2\,\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2$$ **Oxidation of HC (Pt/Pd catalysed):** $$C_xH_y + \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \dfrac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Reduction of NOx (Rh catalysed):** $$2\,\text{NO}_x \rightarrow x\,\text{O}_2 + \text{N}_2 \quad \text{(with CO or HC as reductant)}$$ For example, for $x = 1$: $$2\,\text{NO} + 2\,\text{CO} \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + 2\,\text{CO}_2$$ **Why λ = 1 is Critical:** The air-fuel ratio is described by the **lambda (λ) parameter**: $$\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$$ where $(A/F)_{stoich}$ for petrol is **14.7 kg air / kg fuel**. The light-off window of the TWC for simultaneous 90 %+ conversion of all three pollutants is extremely narrow — approximately **λ = 1.00 ± 0.01** (often called the "lambda window"). A closed-loop control system using a **wideband lambda sensor (UEGO, Heated Exhaust Gas Oxygen sensor)** switches rapidly between slightly rich (λ < 1) and slightly lean (λ > 1) conditions, time-averaging to λ ≈ 1.00. Outside this window: - If λ < 0.99 (rich): NOx reduction is high, but CO and HC oxidation suffers (insufficient O₂) - If λ > 1.01 (lean): CO and HC oxidation is high, but NOx cannot be reduced (no reductant) Therefore, the TWC is uniquely a "three-way" device because it requires this precise stoichiometric balance to convert all three pollutant families simultaneously. --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > **Question B (14 Marks):** > **(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the Real Driving Emissions (RDE) procedure introduced under Euro 6d. How is the Conformity Factor (CF) calculated and what are the regulatory limits? > **(b) (7 Marks)** Compare the Aftertreatment systems used in a Euro 6 petrol car vs. a Euro 6 diesel car in terms of components, working principle, and the pollutant each targets. ### Model Solution for Q.B(a): **RDE Procedure (Commission Regulation (EU) 2018/1832):** Euro 6d (full implementation from 1 January 2020 for all new registrations) mandates that in addition to the **WLTP laboratory test**, the vehicle must demonstrate compliance on public roads using a **Portable Emissions Measurement System (PEMS)**. Key features: 1. **Test equipment:** PEMS (e.g., AVL M.O.V.E, Horiba OBS-ONE) consisting of a gas analyser, a Particle Counter (PN-PEMS), exhaust flow meter, GPS, ambient sensors. 2. **Trip composition:** A complete RDE trip is divided into three segments: - **Urban** — average speed 15 to 40 km/h - **Rural** — average speed 55 to 80 km/h - **Motorway** — average speed 90 to 130 km/h 3. **Trip duration:** 90 to 120 minutes; total distance 16 to 32 km. 4. **Boundary conditions:** The trip must satisfy the "MAW validation" — the moving average of CO₂ emissions per km must lie within tolerance bands of the WLTP CO₂ value. 5. **Conformity Factor (CF):** $$CF = \dfrac{c_{RDE} \text{ (distance-specific emission over RDE trip)}}{c_{WLTP} \text{ (distance-specific WLTP result)}}$$ 6. **Regulatory CF Limits (Euro 6d):** | Pollutant | "Not-to-exceed" CF | |-----------|---------------------| | **NOx** | **1.43** | | **PN** | **1.50** | | **CO** | **1.50** | | **THC + NMOG** | **1.50** | RDE was introduced specifically to close the gap between laboratory NEDC results and real-world NOx emissions exposed in the **2015 Volkswagen "Dieselgate" scandal**, where in-use emissions exceeded the laboratory limits by factors of 10 to 35. ### Model Solution for Q.B(b): **Aftertreatment Comparison — Euro 6 Petrol vs. Diesel:** | Subsystem | Euro 6 Petrol (T-GDI) | Euro 6 Diesel | |-----------|----------------------|---------------| | **Primary catalyst** | **Three-Way Catalyst (TWC)** — Pt/Pd/Rh on cordierite; reduces CO, HC, NOx simultaneously at λ ≈ 1 | **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC)** — Pt/Pd; oxidises CO and HC only | | **NOx control** | Not separately required (handled by TWC at λ = 1) | **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** with AdBlue (32.5 % urea solution) or **Lean NOx Trap (LNT)** | | **Particulate control** | **Gasoline Particulate Filter (GPF)** — wall-flow cordierite or SiC, 23 nm cut-off | **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)** — wall-flow SiC, 23 nm cut-off | | **SCR chemistry** | N/A | $4\,\text{NH}_3 + 4\,\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\,\text{N}_2 + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | | **AdBlue dosing** | None | **DEF injector** upstream of SCR, dosed at ~ 2–6 % of fuel mass flow | | **EGR** | Internal/external for knock control, not for NOx | **High-pressure EGR (HP-EGR) + Low-pressure EGR (LP-EGR)** for primary NOx reduction | | **Sensor suite** | Pre/post TWC UEGO sensors, OBD lambda sensors | Pre-DOC UEGO, **NOx sensor** pre/post SCR, **DPF pressure differential sensor**, **PM sensor** (Euro 6d-II onwards) | | **Operating λ** | λ = 1.00 ± 0.01 (stoichiometric) | λ = 1.30 to 1.80 (lean combustion) | **Key Engineering Insight:** - The petrol engine operates **stoichiometrically** and uses the TWC's three-way redox capability — a single substrate handles all three pollutants. - The diesel engine operates **lean** (excess O₂), which means it cannot use a TWC (no reductant available for NOx). It therefore requires a **two-stage strategy**: (i) in-cylinder NOx reduction via EGR, and (ii) tailpipe NOx reduction via SCR with a reductant (urea-derived NH₃). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (14-Mark Questions):** > - **Mark loss #1:** Students often confuse **Euro 5 and Euro 6** limits for petrol and diesel. Memorise: Euro 6 retained the Euro 5 *numeric* limits for light-duty vehicles but unified the previously differentiated petrol/diesel PM/PN thresholds. > - **Mark loss #2:** Forgetting that **Euro 7 does NOT tighten NOx** for light-duty ICE — it tightens **PM (0.0045 g/km)**, **PN (0.5 × 10¹¹ /km — a 12× reduction)**, and **adds brake-wear** and **tyre-wear** limits. > - **Mark loss #3:** Confusing **NEDC (discontinued 2018)** with **WLTP (active)**. Always state the cycle, year of adoption, distance, and duration explicitly. > - **Mark loss #4:** Stating that **CO₂ is regulated under Euro 1–6** — it is NOT. CO₂ is reported under **Regulation (EU) 2019/631** (fleet CO₂) and is *not* a regulated pollutant in the Euro standard sense (Euro 7 introduces a "CO₂-equivalent" climate pollutant component separately). > - **Mark loss #5:** For heavy-duty engines, confusing **g/km** (light-duty) with **g/kWh** (heavy-duty, WHTC/WHSC test cycles). --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - **Originating Directive:** **70/220/EEC (1970)** — first EU motor vehicle emission directive; **Type Approval Directive.** - **Most recent consolidation (light-duty):** **Regulation (EC) 715/2007** and **Regulation (EU) 2018/858** (framework). - **Most recent (Euro 7):** **Regulation (EU) 2024/1257** — entry into force **28 May 2024**; new type approval **29 Nov 2025**; all registrations **29 Nov 2027** (cars and vans). - **Light-duty limits units:** **g/km.** - **Heavy-duty limits units:** **g/kWh.** - **Default useful life (light-duty):** **160,000 km / 5 years.** - **Euro 7 useful life:** **200,000 km / 10 years.** - **Test cycles:** **NEDC (pre-2017) → WLTP (post-2017).** - **Road test:** **RDE (PEMS-based) mandatory from Euro 6d-TEMP (2017) and full Euro 6d (2020).** - **Conformity Factor (CF) — Euro 6d ceiling:** NOx **1.43**, PN **1.50.** - **PN lower size cut-off:** **23 nm** (matches the **PMP — Particle Measurement Programme** of the UNECE GRPE). - **Stoichiometric A/F for petrol:** **14.7 kg/kg.** - **AdBlue (DEF) composition:** **32.5 % aqueous urea (AUS 32)**, ISO 22241. - **Diesel Euro 6 NOx:** **0.080 g/km** (same as Euro 6 since 2014). - **Petrol Euro 6 NOx:** **0.060 g/km** (slightly stricter than diesel since 2014). - **Euro 6 unified PM/PN:** **0.005 g/km** and **6.0 × 10¹¹ /km** (DI petrol only). - **Euro 7 PN:** **0.5 × 10¹¹ /km** (12× reduction vs. Euro 6). - **Euro 7 brake PM10 limit:** **3 mg/km** (newly regulated). - **Global harmonisation:** India **Bharat Stage VI = Euro 6** (mandatory from April 2020); Australia ADR 80/03 ≈ Euro 5; China **National VI** ≈ Euro 6 with stricter elements. - **On-Board Diagnostics (OBD) regulation:** **Regulation (EU) 2018/858** and (OBD I) Directive 98/69/EC. - **In-Service Conformity (ISC) regulation:** **Commission Regulation (EU) 2018/1832.** - **Heavy-duty test cycles:** **WHTC (World Harmonised Transient Cycle)** and **WHSC (World Harmonised Stationary Cycle)** for Euro VI. > **Mnemonic for Euro 6 Petrol Limits:** "**1 — 1 — 6 — 5 — 60**" (1 g/km CO, 0.1 g/km THC, 6 × 10¹¹ PN, 0.005 g/km PM, 0.06 g/km NOx) — *or just remember "Euro 6 = Euro 5 limits, applied to DI petrol too."* > **Mnemonic for NEDC vs. WLTP:** "**NEDC = 1 1 8 0** (1180 s, 10.93 km, ≤ 1991 standard)" and "**WLTP = 1 8 0 0 2 3** (1800 s, 23.77 km, post-2017 standard)."

topics.title: Estimation of vehicle emissions in light-duty vehicles and heavy-duty vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Estimation of vehicle emissions in light-duty vehicles and heavy-duty vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vehicle emission estimation** is the standardized, quantitative procedure used to determine the mass (or mass per unit distance/work) of regulated gaseous and particulate pollutants released from internal combustion engine vehicles over prescribed laboratory test cycles. The estimation methodology is bifurcated strictly by vehicle category, because the governing physics, test procedures, and emission limit regulations differ fundamentally:

- **Light-Duty Vehicles (LDV):** Passenger cars and light commercial vehicles with a Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW) typically **≤ 3.5 tonnes** (per UNECE/WP.29 classification). Emissions are reported on a **distance-specific basis (g/km)**.
- **Heavy-Duty Vehicles (HDV):** Trucks and buses with a GVW typically **> 3.5 tonnes**, with engines tested on an engine dynamometer. Emissions are reported on a **work-specific basis (g/kWh)** as per the **Wh/kg** approach of the **World Harmonized Stationary/Transient Cycles (WHSC/WHTC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The core distinction is the *reference unit*. LDVs are vehicle-tested (chassis dynamometer) on **transient drive cycles**, whereas HDVs are engine-tested (engine dynamometer) using either **steady-state (WHSC/ESC)** or **transient (WHTC/ETC)** engine cycles.
