This is a high-weightage, frequently repeated question in KTU board examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tap dripping into a measuring cylinder**:

- For a **Light-Duty Vehicle (LDV)**, we attach a bucket to the car's tailpipe and ask, "How many grams of soot and gas escape for every **kilometer driven** on a city trip?" The "trip" is a simulated drive cycle (e.g., FTP-75, WLTP) on a chassis dynamometer, and the result is averaged over total distance.

- For a **Heavy-Duty Vehicle (HDV)**, we instead unbolt the engine, put it on a test bench, and ask, "How many grams of soot does the engine emit for every **kilowatt-hour of useful work** it produces?" The result is averaged over the energy delivered, because a heavy truck's mileage is meaningless (it varies wildly with load), but its **energy efficiency and emissions per unit of work** are stable and meaningful.

> [!NOTE]
> **In Plain English:** LDV emissions = "pollution per km." HDV emissions = "pollution per energy produced." The estimation tools, dilution tunnels, and mathematical reductions are all built around this single conceptual difference.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Reference Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Distance-Specific Emission** | $e_d$ | **g/km** | LDV |
| **Work-Specific Emission** | $e_w$ | **g/kWh** | HDV |
| **Brake-Specific Fuel Consumption** | **BSFC** | **g/kWh** | HDV |
| **Density of air (NTP)** | $\rho_{air}$ | **1.293 kg/m³** | Calculations |
| **Molar volume of gas (NTP)** | $V_m$ | **22.4 L/mol** | Calculations |
| **Molar mass of NO₂ (reference)** | $M_{NO_2}$ | **46.0055 g/mol** | NO→NO₂ conversion |
| **Molar mass of NO** | $M_{NO}$ | **30.0061 g/mol** | NO→NO₂ conversion |
| **Diesel fuel density** | $\rho_f$ | **0.832 kg/L** (typical) | BSFC |
| **GWP of CO₂ (reference)** | GWP | **1** | CO₂-equivalent |

> [!NOTE]
> **NOx is always reported as NO₂-equivalent (NO₂ eq.)** in both LDV and HDV regulations, even though engine-out NOx is predominantly NO. This is because of the regulatory convention set by the EU and UNECE.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept: The Emission Estimation Pipeline

The estimation of vehicle emissions follows a structured, internationally standardized procedure. The general workflow is identical in philosophy, but the sampling hardware and reduction mathematics differ between LDV and HDV.

### 2.1.1 Light-Duty Vehicle (LDV) Estimation Pipeline

1. **Chassis Dynamometer Mounting:** The complete vehicle is placed on a roller dynamometer (single or twin roller) that simulates road load.
2. **Driving Cycle Execution:** The driver/robot follows a prescribed speed-time trace (FTP-75, NEDC, WLTP, or Indian MIDC).
3. **Constant Volume Sampling (CVS):** The raw exhaust is **continuously diluted** with filtered ambient air in a CVS tunnel (positive displacement pump at constant volumetric flow $\dot{V}_{CVS}$).
4. **Bag Sampling:** A slip stream of the diluted exhaust is collected in **Tedlar bags** — typically 3 bags for FTP-75 (Phase 1: cold-start, Phase 2: stabilized, Phase 3: hot-start).
5. **Analyzer Analysis:** Bag concentrations are measured using **NDIR (CO, CO₂), FID (THC), CLD (NOx)**, and **gravimetric PM filters**.
6. **Mass Calculation:** The dilution-corrected mass of each pollutant is computed and divided by the total cycle distance.

### 2.1.2 Heavy-Duty Vehicle (HDV) Estimation Pipeline

1. **Engine Dynamometer Mounting:** The engine alone is coupled to an eddy-current or AC dynamometer.
2. **Cycle Execution:** The engine follows either:
   - **WHSC** — 13 steady-state modes, weighted by their normalized engine speed and load.
   - **WHTC** — Second-by-second transient torque/speed commands over ~1800 s.
3. **Raw or Diluted Exhaust Sampling:** Either full-flow dilution (per WHTC) or partial-flow raw measurement with a smoke meter.
4. **Continuous Modal Measurement:** Each second's concentration is logged, multiplied by the **instantaneous exhaust mass flow rate**, and integrated.
5. **Work Calculation:** Brake work $W_b$ is integrated from torque × angular speed over the cycle.
6. **Reporting:** Final emissions are in **g/kWh** averaged over the cycle, optionally weighted across cold-start and hot-start phases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cold-Start vs. Hot-Start Weighting:** For WHTC, the result is a weighted average of the **Cold-Start WHTC (WHTC-C)**, a **Hot-Start WHTC (WHTC-H)**, and the **WHTC** itself, with weighting factors of 0.14, 0.86, and 0 (per Euro VI / GSR II). The KTU examiner may ask for the cold-start weighted NOx equation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Distance-specific mass emission (LDV)** | $e_d = \dfrac{m_{pollutant}}{D}$ | **g/km** | LDV, CVS bag method |
| 2 | **Mass of pollutant (dilution method)** | $m_i = c_i \times \dot{V}_{CVS} \times t \times \dfrac{M_i}{\rho_{exh,std}}$ | **g** | LDV |
| 3 | **Dilution factor** | $DF = \dfrac{c_{CO_2, dil}}{c_{CO_2, raw}}$ | dimensionless | LDV |
| 4 | **NOx mass (as NO₂ eq.)** | $m_{NOx} = 1.5332 \times c_{NO} \times \dot{V}_{CVS} \times t$ | **g** | LDV & HDV |
| 5 | **Work-specific emission (HDV)** | $e_w = \dfrac{m_{pollutant}}{W_b}$ | **g/kWh** | HDV |
| 6 | **Brake work over a cycle** | $W_b = \int T_b \, \omega \, dt$ | **kJ** or **kWh** | HDV |
| 7 | **Average BSFC** | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{P_b}$ | **g/kWh** | HDV |
| 8 | **Weighted WHTC emission** | $e_{WHTC,final} = 0.14\,e_{WHTC-C} + 0.86\,e_{WHTC-H}$ | **g/kWh** | HDV (Euro VI) |
| 9 | **Distance-specific fuel consumption** | $DFC = \dfrac{V_f \times \rho_f}{D}$ | **L/100 km** or **g/km** | LDV |
| 10 | **Carbon balance for fuel consumption** | $FC = \dfrac{0.866 \times (c_{CO} + c_{CO_2})}{c_{CO_2,\max}}$ | **g/km** | LDV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal NOx conversion factor:** $\dfrac{M_{NO_2}}{M_{NO}} = \dfrac{46.0055}{30.0061} = 1.5332$ — this is the **NO-to-NO₂ equivalent** multiplier. Memorize this for KTU problems.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Regulatory Compliance:** Every vehicle sold in the EU, India (BS-VI), USA (EPA Tier 3), and Japan must pass these standardized estimations before type approval.
- **In-Use Compliance (PEMS):** Post-Euro 6d / BS-VI Stage 2 regulations use **Portable Emissions Measurement Systems (PEMS)** to estimate emissions on real roads (RDE – Real Driving Emissions).
- **Fleet Management & Logistics:** HDV operators use **g/kWh** figures to optimize routes and loads.
- **Environmental Policy:** CO₂ fleet averages (e.g., 95 g/km for cars) directly drive manufacturer penalties and EV mandates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass of Pollutant from CVS Concentration (LDV)

**Problem Statement:** A passenger car is tested on a chassis dynamometer over the FTP-75 cycle using a CVS system. The CVS volumetric flow rate is $\dot{V}_{CVS} = 11.0\, \text{m}^3/\text{min}$ at standard conditions. The CO concentration in the background-diluted bag is $c_{CO} = 6.5\, \text{ppm}$, and the corresponding $\text{CO}_2$ concentration is $c_{CO_2} = 0.45\%$. The cycle duration is $t = 1877\, \text{s}$, and the total distance driven is $D = 17.77\, \text{km}$. Calculate the distance-specific CO emission in g/km.

**Given:**
- $\dot{V}_{CVS} = 11.0\, \text{m}^3/\text{min} = \dfrac{11.0}{60}\, \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 0.18333\, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- $c_{CO} = 6.5\, \text{ppm} = 6.5 \times 10^{-6}$ (volume fraction, dimensionless)
- $t = 1877\, \text{s}$
- $D = 17.77\, \text{km}$
- Standard molar volume $V_m = 22.414\, \text{L/mol}$ at NTP
- Molar mass of CO: $M_{CO} = 28.0101\, \text{g/mol}$

**Step 1: Convert the CVS volume per phase to standard liters.**

$$
V_{CVS} = \dot{V}_{CVS} \times t = 0.18333 \times 1877 = 344.18\, \text{m}^3 = 344{,}180\, \text{L}
$$

*Logic:* Multiplying the volumetric flow rate by the cycle duration gives the total diluted volume drawn through the CVS tunnel.

**Step 2: Compute the total moles of gas sampled at standard conditions.**

$$
n_{total} = \dfrac{V_{CVS}}{V_m} = \dfrac{344{,}180}{22.414} = 15{,}355.4\, \text{mol}
$$

*Logic:* Dividing the volume at standard conditions by the molar volume yields the total moles of diluted gas.

**Step 3: Compute the moles of CO present in the diluted sample.**

$$
n_{CO} = c_{CO} \times n_{total} = (6.5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 15{,}355.4 = 0.09981\, \text{mol}
$$

*Logic:* Since concentration in ppm is a volume/mole fraction, multiplying by total moles gives the moles of CO.

**Step 4: Convert moles of CO to mass.**

$$
m_{CO} = n_{CO} \times M_{CO} = 0.09981 \times 28.0101 = 2.7957\, \text{g}
$$

*Logic:* Using the molar mass of CO converts moles to grams.

**Step 5: Divide by the total distance to obtain distance-specific emission.**

$$
e_{CO} = \dfrac{m_{CO}}{D} = \dfrac{2.7957}{17.77} = 0.1573\, \text{g/km}
$$

*Logic:* The definition of distance-specific emission is mass of pollutant divided by distance traveled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $e_{CO} \approx 0.157\, \text{g/km}$, which is well within BS-VI limits (1.0 g/km for CO).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Work-Specific NOx Emission for HDV (WHTC)

**Problem Statement:** An HDV diesel engine completes the WHTC cycle on an engine dynamometer. The integrated NOx mass over the **cold-start WHTC (WHTC-C)** is $m_{NOx,C} = 28.4\, \text{g}$ and over the **hot-start WHTC (WHTC-H)** is $m_{NOx,H} = 18.6\, \text{g}$. The corresponding brake work integrals are $W_{b,C} = 11.2\, \text{kWh}$ and $W_{b,H} = 11.0\, \text{kWh}$. Calculate the cold-start weighted final NOx emission in **g/kWh** per Euro VI methodology.

**Given:**
- $m_{NOx,C} = 28.4\, \text{g}$; $W_{b,C} = 11.2\, \text{kWh}$
- $m_{NOx,H} = 18.6\, \text{g}$; $W_{b,H} = 11.0\, \text{kWh}$
- Euro VI weighting: $k_C = 0.14$, $k_H = 0.86$

**Step 1: Calculate the work-specific emission for the cold-start phase.**

$$
e_{NOx,C} = \dfrac{m_{NOx,C}}{W_{b,C}} = \dfrac{28.4}{11.2} = 2.5357\, \text{g/kWh}
$$

*Logic:* Direct application of $e_w = m / W_b$ for the cold-start phase.

**Step 2: Calculate the work-specific emission for the hot-start phase.**

$$
e_{NOx,H} = \dfrac{m_{NOx,H}}{W_{b,H}} = \dfrac{18.6}{11.0} = 1.6909\, \text{g/kWh}
$$

*Logic:* Same definition applied to the hot-start phase.

**Step 3: Apply the Euro VI cold-start weighted formula.**

$$
e_{NOx,WHTC} = k_C \cdot e_{NOx,C} + k_H \cdot e_{NOx,H}
$$

$$
e_{NOx,WHTC} = (0.14)(2.5357) + (0.86)(1.6909)
$$

$$
e_{NOx,WHTC} = 0.3550 + 1.4542 = 1.8092\, \text{g/kWh}
$$

*Logic:* The 0.14 / 0.86 weighting reflects the proportional share of cold vs. hot operation in a real-world drive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $e_{NOx,WHTC} \approx 1.81\, \text{g/kWh}$, which is comfortably within Euro VI's $0.46\, \text{g/kWh}$ WHTC limit. (Modern heavy-duty engines have significantly reduced this number; the values used here are for illustration.)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mass Emission Using Dilution Factor (Alternative Method)

**Problem Statement:** The raw exhaust CO₂ concentration is measured as $6.0\%$, while the CVS-diluted CO₂ concentration is $0.45\%$. The diluted CO concentration is $4.0\, \text{ppm}$ and the CVS flow rate is $12.0\, \text{m}^3/\text{min}$ for $t = 1800\, \text{s}$. Calculate the CO mass in raw exhaust.

**Step 1: Compute the dilution factor.**

$$
DF = \dfrac{c_{CO_2,dil}}{c_{CO_2,raw}} = \dfrac{0.45}{6.0} = 0.075
$$

*Logic:* The ratio of diluted to raw CO₂ gives the dilution ratio.

**Step 2: Compute equivalent raw concentration of CO.**

$$
c_{CO,raw} = \dfrac{c_{CO,dil}}{DF} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.075} = 53.33 \times 10^{-6} = 53.33\, \text{ppm}
$$

*Logic:* Multiplying diluted concentration by the inverse of DF recovers the raw (engine-out) concentration.

**Step 3: Convert CVS total volume to standard cubic meters.**

$$
V_{CVS} = 12.0 \times \dfrac{1800}{60} = 360\, \text{m}^3
$$

*Logic:* Same procedure as Derivation 1.

**Step 4: Total moles of raw exhaust gas (equivalent).**

$$
n_{raw} = \dfrac{V_{CVS}}{V_m} = \dfrac{360{,}000}{22.414} = 16{,}061.6\, \text{mol}
$$

*Logic:* Standard state conversion.

**Step 5: Moles of CO in raw exhaust.**

$$
n_{CO} = c_{CO,raw} \times n_{raw} = 53.33 \times 10^{-6} \times 16{,}061.6 = 0.8566\, \text{mol}
$$

*Logic:* Apply concentration to total moles.

**Step 6: Mass of CO.**

$$
m_{CO} = 0.8566 \times 28.0101 = 23.99\, \text{g}
$$

*Logic:* Convert moles to grams using molar mass.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $m_{CO} \approx 24.0\, \text{g}$ of CO in the total raw exhaust over 1800 s.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LDV Emission Estimation Flow (CVS / Chassis Dynamometer)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle on Chassis Dynamometer] --> B[Driver follows Drive Cycle FTP-75 / WLTP]
    B --> C[Raw exhaust to CVS tunnel]
    C --> D[Filtered ambient air dilution]
    D --> E[Constant volume pump V_CVS]
    E --> F1[Bag 1 - Phase]
    E --> F2[Bag 2 - Phase]
    E --> F3[Bag 3 - Phase]
    F1 --> G[NDIR / FID / CLD analyzers]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Concentrations c_CO, c_HC, c_NOx, c_PM]
    H --> I[Mass m_i = c_i * V_CVS * M_i / V_m]
    I --> J[e_d = m_i / D g/km]
    J --> K[Compare with regulatory limit]
```

*Logic Trace:* The chassis dynamometer simulates road load, CVS dilutes the exhaust, analyzers measure concentrations, and the calculation yields distance-specific emissions in g/km.

## 4.2 HDV Emission Estimation Flow (Engine Dynamometer)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine on Dynamometer Bench] --> B[Apply WHTC or WHSC cycle]
    B --> C[Sample raw or diluted exhaust]
    C --> D[Continuous analyzer CLD / FID / FTIR]
    D --> E[Instantaneous concentration c_i t]
    E --> F[Multiply by exhaust mass flow m_exh t]
    F --> G[Integrate: m_i = integral c_i m_exh dt]
    G --> H[Compute brake work: W_b = integral T omega dt]
    H --> I[e_w = m_i / W_b g/kWh]
    I --> J{Cold start phase?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Weight 0.14]
    J -->|No| L[Weight 0.86]
    K --> M[Final weighted result]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Compare with Euro VI / BS-VI Stage 2 limit]
```

*Logic Trace:* The HDV flow emphasizes instantaneous integration rather than bag collection, with the final result expressed per unit of brake work (g/kWh).

## 4.3 CVS Tunnel Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tailpipe] --> B[Probe and primary dilution]
    B --> C[Mixing chamber]
    C --> D[Heat exchanger - filter - secondary dilution]
    D --> E[Critical flow venturi CFV or PDP]
    E --> F[Bag manifold - 3 to 4 bags]
    F --> G[Multi-gas analyzer bench]
    G --> H[Data acquisition - weighting - report]
```

*Logic Trace:* The dilution system removes water and provides a representative sample for analysis. The CFV or PDP (Positive Displacement Pump) maintains constant volumetric flow, which is essential to the validity of the test.

## 4.4 Test Cycle Reference Map (LDV vs. HDV)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LDV ["Light-Duty Vehicles"]
        LDV1[FTP-75 USA]
        LDV2[NEDC EU historical]
        LDV3[WLTP global]
        LDV1 --> M[Distance g/km]
        LDV2 --> M
        LDV3 --> M
    end
    subgraph HDV ["Heavy-Duty Vehicles"]
        HDV1[WHTC transient]
        HDV2[WHSC steady-state]
        HDV3[ESC + ELR historical]
        HDV1 --> N[Work g/kWh]
        HDV2 --> N
        HDV3 --> N
    end
```

*Logic Trace:* LDV cycles yield g/km results; HDV cycles yield g/kWh results. The reference unit difference is the cornerstone distinction in KTU questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between distance-specific (g/km) and work-specific (g/kWh) emission reporting, and state which one is applicable to LDV and HDV.**

**Model Answer:**
Distance-specific emission (g/km) is the mass of pollutant emitted per unit distance traveled and is the **standard for Light-Duty Vehicles (LDV)** tested on a chassis dynamometer over drive cycles such as FTP-75 or WLTP. Work-specific emission (g/kWh) is the mass of pollutant emitted per unit of brake work delivered by the engine and is the **standard for Heavy-Duty Vehicles (HDV)** tested on an engine dynamometer over WHTC/WHSC. The reason for the difference is that for HDV the mileage is highly variable with load, so per-distance values are not meaningful, whereas per-work values are stable and reproducible. The reference standard for g/kWh is the UNECE Regulation 49 / Euro VI / Indian CMV Rules.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Why is NOx always reported as NO₂ equivalent (NO₂ eq.) in vehicle emission regulations?**

**Model Answer:**
Engine-out NOx consists predominantly of NO (~90–95%), with the remainder being NO₂. Although NO is the main species produced, regulatory authorities (UNECE, EU, EPA) require a single representative figure that is conservative and harmonized across laboratories. The convention reports total oxidised nitrogen (NO + NO₂) as if it were entirely NO₂, using the molecular weight conversion factor $\dfrac{M_{NO_2}}{M_{NO}} = \dfrac{46.0055}{30.0061} = 1.5332$. This makes the limit more stringent and removes the ambiguity of dual reporting. The conversion is applied identically to both LDV (FTP/WLTP) and HDV (WHTC/WHSC) results.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the Constant Volume Sampling (CVS) method used for estimating emissions from a light-duty petrol car on the FTP-75 drive cycle. List the analyzers used for CO, THC, NOx, and PM.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A petrol car is tested on the FTP-75 cycle with CVS at a flow rate of 10.5 m³/min. The cycle time is 1877 s. The diluted CO concentration is 5.8 ppm, diluted CO₂ is 0.42%, and the background-corrected CO is 4.9 ppm. Compute the **distance-specific CO emission (g/km)** if the total distance covered is 17.77 km. Take $M_{CO} = 28.01\, \text{g/mol}$ and $V_m = 22.414\, \text{L/mol}$.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — CVS Method Explanation**

The Constant Volume Sampling (CVS) method works on the principle of **proportional sampling**: a known, constant volume of diluted exhaust is drawn through a tunnel regardless of the engine's instantaneous exhaust flow.

**Procedure:**
1. The vehicle is mounted on a **chassis dynamometer**, which applies a road-load curve (coastdown coefficients).
2. The driver (or robot) follows the FTP-75 speed-time trace, divided into **Phase 1 (cold-start, 0–505 s), Phase 2 (stabilized, 506–1372 s), and Phase 3 (hot-start, 1373–1877 s)**.
3. The tailpipe is connected to a CVS tunnel, where raw exhaust is mixed with **filtered ambient dilution air** in a mixing chamber.
4. A **Positive Displacement Pump (PDP)** or **Critical Flow Venturi (CFV)** maintains a constant volumetric flow $\dot{V}_{CVS}$.
5. A slip stream of the diluted mixture is collected in **three Tedlar bags** (one per phase).
6. Background concentrations (in dilution air) are measured separately and subtracted.
7. The diluted sample is analyzed using:
   - **NDIR** for CO and CO₂
   - **FID (Flame Ionization Detector)** for THC
   - **CLD (Chemiluminescence Detector)** for NOx
   - **Gravimetric PM filter** for particulate matter

**Block diagram** (see Section 4.3 for the Mermaid version).

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Step 1:** Convert CVS flow to SI.

$$
\dot{V}_{CVS} = \dfrac{10.5}{60} = 0.175\, \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2:** Total diluted volume over the cycle.

$$
V_{CVS} = 0.175 \times 1877 = 328.475\, \text{m}^3 = 328{,}475\, \text{L}
$$

**Step 3:** Total moles of diluted gas (at NTP).

$$
n_{total} = \dfrac{328{,}475}{22.414} = 14{,}654.5\, \text{mol}
$$

**Step 4:** Moles of CO (using the background-corrected concentration of 4.9 ppm).

$$
c_{CO} = 4.9 \times 10^{-6}, \quad n_{CO} = 4.9 \times 10^{-6} \times 14{,}654.5 = 0.07181\, \text{mol}
$$

**Step 5:** Mass of CO.

$$
m_{CO} = 0.07181 \times 28.01 = 2.011\, \text{g}
$$

**Step 6:** Distance-specific emission.

$$
e_{CO} = \dfrac{2.011}{17.77} = 0.1131\, \text{g/km}
$$

**Final Answer:** $e_{CO} \approx 0.113\, \text{g/km}$ — well within the BS-VI limit of 1.0 g/km for CO.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the World Harmonized Transient Cycle (WHTC) and World Harmonized Steady-state Cycle (WHSC) for heavy-duty engine emission estimation. Compare their key features.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A heavy-duty diesel engine is tested on the WHTC. The integrated NOx masses for the cold-start and hot-start phases are 32.5 g and 19.8 g, respectively. The corresponding brake work integrals are 12.4 kWh and 12.1 kWh. Calculate the **cold-start weighted WHTC NOx emission (g/kWh)** as per Euro VI methodology.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — WHTC vs. WHSC**

| Feature | WHTC | WHSC |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Transient (second-by-second) | Steady-state (13 modes) |
| **Duration** | 1800 s | ~1200 s total per mode |
| **Test type** | Engine dynamometer | Engine dynamometer |
| **Speed/load input** | Time-resolved | Discrete modes |
| **Reference** | UNECE R49 Annex 4B | UNECE R49 Annex 4A |
| **Cold-start weighted?** | Yes (k_C = 0.14, k_H = 0.86) | No, hot-only |
| **Reported unit** | g/kWh | g/kWh |
| **Modal weighting** | Time-integration | Normalized speed × load |

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Step 1:** Cold-start specific emission.

$$
e_{NOx,C} = \dfrac{32.5}{12.4} = 2.621\, \text{g/kWh}
$$

**Step 2:** Hot-start specific emission.

$$
e_{NOx,H} = \dfrac{19.8}{12.1} = 1.636\, \text{g/kWh}
$$

**Step 3:** Weighted result.

$$
e_{NOx,WHTC} = (0.14)(2.621) + (0.86)(1.636) = 0.367 + 1.407 = 1.774\, \text{g/kWh}
$$

**Final Answer:** $e_{NOx,WHTC} \approx 1.77\, \text{g/kWh}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Students often report HDV emissions in g/km or LDV emissions in g/kWh. KTU examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for unit inconsistency.
> 2. **Forgetting the NO→NO₂ conversion:** Whenever NOx is calculated, you must multiply by 1.5332. If the problem does not specify "as NO₂ eq.", explicitly state the assumption.
> 3. **Wrong cycle time for FTP-75:** FTP-75 is 1877 s (not 2400 s or 505 s). Forgetting this is a frequent error.
> 4. **Wh/kg vs. g/kWh:** "Wh/kg" and "g/kWh" are the same base SI, but KTU wants the answer in **g/kWh** (not mg/kWh) unless specified.
> 5. **Background correction skipped:** Always subtract the dilution-air concentration from the diluted exhaust concentration. The KTU key gives **zero credit** for raw (uncorrected) values.
> 6. **Cold-start weighting:** For WHTC, the 0.14/0.86 weights are mandatory in Euro VI. If you write 0.10/0.90 or forget the weighting, **2 marks are lost**.
> 7. **Confusing BSFC with BSNOx:** BSFC = fuel rate / power; BSNOx = NOx rate / power. Same unit, but different numerator.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LDV emissions are in g/km; HDV emissions are in g/kWh.** This is the single most important distinction.
- **CVS (Constant Volume Sampling)** is the universal dilution method for LDV chassis-dyno tests.
- **FTP-75, NEDC, and WLTP** are the three most-cited LDV drive cycles; **WHTC and WHSC** are the modern HDV engine cycles.
- **NOx is reported as NO₂-equivalent** using the **1.5332** conversion factor.
- **Dilution Factor (DF)** is the ratio of diluted to raw CO₂; it is the bridge between raw and diluted concentrations.
- **Euro VI cold-start weighting** for WHTC is **0.14 (cold) / 0.86 (hot)**.
- **PEMS / RDE** is the modern in-use extension of these lab-based methods — relevant for BS-VI Stage 2 and Euro 6d.
- **Analyzers:** NDIR (CO/CO₂), FID (THC), CLD (NOx), Gravimetric (PM), FTIR (advanced multi-gas).
- **PM measurement** is gravimetric; **PN (Particle Number)** is measured using a **CPC (Condensation Particle Counter)** under Euro 6/BS-VI.
- **The CVS tunnel must dilute but not condense** — wall and dilution-air temperatures are tightly controlled.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Hook (for KTU Viva / 14-mark answers):** Always start any numerical answer with: (1) write **Given**, (2) state the **formula** with units, (3) substitute, (4) compute, (5) state the answer with units, and (6) compare with the **regulatory limit**. Examiners reward this structured approach with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crankcase emission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Crankcase emission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Crankcase Emissions** (also universally termed as **Blow-By Gases** in KTU textbooks) are the unburnt and partially burnt combustion gases, along with oil mist and water vapor, that leak past the piston rings of an internal combustion engine and accumulate inside the engine crankcase. These gases represent one of the three primary sources of automotive pollution, the other two being *tailpipe (exhaust) emissions* and *evaporative (fuel) emissions*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT526 - Module 3):** Crankcase emissions form the foundational block for understanding evaporative and blow-by control systems. This topic is a **guaranteed 3 to 7 mark question** in ESE (End Semester Examination) under the regulations module.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Term Mapping (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - Crankcase Emission = Blow-By Emission = Crankcase Ventilation Emission
> - Governing Regulation (India): **AIS-010 / CMR-02** under the Motor Vehicle Rules (TAP 115 / TÜV tested for BS-VI).
> - Governing Regulation (Global): **US EPA Tier 3** and **UN-ECE Regulation 83**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a pressure cooker in your kitchen. When water boils inside, some steam inevitably leaks past the rubber gasket (sealing ring) of the lid. This leaked steam is unwanted, contaminated, and must be channeled away safely. 

A car's engine is exactly the same:
- The **combustion chamber** is the "pressure cooker."
- The **piston rings** are the "rubber gasket."
- Due to enormous cylinder pressures (**up to 80 bar** in diesel and **60 bar** in petrol), a small fraction of hot combustion gases *blows past* the rings.
- These gases collect in the **crankcase** (the engine's "lower half") where the lubricating oil resides.

In the 1960s, manufacturers simply vented this gas to the atmosphere through a road draft tube. The hydrocarbons (HC) in this vented gas were a massive contributor to **smog and ground-level ozone**. To control this, the **Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV)** system was mandated.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Operating Crankcase Pressure:** Typically **−0.5 kPa to −2.0 kPa** (a slight *vacuum*) under normal operation with a functioning PCV system.
- **Blow-By Composition (Volumetric):**
  - Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC): **70 – 80 %**
  - Carbon Monoxide (CO): **2 – 5 %**
  - Carbon Dioxide (CO₂): **5 – 10 %**
  - Water Vapor (H₂O): **5 – 10 %**
  - Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ): Traces
- **Hydrocarbon Emission Rate:** Without PCV, a typical 4-cylinder petrol engine emits approximately **0.5 g/km to 2.0 g/km** of HC through the crankcase.
- **PCV System Service Interval:** Valve replacement recommended every **50,000 km** or 4 years (whichever is earlier) as per OEM service norms.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Crankcase Emission Formation — Stepwise Logic

1. **Combustion Event Generation:** During the power stroke, peak in-cylinder temperature reaches **~2200 °C** and pressure spikes to **60–80 bar**, depending on the fuel (Petrol/Diesel/CNG).
2. **Piston Ring Dynamics:** The top compression ring does not seal perfectly. It has a deliberate *gap* to allow thermal expansion. Under high pressure, gases are forced **downward past the ring gap** into the ring belt zone.
3. **Piston Ring Flutter:** At high engine speeds, the thin oil-control ring *flutters* due to inertia, creating momentary openings that further permit gas leakage.
4. **Accumulation in Crankcase:** The leaked gases (now cooled to ~80–100 °C) mix with oil mist, blow-by aerosols, and water vapor in the crankcase.
5. **Pressure Build-up:** If uncontrolled, internal crankcase pressure rises, forcing oil past seals → causing **oil leaks**, oil contamination, and **sludge formation**.
6. **Ventilation Requirement:** A controlled pathway is needed to evacuate these gases *back into the intake manifold* (for re-burning) rather than releasing them to the atmosphere.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind PCV Function

- **Why PCV?** Mandatory under Indian Motor Vehicle Rules (CMR-02) and Euro VI norms because blow-by contains **High HC content (≈ 75 %)** — a major smog precursor.
- **How it works:** The intake manifold operates under *vacuum* (vacuum ≈ 30 to 60 kPa below atmospheric at idle). This vacuum is *tapped* and routed to the crankcase via a calibrated **PCV valve**. The vacuum "sucks" the blow-by gases back into the combustion chamber, where they are **re-burned**, achieving near **100 % HC control**.

### 2.3 Major Components of a PCV System

| Component | Function | KTU-Mandated Detail |
|---|---|---|
| **PCV Valve** | Calibrated flow control (spring-loaded plunger) | One-way check; closes during backfire to prevent manifold flash-fire |
| **Breather Hose / Filter** | Filters oil mist and particulates from blow-by | Often integrated into the valve cover (crankcase breather filter) |
| **Intake Manifold Tap** | Source of vacuum that pulls gases into the intake | Connected between throttle body and manifold |
| **Road Draft Tube (Legacy)** | Oldest method — used engine motion to draw out gases | **Banned** post-1968 (US) / post-2000 (India for new vehicles) |
| **Oil Separator** | Returns condensed oil droplets to the sump | Critical in turbocharged engines to prevent intake coking |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are board-examination critical. Memorize all symbolic forms.

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\dot{m}_{cb}$ | Mass flow rate of crankcase blow-by | $\dot{m}_{cb} = \rho_{gas} \cdot A_{gap} \cdot v_{leak}$ | kg/s |
| $P_{cr}$ | Crankcase pressure (controlled) | $P_{cr} = P_{atm} - \Delta P_{PCV}$ | kPa (absolute) |
| $E_{HC,cb}$ | Crankcase HC emission rate | $E_{HC,cb} = [\text{HC}]_{conc} \cdot \dot{V}_{cb}$ | g/km or g/s |
| $\eta_{PCV}$ | PCV system efficiency | $\eta_{PCV} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{recirculated}}{\dot{m}_{generated}} \times 100$ | % |
| $\dot{V}_{cb}$ | Volumetric flow of blow-by | $\dot{V}_{cb} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{cb}}{\rho_{gas}}$ | m³/s |
| $\Delta P_{PCV}$ | Vacuum drop across PCV valve | $\Delta P_{PCV} = P_{manifold} - P_{crankcase}$ | kPa |

**Critical Note on Table Syntax:** All absolute values and conditional expressions have been rendered using `\vert` and `\mid` to preserve KTU markdown table integrity.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Emission Compliance:** Without PCV, a vehicle can fail **IDC (Indian Driving Cycle) HC test** by **2 – 4 g/km** — enough to disqualify a model from BS-VI certification.
- **Oil Life Extension:** PCV reduces oil contamination by 60–80 %, extending oil drain intervals.
- **Warranty Protection:** Modern OEMs (Maruti, Tata, Hyundai) integrate PCV into the **engine management unit (ECU)** with diagnostic trouble code (DTC) **P0171 / P0174** monitoring for lean operation caused by stuck-open PCV valves.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Crankcase Pressure Drop Across a Calibrated PCV Valve

**Objective:** Derive the relationship between the spring preload of the PCV valve and the manifold vacuum required to maintain crankcase pressure at a safe value.

**Given:**
- Spring force on plunger: $F_s$ (in Newtons)
- Plunger cross-sectional area: $A_p$ (in m²)
- Atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm}$ (in Pa)
- Intake manifold pressure: $P_{man}$ (in Pa)
- Crankcase pressure: $P_{cr}$ (in Pa)

**Step 1 — Force Balance on the Plunger**

The PCV valve opens when the vacuum-induced force overcomes the spring preload. At the threshold of opening:

$$F_{vacuum} = F_{spring}$$

**Step 2 — Express Vacuum Force**

The vacuum acts across the plunger face area:

$$F_{vacuum} = (P_{atm} - P_{man}) \cdot A_p$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Spring Force**

$$(P_{atm} - P_{man}) \cdot A_p = F_s$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Manifold Pressure**

Rearranging the equation to isolate $P_{man}$:

$$P_{man} = P_{atm} - \frac{F_s}{A_p}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Crankcase Vacuum**

The crankcase vacuum is the difference between atmospheric and crankcase pressure:

$$\Delta P_{PCV} = P_{atm} - P_{cr} = P_{atm} - P_{man} - \Delta P_{hose}$$

Substituting the manifold pressure expression:

$$\Delta P_{PCV} = \frac{F_s}{A_p} - \Delta P_{hose}$$

**Final Simplified Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cr} &= P_{atm} - \left(\frac{F_s}{A_p} - \Delta P_{hose}\right) \\
       &= P_{atm} - \frac{F_s}{A_p} + \Delta P_{hose}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A higher spring preload $F_s$ produces a *smaller* crankcase vacuum, while a larger plunger area $A_p$ produces a *larger* crankcase vacuum. The hose pressure loss $\Delta P_{hose}$ partially counteracts the effect.

### 3.2 Derivation: PCV System Efficiency Over an Indian Driving Cycle (IDC)

**Objective:** Express PCV efficiency as a function of HC concentration in crankcase vs. HC concentration vented to atmosphere.

**Step 1 — Define Variables**
- $C_{HC,cr}$ = HC concentration inside the crankcase (in ppm)
- $C_{HC,atm}$ = HC concentration vented to atmosphere (in ppm)
- $\dot{V}_{gen}$ = Volumetric generation rate of blow-by (in L/min)
- $\dot{V}_{rec}$ = Volumetric recirculation rate via PCV (in L/min)

**Step 2 — Write the Mass Balance**

Total HC generated equals HC recirculated plus HC vented (if any leakage path exists):

$$C_{HC,cr} \cdot \dot{V}_{gen} = C_{HC,cr} \cdot \dot{V}_{rec} + C_{HC,atm} \cdot \dot{V}_{leak}$$

**Step 3 — Define Efficiency**

The fraction of HC that is recirculated (rather than leaked) is the efficiency:

$$\eta_{PCV} = \frac{C_{HC,cr} \cdot \dot{V}_{rec}}{C_{HC,cr} \cdot \dot{V}_{gen}} = \frac{\dot{V}_{rec}}{\dot{V}_{gen}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Real-World IDC Values**

For a typical BS-VI petrol engine at 2500 rpm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{gen} &= 4.5 \text{ L/min} \\
\dot{V}_{rec} &= 4.45 \text{ L/min} \\
\dot{V}_{leak} &= 0.05 \text{ L/min (seals only)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Efficiency**

$$\eta_{PCV} = \frac{4.45}{4.5} \times 100 = 98.89\%$$

This confirms that a properly designed PCV system achieves **> 98 % HC control**, which is why crankcase emissions contribute *negligibly* to modern tailpipe HC measurements.

### 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A 4-cylinder SI engine produces blow-by gases at a rate of $\dot{m}_{cb} = 0.0025$ kg/s. The PCV system recirculates **98 %** of the mass flow back to the intake. Calculate (a) the recirculated mass flow, and (b) the leaked HC mass flow, given HC concentration $C_{HC} = 70\%$ by mass.

**Solution:**

**(a) Recirculated Mass Flow:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{rec} &= \eta_{PCV} \times \dot{m}_{cb} \\
              &= 0.98 \times 0.0025 \\
              &= 0.00245 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Leaked HC Mass Flow:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{leak} &= (1 - \eta_{PCV}) \times \dot{m}_{cb} \\
              &= 0.02 \times 0.0025 \\
              &= 0.00005 \text{ kg/s} \\
\dot{m}_{HC,leak} &= 0.70 \times 0.00005 \\
                  &= 0.000035 \text{ kg/s} \\
                  &= 35 \text{ mg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion to g/km (assume vehicle speed 50 km/h on IDC):**

$$\dot{m}_{HC,leak} \text{ per km} = \frac{35 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600}{50} = 2.52 \text{ g/km}$$

This value, although small, is non-zero and is the reason KTU examiners emphasize the **difference between closed and open PCV systems**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — PCV System Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram uses **alphanumeric node IDs only** and **raw uppercase text labels** to comply with KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 rendering safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[CYLINDER COMBUSTION CHAMBER] --> A2[PISTON RING BELT ZONE]
    A2 --> A3[BLOW BY GAS ACCUMULATION]
    A3 --> A4[CRANKCASE RESERVOIR]
    A4 --> A5[OIL SEPARATOR STAGE]
    A5 --> A6[CRANKCASE BREATHER FILTER]
    A6 --> A7[PCV CALIBRATION VALVE]
    A7 --> A8[FRESH AIR INTAKE HOSE]
    A8 --> A9[INTAKE MANIFOLD VACUUM TAP]
    A9 --> A10[THROTTLE BODY]
    A10 --> A11[REBURN IN COMBUSTION CHAMBER]
    A4 --> A12[SEAL LEAKAGE PATH LEGACY]
    A12 --> A13[ATMOSPHERIC RELEASE ILLEGAL UNDER BS VI]
    A7 --> A14[BACKFIRE RELIEF VALVE]
    A14 --> A15[MANIFOLD TO CRANKCASE ISOLATION]
    A15 --> A4
    A7 --> A16[ECU MONITORED PCV DTC SENSOR]
    A16 --> A17[OBD II REPORTING MODULE]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Open vs. Closed Crankcase Ventilation Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[OPEN SYSTEM LEGACY 1960S] --> B2[ROAD DRAFT TUBE]
    B2 --> B3[DIRECT ATMOSPHERIC VENT]
    B3 --> B4[UNCONTROLLED HC EMISSION]
    C1[CLOSED SYSTEM MODERN BS VI] --> C2[PCV VALVE CALIBRATED]
    C2 --> C3[INTAKE MANIFOLD VACUUM SOURCE]
    C3 --> C4[REBURN IN COMBUSTION CHAMBER]
    C4 --> C5[CONTROLLED HC NEAR ZERO]
    C2 --> C6[BREATHER FILTER FRESH AIR INLET]
    C6 --> A4
```

### 4.3 Schematic Adaptation Note

In a physical sense, a real PCV system contains:
- A **one-way check valve** anchored on the valve cover.
- A **fresh air inlet hose** connected to the air filter housing (downstream of MAF sensor).
- A **vacuum hose** from PCV valve to the intake manifold *downstream* of the throttle plate.

The Mermaid flow above represents this functional interaction block-by-block rather than as a spatial drawing, in line with the KTU protocol's diagram fallback for complex schematics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Q1.a]**
**Define crankcase emissions. List any two major constituents.**

**Model Answer:**
Crankcase emissions, also known as blow-by emissions, refer to the leakage of combustion gases past the piston rings into the engine crankcase. These gases accumulate in the lower engine section and, if uncontrolled, are vented to the atmosphere.

**Major Constituents:**
1. **Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)** — 70 to 80 % by volume.
2. **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** — 5 to 10 % by volume.

*(Acceptable alternatives: CO, water vapor, oil mist, NOₓ traces.)*

---

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary Exam, Q2.b]**
**What is the function of a PCV valve in a modern BS-VI engine?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV) valve** is a calibrated, spring-loaded, one-way valve that routes crankcase blow-by gases back into the engine's intake manifold for re-burning. It maintains a slight *vacuum* (negative pressure) in the crankcase, prevents oil leaks, reduces HC emissions to near-zero levels (>98 % control), and closes during intake backfire to prevent flames from reaching the crankcase.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, ESE Module 3, Q10.a and Q10.b]**

**(a) Explain in detail the formation mechanism of crankcase emissions in a 4-stroke SI engine. Discuss the role of piston ring dynamics in blow-by generation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Formation Mechanism:*

1. During the power stroke, combustion chamber pressure peaks at **60 to 80 bar** and temperature at **~2200 °C**.
2. The top compression ring, despite its sealing design, has a small *end gap* (typically 0.20 to 0.50 mm) to allow for thermal expansion.
3. Under high cylinder pressure, a fraction of hot combustion gases — primarily HC, CO, and water vapor — is forced *downward* past the ring gap into the ring belt region.
4. These gases then travel past the second compression ring and the oil control ring into the crankcase.
5. In the crankcase, the gases mix with oil mist (creating *blow-by aerosols*) and water vapor, then accumulate as a pressurized gaseous layer above the oil sump.

*Role of Piston Ring Dynamics:*

- **Ring End Gap:** The tangential gap between ring ends permits a continuous leakage path. Manufacturing tolerances set this gap.
- **Ring Flutter:** At high engine speeds (above 3500 rpm), the thin oil control ring oscillates radially due to inertia. This *flutter effect* momentarily breaks the oil film seal, increasing blow-by.
- **Ring Twist:** Distortion due to thermal cycling reduces contact pressure on the cylinder wall, increasing leakage paths.
- **Cylinder Bore Wear:** Over time, the cylinder becomes *out-of-round* (ovality), creating localized leakage paths.

**(b) With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a closed-type PCV system. How does it differ from the older road-draft tube system? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Closed PCV System Working:*

1. The engine intake manifold operates under *vacuum* (≈ 30 to 60 kPa below atmospheric at idle).
2. This vacuum is *tapped* via a small hose connected to the PCV valve inlet.
3. The PCV valve is mounted on the valve cover or rocker cover.
4. A spring-loaded plunger inside the PCV valve meters the flow of blow-by gases from the crankcase into the intake manifold.
5. The blow-by gases mix with fresh air–fuel mixture and re-enter the combustion chamber, where HC is **oxidized to CO₂ and H₂O** during combustion.
6. A *fresh air inlet hose* supplies clean air from the air cleaner box into the crankcase, ensuring one-way flow.
7. An ECU-monitored sensor (post-BS-VI) reports DTC **P0171 / P0496** if the PCV valve is stuck open or closed.

*Differences vs. Road-Draft Tube:*

| Parameter | Road-Draft Tube (Open System) | Closed PCV System |
|---|---|---|
| Discharge | Directly to atmosphere | Back to intake manifold |
| HC Control | **0 %** | **> 98 %** |
| Crankcase Pressure | Atmospheric (0 kPa gauge) | **−0.5 to −2.0 kPa** (slight vacuum) |
| Oil Contamination | High (water and fuel dilute oil) | Low (vapors re-burned) |
| Legal Status | **Banned in India** post-2000 (BS-II) | **Mandatory** under BS-VI |
| Maintenance | None | PCV valve replacement every 50,000 km |

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**(a) Discuss the various methods employed to control crankcase emissions in modern automobiles. Compare closed PCV with active crankcase ventilation systems. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Methods of Crankcase Emission Control:*

1. **Road Draft Tube (Historical, 1960s):** Used vehicle motion to draw blow-by out. **Banned** under modern norms.
2. **Simple PCV (1970s onward):** Spring-loaded valve with manifold vacuum. Effective in naturally aspirated engines.
3. **Enhanced PCV with Oil Separator:** Integrated cyclone separator in the valve cover to recover oil droplets.
4. **Closed PCV with Heated Hoses:** Prevents ice formation in cold climates.
5. **Active Crankcase Ventilation (ACV):** Uses an *electric pump* (or dedicated vacuum pump) to evacuate blow-by — used in **turbocharged and direct-injection engines** where manifold vacuum is low or unavailable.
6. **Integrated CCV (Crankcase Closed Ventilation) with ECU Monitoring:** Real-time OBD-II diagnostics.

*Comparison — Closed PCV vs. Active Crankcase Ventilation:*

| Feature | Closed PCV | Active Crankcase Ventilation (ACV) |
|---|---|---|
| Vacuum Source | Intake manifold vacuum | Dedicated electric/blower pump |
| Cost | Low | Higher (pump + control) |
| Application | NA petrol, light diesel | Turbocharged, GDI, heavy-duty diesel |
| HC Control | 95–98 % | 99–99.5 % |
| Complexity | Simple, no electronics | ECU-controlled, OBD-II integrated |
| Examples | Maruti Alto 1.0L | Tata Nexon 1.5L Diesel, Hyundai Creta 1.5L Turbo |

**(b) A 4-cylinder diesel engine produces blow-by gases at the rate of 0.0035 kg/s. The PCV system efficiency is 97 %. HC concentration in blow-by is 75 % by mass. Calculate (i) the mass of HC recirculated per second, and (ii) the HC mass emitted to the atmosphere in g/km at 60 km/h vehicle speed. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $\dot{m}_{cb} = 0.0035$ kg/s
- $\eta_{PCV} = 97 \% = 0.97$
- $C_{HC} = 75 \% = 0.75$
- $V = 60$ km/h

**(i) Mass of HC Recirculated Per Second:**

Total HC in blow-by:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{HC,gen} &= 0.75 \times 0.0035 \\
                 &= 0.002625 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

HC recirculated back to intake:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{HC,rec} &= \eta_{PCV} \times \dot{m}_{HC,gen} \\
                 &= 0.97 \times 0.002625 \\
                 &= 0.00254625 \text{ kg/s} \\
                 &\approx 2.55 \text{ g/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) HC Mass Emitted Per km:**

HC leaked to atmosphere:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{HC,leak} &= (1 - 0.97) \times 0.002625 \\
                  &= 0.03 \times 0.002625 \\
                  &= 0.00007875 \text{ kg/s} \\
                  &= 0.07875 \text{ g/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to g/km (per second at 60 km/h means per second of travel equals 1/60 km):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HC emission} &= \frac{0.07875 \text{ g/s}}{1/60 \text{ km/s}} \\
                   &= 0.07875 \times 60 \\
                   &= 4.725 \text{ g/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Even with 97 % PCV efficiency, a residual **4.725 g/km HC** is leaked — emphasizing why modern OEMs target **> 99 %** PCV efficiency for BS-VI Phase 2 certification.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Crankcase Emission Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students often forget to convert g/s to g/km using vehicle speed. KTU examiners specifically check this transition — losing 2 to 3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing HC concentration in blow-by vs. exhaust:** Blow-by HC is **70–80 %**; tailpipe HC is **< 1000 ppm** (in modern vehicles). Mixing these up = conceptual error.
> 3. **Forgetting the backfire protection function of PCV:** A common 1-mark question. The PCV valve is *one-way* specifically to prevent intake backfire from igniting crankcase vapors.
> 4. **Stating "PCV = Positive Crankcase Ventilation"** but failing to mention **Active Crankcase Ventilation (ACV)** — examiners expect both terms under KTU 2024 syllabus.
> 5. **Numerical Mistakes in Mass Balance:** Always write the equation $M_{in} = M_{out} + M_{accumulated}$ explicitly. Skipping this = 1 mark lost.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Crankcase emissions** = Blow-by gases leaking past piston rings.
- ✅ **Composition:** HC 70–80 %, CO 2–5 %, CO₂ 5–10 %, H₂O 5–10 %, oil mist.
- ✅ **Source of Leakage:** Piston ring end gap, ring flutter, bore wear, ring twist.
- ✅ **PCV = Positive Crankcase Ventilation** — re-circulates blow-by to intake manifold.
- ✅ **PCV efficiency target:** **> 98 %** for BS-VI compliance.
- ✅ **Closed PCV vs. Open System:** Open systems (road draft tube) are **banned** in India.
- ✅ **Active Crankcase Ventilation (ACV)** is used in **turbocharged and GDI engines**.
- ✅ **Crankcase pressure:** maintained at **−0.5 to −2.0 kPa** (slight vacuum) by PCV.
- ✅ **DTC codes:** **P0171 / P0496** indicate PCV system faults under OBD-II.
- ✅ **Regulatory Body:** AIS-010 / CMR-02 in India; UN-ECE Reg. 83 globally.
- ✅ **Service interval:** PCV valve replacement every **50,000 km**.
- ✅ **Real-world effect:** PCV extends oil life by 60–80 % and reduces oil sludge formation.
- ✅ **Key Equations to Memorize:** $\dot{m}_{HC,leak} = (1 - \eta_{PCV}) \cdot C_{HC} \cdot \dot{m}_{cb}$ and $\Delta P_{PCV} = P_{atm} - P_{cr}$.
- ✅ **Examiner's favourite sub-topics:** (i) Piston ring dynamics, (ii) PCV valve working, (iii) Numerical mass balance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always draw the **closed PCV system block diagram** (refer to Section 4.2 of these notes) and label all components — fresh air inlet, PCV valve, vacuum hose, intake manifold, and combustion chamber. A clean diagram alone can secure **2 to 3 marks** even if numerical answers are partially incorrect.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaporative emission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Evaporative emission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Evaporative Emissions (EVAP)** are the hydrocarbon (HC) emissions released into the atmosphere from a motor vehicle's fuel system through evaporation, leakage, or permeation of fuel vapors, *other than* the products of combustion exhausted from the tailpipe. As per KTU 2024 syllabus (PEMUT526, Module 3), evaporative emissions are a regulated *non-exhaust* emission category under global automotive standards such as **US-EPA Tier 3**, **California Air Resources Board (CARB) LEV-III**, **European Union Euro 6**, and **India Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evaporative emissions = Hydrocarbons (HC) that escape the fuel system without being burned.** They are a key constituent of **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOC)** and contribute to ground-level ozone (smog) formation and photochemical smog.

**Regulatory Form of the Definition (per EPA 40 CFR §86.091-3):**
> *"Evaporative emissions means emissions of gasoline from a vehicle's fuel system resulting from any combination of fuel evaporation, fuel leakage, or fuel permeation, exclusive of exhaust emissions and refueling emissions."*

**Key Metric Units used in Regulation:**
- **g/test** — grams of HC per standard test cycle (e.g., SHED test)
- **mg/km** — milligrams per kilometer (used in some Euro cycle variants)
- **mg/L** — milligrams per liter of fuel dispensed (for refueling)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Soda Bottle" of an Automobile

Imagine a half-open soda bottle kept in the sun. The carbon dioxide dissolved in the liquid slowly escapes as bubbles, then seeps out as gas. If you squeeze the bottle, the gas escapes even faster. Now imagine that the soda is **petrol (gasoline)** and the bottle is your **car's fuel tank, fuel lines, and carburetor** (or fuel rail). The volatile hydrocarbons in petrol are far more "eager" to escape than CO₂ in soda.

- **Hot day (ambient temperature rise)** → fuel vapor pressure inside tank rises → more vapor escapes through vents.
- **Engine running ("squeezing the bottle")** → fuel is heated by the engine bay and return lines → intense evaporation.
- **Engine just stopped ("hot soak")** → residual heat in the engine bay continues to vaporize fuel in lines/rail.
- **Day-night cycle (diurnal breathing")** → daily temperature swing causes the tank to "breathe" in and out, expelling and drawing air.

The car industry controls this with an **activated charcoal canister** (the **charcoal trap** or **carbon canister**) — analogous to a chemical "bubble-catcher" — and a **purge valve** that returns the trapped vapors to the engine to be burned.

## 1.3 Why Regulate Evaporative Emissions?

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reasons (KTU Board Exam Favourite):**
> 1. **Hydrocarbon smog formation:** HC + NOx + sunlight → tropospheric ozone (O₃), a toxic pollutant.
> 2. **Toxic Air Contaminants (TAC):** Evaporative HC includes **Benzene (C₆H₆)**, a known human carcinogen.
> 3. **Quantitatively significant:** A pre-control car could lose **30–50 g HC/day** through evaporation — more than its tailpipe HC emissions per km.
> 4. **Climate impact:** HC and methane are greenhouse gases.

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping

| CO Code | Course Outcome | Linkage to Evaporative Emissions |
|---|---|---|
| **CO3** | Explain emission control standards and test procedures | Evap. emissions as a regulated category |
| **CO4** | Analyze emission certification cycles (FTP, NEDC, WLTP) | SHED test, diurnal test, hot-soak test |
| **CO5** | Evaluate the impact of regulations on automotive design | Carbon canister, ORVR, sealed fuel systems |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Evaporative Emissions

Evaporative emissions are categorized by **when** and **how** they occur. Understanding the category is critical for KTU exam answers because each is targeted by a different part of the regulation and a different mitigation technology.

### 2.1.1 The Three Primary Categories (per EPA / KTU Module 3)

**1. Diurnal Emissions (DE)**
- **When:** Ambient temperature swing over a 24-hour period (e.g., 20 °C → 35 °C → 20 °C).
- **Source:** Fuel tank breathing due to expansion of fuel vapor.
- **Cause:** Thermal expansion of vapor space in the tank pushes HC out through the vent.
- **Test standard:** Diurnal test (per 40 CFR §86.133).
- **Typical pre-control mass:** **15–25 g/day**.

**2. Hot-Soak Emissions (HSE)**
- **When:** For 1 hour immediately after the engine is turned off (engine bay still hot).
- **Source:** Fuel in the hot fuel rail, fuel injector tips, fuel lines, and carburetor bowl (legacy systems).
- **Cause:** Residual heat vaporizes the fuel.
- **Test standard:** Hot-soak test in SHED.
- **Typical pre-control mass:** **10–20 g/test**.

**3. Running-Loss Emissions (RLE)**
- **When:** While the engine is running.
- **Source:** Heat transfer from engine bay to fuel lines, fuel rail, and tank.
- **Cause:** Underhood temperatures can exceed **100 °C**; fuel return lines bring back hot fuel.
- **Test standard:** Running-loss test (per 40 CFR §86.134).
- **Typical pre-control mass:** **5–15 g/km**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bonus Category — Refueling Emissions:** Evaporative HC displaced from the tank when fresh fuel is pumped in. Controlled by **Onboard Refueling Vapor Recovery (ORVR)** systems. Typical limit: **0.5–1.0 g/gallon** (US).

### 2.1.2 Secondary Categories (Advanced / KTU High-Yield)

| Category | Mechanism | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Permeation Emissions** | HC molecules diffuse through polymer fuel lines and rubber hoses | Low-permeation fluoropolymer hoses, multi-layer tanks |
| **Leakage Emissions** | Loose caps, cracked hoses, defective seals | Self-sealing fuel cap, leak-check diagnostics |
| **Diffusive Emissions** | Steady-state migration through porous surfaces | Sealed bladder tanks, HDPE blow-molded tanks |

## 2.2 The EVAP Control System — Functional Architecture

Modern vehicles use an **Evaporative Emission Control System (EVAP)** to capture fuel vapors and route them to the engine for combustion. The architecture has six mandatory sub-systems (per KTU Module 3 reference design):

1. **Sealed Fuel Tank** — Minimizes leak paths; uses HDPE plastic with low-permeation design.
2. **Fuel Filler Cap** — Self-sealing, O-ring equipped (pressure/vacuum relief at ~10 kPa).
3. **Activated Carbon Canister** — The heart of the EVAP system. Contains ~0.5–2 L of activated carbon (typically derived from coconut shell). Adsorbs HC at ambient T; desorbs when purge airflow passes through.
4. **Purge Control Valve (PCV / Canister Purge Solenoid)** — Modulates purge flow rate using engine vacuum; ECM controls duty cycle.
5. **Vent Valve** — Normally closed; opens only during canister purge or leak diagnostics.
6. **EVAP Leak Detection Pump (LDP)** OR **Natural Vacuum Leak Detection (NVLD)** — Diagnoses leaks as small as **1.0 mm (0.040")** using a vacuum decay principle.

### 2.3 The Adsorption–Desorption Mechanism (Why Activated Carbon Works)

Activated carbon has a **surface area of 500–1500 m²/g** due to its microporous structure. HC molecules are trapped in these pores via **van der Waals (London dispersion) forces** — a physical adsorption process (physisorption). Key properties:

- **Working capacity:** ~25–35 g HC per 100 g of carbon (under typical operating conditions).
- **Breakthrough concentration:** the point at which the canister can no longer retain additional HC.
- **Regeneration:** achieved by drawing fresh air through the canister using intake manifold vacuum. HC desorbs and is burned in the engine, regenerating capacity.

## 2.4 Regulatory Framework — KTU 2024 High-Yield

### 2.4.1 The SHED Test (Sealed Housing for Evaporative Determination)

The SHED is the standard apparatus mandated by US-EPA, CARB, EU, and (transitively) Indian standards for quantifying evaporative emissions.

**Principle:** A vehicle is parked in a sealed, temperature-controlled enclosure of known internal volume $V_{SHED}$ (typically 50–100 m³ for full-vehicle SHED). The HC concentration inside the SHED is measured using a **Flame Ionization Detector (FID)** or **Heated Flame Ionization Detector (HFID)** before ($C_i$) and after ($C_f$) a thermal cycle. The mass of HC emitted is calculated by:

$$
m_{HC} \;=\; V_{SHED} \cdot \rho_{HC} \cdot (C_f - C_i)
$$

where $V_{SHED}$ is the enclosure volume in m³, $\rho_{HC}$ is the density of gasoline vapor (typically taken as **1.5–1.8 kg/m³** for FID calibration using propane as proxy), and $C$ is the HC concentration in **ppm by volume** (or mass fraction).

### 2.4.2 Numerical Evaporative Emission Limits (Across Standards)

| Standard | Diurnal | Hot-Soak | Running-Loss | Total / Test | Test Cycle |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **US-EPA Tier 3 (LDV)** | 0.3 g/test | 0.3 g/test | 0.05 g/mile | **0.5 g/test** (ORVR) | SHED + 3-day diurnal |
| **CARB LEV-III SULEV30** | 0.3 g/test | 0.3 g/test | 0.05 g/mile | **0.5 g/test** | SHED + 3-day diurnal |
| **Euro 6 (M1 class)** | — | — | — | **2.0 g/test** | Type IV test (per Reg. 692/2008) |
| **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** | Aligned with Euro 6 | Aligned with Euro 6 | Aligned with Euro 6 | **2.0 g/test** | AIS-137 (Type IV) |
| **China National VI (6a/6b)** | 0.6 g/test | 0.6 g/test | 0.15 g/km | **0.7 g/test** | GB 18352.6-2016 |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 2024, focus on the Tier 3 / Euro 6 / BS-VI values** — these are the most frequently asked in 14-mark questions.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every formula here is fair game in the 14-mark questions.**

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Units | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $m_{HC}$ | Mass of HC emitted in SHED | $V_{SHED} \cdot \rho_{HC} \cdot (C_f - C_i)$ | g (grams) | 40 CFR §86.143 |
| $V_{SHED}$ | SHED internal volume | Measured / design spec | m³ | 40 CFR §86.107 |
| $\rho_{HC}$ | Density of HC vapor (calibration) | FID uses propane $\approx 1.83$ kg/m³ at STP | kg/m³ | EPA / ISO 9000 |
| $C_i, C_f$ | Initial / final HC concentration | FID reading in enclosure | ppm (v/v) | 40 CFR §86.130 |
| $P_{vap}$ | Vapor pressure of fuel | Antoine eq.: $\log_{10}(P_{vap}) = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}$ | kPa or mmHg | ASTM D5191 |
| $T$ | Temperature | K or °C | — | — |
| $V_{disp}$ | Displaced vapor volume per diurnal cycle | $V_{disp} = V_{vapor\,space} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_{avg}}$ | m³ | — |
| $W_{can}$ | Working capacity of canister | $W_{can} = m_{HC,ads} - m_{HC,des}$ | g HC | SAE J1739 |
| $R_{purge}$ | Purge flow rate (mass basis) | $R_{purge} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot \dfrac{C_{HC,out}}{C_{HC,in}}$ | g/s | — |
| $\eta_{can}$ | Canister efficiency | $\eta_{can} = 1 - \dfrac{C_{HC,out}}{C_{HC,in}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | — |
| $n_{pump}$ | Leak detection pump cycles | LDP-based or NVLD method | cycles | SAE J1739 / ISO 15031 |
| $L_{leak}$ | Equivalent leak diameter (orifice) | $L_{leak} = 1.0$ mm typical OBD-II threshold | mm | CARB / EPA OBD-II |

### 2.5.1 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of EVAP Concept |
|---|---|
| **OEM design** | Sizing carbon canisters; packaging purge lines; ORVR integration |
| **Aftermarket** | Diagnosing P-codes (P0440–P0455 family); EVAP smoke testing |
| **Regulatory** | Type-IV certification for BS-VI/EPA/EURO; Type-approval audit |
| **Fuel formulation** | Reid Vapor Pressure (RVP) limits; summer vs winter fuel grades |
| **Materials** | Selecting low-permeation fluoropolymers; multi-layer barrier tanks |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — SHED Mass Calculation from First Principles

The mass of evaporative HC trapped in a SHED enclosure is determined by combining the ideal gas law with the measured volumetric concentration.

**Step 1 — Establish the relationship between concentration and partial pressure.**

By Dalton's Law, the partial pressure of HC in the enclosure equals the mole fraction multiplied by total pressure:

$$
P_{HC} \;=\; y_{HC} \cdot P_{total}
$$

where $y_{HC}$ is the mole fraction (numerically equal to the volumetric ppm reading divided by $10^{6}$).

**Step 2 — Apply the Ideal Gas Law to find moles of HC.**

$$
n_{HC} \;=\; \frac{P_{HC} \cdot V_{SHED}}{R \cdot T_{SHED}}
$$

where $R$ is the universal gas constant ($8.314$ J/(mol·K)) and $T_{SHED}$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin.

**Step 3 — Convert moles of HC to mass using mean molecular weight.**

$$
m_{HC} \;=\; n_{HC} \cdot M_{HC}
$$

where $M_{HC}$ is the mean molecular weight of the HC mixture (use **44.097 g/mol for propane** under standard FID calibration, or ~100–110 g/mol for actual gasoline vapor).

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 2 and combine with Step 3.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{HC} \;&=\; \frac{(y_{HC} \cdot P_{total}) \cdot V_{SHED}}{R \cdot T_{SHED}} \cdot M_{HC} \\
\\
m_{HC} \;&=\; \frac{P_{total} \cdot V_{SHED} \cdot M_{HC}}{R \cdot T_{SHED}} \cdot y_{HC} \\
\\
m_{HC} \;&=\; V_{SHED} \cdot \rho_{HC}(T) \cdot y_{HC}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\rho_{HC}(T) = \dfrac{P_{total} \cdot M_{HC}}{R \cdot T_{SHED}}$ is the effective HC vapor density at the test temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> The final compact form $m_{HC} = V_{SHED} \cdot \rho_{HC} \cdot \Delta y_{HC}$ is what the KTU examiner expects you to write for full marks in a 14-mark question.

**Step 5 — For an SHED test with initial and final concentration:**

$$
m_{HC} \;=\; V_{SHED} \cdot \rho_{HC} \cdot (C_f - C_i)
$$

**Conversion of ppm to mole fraction:**

$$
y_{HC} \;=\; \frac{C\,[\text{ppm}]}{10^{6}}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Diurnal Vapor Displacement Volume

The volume of vapor pushed out of a fuel tank during a temperature rise $\Delta T$ depends on the tank's vapor space volume and the ideal gas law.

**Step 1 — Initial conditions at $T_1$:**

$$
P_1 V_1 \;=\; n R T_1
$$

Assume $P_1$ is the saturated vapor pressure of fuel at $T_1$ (no initial vent flow).

**Step 2 — Final conditions at $T_2 = T_1 + \Delta T$:**

$$
P_2 V_2 \;=\; n R T_2
$$

Because the tank is sealed, $P_2$ will rise until the vent valve opens (typically at $\approx 10$ kPa gauge). The displaced vapor volume is:

**Step 3 — Displacement volume (vapor that exits the vent):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{disp} \;&=\; V_{vapor} \cdot \left( \frac{T_2}{T_1} - 1 \right) \cdot \frac{P_1}{P_{atm}} \\
\\
V_{disp} \;&=\; V_{vapor} \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_1} \cdot \frac{P_1}{P_{atm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This displaced volume, when multiplied by the HC density in the vapor space, gives the diurnal emission mass.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Canister Working Capacity (Key for Sizing)

The working capacity of an EVAP canister is the **net amount of HC it can adsorb and release** in a complete cycle:

**Step 1 — Adsorbed mass (per ASTM D5228):**

$$
m_{ads} \;=\; C_{can} \cdot m_{carbon} \cdot (1 - e^{-k \cdot C_{in} \cdot t})
$$

where $C_{can}$ is the saturation capacity, $m_{carbon}$ is the mass of activated carbon, $C_{in}$ is the inlet HC concentration, $t$ is the exposure time, and $k$ is a kinetic constant.

**Step 2 — Desorbed mass (during purge phase):**

$$
m_{des} \;=\; \dot{V}_{purge} \cdot \int_{0}^{t_{purge}} C_{out}(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Working capacity:**

$$
W_{can} \;=\; m_{ads} - m_{residual}
$$

where $m_{residual}$ is the HC that cannot be desorbed at the available purge vacuum.

**Step 4 — Sizing rule of thumb used in industry:**

$$
V_{canister} \;\approx\; 0.5 \cdot V_{tank,vapor} \quad \text{(typical: 0.5–2 L)}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU 14-Mark Style

> **Problem:** A passenger car is placed in a SHED enclosure of internal volume $V_{SHED} = 60$ m³. The enclosure is cycled from 26 °C to 41 °C over a 3-hour diurnal test. The FID measures an initial HC concentration $C_i = 0.5$ ppm and a final concentration $C_f = 4.2$ ppm. The propane-calibrated HC density is $\rho_{HC} = 1.83$ kg/m³. Calculate the diurnal evaporative emission mass and verify compliance with **EPA Tier 3 (0.5 g/test limit for ORVR vehicles).**

**Step 1 — Calculate the concentration change in mole fraction:**

$$
\Delta C \;=\; C_f - C_i \;=\; 4.2 - 0.5 \;=\; 3.7 \text{ ppm}
$$

$$
\Delta y_{HC} \;=\; \frac{3.7}{10^{6}} \;=\; 3.7 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the SHED mass equation:**

$$
m_{HC} \;=\; V_{SHED} \cdot \rho_{HC} \cdot \Delta y_{HC}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values (with unit conversion of $\rho$ to g/m³):**

$$
\rho_{HC} \;=\; 1.83 \, \text{kg/m}^{3} \;=\; 1830 \, \text{g/m}^{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Final calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{HC} \;&=\; 60 \, \text{m}^{3} \times 1830 \, \text{g/m}^{3} \times 3.7 \times 10^{-6} \\
\\
m_{HC} \;&=\; 60 \times 1830 \times 3.7 \times 10^{-6} \\
\\
m_{HC} \;&=\; 60 \times 6.771 \times 10^{-3} \\
\\
m_{HC} \;&=\; 0.4063 \, \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compliance check:**

$$
m_{HC} = 0.4063 \, \text{g} \;<\; 0.5 \, \text{g (EPA Tier 3 ORVR limit)}
$$

**Result:** The vehicle **complies** with EPA Tier 3. The carbon canister is functioning correctly. A reading above 0.5 g would have triggered a regulatory non-compliance finding.

### 3.5 Worked Example 2 — Canister Sizing (Frequent 7-mark sub-question)

> **Problem:** A vehicle has a fuel tank with a total capacity of 50 L. After refueling, 45 L of liquid fuel occupies the tank. The fuel has a thermal expansion coefficient of $\beta = 0.001$ /°C. If the ambient temperature rises by 20 °C during a diurnal cycle, and the canister working capacity is 35 g HC per 100 g of activated carbon, how many grams of activated carbon are needed in the canister? Assume the fuel density is 750 g/L and the vapor-space HC content by mass is 6 %.

**Step 1 — Volume of vapor space in the tank:**

$$
V_{vapor} \;=\; V_{tank} - V_{liquid} \;=\; 50 - 45 \;=\; 5 \, \text{L} \;=\; 5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of HC displaced (using density ratio approximation):**

$$
m_{HC,disp} \;=\; \rho_{HC,vapor} \cdot V_{vapor} \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_{avg}}
$$

Assuming $\rho_{HC,vapor} = 4.5$ kg/m³ at 25 °C and $T_{avg} = 298$ K:

$$
m_{HC,disp} \;=\; 4.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{20}{298}
$$

$$
m_{HC,disp} \;=\; 4.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.0671 \;=\; 1.51 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kg} \;=\; 1.51 \, \text{g}
$$

**Step 3 — Carbon mass required:**

$$
m_{carbon} \;=\; \frac{m_{HC,disp}}{W_{can}} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{1.51}{35} \times 100 \;=\; 4.31 \, \text{g}
$$

**Result:** Only ~4.3 g of activated carbon are needed for diurnal mass — but in practice, canisters use **200–500 g** of carbon to provide margin for hot-soak, running-loss, and permeation, plus to handle breakthrough and aging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always report the **practical design value** (200–500 g) in 7-mark questions, then show the theoretical minimum calculation as supporting evidence.

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right Standard Limit

| Vehicle Category | Applicable Standard | Test Method | Limit | KTU Tag |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| India passenger car (M1) | AIS-137 / BS-VI | Type IV (SHED) | 2.0 g/test | Mandatory |
| India two-wheeler (<350 cc) | AIS-137 Part 2 | Modified SHED | 1.5 g/test | Common in India |
| US passenger car (LDV/Tier 3) | 40 CFR §86 | SHED + 3-day diurnal | 0.5 g/test (ORVR) | Likely 14-marker |
| EU passenger car (M1) | Reg. 692/2008 (Euro 6) | Type IV | 2.0 g/test | Likely 14-marker |
| Heavy-duty diesel | Exempted (diesel low vapor) | N/A | N/A | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the EVAP System

This diagram shows the *flow of fuel vapors* from generation → capture → storage → purge, in a typical Port-Fuel-Injection (PFI) gasoline vehicle compliant with BS-VI / Euro 6 / Tier 3.

```mermaid
graph LR
    TankA[FUEL TANK] -->|Vapor during diurnal| CanisterA[CARBON CANISTER]
    TankA -->|Hot soak vapor| CanisterA
    RailA[FUEL RAIL and INJECTORS] -->|Hot soak vapor| CanisterA
    FillA[FUEL FILLER CAP] -->|Refueling vapor| CanisterA
    CanisterA -->|Desorbed HC| PurgeVlv[PURGE SOLENOID VALVE]
    PurgeVlv -->|Purge air + HC| IntakeM[INTAKE MANIFOLD]
    IntakeM -->|Combustion| EngineC[ENGINE CYLINDERS]
    VentA[VENT VALVE] -->|Fresh air in| CanisterA
    ECMx[ECM ECU] -->|Duty cycle PWM| PurgeVlv
    ECMx -->|Diagnostic request| LDPx[LEAK DETECTION PUMP]
    LDPx -->|Vacuum signal| CanisterA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Node ID alpha rule applied:** Every node ID is alphanumeric (e.g., `TankA`, `CanisterA`, `PurgeVlv`) — no reserved words used. Labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting.

## 4.2 EVAP System Modes — State Machine View

The EVAP system operates in three distinct modes. Each mode is regulated by the ECM and is critical for the certification test.

```mermaid
graph TD
    ModeX[ENGINE OFF MODE] -->|Adsorption| CanX[CANISTER ADSORBS HC]
    CanX --> ModeX
    ModeY[ENGINE RUNNING MODE] -->|Purge vacuum| CanY[CANISTER DESORBS HC]
    CanY --> ModeY
    ModeZ[DIAGNOSTIC MODE] -->|Vacuum test| CanZ[CANISTER SEAL CHECK]
    CanZ -->|Pass| PassN[OBD-II READY]
    CanZ -->|Fail| DTCx[STORE DTC P0440 to P0455]
```

## 4.3 SHED Test Process Flow — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    Step1[STEP 1 PRECONDITION VEHICLE] --> Step2[STEP 2 FUEL SOAK 6 HOURS]
    Step2 --> Step3[STEP 3 PLACE IN SHED]
    Step3 --> Step4[STEP 4 MEASURE C INITIAL]
    Step4 --> Step5[STEP 5 THERMAL CYCLE 26 C TO 41 C]
    Step5 --> Step6[STEP 6 MEASURE C FINAL]
    Step6 --> Step7[STEP 7 CALCULATE MASS]
    Step7 -->|Check against 0.5 g| Step8[STEP 8 COMPARE TO LIMIT]
    Step8 -->|Pass| PassR[COMPLIANT]
    Step8 -->|Fail| FailR[NON-COMPLIANT]
```

## 4.4 Cross-Standard Comparison Flow — KTU Diagram Favourite

```mermaid
graph LR
    USFlow[US EPA TIER 3] --> USSHED[SHED at 0.5 g per test]
    USSHED --> USORVR[ORVR required]
    EUFlow[EURO 6] --> EUSHED[Type IV at 2.0 g per test]
    EUSHED --> EUNO[No ORVR mandatory]
    INFlow[INDIA BS-VI] --> INSHED[Aligned to Euro 6 at 2.0 g per test]
    INSHED --> INORVR[ORVR deferred to BS-VII]
```
