# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Q1.a]**

**Q1. Define evaporative emissions. List the three primary categories with one example source for each.**
*(3 Marks — Remember / Understand level)*

**Model Answer:**

Evaporative emissions are the hydrocarbon (HC) emissions released into the atmosphere from a vehicle's fuel system through evaporation, leakage, or permeation, excluding tailpipe exhaust.

The three primary categories are:

1. **Diurnal Emissions** — HC released due to the daily ambient temperature swing heating the fuel tank; source: fuel tank vapor space venting.
2. **Hot-Soak Emissions** — HC released in the 1-hour period after engine shutdown due to residual heat; source: fuel rail and injector tips.
3. **Running-Loss Emissions** — HC released while the engine runs; source: underhood heating of fuel lines and return fuel.

**Marking Distribution:** Definition 1m + 3 categories 1m + sources 1m = 3m.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Q1.b]**

**Q2. What is a SHED? What is its function in evaporative emission testing?**
*(3 Marks — Understand level)*

**Model Answer:**

SHED stands for **Sealed Housing for Evaporative Determination**. It is a temperature-controlled, hermetically sealed enclosure (typically 50–100 m³) into which a test vehicle is placed for the evaporative emission certification test.

**Function:** The SHED captures all HC vapors emitted by the vehicle during a thermal cycle. A Flame Ionization Detector (FID) measures the HC concentration inside the enclosure before and after the cycle. The mass of HC emitted is computed as $m_{HC} = V_{SHED} \cdot \rho_{HC} \cdot (C_f - C_i)$. The result is compared against the regulatory limit (e.g., 0.5 g for EPA Tier 3 ORVR, 2.0 g for Euro 6 / BS-VI).

**Marking Distribution:** Full form 1m + function 1m + equation/limit 1m = 3m.

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Q2] — INTERNAL CHOICE**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q. (a)** Explain the functional architecture of a modern Evaporative Emission Control System (EVAP). With a neat block diagram, describe the working of each subsystem. *(7 Marks — Understand / Apply level)*

**Model Solution:**

A modern EVAP system consists of six mandatory sub-systems:

1. **Sealed Fuel Tank (HDPE multi-layer):** Stores liquid fuel and provides a sealed vapor space. Eliminates leak paths.

2. **Fuel Filler Cap (Self-Sealing):** Equipped with pressure-vacuum relief and O-ring seal to prevent vapor escape during refueling and operation.

3. **Activated Carbon Canister:** The heart of the system. Filled with 0.5–2 L of activated carbon (surface area 500–1500 m²/g). Adsorbs HC vapors via van der Waals forces. The working capacity is typically 25–35 g HC per 100 g carbon.

4. **Purge Control Valve (PCV Solenoid):** Modulates purge flow using engine vacuum. ECM controls duty cycle (PWM) to draw desorbed HC from the canister into the intake manifold.

5. **Vent Valve:** Normally closed; opens only during purge or leak-diagnostic mode to admit fresh air into the canister for desorption.

6. **Leak Detection System (LDP or NVLD):** Diagnoses leaks as small as 1.0 mm using vacuum-decay principle. Sets OBD-II DTCs (P0440–P0455) on failure.

**Working Principle:**
- *Engine Off / Diurnal:* Vapors from the fuel tank are routed through the vapor line to the canister, where HC is adsorbed onto activated carbon. Fresh air enters through the vent valve.
- *Engine Running:* ECM actuates the purge valve, drawing fresh air through the canister (vent → canister → purge → intake). The air desorbs HC from the carbon and carries it to the engine, where it is burned.

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram.

**Marking Distribution:** 6 sub-systems × 1m = 6m + working 1m = 7m.

---

**Q. (b)** A passenger car is tested in a SHED of volume 75 m³ at 28 °C. The initial HC concentration is 1.2 ppm; after a hot-soak test, the final concentration is 6.8 ppm. Propane-calibrated HC density is 1.83 kg/m³. Calculate the HC mass emitted and determine compliance with the **Euro 6 limit of 2.0 g/test**. *(7 Marks — Apply / Analyze level)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute concentration change:**

$$
\Delta C \;=\; C_f - C_i \;=\; 6.8 - 1.2 \;=\; 5.6 \text{ ppm} \;=\; 5.6 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the SHED mass equation:**

$$
m_{HC} \;=\; V_{SHED} \cdot \rho_{HC} \cdot \Delta C
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values (convert $\rho$ to g/m³):**

$$
\rho_{HC} \;=\; 1.83 \, \text{kg/m}^{3} \;=\; 1830 \, \text{g/m}^{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{HC} \;&=\; 75 \times 1830 \times 5.6 \times 10^{-6} \\
\\
m_{HC} \;&=\; 75 \times 1.0248 \times 10^{-2} \\
\\
m_{HC} \;&=\; 0.7686 \, \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compliance check:**

$$
m_{HC} = 0.7686 \, \text{g} \quad < \quad 2.0 \, \text{g (Euro 6 limit)}
$$

**Conclusion:** The vehicle **complies** with Euro 6. The carbon canister is functioning correctly.

**Marking Distribution:** Step 1 (1m) + Step 2 (1m) + Step 3 (1m) + Step 4 (2m) + Step 5 (2m) = 7m.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q. (a)** Compare and contrast the EPA Tier 3, Euro 6, and India BS-VI regulations for evaporative emissions. Tabulate the test method, limit, and key requirement for each. *(7 Marks — Analyze level)*

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | US-EPA Tier 3 | Euro 6 (EU) | India BS-VI (AIS-137) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Issuing Body** | EPA / CARB | European Commission | MoRTH / ARAI |
| **Test Method** | SHED + 3-day diurnal (40 CFR §86.133) | Type IV (UNECE Reg. 692/2008) | Aligned to Euro 6 (Type IV) |
| **Diurnal Limit** | 0.3 g/test | (included in total) | (included in total) |
| **Hot-Soak Limit** | 0.3 g/test | (included in total) | (included in total) |
| **Total Limit** | 0.5 g/test (with ORVR) | 2.0 g/test | 2.0 g/test |
| **ORVR Required** | Yes (since 2006) | No (optional) | No (deferred to BS-VII) |
| **Test Temperature** | 26 °C → 41 °C | 20 °C → 40 °C | 20 °C → 40 °C |
| **In-Use Compliance** | In-use testing mandatory | In-service conformity (ISC) | In-use testing (limited) |
| **OBD Threshold** | 1.0 mm leak | 1.0 mm leak (post-EOBD) | 1.0 mm leak |

**Key Insights:**
- US Tier 3 is **most stringent** in numeric terms (0.5 g vs 2.0 g).
- Euro 6 / BS-VI is **less stringent numerically** but emphasizes **Type-IV test repeatability** and in-use conformity.
- The difference of 4× in limit reflects a **3-decade regulatory lag** in the Euro/Indian framework vs. the US framework.

**Marking Distribution:** Table 4m + Key insights 2m + Closing statement 1m = 7m.

---

**Q. (b)** With the help of the Antoine equation, derive the vapor pressure of gasoline at 38 °C and explain its role in diurnal evaporative emissions. Assume Antoine constants A = 6.877, B = 1171.5, C = 224.0 (for gasoline surrogate, T in °C, P in mmHg). *(7 Marks — Apply / Analyze level)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the Antoine equation:**

$$
\log_{10}(P_{vap}) \;=\; A - \frac{B}{C + T}
$$

where $P_{vap}$ is in mmHg and $T$ is in °C.

**Step 2 — Substitute the values for T = 38 °C:**

$$
\log_{10}(P_{vap}) \;=\; 6.877 - \frac{1171.5}{224.0 + 38}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator:**

$$
224.0 + 38 \;=\; 262.0
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the fraction:**

$$
\frac{1171.5}{262.0} \;=\; 4.4714
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the logarithm:**

$$
\log_{10}(P_{vap}) \;=\; 6.877 - 4.4714 \;=\; 2.4056
$$

**Step 6 — Take anti-logarithm to obtain vapor pressure:**

$$
P_{vap} \;=\; 10^{2.4056} \;=\; 254.4 \, \text{mmHg}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to kPa (for SI unit consistency):**

$$
P_{vap} \;=\; 254.4 \times 0.1333 \;=\; 33.91 \, \text{kPa}
$$

**Role in Diurnal Emissions:**

The diurnal emission mass is directly proportional to the saturated vapor pressure of the fuel at the maximum diurnal temperature. As $P_{vap}$ increases:
- Vapor space partial pressure of HC increases.
- The vapor displaced out of the vent valve per °C of temperature rise increases linearly.
- Therefore, **fuels with high RVP (Reid Vapor Pressure)** emit more diurnal HC.

This is why **regulatory fuel specifications (e.g., ASTM D4814, IS 2796)** impose **RVP caps** of 50–62 kPa in summer to control diurnal emissions at the source.

**Marking Distribution:** Antoine statement 1m + Substitution 1m + Denominator 1m + Fraction 1m + Final calc 2m + Diurnal role 1m = 7m.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting to convert ppm to mole fraction** by dividing by $10^{6}$. A 0.5 vs $5 \times 10^{-7}$ error leads to a 6-orders-of-magnitude mistake in $m_{HC}$. (Penalty: 2–3 marks)
> - **Not converting kg/m³ to g/m³** when reporting mass in grams. (Penalty: 1 mark)
> - **Confusing Euro 6 (2.0 g) with US Tier 3 (0.5 g) limits.** Always read the question stem carefully for the standard being tested. (Penalty: 2 marks)
> - **Reporting "evaporative emissions = exhaust emissions."** Evaporative emissions are a **non-exhaust** category. (Penalty: 1 mark, conceptual)
> - **In Antoine equation, mixing °C and K in the denominator.** Stay consistent with the constants' reference temperature. (Penalty: 1 mark)
> - **For OBD-II, students often forget that P0442 corresponds to a "small leak" (1.0 mm) and P0455 to "large leak."** (Penalty: 1 mark, professional knowledge)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Evaporative Emissions**

### **A. Core Definitions (Must remember)**
- Evaporative emissions = non-exhaust HC from fuel system.
- SHED = Sealed Housing for Evaporative Determination.
- FID = Flame Ionization Detector (used in SHED).
- ORVR = Onboard Refueling Vapor Recovery.
- LDP = Leak Detection Pump.
- NVLD = Natural Vacuum Leak Detection.
- RVP = Reid Vapor Pressure (fuel-side control).
- EVAP = Evaporative Emission Control System.

### **B. Three Primary Categories (Most Asked in 3-markers)**
- **Diurnal** — daily temp swing; source: fuel tank.
- **Hot-Soak** — first hour after shutdown; source: fuel rail/injectors.
- **Running-Loss** — during engine operation; source: underhood heating.

### **C. Standard Limits (Numerical — Do Not Forget)**
- **US-EPA Tier 3 ORVR:** 0.5 g/test
- **Euro 6 (Type IV):** 2.0 g/test
- **India BS-VI (AIS-137):** 2.0 g/test

### **D. The Master Equation (For all numerical problems)**
- **SHED mass:** $m_{HC} = V_{SHED} \cdot \rho_{HC} \cdot (C_f - C_i) \cdot 10^{-6}$
- **Antoine equation:** $\log_{10}(P_{vap}) = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}$

### **E. Six Sub-Systems of Modern EVAP (For 7-marker Block Diagram)**
1. Sealed fuel tank
2. Self-sealing filler cap
3. Activated carbon canister
4. Purge control valve
5. Vent valve
6. Leak detection system (LDP / NVLD)

### **F. Test Methods to Remember**
- **SHED test** (per 40 CFR §86 / Euro 6 Type IV)
- **Diurnal test** (3-day, 26–41 °C)
- **Hot-soak test** (1 hour post-shutdown)
- **Running-loss test** (chassis dyno)
- **Refueling test** (with ORVR)

### **G. OBD-II DTC Family (For Diagnostic-Question Bonus Marks)**
- P0440 — General EVAP system fault
- P0442 — Small leak detected (~1.0 mm)
- P0443 — Purge valve circuit
- P0455 — Large leak detected
- P0456 — Very small leak (~0.5 mm)

### **H. Engineering Design Tip (For Applied Questions)**
- Always size canister at **2–3× the theoretical minimum** to handle aging, breakthrough, and stack-up of all three emission categories (diurnal + hot-soak + running-loss).
- Specify **low-permeation hoses (SAE J30R12 / J30R13)** for Tier 3 compliance.
- Specify **multi-layer HDPE fuel tanks** with EVOH barrier for permeation control.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Refuelling emissions, fuel options for controlling emissions, gasoline engines, lead and octane number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Refuelling Emissions — The "Invisible Puff"

### Formal KTU Definition
**Refuelling emissions** are the hydrocarbons (HC) released to the atmosphere when fuel vapours already present in the headspace of a vehicle's fuel tank are displaced by the incoming liquid fuel during the refuelling process. They are a subset of **Evaporative Emissions (EVAP)** but, unlike diurnal or running-loss emissions, they are a *displacement* phenomenon governed by the volume balance of the tank.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Refuelling emissions are legislated separately under Indian AIS-137 (BIS), US EPA Tier-3, and Euro-6 standards. They typically contribute **0.2–0.5 g HC per refuelling event** for an uncontrolled vehicle, and up to **1.5 g HC** in hot-soak conditions.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Squeezed Water Balloon"
Imagine a transparent water balloon already half-filled with air. Now pour water into it through a small nozzle. The air inside has nowhere to go except out through the same opening — it is *displaced*. A fuel tank behaves identically. The space above the fuel (the **ullage**) is saturated with HC vapours (sometimes called "gasoline vapour"), and the moment fresh fuel rushes in, these vapours are pushed out into the station forecourt (or the surrounding air if the vehicle is filled at a hand pump). That escaping puff is the refuelling emission.

### Key Physical Quantities (Bold Standards)
- **Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP):** typically **45–65 kPa** for summer-grade petrol; the dominant driver of refuelling emissions.
- **Volumetric Displacement Ratio:** approximately **1 L of liquid fuel displaces 1.0–1.4 L of saturated vapour**, depending on temperature and fuel composition.
- **Onboard Refuelling Vapor Recovery (ORVR):** captures **≥ 95%** of these displaced vapours and routes them to the carbon canister.

---

## 1.2 Fuel Options for Controlling Emissions — The "Cleaner Burn Toolkit"

### Formal Definition
Fuel options for emission control are the **refining, blending, and substitution strategies** applied to gasoline and diesel to reduce regulated tailpipe and evaporative pollutants (CO, HC, NOₓ, PM), without compromising engine performance, drivability, or commercial viability.

### Five Major Fuel Pathways
| # | Strategy | Mechanism |
|---|----------|-----------|
| 1 | **Reformulated Gasoline (RFG)** | Lower RVP, lower olefins/aromatics, lower sulphur |
| 2 | **Oxygenates** (MTBE, ETBE, Ethanol) | Add bound oxygen → leaner combustion → lower CO & HC |
| 3 | **Low-Sulphur Fuels** (≤ 10 ppm S, BS-VI) | Protect catalyst & reduce SO₂ + PM |
| 4 | **Lead Phase-out** | Stop TEL addition → enable closed-loop catalyst operation |
| 5 | **Alternative Fuels** (CNG, LPG, E85, E100, H₂, Biodiesel, Electricity) | Different molecular structure → fundamentally different combustion emissions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A fuel is essentially a "delivery vehicle" for hydrocarbons. The trick is to choose hydrocarbons (or oxygenates) that *burn cleanly, vaporise moderately, and do not poison the after-treatment device*.

---

## 1.3 Gasoline Engines (Spark-Ignition, SI) — The "Controlled Spark"

### Formal Definition
A **Gasoline Engine** is an internal combustion engine that uses a **spark plug** to ignite a pre-mixed homogeneous charge of air and gasoline (petrol). It operates on the **Otto cycle** and produces power through four strokes: Intake, Compression, Power, Exhaust.

### Key Operating Parameters
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR):** **14.7 : 1** (by mass) — required for theoretical complete combustion with no excess O₂ or fuel.
- **Compression Ratio:** typically **8:1 to 12:1** (limited by knock resistance of the fuel).
- **Lambda (λ):** the normalised AFR; λ = 1 at stoichiometric, λ > 1 lean, λ < 1 rich.
- **Three-Way Catalyst (TWC):** requires λ ≈ 1 ± 0.01 for simultaneous CO, HC, and NOₓ conversion.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Precision Flamethrower"
Think of a gasoline engine as a kitchen gas stove. The stove mixes gas and air *before* ignition, then a small spark lights it cleanly. If the mix is wrong (too rich or too lean), the flame is sooty (HC, CO) or too hot (NOₓ). The **catalytic converter** acts as a special filter that cleans the *leftover* burnt gases — but only works when the stove is set exactly right (λ = 1).

---

## 1.4 Lead and Octane Number — The "Anti-Knock Duel"

### Lead (Tetraethyl Lead, TEL)
**Tetraethyl lead, Pb(C₂H₅)₄**, is a colourless, oily organometallic liquid that was added to gasoline from the **1920s to the 1990s (India phase-out by 2000)** as a cost-effective **anti-knock additive**. Typical dosage: **0.1–0.4 g Pb/L** of fuel.

### Why Lead Was Used (Anti-Knock Mechanism)
TEL decomposes in the combustion chamber to form **lead oxide (PbO) and lead radicals (Pb·)**, which:
1. Quench the **chain-branching reactions** of the end-gas auto-ignition.
2. Provide catalytic surfaces that terminate free-radical propagation.
3. Effectively raise the fuel's **octane number** by 10–15 points at very low concentrations.

### Why Lead Was Banned
1. **Neurotoxicity** — causes cognitive impairment, especially in children (lead-line on gums, reduced IQ).
2. **Catalyst Poisoning** — Pb deposits permanently poison the three-way catalyst's precious metals.
3. **Environmental Persistence** — lead does not degrade; it accumulates in soil and the food chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Fact:** India's Bharat Stage-IV (BS-IV, 2010) onwards mandated unleaded petrol; BS-VI (2020) reduced sulphur to **< 10 ppm** and made closed-loop three-way catalytic systems mandatory for all petrol cars.

### Octane Number — The "Knock Resistance Meter"
**Octane number (ON)** is a dimensionless numerical measure of a fuel's resistance to **engine knock (detonation)** — the uncontrolled auto-ignition of the end-gas mixture ahead of the flame front. It is defined by comparing the test fuel's knocking tendency to that of a reference blend of:
- **Iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane):** assigned octane number **100** (knock-resistant).
- **n-Heptane:** assigned octane number **0** (highly knock-prone).

> A fuel that knocks the same as a blend of 95% iso-octane + 5% n-heptane is said to have an **octane number of 95**.

### Two Test Methods
- **Research Octane Number (RON)** — ASTM D2699, 600 rpm, mild conditions → represents *low-to-medium severity*.
- **Motor Octane Number (MON)** — ASTM D2700, 900 rpm, advanced timing, hotter mixture → represents *high severity*.
- **Anti-Knock Index (AKI)** = (RON + MON) / 2 — used at the fuel pump in North America.
- **Posted Octane Number** = RON — used in Europe, India, and most of Asia.

> [!NOTE]
> **India uses RON** at the pump. Indian regular petrol is **RON 91**, premium is **RON 95**, and high-performance fuels are **RON 97–102**.

### Typical Octane Numbers of Common Fuels
| Fuel | RON | MON | AKI |
|------|-----|-----|-----|
| n-Heptane | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Regular Gasoline (India) | 91 | 82 | 86.5 |
| Premium Gasoline (India) | 95 | 85 | 90 |
| Euro Premium | 98 | 88 | 93 |
| E10 (10% Ethanol blend) | 93–94 | 84 | 88.5–89 |
| E85 (Flex-fuel) | 100–105 | 85–90 | 92.5–97.5 |
| Iso-octane (ref) | 100 | 100 | 100 |
| Pure Ethanol (E100) | 108 | 89 | 98.5 |
| Toluene | 124 | 112 | 118 |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Refuelling Emissions — Physics of Displacement

### 2.1.1 The Volumetric Displacement Principle
When a quantity of liquid fuel $V_f$ is poured into a tank that is already partially filled and has a ullage volume $V_u$ saturated with HC vapour, the mass of HC released $M_{HC,disp}$ is:

$$
M_{HC,disp} = V_{disp} \times \rho_{vapour}
$$

Where:
- $V_{disp}$ = volume of vapour displaced = $V_f \times (1 - \beta_{thermal})$
- $\rho_{vapour}$ = density of saturated HC vapour at tank temperature (g/m³)
- $\beta_{thermal}$ = thermal expansion correction (≈ 0 for fast fill)

### 2.1.2 Saturated Vapour Density (Ideal Gas Approximation)

$$
\rho_{vapour} = \frac{P_{vap}}{R \cdot T} \times M_{HC}
$$

Where:
- $P_{vap}$ = vapour pressure of the fuel (Pa) — closely related to RVP.
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**.
- $T$ = absolute temperature in the ullage (K).
- $M_{HC}$ = average molecular weight of vapour (≈ 60–70 g/mol for gasoline).

### 2.1.3 Why Refuelling Emissions Matter
- One uncontrolled fill at **40 L** in summer (RVP 60 kPa, 35 °C) can release **0.8–1.5 g HC**.
- At a busy petrol station doing 1,000 fills/day, the cumulative HC emission rivals that of **hundreds of running vehicles** for that hour.
- ORVR plus Stage-II vapour recovery at the dispenser brings this down to **< 0.05 g HC per fill**.

### 2.1.4 Control Hardware
- **Carbon Canister** (charcoal, typically 0.5–2.0 L) — stores vapour during parking, purges it into the intake during running.
- **ORVR (Onboard Refuelling Vapour Recovery)** — a vent valve on the filler neck that captures displaced vapour back into the canister.
- **Stage-II (Vapour Recovery Nozzle)** — a bellows on the dispenser pump that sucks back vapour during fill.

---

## 2.2 Fuel Options — Mechanism of Each Strategy

### 2.2.1 Reformulated Gasoline (RFG)
Reduces the photochemical reactivity of evaporative and tailpipe emissions. The **CO/HC/NOₓ Mobile-Source Air Toxics (MSAT)** reductions range from **15–25%** versus conventional gasoline.

### 2.2.2 Oxygenates — Lean Combustion Effect
When **ethanol (C₂H₅OH)** or **MTBE (CH₃OC(CH₃)₃)** is blended, the fuel's stoichiometric requirement changes. The modified stoichiometric AFR for a gasoline-ethanol blend containing mass fraction $x_E$ of ethanol:

$$
AFR_{stoich} = \frac{14.7 \times (1 - x_E) + 9.0 \times x_E}{1}
$$

For E10 ($x_E = 0.10$): $AFR_{stoich} \approx 14.7 \times 0.90 + 9.0 \times 0.10 = 14.13 : 1$

This **lean shift** lowers CO and HC but slightly increases NOₓ.

### 2.2.3 Alternative Fuels — Comparative Emissions (g/km, light-duty)
| Fuel | CO | HC | NOₓ | PM | CO₂ (g/km) |
|------|----|----|-----|-----|------------|
| Gasoline (BS-VI) | 1.00 | 0.10 | 0.06 | 0.005 | 120 |
| E10 | 0.90 | 0.09 | 0.07 | 0.005 | 115 |
| CNG | 0.40 | 0.05 | 0.05 | 0.001 | 90 |
| LPG | 0.60 | 0.06 | 0.06 | 0.002 | 105 |
| Diesel (BS-VI) | 0.50 | - | 0.08 | 0.005 | 110 |
| BEV (Battery EV) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 (TTW) |

> "TTW" = Tank-to-Wheel. BEV has zero *tank-to-wheel* emissions; well-to-wheel analysis includes the power-plant CO₂.

---

## 2.3 Gasoline Engine Combustion & Emission Formation

### 2.3.1 The Four-Stroke Otto Cycle
1. **Intake** — piston descends, intake valve opens, air-fuel mixture drawn in.
2. **Compression** — both valves close, piston rises, mixture compressed (CR = 8–12).
3. **Power** — spark plug fires near TDC, flame kernel grows, pressure pushes piston down.
4. **Exhaust** — exhaust valve opens, burnt gases expelled.

### 2.3.2 Chemical Balance for Iso-Octane Combustion (Stoichiometric)

$$
C_8H_{18} + 12.5\,(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O + 47.0\,N_2
$$

### 2.3.3 Why λ = 1 Matters for the Three-Way Catalyst
- **CO and HC oxidation** require *excess O₂* (favoured at λ > 1).
- **NOₓ reduction** requires *reducing environment* (favoured at λ < 1).
- The two requirements intersect only in a narrow window: **λ = 1.00 ± 0.01** (an "air-fuel window" of < 1% wide). Outside this window, one pollutant shoots up.
- The **lambda (oxygen) sensor** in the exhaust feeds back to the ECU, which adjusts injector pulse-width at a 1 Hz closed-loop rate.

### 2.3.4 Cold-Start Emissions — The "Catalyst Cold Zone"
- The TWC lights off only at **~250–300 °C**.
- Cold-start (first 60 s) accounts for **60–80%** of total HC emissions on a short test cycle (NEDC, WLTC).
- Mitigations: close-coupled catalyst (within 30 cm of manifold), secondary air injection, HC trap.

### 2.3.5 Crankcase Blow-by
- High-pressure combustion gases leak past piston rings into the crankcase.
- Pre-controlled (before 1961 in the US): vented to atmosphere = **major HC source**.
- Controlled (PCV — Positive Crankcase Ventilation): blow-by gases returned to intake.

---

## 2.4 Octane Number — Quantitative Framework

### 2.4.1 Definition Recap
$$
\text{Octane Number of Fuel X} = \% \text{ (by volume) of iso-octane in a blend with n-heptane that matches X in knock intensity, under standard CFR engine conditions}
$$

### 2.4.2 Sensitivity
$$
\text{Sensitivity} = RON - MON
$$
- **Paraffins:** low sensitivity (RON ≈ MON).
- **Aromatics:** high sensitivity (RON >> MON).
- **Olefins:** moderate sensitivity.
- **Ethanol:** sensitivity ≈ 19 (RON 108, MON 89).
- **Toluene:** sensitivity ≈ 12.

### 2.4.3 Blending Octane (Linear Approximation)

$$
ON_{blend} = \sum_i x_i \cdot ON_i
$$

Where $x_i$ is the volume fraction of fuel $i$. Real-world blending shows slight non-linearity (synergy/antagonism), but the linear rule is good for first-order KTU problems.

### 2.4.4 Anti-Knock Index (AKI / Pump Octane)

$$
AKI = \frac{RON + MON}{2}
$$

### 2.4.5 Anti-Knock Quality of TEL
TEL was a "magic" additive because **0.5 g Pb/L raised RON by 10–15 points** — far more efficiently than refining (e.g., catalytic reforming, alkylation, isomerization).

### 2.4.6 Relationship Between Compression Ratio and Required Octane
Empirically, modern engines require:

$$
ON_{required} \approx 4 \times (CR - 7) + 80
$$

For CR = 10: $ON_{required} \approx 4(3) + 80 = 92$ RON.
For CR = 12: $ON_{required} \approx 4(5) + 80 = 100$ RON.

> This is why high-performance engines (CR 11–12) demand 95–98 RON fuel, while economy engines (CR 9–10) can run on 91 RON.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio of Gasoline (C₈H₁₈)

### Step 1 — Start with the molecular representation
We approximate gasoline as **iso-octane, C₈H₁₈** (molecular mass $M_f = 8 \times 12 + 18 \times 1 = 114$ g/mol).

### Step 2 — Write the balanced combustion equation
Complete combustion with O₂ only (ignoring N₂ for mass balance):

$$
C_8H_{18} + a\,O_2 \rightarrow b\,CO_2 + c\,H_2O
$$

### Step 3 — Balance Carbon atoms
Carbon balance: $b = 8$

### Step 4 — Balance Hydrogen atoms
Hydrogen balance: $2c = 18 \Rightarrow c = 9$

### Step 5 — Balance Oxygen atoms
Oxygen balance: $2a = 2b + c = 16 + 9 = 25 \Rightarrow a = 12.5$

### Step 6 — Final balanced equation

$$
C_8H_{18} + 12.5\,O_2 \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O
$$

### Step 7 — Convert to mass basis
- Mass of fuel: $114$ g
- Mass of O₂: $12.5 \times 32 = 400$ g
- Air is **23.2% O₂ by mass** (because N₂/O₂ volume ratio is 3.76, mass ratio is $3.76 \times 28/32 = 3.29$, so O₂ mass fraction = $1/(1+3.29) = 0.233$).

$$
\text{Mass of air required} = \frac{400}{0.233} = 1716.7\,\text{g}
$$

### Step 8 — Compute AFR (stoichiometric)

$$
AFR_s = \frac{1716.7}{114} \approx 15.07
$$

> **Note:** The standard textbook value of 14.7 is derived using gasoline's effective empirical formula **CH₁.₈₅** (a slightly different "average" hydrocarbon), giving 14.7. For pure iso-octane the strict value is **15.07**, but KTU convention uses **14.7**. Always use 14.7 in exams unless otherwise stated.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Anti-Knock Index (AKI) for a Methanol-Gasoline Blend

A fuel station blends **80% (by volume) gasoline (RON 91, MON 82)** with **20% methanol (RON 108, MON 89)**. Compute RON, MON, and AKI of the blend using the linear blending rule.

### Step 1 — Compute blend RON
Using the linear blending rule:

$$
RON_{blend} = x_g \cdot RON_g + x_m \cdot RON_m
$$

$$
RON_{blend} = 0.80 \times 91 + 0.20 \times 108
$$

$$
RON_{blend} = 72.8 + 21.6 = 94.4
$$

### Step 2 — Compute blend MON
Similarly:

$$
MON_{blend} = x_g \cdot MON_g + x_m \cdot MON_m
$$

$$
MON_{blend} = 0.80 \times 82 + 0.20 \times 89
$$

$$
MON_{blend} = 65.6 + 17.8 = 83.4
$$

### Step 3 — Compute AKI

$$
AKI = \frac{RON + MON}{2} = \frac{94.4 + 83.4}{2}
$$

$$
AKI = \frac{177.8}{2} = 88.9
$$

### Step 4 — Sensitivity

$$
S = RON - MON = 94.4 - 83.4 = 11.0
$$

### Step 5 — Conclusion
The M20 blend has **RON 94.4**, **MON 83.4**, **AKI 88.9**, and **sensitivity 11.0**. It satisfies Indian "Premium" specification (RON ≥ 95) marginally below, but AKI meets the typical Indian "Regular" market (AKI ~86).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Refuelling Emission Mass — Worked Example

A car has a fuel tank with **35 L of ullage volume** saturated with gasoline vapour at **35 °C (308 K)**. RVP of the fuel is **60 kPa**. The average molecular weight of the vapour is **65 g/mol**. Calculate the mass of HC displaced if **20 L of liquid fuel** is added.

### Step 1 — Identify the relevant formula
Saturated vapour density:

$$
\rho_{vap} = \frac{P_{vap} \cdot M_{HC}}{R \cdot T}
$$

### Step 2 — Insert the values

$$
\rho_{vap} = \frac{60{,}000 \, \text{Pa} \times 0.065 \, \text{kg/mol}}{8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)} \times 308 \, \text{K}}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute numerator

$$
60{,}000 \times 0.065 = 3{,}900 \, \text{Pa·kg/mol}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute denominator

$$
8.314 \times 308 = 2{,}560.7 \, \text{J/mol}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute vapour density

$$
\rho_{vap} = \frac{3{,}900}{2{,}560.7} = 1.523 \, \text{kg/m}^3
$$

### Step 6 — Convert vapour density to g/L

$$
\rho_{vap} = 1.523 \, \text{kg/m}^3 = 1.523 \, \text{g/L}
$$

### Step 7 — Compute mass of HC displaced
20 L of liquid fuel displaces approximately **20 L of vapour** (thermal correction ignored for fast fill):

$$
M_{HC} = 20 \, \text{L} \times 1.523 \, \text{g/L} = 30.46 \, \text{g}
$$

### Step 8 — Practical adjustment
Real-world refuelling emissions are lower because:
- The vapour space is not 100% saturated; the saturation factor ≈ 0.6–0.8.
- Some vapour remains in the headspace (mixing inefficiency).
- The temperature drops slightly as new, cooler fuel mixes in.

Effective emission ≈ $30.46 \times 0.7 = \mathbf{21.3 \, g \, HC}$ per fill.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reality Check:** This 21 g value is large enough to be visible. Without ORVR, you would see a strong "petrol smell" at the forecourt. With ORVR + Stage-II, this drops to < 0.5 g.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Required Octane Number vs Compression Ratio

A high-performance engine has **CR = 11.5:1**. Using the empirical relation $ON = 4(CR-7) + 80$, find the required RON.

### Step 1 — Substitute

$$
ON = 4 \times (11.5 - 7) + 80
$$

### Step 2 — Simplify

$$
ON = 4 \times 4.5 + 80 = 18 + 80
$$

### Step 3 — Final answer

$$
\boxed{ON = 98 \, \text{RON}}
$$

> The engine will knock on Indian regular (RON 91) and must use **Premium 95** at minimum, ideally **RON 97–98**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Rating of Anti-Knock Strategies

| Strategy | RON Boost per unit | Cost Impact | Side-Effects | Status in India |
|----------|-------------------|-------------|--------------|-----------------|
| Catalytic Reforming | +10 to +15 | Medium | None | Widely used in refineries |
| Alkylation | +12 to +15 | High | None | Used in refineries |
| Isomerization | +5 to +10 | Low–Medium | None | Used in refineries |
| Cracking (FCC) | –3 to +3 | Low | Olefins → gum | Refinery |
| MTBE blending | +3 per 5% vol | Medium | Groundwater issues | Discontinued in US |
| ETBE blending | +3 per 7% vol | Medium | None major | Limited use |
| Ethanol (E10) | +3 RON | Medium (subsidy needed) | Slightly leaner, attracts water | Mandated up to E20 (target 2025) |
| TEL (Pb) | +10 to +15 per 0.3 g/L | Low (historically) | Toxic, catalyst killer | **Banned since 2000** |
| MMT (Manganese) | +3 to +8 | Low | Catalyst ash poisoning | Banned in many regions |

---

## 3.6 Engineering Application: Why 14.7 Matters for After-Treatment

The lambda window for a three-way catalyst (TWC) is **λ = 1.00 ± 0.01**. Convert this to AFR window:

$$
AFR_{min} = 0.99 \times 14.7 = 14.553
$$

$$
AFR_{max} = 1.01 \times 14.7 = 14.847
$$

Thus the ECU must maintain AFR within a band of **0.294 units** (about **2%**). This is achieved with a **heated wide-band lambda sensor** (UEGO) and **closed-loop fuel control** at ~1 Hz. Any drift outside this band causes:
- λ > 1.01 → NOₓ rises sharply (oxygen excess drives NOₓ formation)
- λ < 0.99 → CO and HC rise (reducing environment, incomplete combustion)

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define refuelling emissions. How do they differ from running-loss evaporative emissions?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Refuelling emissions are the hydrocarbons released to the atmosphere when incoming liquid fuel displaces saturated HC vapour from the headspace of a vehicle's fuel tank during refuelling.
- **Difference (1.5 marks):**
  - *Refuelling emissions* occur only during the refuelling event; they are a **volumetric displacement** phenomenon and depend on RVP and ambient temperature.
  - *Running-loss emissions* occur while the vehicle is being driven, caused by **diurnal heating of the fuel tank** and hot-soak conditions; they depend on underbody heat soak, fuel sloshing, and tank temperature cycling.

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is octane number? Distinguish between RON and MON.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Octane number is a numerical measure of a fuel's resistance to engine knock (detonation), determined by comparing the test fuel against blends of iso-octane (ON = 100) and n-heptane (ON = 0) in a standard CFR engine.
- **RON vs MON (2 marks):**
  - **RON (Research Octane Number):** ASTM D2699, 600 rpm, 0% BTDC spark advance, mild intake air; represents *low-to-medium severity*.
  - **MON (Motor Octane Number):** ASTM D2700, 900 rpm, variable timing, hotter mixture; represents *high-speed / high-load severity*.
  - The **sensitivity** of a fuel is $S = RON - MON$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question B1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]

**(a)** With the help of a balanced chemical equation, derive the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for gasoline (C₈H₁₈). State clearly all assumptions and the standard value used in engineering practice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle of the three-way catalytic converter. Why is λ = 1.000 ± 0.01 critical for its operation? Describe the role of the lambda sensor in maintaining this window. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to B1(a) — Stoichiometric AFR Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Assumption (1 mark):** Gasoline is represented as iso-octane, C₈H₁₈, with molecular mass $M_f = 114$ g/mol. Air is 23.2% O₂ by mass (3.76 moles of N₂ per mole of O₂).

2. **Balanced equation (2 marks):**

$$
C_8H_{18} + 12.5\,O_2 \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O
$$

3. **Moles of O₂ (1 mark):** Per 1 mole of fuel, 12.5 moles of O₂ are required.

4. **Mass calculation (2 marks):**
   - Mass of O₂ = $12.5 \times 32 = 400$ g
   - Mass of air = $400 / 0.232 = 1724$ g
   - $AFR_{stoich} = 1724 / 114 = 15.12$ (theoretical)
   - Engineering standard value used: **14.7 : 1** (using effective CH₁.₈₅ formula)

5. **Conclusion (1 mark):** The stoichiometric AFR of gasoline is **14.7 kg air per kg fuel**.

---

**Solution to B1(b) — Three-Way Catalytic Converter & Lambda Control (7 marks):**

1. **TWC definition (2 marks):** A three-way catalyst simultaneously oxidises CO to CO₂, oxidises unburnt HC to CO₂ and H₂O, and reduces NOₓ to N₂, using platinum, palladium, and rhodium on a ceramic honeycomb.

2. **Two opposing requirements (2 marks):**
   - CO and HC oxidation need *oxidising* conditions (λ > 1).
   - NOₓ reduction needs *reducing* conditions (λ < 1).
   - Both can occur only in a narrow window: **λ = 1.00 ± 0.01** (≈ ±0.15 AFR units, ≈ 2% wide).

3. **Lambda sensor operation (2 marks):** A heated UEGO sensor outputs a voltage proportional to residual oxygen. The ECU reads it at ~1 Hz and adjusts injector pulse-width:
   - Lean (high O₂) → ECU increases fuel.
   - Rich (low O₂) → ECU decreases fuel.
   - This oscillates around λ = 1.000, integrating to a stoichiometric mean.

4. **Failure mode (1 mark):** If the lambda sensor fails, the ECU reverts to open-loop mode using a pre-programmed map; emissions rise by 5–10× and OBD-II stores a fault code (P0130–P0167 family).

---

### Question B2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of engine knock in a gasoline engine. How does TEL provide anti-knock protection, and what were the main reasons for its eventual global phase-out? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the major fuel options available for controlling emissions from gasoline vehicles. Compare the environmental and performance impact of E0, E10, and E85 blends. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to B2(a) — Knock Mechanism & TEL Anti-Knock Action (7 marks):**

1. **Definition of knock (1.5 marks):** Knock (detonation) is the *uncontrolled auto-ignition* of the end-gas mixture (the unburnt charge ahead of the advancing flame front) due to combined high temperature and pressure near the end of the compression stroke.

2. **Mechanism of knock (2 marks):**
   - Compression raises the end-gas temperature adiabatically.
   - If the induction time of the fuel is shorter than the time taken for the spark-ignited flame to reach it, the end-gas auto-ignites spontaneously.
   - This creates two colliding pressure waves, producing the characteristic metallic "ping" sound.
   - Sustained knock causes piston overheating, ring breakage, and bearing damage.

3. **TEL anti-knock mechanism (2 marks):**
   - TEL decomposes in the combustion chamber: $Pb(C_2H_5)_4 \rightarrow Pb + 4\,C_2H_5\cdot$
   - The free radicals and PbO particles **quench the chain-branching reactions** in the end-gas by:
     - Reacting with H·, OH·, and O· radicals to terminate propagation.
     - Providing solid surfaces that catalytically recombine radicals.
   - The induction time is lengthened, suppressing auto-ignition.

4. **Reasons for phase-out (1.5 marks):**
   - **Toxicity:** Lead causes irreversible neurological damage, especially in children.
   - **Catalyst poisoning:** Pb permanently deactivates the platinum group metals in TWC.
   - **Environmental persistence:** Lead is non-biodegradable; accumulates in soil and the food chain.
   - **Regulatory:** Phased out globally from the 1970s onwards; India banned leaded petrol in 2000.

---

**Solution to B2(b) — Fuel Options for Emission Control (7 marks):**

1. **Reformulated Gasoline (RFG) (1.5 marks):** Lower RVP, reduced olefins/aromatics, lower sulphur; reduces evaporative HC and tailpipe toxic emissions by 15–25%.

2. **Oxygenates (1.5 marks):** MTBE, ETBE, and ethanol add bound oxygen, allowing leaner combustion. MTBE was withdrawn in the US due to groundwater contamination. Ethanol is now the preferred oxygenate.

3. **Low-sulphur fuels (1 mark):** Sulphur below 10 ppm protects the TWC and reduces SO₂ emissions.

4. **Lead phase-out (1 mark):** Removes catalyst poison, enabling closed-loop three-way control.

5. **Comparison table of E0 / E10 / E85 (2 marks):**

| Parameter | E0 (Pure Gasoline) | E10 (10% Ethanol) | E85 (85% Ethanol) |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| RON | 91–95 | 93–94 | 100–105 |
| Stoich AFR | 14.7 | 14.1 | 9.8 |
| Energy density (MJ/L) | 32 | 30.6 | 25.6 |
| Cold-start | Easy | Easy | Difficult (needs flex-fuel ECU) |
| CO emission | Baseline | −10% | −25 to −40% |
| NOₓ emission | Baseline | +5% | +5 to +15% |
| HC emission | Baseline | −5% | −20% |
| Material compat. | All | All | Requires SS, special hose |
| Vehicle use | All | All (FFV needed for >E20) | Flex-fuel only |

6. **Conclusion:** E10 is the immediate Indian sweet spot (compatible with all vehicles); E20 is the 2025 national target; E85 requires dedicated flex-fuel vehicles.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Avoid These Mistakes!**
>
> 1. **Stoichiometric AFR Value (–1 mark):** Students often write "AFR = 14.7" without stating *by mass* and *for gasoline*. Always specify the **fuel** and the **basis** (mass or molar).
>
> 2. **RON vs MON Confusion (–1.5 marks):** If asked for "octane number" in an Indian context, the answer is **RON**, not AKI. Do not mix the two.
>
> 3. **TEL Question — Don't Skip Health Impact (–1 mark):** Examiners award marks for the *health and environmental* dimensions of the lead phase-out, not just the catalyst-poisoning reason. Mention neurotoxicity explicitly.
>
> 4. **Refuelling Emissions vs Evaporative Emissions (–1 mark):** Use precise terminology. "Refuelling emissions are a *type* of evaporative emission, not a separate phenomenon." Avoid saying they are unrelated to EVAP systems.
>
> 5. **λ = 1 ± 0.01 — Cite the *units* (–1 mark):** A common error is writing "λ = 1 ± 0.01" without explaining that the corresponding AFR window is **14.55 to 14.85** (i.e., ±0.15).
>
> 6. **Use of 'γ' or 'θ' for θ in chemical equations (–0.5 mark):** Be careful with subscripts and superscripts. Always double-check the balancing: total C, H, O, and N atoms on both sides.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Refuelling emissions** = displaced saturated HC vapour from tank ullage; depends on RVP, temperature, fill volume.
- [x] **ORVR** + **Stage-II** reduce refuelling HC by **> 95%**.
- [x] **Fuel options** for emission control: RFG, oxygenates, low-sulphur, unleaded, alternative fuels.
- [x] **Gasoline engine** = spark-ignition, Otto cycle, AFR 14.7 (stoichiometric), CR 8–12.
- [x] **Three-way catalyst** operates only at **λ = 1.00 ± 0.01**; lambda sensor + ECU feedback maintains this.
- [x] **Cold-start HC** accounts for 60–80% of cycle emissions; mitigated by close-coupled catalyst.
- [x] **PCV system** returns crankcase blow-by gases to intake.
- [x] **Tetraethyl lead (TEL)** was an effective anti-knock additive, raising RON by 10–15 points at 0.3 g Pb/L.
- [x] **Lead banned in India since 2000**; reasons: neurotoxicity, catalyst poisoning, environmental persistence.
- [x] **Octane number** = % iso-octane in iso-octane/n-heptane blend matching knock intensity.
- [x] **RON** (mild) vs **MON** (severe) vs **AKI = (RON+MON)/2** (North America) vs **Posted Octane = RON** (India/Europe).
- [x] **Indian pump octane** = RON: regular 91, premium 95, ultra 97+.
- [x] **Sensitivity** = RON − MON; aromatics and alcohols have high sensitivity; paraffins low.
- [x] **Empirical relation:** $ON_{required} \approx 4(CR - 7) + 80$; CR 10 needs ~92 RON; CR 12 needs ~100 RON.
- [x] **Stoichiometric AFR values to memorise:** Gasoline 14.7, Diesel 14.5, Ethanol 9.0, Methanol 6.43, CNG 17.2, LPG 15.2.
- [x] **Combustion of iso-octane:** $C_8H_{18} + 12.5\,O_2 + 47\,N_2 \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O + 47\,N_2$.
- [x] **Modern BS-VI petrol:** sulphur ≤ 10 ppm, RON 91 or 95, mandatory ORVR + TWC, lead-free certified.
- [x] **E20 ethanol blending** is India's national target by 2025 (Ethanol Blended Petrol Programme).
- [x] **No fuel modification** can substitute for a properly functioning **catalyst + lambda sensor + ECU closed-loop**; the three must work together.
- [x] **Knock suppression mechanisms in modern fuels:** isomerisation, alkylation, catalytic reforming, ethanol blending, high-octane aromatics — all without lead.
- [x] **Key exam numbers to commit:** 14.7, 1.00, 91, 95, 10 ppm, 0.3 g/L, ±0.01, 95%, 60–80%.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Olefins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Olefins # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Olefins (Automotive Fuel Standards Context)** > Olefins, also known as **alkenes**, are a class of unsaturated aliphatic hydrocarbons containing one or more **carbon-carbon double bonds (C=C)** in their molecular structure. In the context of automotive fuel standards and emissions regulations, olefins refer specifically to the total concentration (expressed in **volume percent, vol %** or **weight percent, wt %**) of these unsaturated hydrocarbons present in finished motor gasoline/petrol. **General Molecular Formula:** - Mono-olefins (one double bond): **CₙH₂ₙ** - Di-olefins/diolefins (two double bonds): **CₙH₂ₙ₋₂** - Tri-olefins: **CₙH₂ₙ₋₄** **Examples Relevant to Gasoline Pool:** | Compound | Formula | Boiling Point (°C) | Octane Number (RON) | |----------|---------|--------------------|--------------------| | Ethylene | C₂H₄ | -103.7 | — | | Propylene | C₃H₆ | -47.6 | — | | 1-Butene | C₄H₈ | -6.3 | — | | Isobutylene | C₄H₈ | -6.9 | — | | 2-Pentene | C₅H₁₀ | 36.4 | ~95 | | 1-Hexene | C₆H₁₂ | 63.5 | ~76 | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy: "The Reactive Teenagers of the Fuel Family"** > Imagine the hydrocarbon molecules in petrol as people at a party. **Paraffins** (alkanes) are calm, well-behaved guests who sit quietly. **Aromatics** are the stylish, ring-shaped celebrities. **Olefins** are the *reactive teenagers* — they have a "double bond" (an extra connection) that makes them energetic, easily excited, and quick to react with other molecules. > > In the atmosphere, this energy translates into forming **ground-level ozone and smog** when sunlight hits them. Inside your engine, they tend to form **gummy deposits** on intake valves. Regulators (BIS, EPA, EU) therefore act like strict party bouncers — limiting how many "teenagers" (olefins) can enter the fuel. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Key Reference Values (Bolded for KTU High-Yield Recall)** > - **BIS IS 2796:2017 (India) limit:** **≤ 21.0 vol %** (BS-VI petrol) > - **Euro VI limit (EN 228):** **≤ 18.0 vol %** > - **California LEV-III / EPA Tier 3 limit:** **≤ 10 vol %** (for lowest sulfur gasoline) > - **Density of typical C₅–C₉ olefins:** **0.64 – 0.73 g/cm³** at 20 °C > - **Photochemical Reactivity (MIR scale, g O₃/g VOC):** Olefins score **3 – 6× higher** than paraffins > - **Heat of combustion:** **~ 45 – 47 MJ/kg** (slightly lower than paraffins) > - **Measurement standard:** **ASTM D1319** (FIA method) or **ASTM D6839** (GC method) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Origin of Olefins in Gasoline Olefins are introduced into the gasoline pool primarily through **secondary refining processes**, not from straight-run distillation of crude oil: 1. **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** — Largest contributor (~25–35% olefins in FCC naphtha) 2. **Steam Cracking** — Produces ethylene, propylene, butenes for petrochemicals 3. **Coking / Visbreaking** — Thermal cracking processes 4. **Catalytic Reforming** — Minor source (mostly converts paraffins → aromatics) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Regulators Target Olefins?** > Unlike paraffins and aromatics, olefins possess a **π-bond** (pi-bond) that is highly susceptible to cleavage by **NOₓ + UV radiation**, forming **ozone (O₃)**, **peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN)**, and other **photochemical oxidants**. This is quantified by the **Maximum Incremental Reactivity (MIR)** scale developed by Dr. William Carter (UC Riverside). ## 2.2 The Three Regulatory Concerns | Concern | Mechanism | Engineering Impact | |---------|-----------|-------------------| | **Photochemical Smog** | Olefins + NOₓ + UV → O₃, PAN | Respiratory illness, urban haze | | **Engine Deposits** | Olefins oxidize/polymerize at high T | Intake valve deposits, injector fouling | | **Catalyst Degradation** | Olefins generate exotherms on catalyst | Three-way catalyst (TWC) thermal aging | ## 2.3 Photochemical Reactivity Equation (Simplified) The atmospheric oxidation of a generic olefin (e.g., propene) follows: $$\text{C}_3\text{H}_6 + 4\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{UV, NO}_x} 3\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ With intermediate formation of: - **Ozone (O₃)** — primary pollutant of concern - **Formaldehyde (HCHO)** — toxic air contaminant - **Acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO)** — toxic air contaminant - **Peroxyacetyl Nitrate (PAN)** — lachrymator, phytotoxin ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Table for KTU 2024 ESE — Memorize These** | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units | KTU Significance | |---------------------|-----------|-------|------------------| | General alkene formula | CₙH₂ₙ | — | Identifies unsaturation | | Degree of unsaturation (DoU) | DoU = (2C + 2 − H − X + N) / 2 | dimensionless | Detect C=C bonds | | MIR (propene reference) | ~ 9.40 g O₃ / g VOC | g/g | Reference for reactivity scaling | | MIR (toluene reference) | ~ 4.00 g O₃ / g VOC | g/g | Compared vs. olefins | | Bromine number | g Br₂ / 100 g sample | g/100g | Measures total unsaturation | | FIA volume % olefins | ASTM D1319 column | vol % | Standard test method | | GC olefin content | ASTM D6839 / EN 14517 | wt % | Modern precise method | | Cetane sensitivity (olefinic) | Δ CN = CN_M − CN_I | dimensionless | Affects diesel ignition too | | Octane boost per 1% olefin | ~ +0.5 to +0.8 RON | RON/% | Why refiners like olefins | **⚠ Markdown Safety Note:** All absolute-value or set-membership symbols in formulas (e.g., \vert x \vert) are written using LaTeX `\vert` to prevent table-breaking. ## 2.5 Engineering Trade-off: The Refiner's Dilemma Olefins are a **double-edged sword** for refiners: - **PRO:** High research octane number (RON ≈ 92–98 for C₆–C₈ olefins) - **CON:** Capped by environmental regulations This is why modern refineries use **alkylation units** (producing iso-paraffins) and **isomerization** to replace FCC olefins with cleaner, high-octane alternatives — a direct response to standards like **BIS IS 2796, Euro VI, and EPA MSAT-II (Mobile Source Air Toxics)**. ## 2.6 Cross-Subject Utility Olefin regulation connects multiple KTU subjects: - **Fuel Technology** — refining processes - **Internal Combustion Engines** — combustion & deposits - **Environmental Engineering** — air quality modeling - **Polymer/Chemical Engineering** — feedstock chemistry # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Degree of Unsaturation (DoU) for Olefins The **Degree of Unsaturation (DoU)**, also called the **Index of Hydrogen Deficiency (IHD)**, is a fundamental analytical tool. Let us derive it from first principles. **Starting Assumption:** A fully saturated, acyclic alkane follows: $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \quad \text{(e.g., CH}_4, \text{C}_2\text{H}_6, \text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{)}$$ **Step 1:** For a generic hydrocarbon **CₐHᵦ**, the number of missing hydrogen pairs (relative to the saturated form) gives the number of double bonds + rings. **Step 2:** Compare actual H count to saturated H count: $$\Delta H = (2a + 2) - b$$ **Step 3:** Each missing pair = 1 unit of unsaturation. So: $$\text{DoU} = \frac{(2a + 2) - b}{2}$$ **Step 4 (Generalized with heteroatoms):** - Halogens (X) count as H (replace H) - Nitrogen (N) adds 1 to H count - Oxygen (O) is ignored $$\text{DoU} = \frac{2C + 2 + N - H - X}{2}$$ **Verification on Propene (C₃H₆):** $$\text{DoU}_{\text{C}_3\text{H}_6} = \frac{2(3) + 2 - 6}{2} = \frac{8 - 6}{2} = 1$$ ✅ Correct — one C=C double bond. **Verification on 1,3-Butadiene (C₄H₆):** $$\text{DoU}_{\text{C}_4\text{H}_6} = \frac{2(4) + 2 - 6}{2} = \frac{10 - 6}{2} = 2$$ ✅ Correct — two C=C double bonds. **Verification on Cyclohexene (C₆H₁₀):** $$\text{DoU}_{\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}} = \frac{2(6) + 2 - 10}{2} = \frac{14 - 10}{2} = 2$$ ✅ Correct — one C=C (1) + one ring (1) = 2. ## 3.2 Derivation: Bromine Number (Quantitative Olefin Measure) The **Bromine Number (BN)** quantifies total olefinic unsaturation: **Reaction Principle:** Each C=C consumes 1 mole of Br₂: $$\text{C}=\text{C} + \text{Br}_2 \rightarrow \text{CBr} - \text{CBr}$$ **Step 1:** Definition: $$\text{BN} = \frac{\text{mass of Br}_2 \text{ consumed}}{\text{mass of oil sample}} \times 100 \quad \text{[g Br}_2 / 100 \text{ g sample]}$$ **Step 2:** For a sample of mass **m (g)** with **n** moles of C=C bonds: $$n_{\text{Br}_2} = n_{\text{C=C}} = \frac{m_{\text{Br}_2}}{M_{\text{Br}_2}} = \frac{m_{\text{Br}_2}}{159.808}$$ **Step 3:** Hence: $$m_{\text{Br}_2} = n_{\text{C=C}} \times 159.808 \text{ g}$$ **Step 4:** Substituting back: $$\text{BN} = \frac{n_{\text{C=C}} \times 159.808 \times 100}{m_{\text{sample}}}$$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style) > **Problem (KTU University Exam - Jun 2023 style):** A gasoline sample weighs **2.50 g**. Complete bromination consumes **0.480 g of Br₂**. Calculate: (a) the Bromine Number, (b) the moles of C=C bonds, and (c) the average molecular weight assuming all unsaturation comes from mono-olefins of formula CₙH₂ₙ. **Solution:** **(a) Bromine Number:** $$\text{BN} = \frac{0.480}{2.50} \times 100 = 19.2 \text{ g Br}_2 / 100 \text{ g sample}$$ **(b) Moles of Br₂ consumed:** $$n_{\text{Br}_2} = \frac{0.480 \text{ g}}{159.808 \text{ g/mol}} = 3.004 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}$$ Since 1 C=C : 1 Br₂: $$n_{\text{C=C}} = 3.004 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}$$ **(c) Mass of olefinic fraction:** $$m_{\text{olefin}} = 2.50 \text{ g (assuming all unsaturated)}$$ **Molar mass of the average olefin:** $$M_{\text{avg olefin}} = \frac{m_{\text{olefin}}}{n_{\text{C=C}}} = \frac{2.50}{3.004 \times 10^{-3}} = 832.2 \text{ g/mol}$$ ⚠ This is unrealistically high → indicates the sample is only **partially olefinic** (consistent with real gasoline ~ 18 vol % olefins). **Correction — actual olefin mass fraction:** If olefin content is **21 vol %** with average olefin density **0.70 g/cm³** and gasoline density **0.74 g/cm³**: $$w_{\text{olefin}} = \frac{0.21 \times 0.70}{0.74} = 0.1986 \text{ (i.e., 19.86 wt \%)}$$ $$m_{\text{actual olefin}} = 0.1986 \times 2.50 = 0.4965 \text{ g}$$ $$M_{\text{avg olefin}} = \frac{0.4965}{3.004 \times 10^{-3}} = 165.3 \text{ g/mol} \approx \text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{24}$$ ✅ Realistic for the C₉–C₁₂ gasoline range. ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Test | Test Method | Standard | Principle | Best For | |-------------|----------|-----------|----------| | **FIA (Fluorescent Indicator Adsorption)** | ASTM D1319 | Silica-gel column separation into saturates, olefins, aromatics | Routine refinery QC | | **GC (Gas Chromatography)** | ASTM D6839, EN 14517 | PIONA speciation | Precise speciation, regulatory reporting | | **Bromine Number** | ASTM D1159, IP 130 | Titration with Br₂ | Quick unsaturation screening | | **FT-IR Spectroscopy** | ASTM D7576 | C=C stretch at 1640–1680 cm⁻¹ | Rapid, non-destructive | | **¹H-NMR** | In-house | Vinyl proton at 4.5–6.5 ppm | Research-grade identification | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Bromine Number (BN)** with **Bromine Index (BI)**. BN is in g Br₂/100 g; BI is in mg Br₂/100 g. Use BN for gasoline-range samples (high olefin), BI for lighter hydrocarbons like LPG. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Origin & Regulatory Chain of Olefins in Gasoline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Crude Oil Distillation] --> B[Straight-Run Naphtha
Low Olefins less than 1 vol%] C[Fluid Catalytic Cracking FCC] --> D[FCC Naphtha
High Olefins 25 to 35 vol%] E[Steam Cracking] --> F[Pyrolysis Gasoline
Very High Olefins 40 to 60 vol%] G[Catalytic Reforming] --> H[Reformate
Near Zero Olefins High Aromatics] B --> I[Gasoline Blender] D --> I F --> I H --> I I --> J[Finished Gasoline Pool
Regulated: less than 18 to 21 vol% Olefins] J --> K[BIS IS 2796 India] J --> L[Euro VI EN 228 EU] J --> M[EPA MSAT II USA] J --> N[Vehicle Engine and TWC] N --> O[Tailpipe Emissions
CO, NOx, HC, O3 precursors] O --> P[Urban Air Quality
Ozone and Smog] ``` ## 4.2 Block Architecture — Olefin's Atmospheric Reaction Pathway ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph EM[Engine Exhaust Aftertreatment] TWC[Three Way Catalyst TWC] end subgraph AT[Atmosphere] OLEFIN[Olefin RCH equals CH2] NOX[NOx from Engine] UV[UV Radiation from Sun] RAD[Alkyl Radicals R dot] RO2[Peroxy Radicals RO2 dot] NO2[NO2] O3[Ozone O3] PAN[Peroxyacetyl Nitrate PAN] HCHO[Formaldehyde HCHO] end TWC -->|Slip unburned olefins| OLEFIN OLEFIN -->|Photo-dissociation| RAD UV --> RAD RAD --> RO2 RO2 -->|Oxidize NO to NO2| NOX NO2 -->|Photolysis| O3 RO2 -->|With NO2| PAN RAD --> HCHO ``` ## 4.3 Olefin Specification Pyramid — Regulatory Limits by Region ```mermaid flowchart TD TOP[Global Automotive Olefins Regulation Pyramid] TOP --> T1[Most Stringent
California LEV III and EPA Tier 3
Limit less than 10 vol%] TOP --> T2[Highly Stringent
Euro VI and Bharat Stage VI
Limit 18 vol%] TOP --> T3[Moderately Stringent
Euro IV and Bharat Stage IV
Limit 21 to 24 vol%] TOP --> T4[Historical Baseline
Euro II and Earlier
No Olefins Cap] T1 --> M1[Measurement: GC PIONA ASTM D6839] T2 --> M1 T3 --> M2[Measurement: FIA ASTM D1319] T4 --> M2 ``` ## 4.4 Process Flow: Olefins from Crude to Tailpipe ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Crude Oil Enters Refinery] --> S2[Step 2: Atmospheric Distillation
Yields Straight Run Naphtha] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Secondary Conversion Units] S3 --> S4A[FCC Unit - High Olefins] S3 --> S4B[Alkylation Unit - Zero Olefins] S3 --> S4C[Isomerization Unit - Zero Olefins] S4A --> S5[Step 4: Blending Pool
Olefin Content 18 to 21 vol%] S4B --> S5 S4C --> S5 S5 --> S6[Step 5: Storage and Distribution] S6 --> S7[Step 6: Vehicle Fuel Tank] S7 --> S8[Step 7: Engine Combustion Chamber] S8 --> S9[Step 8: Tailpipe and TWC Aftertreatment] S9 --> S10[Step 9: Atmospheric Release
Olefin reacts with NOx to form O3] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** All node IDs (S1, S2, S4A, etc.) are alphanumeric and avoid reserved Mermaid keywords. Labels are plain uppercase text — no bold or italics tags inside the double-quoted node labels — per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **Define olefins. Why are they regulated in automotive gasoline under Euro VI norms?** **Model Answer:** > **Definition (1 mark):** Olefins are unsaturated aliphatic hydrocarbons containing one or more carbon-carbon double bonds (C=C), with the general formula **CₙH₂ₙ** for mono-olefins. > **Regulatory Reasoning (2 marks):** Olefins are highly **photochemically reactive** in the atmosphere. In presence of **NOₓ** and **UV radiation**, they undergo complex oxidation cycles producing **ground-level ozone (O₃)**, **PAN (peroxyacetyl nitrate)**, and **aldehydes** — the primary constituents of **photochemical smog**. Euro VI caps olefins at **≤ 18 vol %** to: > - Reduce urban ozone formation (MIR ≈ 3–9 g O₃/g olefin) > - Minimize engine intake-valve deposits > - Protect three-way catalyst (TWC) from thermal aging > - Align with WHO air quality guidelines --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2023]** **List any three standard test methods for determining olefin content in gasoline. State the principle of one.** **Model Answer:** | Method | Standard | Principle | |--------|----------|-----------| | **FIA** | ASTM D1319 | Adsorption chromatography on silica gel; olefins elute with fluorescent indicator dye. | | **GC** | ASTM D6839 | Gas chromatography with PIONA (Paraffins, Isoparaffins, Olefins, Naphthenes, Aromatics) speciation. | | **Bromine Number** | ASTM D1159 | Titration with Br₂; each C=C consumes 1 mol Br₂. | | **FT-IR** | ASTM D7576 | Mid-IR absorption at 1640–1680 cm⁻¹ (C=C stretch). | **Principle of FIA (2 marks):** A small sample is passed through a silica-gel column with fluorescent dyes. **Saturates** move to the top, **olefins** form the middle yellow band, and **aromatics** form the bottom red/purple band. The **length of each band** is proportional to its volume percent in the sample. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU ESE 2024):** Students answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **origin, classification, and chemical structure** of olefins present in automotive gasoline. Discuss their **role in atmospheric photochemical smog formation** with relevant equations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the **olefin content specifications** prescribed under **BIS IS 2796 (BS-VI)** and **Euro VI (EN 228)**. What are the **engine and aftertreatment effects** of high olefin content? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Answer for (a):** **Origin (2 marks):** Olefins in gasoline are predominantly produced by: - **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC):** Largest source; FCC gasoline contains 25–35 vol % olefins. Uses zeolite (ZSM-5, USY) catalysts at 480–540 °C. - **Steam Cracking / Pyrolysis:** 40–60 vol % olefins; primarily for petrochemical feed. - **Coking and Visbreaking:** Thermal cracking; minor contributors. **Classification (2 marks):** - **Mono-olefins** (CₙH₂ₙ) — one C=C: e.g., 1-pentene, iso-octene - **Di-olefins / Diolefins** (CₙH₂ₙ₋₂) — two C=C: e.g., 1,3-butadiene (regulated as toxic air contaminant) - **Cyclic olefins** — e.g., cyclohexene - Based on substitution: **α-olefins** (terminal C=C) vs **internal olefins** **Smog Formation (3 marks):** The Carter-Atkinson atmospheric reaction mechanism: $$\text{RCH=CH}_2 + \text{OH}^\bullet \rightarrow \text{RCH}^\bullet\text{-CH}_2\text{OH} \quad \text{(rate-determining step)}$$ $$\text{RCH}^\bullet\text{-CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{RCH(OO}^\bullet\text{)-CH}_2\text{OH}$$ $$\text{RCH(OO}^\bullet\text{)-CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{RCH(O}^\bullet\text{)-CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{NO}_2$$ $$\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}$$ $$\text{O} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{O}_3$$ The **MIR (Maximum Incremental Reactivity)** for propene is **~ 9.40 g O₃/g VOC**, making olefins the most reactive hydrocarbon class in gasoline. --- #### **Model Answer for (b):** **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Parameter | BIS IS 2796 (BS-VI) | Euro VI (EN 228) | |-----------|--------------------|------------------| | Max olefins | **21.0 vol %** | **18.0 vol %** | | Max aromatics | 35.0 vol % | 35.0 vol % | | Max benzene | 1.0 vol % | 1.0 vol % | | Sulfur | 10 ppm | 10 ppm | | Test method | EN 14517 / ASTM D6839 | EN 14517 | **Engine Effects (2 marks):** - **Intake Valve Deposits (IVD):** Olefins polymerize at 200–300 °C in the intake port. - **Combustion Chamber Deposits (CCD):** Olefins contribute to octane requirement increase (ORI). - **Injector Fouling:** Olefin oxidation products (peroxides, acids) attack injector seals. **Aftertreatment Effects (2 marks):** - **TWC Exotherm:** Olefins + O₂ over Pt/Pd/Rh catalyst release ~ 1000–1500 °C localized heat, sintering the washcoat. - **NOₓ Storage Reduction (NSR) Deactivation:** Diolefins poison BaCO₃ trap sites. - **DPF Soot Loading:** Higher HC slip → secondary soot formation. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]** **(a)** Derive the **general formula for the Degree of Unsaturation (DoU)** for a hydrocarbon CₐHᵦ. Apply it to determine the DoU of **(i)** propene (C₃H₆) and **(ii)** 1,3-butadiene (C₄H₆). **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **FIA (Fluorescent Indicator Adsorption) method** as per **ASTM D1319** for olefin determination. Mention its **advantages and limitations** versus GC methods. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Answer for (a):** **Derivation (4 marks):** Starting with the saturated acyclic alkane formula: $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \quad \text{(Reference saturated form)}$$ For a real hydrocarbon CₐHᵦ, the hydrogen deficiency: $$\Delta H = (2a + 2) - b$$ Each unit of unsaturation (one C=C or one ring) removes 2 H atoms from the saturated form. So: $$\text{DoU} = \frac{(2a + 2) - b}{2} = \frac{2a + 2 - b}{2}$$ For a generic CₐHᵦOᵧNᵨXₓ compound: $$\text{DoU} = \frac{2a + 2 + c - b - x}{2}$$ where: a = C, b = H, c = N, x = halogen; O is ignored. **Application 1 — Propene (C₃H₆) (1.5 marks):** $$\text{DoU}_{\text{C}_3\text{H}_6} = \frac{2(3) + 2 - 6}{2} = \frac{8 - 6}{2} = 1$$ ✅ **One C=C double bond.** **Application 2 — 1,3-Butadiene (C₄H₆) (1.5 marks):** $$\text{DoU}_{\text{C}_4\text{H}_6} = \frac{2(4) + 2 - 6}{2} = \frac{10 - 6}{2} = 2$$ ✅ **Two C=C double bonds.** --- #### **Model Answer for (b):** **Principle of FIA (3 marks):** ASTM D1319 uses a glass chromatographic column packed with **silica gel** of controlled activity. A small gasoline sample (~ 0.5 mL) is introduced at the top, then **isopropyl alcohol** is added to develop the column. Three distinct colored bands form: 1. **Top — Saturates (paraffins + naphthenes):** Colorless or faint yellow 2. **Middle — Olefins:** **Bright yellow** band (revealed by fluorescent dye, e.g., 2,6-di-tert-butyl-p-cresol) 3. **Bottom — Aromatics:** Red/purple band (revealed by dye such as Rhodamine B) The **length of each colored zone**, measured to ±0.5 mm, is proportional to the **volume percent** of each hydrocarbon class in the sample. Total recovery must be 95–100 %; otherwise the test is invalid. **Procedure Summary (2 marks):** 1. Pack column with activated silica gel (2 h at 175 °C) 2. Pre-wet with isopropyl alcohol 3. Inject 0.5 mL sample 4. Add alcohol to develop bands 5. Measure band lengths with vernier caliper 6. Calculate vol % from length ratios **Advantages vs. GC (1 mark):** - Low capital cost (~ ₹ 50,000 vs. ₹ 50 lakh for GC) - Fast (~ 30 min per test) - Suitable for routine refinery QC **Limitations vs. GC (1 mark):** - **Poor accuracy for diolefins** (they co-elute with aromatics) - **Cannot distinguish individual olefins** (no speciation) - **Subjective visual band reading** — operator-dependent - **Errors ± 2–3 vol %** vs. ± 0.1 vol % for GC > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistakes** > 1. **DoU Derivation Trap:** Students often write the formula but skip the **derivation logic** (why divide by 2). Examiners award full 4 marks only if the "saturated alkane reference" argument is explicitly stated. > 2. **FIA Trap:** Many confuse the **band order**. Remember: **Top = Saturates, Middle = Olefins, Bottom = Aromatics**. Reversing this is an instant 0.5 mark deduction. > 3. **MIR vs. Reactivity Confusion:** Do NOT equate MIR with toxicity. MIR measures **ozone-forming potential**, not direct human toxicity. > 4. **Units Slip:** Vol % vs wt % confusion. ASTM D1319 reports **vol %**; ASTM D6839 may report **wt %**. Always check the standard. > 5. **Forgetting the "Reference" in DoU:** A DoU calculation without stating the saturated reference CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ will lose 1 mark. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Memory Bank — Olefins** - [x] **Definition:** Unsaturated hydrocarbons with C=C; general formula CₙH₂ₙ (mono-olefins) - [x] **Origin:** Predominantly from **FCC** unit (25–35 vol %); also steam cracking, coking - [x] **BIS IS 2796 (BS-VI) limit:** **≤ 21.0 vol %** - [x] **Euro VI (EN 228) limit:** **≤ 18.0 vol %** - [x] **EPA Tier 3 / California limit:** **≤ 10 vol %** - [x] **DoU formula:** DoU = (2C + 2 + N − H − X) / 2 (Oxygen ignored) - [x] **MIR (propene) ≈ 9.40 g O₃/g VOC** — highest among hydrocarbon classes - [x] **Bromine Number units:** g Br₂ / 100 g sample - [x] **FIA method (ASTM D1319):** Silica-gel column; band order = saturates (top) → olefins (middle, yellow) → aromatics (bottom) - [x] **GC method (ASTM D6839):** PIONA speciation; ±0.1 vol % accuracy - [x] **Stoichiometry:** 1 C=C : 1 Br₂ - [x] **Engine concern:** Intake valve deposits (IVD), combustion chamber deposits (CCD), octane requirement increase (ORI) - [x] **Aftertreatment concern:** TWC exotherm, NSR deactivation, DPF soot loading - [x] **Refining response:** Replace FCC olefins via **alkylation** + **isomerization** (zero-olefin high-octane routes) - [x] **Photochemical smog equation:** Olefin + OH• → alkyl radical → RO₂• + NO → NO₂ → NO₂ + hν → O + O₂ → O₃ - [x] **Williams–Carter reactivity scale:** Olefins > aromatics > paraffins in atmospheric reactivity - [x] **Bromine Number vs. Index:** BN = g/100g (heavy); BI = mg/100g (light hydrocarbons) - [x] **α-olefins** (terminal) vs **internal olefins** — chemistry differs slightly - [x] **Diolefins (e.g., 1,3-butadiene)** — separately regulated as toxic air contaminant - [x] **Cross-link to other modules:** Fuel standards (Module 2), Emission norms (Module 4), Refining (Module 5)

topics.title: Aromatic hydrocarbons and diesel engines: cetene number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Aromatic hydrocarbons and diesel engines: cetene number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Aromatic Hydrocarbons – Refresher

**Aromatic hydrocarbons** are a class of organic compounds characterised by the presence of one or more planar, cyclic, conjugated **benzene rings** ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_6$ backbone). They follow **Hückel's Rule** of $(4n+2)\,\pi$ electrons and exhibit unusual stability due to resonance delocalisation.

| Family Member | Formula | Rings | Boiling Point (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Benzene | $\text{C}_6\text{H}_6$ | 1 (mono) | 80.1 |
| Toluene | $\text{C}_7\text{H}_8$ | 1 (mono) | 110.6 |
| Xylenes | $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{10}$ | 1 (mono) | 138 – 144 |
| Naphthalene | $\text{C}_{10}\text{H}_8$ | 2 (poly) | 218 |
| Anthracene | $\text{C}_{14}\text{H}_{10}$ | 3 (poly) | 340 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In diesel fuel standards, aromatics are split into **Mono-Aromatic Hydrocarbons (MAH)** and **Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAH)**. PAH (≥ 2 fused rings) are the *most* harmful — both for engine deposits and for human carcinogenicity of exhaust soot.

## 1.2 Diesel Engine Combustion — The Need for a Quality Index

A **Compression Ignition (CI) engine** has no spark plug. The fuel is injected into air that has been compressed to ~ 30 – 55 bar and heated to ~ 700 – 900 K. Combustion therefore depends entirely on the fuel's ability to **spontaneously auto-ignite** after a short **ignition delay period** (the time between the start of injection and the start of combustion).

**Key definition (KTU syllabus):**
> The **Cetane Number (CN)** — historically also called *Cetene Number* in early (pre-1950) literature — is the dimensionless measure of a diesel fuel's **auto-ignition quality**, i.e., the inverse of its ignition delay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical note (exam-favourite):** The name originates from **cetene** ($\text{C}_{16}\text{H}_{32}$, an alkene) which was originally used as the high-quality reference. It was later replaced by the saturated alkane **n-cetane** (n-hexadecane, $\text{C}_{16}\text{H}_{34}$), and the modern standard **ASTM D613 / ISO 5165** uses n-cetane. The two terms are treated as synonyms in current KTU/automotive usage.

## 1.3 The Cetane Number Scale

The scale is **arbitrary but linear**, fixed by two reference fuels:

$$ \text{n-Cetane } (\text{n-C}_{16}\text{H}_{34}) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{CN} = 100 \quad (\text{short delay, ignites easily}) $$

$$ \alpha\text{-Methylnaphthalene } (\text{C}_{11}\text{H}_{10}) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{CN} = 0 \quad (\text{long delay, resists ignition}) $$

A fuel with $\text{CN} = X$ behaves, in a standard CFR engine, exactly like a blend of $X\,\%$ n-cetane and $(100-X)\,\% \alpha$-methylnaphthalene.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Popcorn Test"

Imagine two saucepans of popcorn kernels on identical hot plates:

* **n-Cetane (CN 100):** The kernels pop the **moment** they touch the hot plate — almost no waiting. The piston "sees" a controlled, fast pressure rise.
* **α-Methylnaphthalene (CN 0):** The kernels sit on the hot plate for several seconds, accumulating heat, and then **all pop violently at once** — a pressure shock-wave (engine knock / diesel knock) and a lot of unburned residue (smoke).
* **A typical diesel (CN ≈ 50):** A balanced, fast, predictable pop. ⏱

**Aromatic hydrocarbons behave like the second pan** — they sit and wait, then explode. This is *exactly* why high aromatic content = poor diesel quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU:**
> Longer, straighter, saturated chains → **higher** CN.
> More rings, branching, unsaturation → **lower** CN.
> Order: **n-paraffins > iso-paraffins > olefins > naphthenes > aromatics**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Aromatics Resist Auto-Ignition — The Chemistry

1. **High C : H ratio** — Aromatics like naphthalene have C:H ≈ 1.25, while n-cetane has 0.5. Less hydrogen means fewer free radicals per unit mass are available for the chain-branching reactions that drive auto-ignition.
2. **Resonance stability of the benzene ring** — Delocalised π-electrons resist homolytic cleavage, delaying the formation of the OH• and HO₂• radicals needed for cool-flame and hot-flame ignition.
3. **High bond-dissociation energy of ring C–H bonds** (~ 460 kJ mol⁻¹) vs. paraffinic C–H (~ 410 kJ mol⁻¹) → more thermal energy is needed to start the radical pool.
4. **High auto-ignition temperature** — Naphthalene ~ 1050 K vs. n-cetane ~ 475 K.

## 2.2 Effect of Aromatic Content on Engine Performance

| Parameter | High Aromatic Content | Low Aromatic Content |
|---|---|---|
| Ignition delay | Long | Short |
| Peak pressure rise rate | Sharp (knocking) | Smooth |
| Cold starting | Poor | Good |
| Smoke (PM) emission | **High** | Low |
| NOx emission | Slightly higher | Lower |
| Specific fuel consumption | Higher | Lower |
| Engine deposits | Higher | Lower |
| Thermal efficiency | Lower | Higher |

## 2.3 Cetane Number Requirements by Engine Class

| Application | Minimum CN Required |
|---|---|
| Light-duty automotive (BS-VI / Euro 6) | **51 – 55** |
| Heavy-duty trucks (BS-VI) | **48 – 52** |
| High-speed marine / locomotive | **45 – 50** |
| Medium-speed industrial | **35 – 45** |
| Low-speed (large marine) | **25 – 35** |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All equations below are exam-grade. Memorise the boxed ones; the rest are derivable.

**① Linear Blending of Cetane Number**
For a blend of $i$ streams with volume fractions $V_i$ and individual CN$_i$:
$$\boxed{\;\text{CN}_{\text{blend}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n}\, V_i \cdot \text{CN}_i \quad;\quad \sum V_i = 1\;}$$

**② Cetane Index (CI) — ASTM D976 (4-variable)**
$$\boxed{\;\text{CI} \;=\; -0.4748 \;+\; 0.000388\,T_{10} \;+\; 0.000555\,T_{50} \;+\; 0.000193\,T_{90} \;+\; 8.10\,^{\circ}\!\text{API}\;}$$
where $T_{10},T_{50},T_{90}$ are the °C at which **10, 50 and 90 %** of fuel distils.

**③ API Gravity from Specific Gravity**
$$\boxed{\;^{\circ}\!\text{API} \;=\; \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{60/60^\circ\text{F}}} \;-\; 131.5\;}$$

**④ Approximate CN ↔ Ignition Delay (ID, in crank-angle degrees)**
$$\text{ID} \;\approx\; \dfrac{K}{\text{CN}}$$
where $K$ is a constant depending on engine speed and load. This is a *qualitative* relationship only.

**⑤ Aniline Point ↔ CN (correlation)**
Lower aniline point $\Rightarrow$ higher aromatic content $\Rightarrow$ **lower** CN.

**⑥ Ignition Delay Components (Wolfer / Arrhenius form)**
$$\tau_{\text{id}} \;=\; A\,\phi^{-n}\,e^{\,E_a/RT_c}$$
where $\tau_{\text{id}}$ = ignition delay, $\phi$ = equivalence ratio, $T_c$ = compressed gas temperature. Higher $T_c$ (from higher CN fuel / earlier combustion) collapses $\tau_{\text{id}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> In all the above tables, use $\vert$ instead of $\,\mid\,$ if you are writing absolute value. Example: $\vert \Delta \text{CN} \vert \le 4$ means the difference between CI and measured CN should be within 4 units.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Refinery blending engineers** use Eqn. ① every day to certify diesel batches meet the BIS 1460 / EN 590 minimum CN of 51.
* **Additive chemists** (e.g., Innospec, BASF, Lubrizol) use 2-EHN (2-ethylhexyl nitrate) to lift CN by 3 – 8 numbers.
* **Emission-control legislators** cap PAH at **11 % wt** (EN 590) and total aromatics at **≤ 30 – 35 %** because of the well-known soot ↔ PAH correlation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Linear Blending Rule for CN

**Assumption:** Ignition delay is a linear, additive function of the mole/volume fractions of components, because the radical-pool kinetics superimpose.

Let the blend contain $V_A$ fraction of fuel A (CN$_A$) and $V_B$ fraction of fuel B (CN$_B$). By linearity of the delay parameter:

$$\text{ID}_{\text{blend}} \;=\; V_A\cdot \text{ID}_A \;+\; V_B\cdot \text{ID}_B$$

Using the qualitative relation $\text{ID} \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{CN}}$ and normalising to the same constant $K$:

$$\dfrac{K}{\text{CN}_{\text{blend}}} \;=\; V_A\cdot \dfrac{K}{\text{CN}_A} \;+\; V_B\cdot \dfrac{K}{\text{CN}_B}$$

Cancelling $K$ and re-arranging:

$$\dfrac{1}{\text{CN}_{\text{blend}}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_A}{\text{CN}_A} \;+\; \dfrac{V_B}{\text{CN}_B}$$

**However**, experimentally CN is *not* perfectly hyperbolic — it is **empirically linear** in volume fraction. Hence the working KTU/industry formula is the linear one (Eqn. ①):

$$\boxed{\;\text{CN}_{\text{blend}} \;=\; V_A\,\text{CN}_A \;+\; V_B\,\text{CN}_B\;}$$

The linear formula is the one used in **all KTU numerical problems and BIS/EN standards**.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Cetane Number by Blending

> **Problem (KTU Module-3 typical):** A refinery blends 60 % straight-run diesel (CN 58) with 25 % light cycle oil (CN 30) and 15 % kerosene (CN 45). Calculate the cetane number of the blend.

**Step 1 — Identify the streams and verify volume fractions sum to 1.**

$$V_1 + V_2 + V_3 = 0.60 + 0.25 + 0.15 = 1.00 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Apply the linear blending rule.**

$$\text{CN}_{\text{blend}} = V_1\,\text{CN}_1 + V_2\,\text{CN}_2 + V_3\,\text{CN}_3$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$\text{CN}_{\text{blend}} = (0.60)(58) + (0.25)(30) + (0.15)(45)$$

$$\text{CN}_{\text{blend}} = 34.80 + 7.50 + 6.75$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{CN}_{\text{blend}} = 49.05 \;\approx\; 49\;}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** A blend CN of 49 is *borderline* for BS-VI (≥ 51). The refiner must either (a) add 2-EHN cetane improver, or (b) reduce the proportion of LCO (which is high in aromatics).

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Cetane Index by ASTM D976

> **Problem:** A diesel sample has the following data. Compute the Cetane Index.
> * Distillation: $T_{10}=210\,°\text{C},\;T_{50}=275\,°\text{C},\;T_{90}=340\,°\text{C}$
> * Specific gravity at 15.6 °C = 0.860

**Step 1 — Convert SG to °API (Eqn. ③).**

$$^{\circ}\!\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{0.860} - 131.5$$

$$^{\circ}\!\text{API} = 164.535 - 131.5 = 33.04$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the CI formula (Eqn. ②).**

$$\text{CI} = -0.4748 + 0.000388\,T_{10} + 0.000555\,T_{50} + 0.000193\,T_{90} + 8.10\,^{\circ}\!\text{API}$$

$$\text{CI} = -0.4748 + (0.000388)(210) + (0.000555)(275) + (0.000193)(340) + (8.10)(33.04)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term.**

$$0.000388 \times 210 = 0.08148$$
$$0.000555 \times 275 = 0.15263$$
$$0.000193 \times 340 = 0.06562$$
$$8.10 \times 33.04 = 267.624$$

**Step 4 — Sum.**

$$\text{CI} = -0.4748 + 0.08148 + 0.15263 + 0.06562 + 267.624$$

$$\text{CI} = 267.449 \;\;\text{(this exceeds 100 — the form is invalid!)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The version of ASTM D976 with the **5-term linear form** is a *mid-distillate correlation valid only* for $T_{50}$ in the range 140 – 320 °C and °API in the range 30 – 60. For high-distillation heavy diesels, the **ASTM D4737 (two-variable)** form using density and $T_{50}$ is preferred, and our input $T_{90}=340\,°\text{C}$ lies *outside* the standard's domain. In KTU, if such numbers appear, examiners expect you to use the **simplified two-variable form**:
