# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Format:** Each Part A question carries **3 marks**, expected answer length 80–120 words. KTU 2024 scheme mandates at least one CO-level question per module in Part A.

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]

**Q: Define *semi-integral bus body construction* and list three advantages over conventional body-on-chassis construction.**

**Model Answer:**

Semi-integral bus body construction is a type of bus body in which the passenger superstructure is **welded to the chassis frame** (typically to outriggers or cruciform reinforcements), so that **40–60 % of the bending and torsional loads are shared by the body shell** in addition to the chassis rails, while the remaining 40–60 % continues to be reacted by the chassis.

Three advantages over conventional construction are:
1. **Lower kerb weight** (10–15 % reduction), improving fuel economy and payload.
2. **Lower floor height** (entry step 400–600 mm vs. 600–800 mm), aiding passenger accessibility in city service.
3. **Higher torsional rigidity** (typically 30,000–45,000 Nm/deg), improving NVH and rollover survival space.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for naming the load-sharing mechanism, 1 for the three correctly-typed advantages.)*

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]

**Q: What is the role of AIS-052 in the context of Indian bus body building? Name two key constructional parameters it specifies.**

**Model Answer:**

**AIS-052 (Rev 1): 2017 — Automotive Vehicles — Bus Body Design and Construction Requirements** is the **Type Approval standard** notified under **CMVR Rule 124 (3)** which prescribes mandatory minimum constructional and safety requirements for all buses manufactured or imported into India. It is enforced by testing agencies **ARAI (Pune), iCAT (Manesar) and VRDE (Ahmednagar)**.

Two key constructional parameters specified by AIS-052 are:
1. **Floor strength and anti-skid coefficient** (anti-skid flooring with minimum coefficient of friction ≥ 0.5 under wet condition).
2. **Superstructure integrity** — minimum yield strength of primary load-bearing steel members (≥ 210 MPa ordinary routes, ≥ 240 MPa hilly / ghat routes) and minimum section sizes for pillars, rails, and girth frames.

*(3 marks — 1 for the role/notification, 1 for naming AIS-052 correctly, 1 for the two parameters.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question is for **14 marks**, has an internal choice (OR), and is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Sub-part (a) is usually descriptive; sub-part (b) is either descriptive, comparative, or numerical.

---

### Question B.1(A) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]

**Q: (a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, explain the **three types of bus body construction** with reference to load path, materials used, and Indian OEM examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **major structural elements** (longitudinal, transverse, vertical) of a typical semi-integral bus body. State the AIS-052 minimum requirements for each.

#### Model Solution for B.1(A)(a) — Three Types of Bus Body Construction

The three types of bus body construction recognised under **AIS-052 (Rev 1): 2017** are:

**1. Conventional (Body-on-Chassis) Construction**

In this, the body is a **non-stressed cage** of mild-steel rectangular hollow sections (RHS) / square hollow sections (SHS) bolted to a separate ladder chassis frame. **Load path:** Road → Axles → Chassis Rails → Body Mounts → Body Skeleton. The chassis carries **100 % of bending and torsional loads**. Examples: **Tata LP 1512 BS-VI, Ashok Leyland Viking BS-VI, Eicher Skyline 3012**. Advantages: low cost, easy repair. Disadvantages: high kerb weight, low floor rigidity.

**2. Semi-Integral (Semi-Monocoque) Construction**

Body skeleton is **welded to the chassis frame** via outriggers, sharing the loads. **Load path:** Road → Axles → Chassis + Body Shell (jointly, ~50:50). Examples: **Ashok Leyland Lynx Smart, Tata Starbus EV (diesel), Eicher Skyline Pro 3016**. Advantages: 10–15 % lower kerb weight, lower floor height, higher torsional stiffness.

**3. Integral / Monocoque Construction**

**Chassis is eliminated**; body is a stressed-skin monocoque. **Load path:** Road → Axles → Sub-frames → Body Shell (100 %). Examples: **Tata Starbus Ultra, AL Oyster, BYD K9 electric, Optare Solo**. Advantages: lowest weight, lowest floor (≤ 400 mm), best NVH, best crash energy management. Disadvantages: highest tooling cost.

*(7 marks — 1 each for definition, load path, advantages + 1 for OEM examples, 1 for sketch indication, 1 for overall comparison.)*

#### Model Solution for B.1(A)(b) — Structural Elements of Semi-Integral Body

**Longitudinal members:**
- **Sill rail (bottom side member):** 75 × 38 × 4 mm RHS minimum, IS 2062, primary load transfer to chassis outriggers.
- **Waist rail:** at window-sill level, typically 50 × 25 × 2 mm RHS, resists side-impact bending.
- **Cant rail:** at roof-side junction, 50 × 25 × 2 mm RHS, supports roof bows.
- **Crown rail / Roof rail:** top longitudinal, ties roof bows.

**Transverse members:**
- **Girth frames:** at 600–760 mm pitch, formed of 25 × 25 × 2 mm SHS or press-formed channel, maintain body cross-section.
- **Floor cross members:** 50 × 25 × 2 mm RHS, carry passenger UDL, span between sill rails.
- **Roof bows:** 40 × 25 × 2 mm RHS, arched, carry AIS-046 roof crush load (1.5 × GVW).
- **Front / rear bulkheads:** full-depth, take frontal / rear crash pulse.

**Vertical members (Pillars):**
- **A, B, C, D pillars** of 50 × 25 × 2 mm RHS minimum, support roof and resist rollover intrusion.

**AIS-052 minimum requirements:**
- Yield strength of primary steel ≥ 210 MPa (ordinary), ≥ 240 MPa (ghat).
- Floor deflection under full load ≤ L/300.
- Roof crush survival space post-test ≥ 1,440 mm.
- Skin panel thickness 0.8 mm (roof) to 1.6 mm (floor).

*(7 marks — 2 for longitudinal, 2 for transverse, 2 for vertical, 1 for AIS-052 minimum values.)*

---

### Question B.1(B) [Alternative Choice for Internal OR — KTU 2024]

**Q: (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **mandatory provisions of AIS-052 (Rev 1)** for bus body construction. Include provisions for superstructure strength, flooring, and emergency exits.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A roof bow of a 12 m city bus is a simply supported beam of span 2.4 m carrying a uniformly distributed load of 4.5 kN/m. The bow is a rectangular hollow section 50 × 30 × 2 mm of steel ($E = 2.1 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm², $\sigma_{allow} = 165$ MPa). Calculate: (i) Maximum bending stress, (ii) Maximum deflection, and (iii) State whether the section satisfies AIS-052 deflection limit of L/300.

#### Model Solution for B.1(B)(a) — AIS-052 Mandatory Provisions

**AIS-052 (Rev 1): 2017** prescribes the following key mandatory provisions:

**1. Superstructure Strength:**
- All primary load-bearing members must be of steel with yield strength ≥ 210 MPa for ordinary routes and ≥ 240 MPa for ghat / hilly routes (per IS 2062).
- Roof crush test under AIS-046: applied load = 1.5 × GVW; survival space post-test ≥ 1,440 mm from intrusion.
- Floor must withstand a UDL of 4.71 kN/m² (200 kg/m²) without exceeding deflection of L/300.
- Pillars and girth frames must have minimum section modulus to ensure rollover survival.

**2. Flooring:**
- Floor surface must be **anti-skid** with coefficient of friction ≥ 0.5 under wet conditions.
- Material may be **18 mm phenol-bonded plywood (IS 303) + 2 mm PVC** OR **1.5 mm aluminium chequered plate**.
- Floor must be sealed to prevent ingress of water and exhaust gases.

**3. Emergency Exits (per AIS-063 read with AIS-052):**
- Minimum **4 emergency exits** for buses carrying more than 22 passengers, comprising **2 doors + 2 windows / roof hatches**.
- Each exit must be operable from inside and outside, with clearly visible signage.
- Emergency hammers and breaking glasses must be provided at every emergency window.

*(7 marks — 2 each for the three sub-headings + 1 for cross-reference to AIS-046 / AIS-063.)*

#### Model Solution for B.1(B)(b) — Roof Bow Numerical

**Given:** $L = 2.4$ m $= 2{,}400$ mm, $w = 4.5$ kN/m $= 4.5$ N/mm, RHS 50 × 30 × 2 mm, $E = 2.1 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm², $\sigma_{allow} = 165$ MPa.

**Step 1 — Section properties of RHS 50 × 30 × 2 mm**

$$I = \frac{50 \times 30^{3}}{12} - \frac{46 \times 26^{3}}{12} = \frac{50 \times 27{,}000}{12} - \frac{46 \times 17{,}576}{12}$$

$$I = 112{,}500 - 67{,}374.67 = 45{,}125.33 \; \text{mm}^{4}$$

Section modulus:

$$Z = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{45{,}125.33}{15} = 3{,}008.36 \; \text{mm}^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum bending moment (simply supported, UDL)**

$$M_{max} = \frac{w \cdot L^{2}}{8} = \frac{4.5 \times (2400)^{2}}{8} = \frac{4.5 \times 5{,}760{,}000}{8} = 3.24 \times 10^{6} \; \text{N·mm}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum bending stress (i)**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{3.24 \times 10^{6}}{3{,}008.36} = 1{,}077.0 \; \text{MPa}$$

**Conclusion (i):** $\sigma_{max} = 1{,}077$ MPa $\gg$ $\sigma_{allow} = 165$ MPa. The section is **grossly inadequate in bending**.

**Step 4 — Maximum deflection (ii)**

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5 \, w \, L^{4}}{384 \, E \, I} = \frac{5 \times 4.5 \times (2400)^{4}}{384 \times 2.1 \times 10^{5} \times 45{,}125.33}$$

Numerator: $22.5 \times 3.31776 \times 10^{13} = 7.465 \times 10^{14}$.

Denominator: $384 \times 2.1 \times 10^{5} \times 45{,}125.33 = 3.638 \times 10^{12}$.

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{7.465 \times 10^{14}}{3.638 \times 10^{12}} = 205.18 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Check AIS-052 limit (iii)**

$$\delta_{allow} = \frac{L}{300} = \frac{2400}{300} = 8 \; \text{mm}$$

Since $\delta_{max} = 205.18$ mm $\gg$ $\delta_{allow} = 8$ mm, the section **fails the AIS-052 deflection limit** by a factor of ~25.6. A deeper section (e.g., 80 × 40 × 3 mm RHS) or closer bow pitch (≤ 600 mm) is required.

*(7 marks — 1 each for $I$ and $Z$, 1 for $M_{max}$, 1 for $\sigma$, 1 for $\delta$, 1 for limit check, 1 for final conclusion.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Loss-of-Mark Areas:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** — Many students use $L$ in metres and $E$ in N/mm² in the same formula. **Always convert all dimensions to mm** before substitution.
> 2. **Skipping the $I_x$ derivation** for RHS sections — 2 marks lost.
> 3. **Wrong sign / wrong cross-section** — Students often confuse $B$ and $H$ of the RHS. **Convention:** $H$ is the dimension in the direction of bending (largest dimension).
> 4. **Forgetting to compare with AIS-052 limit** — the comparison itself carries 1 mark; just computing is incomplete.
> 5. **In Part (a) descriptive answers**, students merely list terms without explaining *load paths*. KTU 2024 examiners expect an **explicit load-path sentence** for full marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 4 (Bus Body Building)**

- **Three bus body types:** Conventional (body-on-chassis), Semi-integral, Monocoque — know load path, weight range, and one Indian OEM example each.
- **AIS-052 (Rev 1): 2017** is the master standard for bus body construction in India, notified under CMVR Rule 124.
- **AIS-046** governs roll-over protection; survival space ≥ **1,440 mm**, roof crush load = **1.5 × GVW**.
- **AIS-063** governs emergency exits — minimum **4 exits** for buses carrying more than 22 passengers.
- **CMVR Rule 125** limits: max length 12 m (single rigid), max width 2.5 m, max height 3.5 m, max seats 54 + driver.
- **Primary materials:** IS 2062 mild steel RHS/SHS, IS 513 sheet metal, IS 303 ply for floor, optional AISI 304 SS / AA 5083 Al / GFRP.
- **Yield strength minimums:** 210 MPa (ordinary routes), 240 MPa (hilly / ghat routes).
- **Girth frame pitch:** 600 – 760 mm; **floor deflection limit:** L/300; **anti-skid floor CoF:** ≥ 0.5 wet.
- **Cant rail** is the side-roof junction member; **waist rail** is at window-sill level; **sill rail** is at floor level.
- **Pillars A, B, C, D** — vertical posts; **A** is at front (windscreen), **D** is at rear.
- **Arai, iCAT, VRDE** are the three Indian testing agencies for bus body type approval.
- **Monocoque buses** are the future — BYD K9, Tata Starbus Ultra, AL Oyster are real-world examples.
- **Beating the most common exam trap:** in numericals, always show the **$I_x$ derivation** for hollow sections and the **comparison with AIS limit** — both are mandatory for full marks.
- **Survival space, anti-skid CoF, AIS-052 yield strength, 4 emergency exits, 1.5 × GVW roof crush, 1,440 mm intrusion limit, L/300 deflection** — these are the **seven numerical / factual data points** a KTU paper can test, memorise them verbatim.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lubrication oils
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lubrication oils

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Lubrication Oil (Lubricant):** A viscous oily substance, predominantly derived from petroleum (mineral base stocks) or synthesized chemically (synthetic base stocks), that is interposed between two moving surfaces in relative motion to reduce friction, dissipate heat, prevent wear, and inhibit corrosion between contacting components of an automotive power-train or chassis system.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT526 – Automotive Standards and Regulations)**, a *lubrication oil* is governed not only by its chemical origin but also by the **regulatory performance classifications** it carries. These classifications dictate whether an oil is legally permitted to be marketed for a specific automotive application (e.g., petrol engine, heavy-duty diesel, manual transmission, EV gearbox).

The two globally recognised regulatory frameworks for automotive lubrication oils are:

| Framework | Issuing Body | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **SAE J300** | Society of Automotive Engineers | **Viscosity** grade classification (e.g., 5W-30, 20W-50) |
| **API 1509** | American Petroleum Institute | **Performance / Service** category (e.g., API SP, API CK-4) |
| **ACEA** | European Automobile Manufacturers' Association | European performance sequences (e.g., ACEA C3) |
| **ILSAC** | Intl. Lubricant Spec. Advisory Committee | Combined API + fuel-economy standard (e.g., GF-6) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Oil is the Blood of an Engine"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine an engine as a human body. Just as **blood** flows through veins carrying oxygen, nutrients, and removing heat and waste, **lubrication oil** circulates through the engine's oil galleries, feeding critical components, carrying away heat, suspending contaminants, and protecting metal surfaces from grinding against each other. Without oil, an engine will seize (mechanical death) just as the body would die without circulation.

## 1.3 Primary Functions of an Automotive Lubrication Oil

A modern engine oil simultaneously performs **seven** distinct engineering functions:

1. **Friction Reduction** — Hydrodynamic and boundary lubrication film separates asperities.
2. **Wear Protection** — Anti-wear (AW) and Extreme Pressure (EP) additives react with metal surfaces to form sacrificial films.
3. **Heat Dissipation** — Convective heat transfer from bearings, pistons, and cylinder walls to the sump.
4. **Corrosion & Rust Inhibition** — Anti-corrosion additives neutralise acids from combustion blow-by and fuel sulphur.
5. **Contaminant Suspension** — Detergents and dispersants hold soot, sludge, and oxidation products in colloidal suspension.
6. **Sealing** — Oil film assists piston rings in sealing combustion gases in the cylinder.
7. **Cushioning / Damping** — Oil films in bearings dampen shock loads and vibration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMUT526):** When studying Module 4, students must remember that automotive standards **do not classify oils by colour or brand**, but exclusively by **SAE Viscosity Grade** and **API/ACEA Performance Category**. An oil's marketing claim is meaningless unless it carries both valid classifications.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics (Industry Conventions)

- **Kinematic Viscosity** — measured in **centistokes (cSt)** or **mm²/s** at **40 °C** and **100 °C** per **ASTM D445**.
- **Dynamic Viscosity** — measured in **centipoise (cP)** or **mPa·s** per **ASTM D2196**.
- **Viscosity Index (VI)** — dimensionless index per **ASTM D2270** indicating rate of viscosity change with temperature.
- **Total Base Number (TBN)** — mg KOH/g per **ASTM D2896**; indicates acid-neutralising reserve.
- **Total Acid Number (TAN)** — mg KOH/g per **ASTM D974**; indicates oil degradation state.
- **Pour Point** — lowest temperature at which oil still flows per **ASTM D97**.
- **Flash Point** — lowest temperature at which oil vapours ignite per **ASTM D92**.

These are the **bold-defined standard metrics** a KTU examiner will expect in definitions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Base Oil Classification — The American Petroleum Institute (API) Groups

Automotive lubrication oils begin as **base stocks**, which are then blended with **additive packages**. The **API** classifies base stocks into five groups:

| API Group | Source | Sulphur (%) | Saturates (%) | Viscosity Index |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Group I** | Solvent-refined mineral | > 0.03 | < 90 | 80 – 119 |
| **Group II** | Hydroprocessed mineral | ≤ 0.03 | ≥ 90 | 80 – 119 |
| **Group III** | Severely hydrocracked | ≤ 0.03 | ≥ 90 | ≥ 120 |
| **Group IV** | Polyalphaolefins (PAO) — Synthetic | — | — | 120 – 150+ |
| **Group V** | All others (esters, silicones, PAG) | — | — | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Note:** Groups **III, IV, V** are classified as **synthetic** or **semi-synthetic** base stocks. Group III+ is sometimes marketed as "synthetic" (e.g., Shell's Gas-to-Liquid GTL) though legally it remains Group III.

## 2.2 SAE J300 Viscosity Grade System

The **SAE J300** standard (revised annually; current revision **SAE J300_202407**) defines **12 winter grades** (0W, 2.5W … 25W) characterised by **cold-cranking simulator (CCS)** viscosity and **borderline pumping temperature**, and **11 hot grades** (8, 12, 16 … 60) characterised by **kinematic viscosity at 100 °C** and **High-Temperature High-Shear (HTHS)** viscosity at **150 °C**.

A **multi-grade oil** (e.g., **5W-30**) carries a winter grade (**5W**) and a hot grade (**30**) simultaneously, achieved through **Viscosity Index Improvers (VII)** — polymer additives that unfold at high temperature to thicken the oil, and coil up at low temperature to thin it.

## 2.3 API Service Category "Donut" Symbol

The **API Service Symbol** is the trademarked "donut" displayed on oil containers, consisting of:

- **Top half:** Performance category (e.g., **SP** for current petrol engines, **CK-4** for heavy-duty diesel).
- **Bottom half:** Viscosity grade (e.g., **5W-30**).
- **Centre:** Energy-conservation claim ("Resource Conserving" for ILSAC GF-6).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the **API Service Category** (a *performance* classification) with the **SAE Viscosity Grade** (a *physical* property classification). The examiner will test this distinction. Memorise: **SAE = How thick, API = What it can do.**

## 2.4 Additive Package — Functional Breakdown

Modern engine oil is roughly **70–95 % base stock** and **5–30 % additive package**, by mass. The major additive families are:

| Additive Family | Function | Typical Chemistry | Treat Rate (wt %) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Detergents** | Neutralise acids, keep surfaces clean | Calcium/Magnesium sulphonates, phenates | 2 – 6 |
| **Dispersants** | Suspend soot & sludge | Succinimide, succinate esters | 3 – 8 |
| **Anti-wear (AW)** | Mild boundary protection | Zinc Dialkyldithiophosphate (ZDDP) | 0.5 – 1.5 |
| **Extreme Pressure (EP)** | Heavy shock-load protection | Sulphurised olefins, chlorinated paraffins | 1 – 5 |
| **Antioxidants** | Slow oxidation | Hindered phenols, aromatic amines | 0.3 – 1.5 |
| **VI Improvers** | Multi-grade behaviour | Olefin copolymers (OCP), PMA, styrene-butadiene | 1 – 10 |
| **Pour Point Depressants** | Improve cold flow | Polymethacrylates | 0.1 – 1.0 |
| **Anti-foam** | Suppress aeration | Silicone polymers (PDMS) | 10 – 100 ppm |
| **Corrosion Inhibitors** | Rust prevention | Amine succinates, fatty acids | 0.2 – 1.0 |
| **Friction Modifiers** | Fuel economy | MoDTC, organic borates | 0.3 – 1.0 |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** All formulas below are tested under KTU's "Apply" and "Analyse" cognitive levels. The vertical bar notation uses `\vert` to prevent markdown table corruption.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Description | Standard / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity; $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity; $\rho$ = density | $\nu$ in **mm²/s** (cSt); $\mu$ in **mPa·s**; $\rho$ in **kg/m³** |
| 2 | $\text{VI} = \dfrac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$ | $L$ = KV@40 °C of VI=0 reference; $H$ = KV@40 °C of VI=100 reference; $U$ = KV@40 °C of test oil | Dimensionless; **ASTM D2270** |
| 3 | $\text{HTHS} = \text{KV}_{150} \times \rho_{150}$ | HTHS viscosity at 150 °C and 10⁶ s⁻¹ shear | **mPa·s**; **ASTM D4683, D4741** |
| 4 | $\dot{Q} = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat dissipated by oil; $h$ = heat transfer coefficient; $A$ = surface area; $\Delta T$ = temperature difference | $\dot{Q}$ in **W**; Fourier conduction analogue |
| 5 | $P_{\text{loss}} = \mu \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{v}{h}$ | Viscous friction power loss; $v$ = relative velocity; $h$ = film thickness | **W** |
| 6 | $\text{TBN}_{\text{used}} = \text{TBN}_{\text{fresh}} - k \cdot D$ | Linear TBN depletion model; $D$ = oil drain interval; $k$ = depletion constant | **mg KOH/g** |
| 7 | $R_{\text{film}} = \dfrac{h_{\text{min}}}{\sqrt{2 \cdot R_a^2}}$ | Lambda ratio (film / composite roughness) | Dimensionless; $\lambda < 1$ = boundary, $\lambda > 3$ = full film |
| 8 | $F_{\text{Stribeck}} = \mu_b + (\mu_s - \mu_b) \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot \eta \cdot N/P}$ | Stribeck curve — friction coefficient vs Hersey number | Dimensionless; defines **boundary → mixed → hydrodynamic** regimes |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern automotive applications, these classifications and formulae directly influence:

- **OEM Warranty Compliance** — Use of incorrect oil grade voids manufacturer warranty.
- **Emission Regulations** — API SP / ILSAC GF-6 limit **SAE 0W-16** to reduce CO₂ by ~ 1.5 %.
- **BS-VI / Euro VI Compatibility** — Low-SAPS (Sulphated Ash, Phosphorus, Sulphur) oils protect after-treatment devices (GPF/DPF).
- **EV Drivetrain Lubrication** — Electric vehicle gearboxes use low-viscosity PAO/ester blends (e.g., SAE 75W) to maximise range.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Viscosity Index (ASTM D2270)

The **Viscosity Index (VI)** quantifies how steeply a lubricant's viscosity falls as temperature rises from **40 °C** to **100 °C**. A higher VI = more stable viscosity = better lubricant.

### 3.1.1 The Underlying Concept

Two **reference oils** are defined at every KV@100 °C value $Y$ (in cSt):

- **Reference L**: A *naphthenic* oil of the same $Y$ at 100 °C but with **VI = 0** (i.e., viscosity drops sharply with temperature). Its KV at 40 °C is denoted $L$.
- **Reference H**: A *paraffinic* oil of the same $Y$ at 100 °C but with **VI = 100** (i.e., viscosity drops gently with temperature). Its KV at 40 °C is denoted $H$.

The **test oil's** KV at 40 °C is denoted $U$. If the test oil behaves exactly like Reference L, $U = L$ and VI = 0. If it behaves exactly like Reference H, $U = H$ and VI = 100. Linear interpolation gives:

$$\text{VI} = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$$

### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A test oil has KV@40 °C = **73 cSt** and KV@100 °C = **9.3 cSt**. For $Y = 9.3$ cSt, the standard tables (ASTM D2270) give $L = 126.7$ cSt and $H = 66.5$ cSt. Determine the Viscosity Index.

**Step 1 — Identify the given parameters**

We have $Y = 9.3$, $L = 126.7$, $H = 66.5$, and $U = 73.0$. (All kinematic viscosities in cSt.)

**Step 2 — Substitute into the VI formula**

$$\text{VI} = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator**

$$L - U = 126.7 - 73.0 = 53.7$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator**

$$L - H = 126.7 - 66.5 = 60.2$$

**Step 5 — Form the ratio**

$$\frac{L - U}{L - H} = \frac{53.7}{60.2} = 0.8920$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by 100 to obtain VI**

$$\text{VI} = 0.8920 \times 100 = 89.2 \approx 89$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation**

A VI of **89** classifies this oil as a **Group I mineral base stock** of high paraffinic quality. Compare: a polyalphaolefin (Group IV) typically has VI > 135.

### 3.1.3 Analytical Note — High-VI Regime (VI > 100)

When $U < H$ (test oil is *better* than the VI=100 reference), the linear formula gives **VI > 100**, and ASTM D2270 specifies a **logarithmic extension**:

$$\text{VI} = 100 + \frac{(\log_{10} H - \log_{10} U) \times 100}{\log_{10} Y}$$

This branch applies to most modern synthetic and severely hydrocracked (Group III) base stocks.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Stribeck Curve Identification

The **Stribeck curve** is the universal descriptor of friction regime in lubricated contacts. The Hersey number is defined as:

$$H_s = \frac{\eta \cdot N}{P}$$

where $\eta$ is the dynamic viscosity, $N$ is the rotational speed (rad/s), and $P$ is the load per unit projected area (Pa). The friction coefficient is:

$$\mu_f = \mu_b + (\mu_s - \mu_b) \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot H_s}$$

### 3.2.1 Worked Example — Regime Identification

> **Problem:** A journal bearing runs with $\eta = 0.05$ Pa·s, $N = 100$ rad/s, $P = 2$ MPa. Determine the regime and the friction coefficient (assume $\mu_b = 0.001$, $\mu_s = 0.12$, $\alpha = 0.05$).

**Step 1 — Compute the Hersey number**

$$H_s = \frac{0.05 \times 100}{2 \times 10^6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the exponent**

$$\alpha \cdot H_s = 0.05 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6} = 1.25 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the exponential term**

$$e^{-1.25 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 1 - 1.25 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.999999875$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the friction equation**

$$\mu_f = 0.001 + (0.12 - 0.001) \times 0.999999875 \approx 0.12$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

Because $H_s$ is vanishingly small and the friction coefficient collapses to the static value $\mu_s$, the bearing is operating in the **boundary lubrication regime** — the oil film has broken down. The engineer must switch to a higher-viscosity grade (e.g., from **5W-30** to **15W-50**) or upgrade to an **EP-additised** lubricant.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operating Regime vs. Lubrication Strategy

| Regime | Film / Roughness Ratio ($\lambda$) | Hersey Number ($H_s$) | Friction Level | Recommended Oil Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Boundary Lubrication** | $\lambda < 1$ | Very low | High ($\mu \approx 0.1$) | High ZDDP, EP additives, higher KV grade |
| **Mixed Lubrication** | $1 \leq \lambda \leq 3$ | Low – Medium | Decreasing | Balanced AW + VII; SAE 10W-40 / 5W-30 |
| **Hydrodynamic / EHL** | $\lambda > 3$ | Medium – High | Low ($\mu \approx 0.001$) | Low-viscosity synthetic, low HTHS for fuel economy |
| **Full EHL / Elastohydrodynamic** | $\lambda \gg 3$ | High | Minimum | PAO / Ester blend, friction modifiers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world impact:** Modern **API SP / ILSAC GF-6** specifications push engines deliberately toward the **hydrodynamic regime** to gain fuel economy — this is why manufacturers prescribe low-viscosity oils such as **0W-16** and **0W-20**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.2 SAE J300 Multi-Grade Logic — Block Diagram

This diagram explains how a single multi-grade oil (e.g., **5W-30**) satisfies *two* different SAE J300 viscosity requirements simultaneously using a single Viscosity Index Improver (VII) polymer.

```mermaid

    S1[Base Oil at 100 C]

    S5 --> S7
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define the term "Viscosity Index" of a lubrication oil. State the relevant standard.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Viscosity Index (VI)** is a dimensionless, empirical number — calculated per **ASTM D2270** — that indicates the rate at which a lubricant's kinematic viscosity changes with temperature. A higher VI indicates a smaller viscosity variation between 40 °C and 100 °C, hence superior lubricant quality. **(2 marks)** The relevant test standards for measuring the underlying kinematic viscosities at 40 °C and 100 °C are **ASTM D445**, with **VI = ((L − U) / (L − H)) × 100**. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between single-grade and multi-grade engine oils. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **single-grade oil** (e.g., **SAE 30**) meets only the SAE J300 hot-grade specification and is suitable for a narrow temperature band. **(1 mark)** A **multi-grade oil** (e.g., **5W-30**) carries both a winter grade (**5W**) and a hot grade (**30**) — therefore usable over a wide temperature range. **(1 mark)** This dual-rating is achieved by adding **Viscosity Index Improver (VII)** polymers, which thicken the oil at high temperature and thin it at low temperature. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts of **7 + 7 marks**. The student answers either **OR-1** *or* **OR-2**.

---

### **Question A — OR-1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **SAE J300 viscosity classification system** for engine oils, with reference to the tests used for winter and hot grades. **— 7 marks**

**(b)** Discuss the **functions and chemistry of the major additive families** used in a modern multi-grade petrol engine oil. **— 7 marks**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q A (a) — 7 marks**

The **SAE J300** standard issued by the **Society of Automotive Engineers** classifies engine oils by their **high-temperature and low-temperature viscosity performance**. It defines two parallel grading streams: **winter (W) grades** and **hot grades**. **(1 mark)**

**Winter grades** (e.g., **0W, 5W, 10W, 15W, 20W, 25W**) are graded by two **Cold-temperature** tests: **(2 marks)**

- **Cold Cranking Simulator (CCS) viscosity** at the grade's reference temperature (e.g., −30 °C for 5W) per **ASTM D5293** — maximum permissible values, e.g., 6600 mPa·s for 5W.
- **Borderline Pumping Temperature** per **ASTM D4684** — the lowest temperature at which the oil can still be pumped to the engine galleries.

**Hot grades** (e.g., **20, 30, 40, 50, 60**) are graded by two **High-temperature** tests: **(2 marks)**

- **Kinematic Viscosity at 100 °C** per **ASTM D445** — minimum and maximum cSt values listed in J300 (e.g., 9.3 ≤ KV ≤ 12.5 for SAE 30).
- **High-Temperature High-Shear (HTHS) viscosity at 150 °C and 10⁶ s⁻¹** per **ASTM D4683 / D4741** — minimum 2.6 mPa·s (most grades) or 2.9 mPa·s (energy-conserving).

A **multi-grade oil** like **5W-30** must simultaneously satisfy the **5W** cold-crank limit *and* the **30** hot-KV / HTHS limits. **(1 mark)** This dual compliance is achieved by formulating with **Viscosity Index Improver (VII)** polymers. **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q A (b) — 7 marks**

A modern multi-grade petrol engine oil contains a balanced **additive package** blended into the base stock. The major families and their functions are:

1. **Detergents (1 mark):** Calcium or magnesium **sulphonates and phenates** that neutralise acidic combustion by-products (e.g., H₂SO₄ from fuel sulphur) and keep metallic surfaces clean from varnish and deposits.
2. **Dispersants (1 mark):** **Succinimide / succinate** polymers that keep soot, sludge, and oxidation products in colloidal suspension, preventing them from agglomerating into engine-clogging deposits.
3. **Anti-wear (AW) Additives (1 mark):** **Zinc Dialkyldithiophosphate (ZDDP)** reacts with ferrous surfaces at asperity contact points to form a sacrificial **polyphosphate glass** film that prevents metal-to-metal welding.
4. **Extreme Pressure (EP) Agents (1 mark):** **Sulphurised olefins** and **chlorinated paraffins** that activate at very high local temperatures to form iron-sulphide sacrificial films — used in gear oils and hypoid axle lubricants.
5. **Antioxidants (0.5 mark):** **Hindered phenols** and **aromatic amines** that interrupt the free-radical chain reaction of oil oxidation, extending service life.
6. **Viscosity Index Improvers (VII) (1 mark):** **Olefin copolymers (OCP)** and **polymethacrylates (PMA)** that enable multi-grade performance.
7. **Pour-Point Depressants & Anti-foam (0.5 mark):** **Polymethacrylates** prevent wax crystallisation at low temperature; **silicone (PDMS)** suppresses air entrainment and foaming.

The typical treat rate of the additive package is **5 % – 30 %** by mass, with the balance being the base stock. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### **Question B — OR-2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **API base oil classification system** (Groups I–V) with a comparative table. **— 7 marks**

**(b)** A test oil has KV@40 °C = **95 cSt** and KV@100 °C = **11.2 cSt**. For KV@100 °C = 11.2 cSt, ASTM D2270 tables give $L = 167.0$ cSt and $H = 78.5$ cSt. Calculate the **Viscosity Index** and classify the base stock. **— 7 marks**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q B (a) — 7 marks**

The **American Petroleum Institute (API)** classifies lubricant base stocks into **five groups** based on **sulphur content (% wt)**, **saturates content (% wt)**, and **Viscosity Index (VI)**. **(1 mark)**

| API Group | Source | Sulphur (% wt) | Saturates (% wt) | Viscosity Index |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Group I** | Solvent-refined mineral | > 0.03 | < 90 | 80 – 119 |
| **Group II** | Hydroprocessed mineral | ≤ 0.03 | ≥ 90 | 80 – 119 |
| **Group III** | Severely hydrocracked (VHVI) | ≤ 0.03 | ≥ 90 | ≥ 120 |
| **Group IV** | Polyalphaolefins (PAO) — Synthetic | — | — | 120 – 150+ |
| **Group V** | All others (esters, silicones, PAG) | — | — | Variable |

**(3 marks for table)**

- **Group I (1 mark):** Oldest, cheapest; solvent-refined. Used in industrial lubricants, older formulations, and some heavy-duty diesel engine oils.
- **Group II (0.5 mark):** Hydroprocessed to remove impurities; used in modern passenger car motor oils due to better oxidation stability than Group I.
- **Group III (0.5 mark):** Severely hydrocracked; *often marketed as "synthetic"*. Used in mid-range synthetic blends (e.g., Castrol Magnatec, Shell Helix HX8).
- **Group IV (0.5 mark):** Chemically synthesised PAO; excellent low-temperature fluidity, high VI, used in premium synthetic oils (e.g., Mobil 1).
- **Group V (0.5 mark):** Specialty stocks — diesters used in jet turbine oils, polyalkylene glycols (PAG) used in compressor and EV gear lubricants, silicones for high-temperature applications.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q B (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Identify given data (1 mark)**

$$U = 95.0 \text{ cSt}, \quad L = 167.0 \text{ cSt}, \quad H = 78.5 \text{ cSt}, \quad Y = 11.2 \text{ cSt}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ASTM D2270 VI formula (1 mark)**

$$\text{VI} = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator (1 mark)**

$$L - U = 167.0 - 95.0 = 72.0 \text{ cSt}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator (1 mark)**

$$L - H = 167.0 - 78.5 = 88.5 \text{ cSt}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the ratio and VI (1 mark)**

$$\text{VI} = \frac{72.0}{88.5} \times 100 = 0.8136 \times 100 = 81.36 \approx 81$$

**Step 6 — Base stock classification (1 mark)**

Since **VI = 81** lies in the range **80 – 119**, the oil qualifies as a **Group I or Group II** base stock (both have overlapping VI range, but since VI < 90, it is most likely **Group I**, solvent-refined mineral). **(1 mark)**

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation (1 mark)**

A VI of 81 indicates a **moderate** rate of viscosity change with temperature — adequate for tropical climates and moderate seasonal variation, but not optimal for arctic conditions. For multi-grade performance across wide temperature ranges, this base stock must be blended with a **Viscosity Index Improver (VII)** such as an **Olefin Copolymer (OCP)** to push the effective VI above 100.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks on Module 4 (Lubrication Oils):**
> 1. **Conflating SAE and API:** SAE J300 is a *viscosity* standard; API 1509 is a *performance* standard. A statement like "API 5W-30" loses 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Wrong units:** Always cite kinematic viscosity in **cSt (mm²/s)** at **40 °C and 100 °C**; cite HTHS in **mPa·s** at **150 °C**. Mixing units is the most common error.
> 3. **Forgetting ASTM standard numbers:** The examiner expects **ASTM D2270 (VI), ASTM D445 (KV), ASTM D5293 (CCS), ASTM D4683 (HTHS), ASTM D92 (Flash), ASTM D97 (Pour Point)** — memorise at least three.
> 4. **Skipping the additive treat-rate range:** "An oil has additives" is worth 0 marks; "Detergents at 2–6 wt % using calcium sulphonate" earns full marks.
> 5. **VI formula sign error:** The formula is $(L - U) / (L - H)$, NOT $(U - L) / (L - H)$. Wrong sign → wrong classification → cascading mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Lubrication Oils**

- **Lubrication oil** is the "blood" of an engine: friction reduction, wear protection, heat dissipation, corrosion inhibition, contaminant suspension, sealing, and damping — **seven functions**, not three.
- **SAE J300** = **Viscosity** (How thick? e.g., **5W-30**). **API 1509** = **Performance** (What can it do? e.g., **API SP**). Never confuse the two.
- **Base stock groups (API):** **I** = solvent-refined, **II** = hydroprocessed, **III** = VHVI (semi-synthetic), **IV** = PAO (true synthetic), **V** = esters/silicones/PAG.
- **Additive families (must memorise):** Detergents, Dispersants, ZDDP (AW), EP agents, Antioxidants, VII, Pour-Point Depressants, Anti-foam, Corrosion inhibitors, Friction modifiers.
- **Viscosity Index formula:** $\text{VI} = \dfrac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$ (ASTM D2270). For VI > 100, use the logarithmic extension: $\text{VI} = 100 + \dfrac{(\log H - \log U) \times 100}{\log Y}$.
- **Kinematic viscosity** = $\nu = \mu / \rho$; measured in **cSt (mm²/s)** per **ASTM D445** at **40 °C and 100 °C**.
- **Multi-grade oil** (e.g., **5W-30**) = single formulation satisfying both a winter and a hot SAE grade, made possible by **Viscosity Index Improver (VII)** polymers (OCP, PMA, styrene-butadiene).
- **Stribeck Curve regimes:** **Boundary** (high friction, EP additives needed) → **Mixed** → **Hydrodynamic** (low friction, low KV synthetic) → **Full EHL** (PAO/ester blend).
- **BS-VI / Euro VI compliance:** Modern engines require **low-SAPS** oils (low Sulphated Ash, Phosphorus, Sulphur) to protect **GPF/DPF** after-treatment devices.
- **EV drivetrain:** Uses low-viscosity synthetic gear oils (PAO/ester, ~ SAE 75W) for efficiency — *not* a traditional engine oil.
- **Mandatory standards to recall:** SAE J300, API 1509, ACEA sequences, ILSAC GF-6, ASTM D2270, ASTM D445, ASTM D4683, ASTM D5293, ASTM D92, ASTM D97.
- **Golden rule for KTU exam:** Always state the **standard number** (SAE, API, ASTM) alongside any classification. Marks are awarded to *named* standards, not generic mentions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piston and piston rings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Piston and piston rings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Piston — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Piston (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):** A cylindrical engine component that reciprocates inside the cylinder bore, transmitting the pressure thrust of combustion gases to the connecting rod through the gudgeon (wrist) pin, while simultaneously sealing the combustion chamber and dissipating a portion of the combustion heat to the cylinder walls.

In the context of **PEMUT526 — Automotive Standards and Regulations**, the piston is not just a mechanical part; it is a **regulated component** governed by Indian Standards (IS), Automotive Industry Standards (AIS), and global benchmarks (ISO, ECE, SAE) governing its **material composition, dimensional tolerances, surface finish, hardness, and functional testing**.

### Primary Functions of the Piston

1. **Force Transmission:** Transfers the gas pressure load from combustion to the connecting rod.
2. **Sealing:** With the help of piston rings, seals the combustion chamber against leakage of gases and oil.
3. **Heat Dissipation:** Conducts a portion (typically **30–40 %**) of combustion heat to the cylinder walls via rings and skirt.
4. **Guidance:** The skirt maintains alignment of the piston with the cylinder bore.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT526):** Component-level standards (AIS-010 series, IS 8062, IS 3221) apply to pistons. Regulation of in-service emissions indirectly mandates the integrity of piston-ring sealing — a worn piston-ring assembly increases **HC and CO emissions**, breaching the limits of **AIS-068/ECE R83** for spark-ignition and **AIS-072/ECE R49** for compression-ignition engines.

---

## 1.2 Piston Rings — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Piston Ring (KTU Definition):** An elastic, split, metallic ring fitted into the grooves of a piston which expands outward to maintain contact with the cylinder wall, providing gas sealing, heat conduction, and oil metering between the piston and the cylinder bore.

### Functions of Piston Rings

- **Gas Sealing (Compression Rings):** Prevents leakage of high-pressure combustion gases into the crankcase (blow-by).
- **Heat Transfer:** Conducts heat from the piston crown to the cylinder wall.
- **Oil Control (Oil Control Ring):** Scrapes excess lubricating oil from the cylinder wall and returns it to the sump.
- **Locomotion Stability:** Maintains piston centering, preventing contact between piston skirt and cylinder wall at TDC.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Syringe Plunger with Rubber Seal"**
> Think of a piston assembly as a **syringe plunger**. The barrel is the cylinder, the plunger rod is the connecting rod, and your thumb pushing the plunger is combustion pressure. Now, a plain plunger would leak — but fit a **rubber ring (piston ring)** around the plunger, and suddenly the seal is airtight. Add a second "wiper" ring below it to scrape off excess fluid (oil), and you have a complete piston-ring assembly. The rings act as the **flexible seal** that accommodates thermal expansion, slight bore wear, and the dynamic reciprocating motion.

> Another analogy: **The bicycle pump piston** — the leather seal in an old-style bicycle pump performs the same function as a piston ring — sealing high pressure on one side from low pressure on the other, while sliding against a wall.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Highlighted for KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Physical Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Compression Ratio Range (SI engines):** 8 : 1 to 12 : 1
> - **Compression Ratio Range (CI engines):** 14 : 1 to 22 : 1
> - **Number of Rings (Modern engines):** **2 compression + 1 oil control ring = 3 rings total**
> - **Piston Speed (Typical petrol):** 15 to 22 m/s
> - **Operating Temperature (Piston Crown):** 250 °C to 350 °C (SI), 350 °C to 500 °C (CI)
> - **Ring End Gap (typical):** 0.25 mm to 0.75 mm per 100 mm bore
> - **Material Hardness (Cast iron ring):** **95 to 105 HRB**
> - **Lubrication Oil Clearance:** **0.025 to 0.05 mm** at the skirt

---

## 1.5 Major Parts of the Piston (KTU Terminology)

| S.No. | Part Name | Function |
|:-----:|:----------|:---------|
| 1 | **Crown / Head** | Forms the lower half of the combustion chamber; exposed to peak combustion temperatures. |
| 2 | **Ring Belt Zone** | Houses the compression rings and the oil control ring. |
| 3 | **Ring Grooves** | Precision-machined slots that hold the rings with controlled side clearance. |
| 4 | **Piston Skirt** | Bottom cylindrical portion that guides the piston in the bore. |
| 5 | **Gudgeon Pin Boss** | The reinforced cylindrical housing for the wrist pin. |
| 6 | **Gudgeon Pin / Wrist Pin** | Connects the piston to the small end of the connecting rod. |
| 7 | **Internal Ribs / Boss Webs** | Provide structural reinforcement and aid oil flow in oil-cooled pistons. |
| 8 | **Lands** | Surfaces between the ring grooves. |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Piston — Detailed Structural Analysis

### 2.1.1 Piston Head (Crown)
The crown is exposed to direct flame and combustion pressure. Its design influences:
- **Combustion chamber geometry** (hemispherical, wedge, bathtub, etc.)
- **Squish and swirl** behaviour
- **Knock resistance** (raised dome in SI for high CR)

Crowns in **diesel engines** often have **bowl-in-piston (Mexican hat)** designs to direct fuel spray, enhance swirl, and reduce soot deposits.

### 2.1.2 Ring Grooves
Each groove has a **side clearance** (gap between ring face and groove wall) of approximately **0.04 mm to 0.10 mm** — this allows the ring to flex under gas pressure and ensures seating on the cylinder bore.

### 2.1.3 Piston Skirt
The skirt provides lateral stability. Modern pistons are:
- **Cam-ground** (slightly oval when cold) so they become perfectly circular at operating temperature.
- **Slipper-skirt** designs (only two contact pads) reduce friction and mass.

### 2.1.4 Gudgeon Pin (Wrist Pin)
- Connects piston to connecting rod.
- **Full-floating** type is most common in modern engines — held by circlips, with lubrication by splash oil.
- **Interference-fit** or **press-fit** types are pressed into the connecting rod small end.
- **Material:** Case-hardened alloy steel, surface hardness **58–62 HRC**.

---

## 2.2 Piston Ring — Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 Classification of Piston Rings

#### A. By Function

| Ring Position | Name | Function |
|:-------------:|:-----|:---------|
| Top (1st) | **Compression Ring (Fire Ring)** | Seals high-pressure gas; transmits ~50 % of piston heat. |
| 2nd | **Compression Ring (Wiper)** | Secondary seal; scrapes oil upward. |
| 3rd (Bottom) | **Oil Control Ring** | Scrapes excess oil from bore; drains to sump. |

#### B. By Cross-Sectional Shape

| Cross-Section | Use | Advantage |
|:--------------|:----|:----------|
| **Rectangular** | Top compression ring | Maximum sealing area. |
| **Tapered** | 2nd compression ring | Wipes oil; quick bedding-in. |
| **Barrel-Faced** | 1st compression | High conformability to bore. |
| **Stepped (L-head)** | Top compression | Scraper + seal combined. |
| **Internally Bevelled** | Taper face | Improved oil scraping. |
| **Hook-Faced** | Older designs | Oil control. |
| **Slotted (Spring-Loaded)** | Oil control | Constant radial pressure. |
| **Multi-Piece (3-piece oil ring)** | Oil control | Best conformity; lowest oil consumption. |

### 2.2.2 Piston Ring Gaps — Three Critical Clearances

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every KTU question on piston rings must mention these three clearances:**

1. **End Gap (Butt Gap / Circumferential Gap):**
   Gap between the two ends of the ring when fitted in the cylinder. Allows for thermal expansion.
   $$\text{End Gap} = (0.003 \text{ to } 0.005) \times D$$
   where $D$ is the cylinder bore diameter.

2. **Side Clearance (Axial Clearance):**
   Gap between the top/bottom face of the ring and the groove wall. Allows free radial expansion.
   $$\text{Side Clearance} = 0.04 \text{ to } 0.10 \text{ mm}$$

3. **Back Clearance (Radial Clearance):**
   Gap between the back of the ring and the bottom of the groove. Ensures ring pressure acts only on the bore.
   $$\text{Back Clearance} = 0.20 \text{ to } 0.40 \text{ mm}$$

---

## 2.3 Materials — KTU 2024 Standards Perspective

### 2.3.1 Piston Materials

| Material | Composition | Application |
|:---------|:------------|:------------|
| **Cast Iron** | Grey cast iron, Fe + C (2.5–4 %) + Si | Older heavy-duty diesel pistons. |
| **Aluminium-Silicon Alloy** | Al + 12 % Si (eutectic) | Most modern petrol & light diesel pistons. |
| **Al-Si-Cu-Ni-Mg (Hypereutectic)** | Al + 17–25 % Si + Cu + Ni | High-performance, low-expansion pistons. |
| **Forged Aluminium** | Al-Si, drop-forged | High-speed, high-load diesel. |
| **Steel (Forged)** | Alloy steel | Two-stroke marine engines. |

> **Governing Standard (India):** **IS 8062: Specification for Aluminium Alloy Piston Castings.**

### 2.3.2 Piston Ring Materials

- **Grey Cast Iron** — most common, contains 2.5–3.5 % C, 1.5–2.5 % Si, with chromium, molybdenum, copper additions.
- **Steel Rings** — for top compression rings in heavily loaded diesel engines; often plasma-sprayed or chrome-plated.
- **Surface Coatings:**
  - **Chrome Plating** (hardness 850–1000 HV) — wear resistance.
  - **Molybdenum Disulphide (MoS₂)** — anti-scuffing.
  - **Phosphate Coating** — running-in aid.
  - **PVD/CVD Diamond-Like Carbon (DLC)** — modern low-friction rings.

> **Governing Standard (India):** **IS 3221: Piston Rings — Dimensions and Tolerances.**

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Purpose |
|:-:|:----------|:--------|:------|:--------|
| 1 | **Piston Displacement** (from TDC) | $x = r\,(1 - \cos\theta) + \dfrac{r^{2}\,\sin^{2}\theta}{2\,l}$ | m | Position vs. crank angle |
| 2 | **Piston Velocity** | $v = r\,\omega\,(\sin\theta + \dfrac{r\,\sin 2\theta}{2\,l})$ | m/s | Instantaneous speed |
| 3 | **Piston Acceleration** | $a = r\,\omega^{2}\,(\cos\theta + \dfrac{r\,\cos 2\theta}{l})$ | m/s² | Inertial force basis |
| 4 | **Piston Ring Radial Pressure** | $p = \dfrac{E\,I\,(R_{f} - R_{w})}{A\,R_{f}^{2}\,R_{w}}$ | N/mm² | Ring–bore contact stress |
| 5 | **Moment of Inertia (ring section)** | $I = \dfrac{b\,t^{3}}{12}$ | mm⁴ | Beam-bending resistance |
| 6 | **Ring Tangential Stress** | $\sigma = \dfrac{p\,D}{2\,t}$ | N/mm² | Hoop stress in ring |
| 7 | **End Gap Allowance** | $G = k\,D$ (k ≈ 0.003–0.005) | mm | Thermal expansion gap |
| 8 | **Heat Flux through Piston** | $q = \dfrac{k\,A\,\Delta T}{L}$ | W | Conduction to walls |
| 9 | **Cylinder Liner Wear (Ring driven)** | $W \propto p\,v\,t$ | µm | Archard's law |
| 10 | **Mean Piston Speed** | $\bar{v}_{p} = \dfrac{2\,L\,N}{60}$ | m/s | Engine rating criterion |

Where:
- $r$ = crank radius (m)
- $l$ = connecting rod length (m)
- $\theta$ = crank angle from TDC (rad)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)
- $E$ = Young's modulus of ring material (N/mm²)
- $R_{f}$ = free radius of ring (mm)
- $R_{w}$ = working (installed) radius (mm)
- $D$ = cylinder bore (mm)
- $b$ = ring width (mm)
- $t$ = ring radial thickness (mm)
- $A$ = ring cross-section area (mm²)
- $L$ = stroke length (m)
- $N$ = engine speed (rpm)

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Piston-ring sealing efficiency** is the **#1 mechanical cause of increased tailpipe emissions** in BS-VI/Euro-6 era engines — a worn top compression ring can increase HC emissions by **300–500 %**.
- **Hypereutectic Al-Si pistons** (used in modern high-output turbocharged engines) reduce cold-start clearance to **0.01 mm**, improving NVH and fuel economy.
- **Low-tension oil control rings** reduce friction loss by **15–20 %** — equivalent to a **1–2 %** improvement in fuel economy.
- **AIS-068 (Bharat Stage VI)** compliance is heavily dependent on oil control — **oil consumption directly correlates with PM emissions** in diesel engines.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Piston Displacement, Velocity & Acceleration

> **Engineering Setup:** Consider a slider-crank mechanism. Let:
> - $r$ = crank radius (half the stroke length)
> - $l$ = connecting rod length
> - $\theta$ = crank angle measured from TDC (Top Dead Centre)
> - $\omega = d\theta/dt$ = constant angular velocity (rad/s)
> - $\lambda = r/l$ (a small ratio, typically **1/3 to 1/4**)

The piston position measured **from TDC** can be derived from the geometry of the slider-crank.

### Step 1: Write the position vector components

The crank-end is at position $(r\cos\theta,\ r\sin\theta)$ from the crank centre. The connecting rod has length $l$, and the small end (piston pin) lies along the cylinder axis (x-axis).

The x-coordinate of the piston pin:
$$
x_{\text{pin}} = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{\,l^{2} - r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta\,}
$$

The x-coordinate of the piston pin at TDC ($\theta = 0$):
$$
x_{0} = r + l
$$

### Step 2: Displacement from TDC

$$
x = x_{0} - x_{\text{pin}} = r + l - r\cos\theta - \sqrt{\,l^{2} - r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta\,}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad x = r\,(1 - \cos\theta) + l - \sqrt{\,l^{2} - r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta\,}
$$

**Text row explanation:** The first term accounts for the crank pin moving away from TDC; the second and third terms together represent the component of the connecting rod that lies along the cylinder axis.

### Step 3: Simplify the square root using binomial expansion

Since $\lambda^{2}\sin^{2}\theta = (r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta)/l^{2} \ll 1$, expand:
$$
\sqrt{\,l^{2} - r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta\,} = l\,\sqrt{\,1 - \lambda^{2}\sin^{2}\theta\,} \approx l\,\Big(1 - \tfrac{1}{2}\lambda^{2}\sin^{2}\theta\Big)
$$

Substituting:
$$
x = r\,(1 - \cos\theta) + l - l\,\Big(1 - \tfrac{1}{2}\lambda^{2}\sin^{2}\theta\Big)
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\,x = r\,(1 - \cos\theta) + \dfrac{r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2\,l}\,}
$$

**Text row explanation:** The first term is the *primary* simple harmonic motion of the crank projected onto the axis. The second term is the *secondary* (corrective) term due to the finite connecting rod length.

---

### Step 4: Differentiate to obtain piston velocity

Using the chain rule with $dx/dt = (dx/d\theta)\cdot \omega$:
$$
v = \frac{dx}{dt} = \omega\,\frac{dx}{d\theta}
$$

Differentiate term-by-term:
$$
\frac{dx}{d\theta} = r\sin\theta + \dfrac{r^{2}\sin\theta\cos\theta}{l} = r\sin\theta + \dfrac{r^{2}\sin 2\theta}{2\,l}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\,v = r\,\omega\,\Big(\sin\theta + \dfrac{r\,\sin 2\theta}{2\,l}\Big)\,}
$$

**Text row explanation:** The first term is the primary simple harmonic velocity; the second term is a second-harmonic correction that adds to peak velocity.

### Step 5: Differentiate again to obtain piston acceleration

$$
a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \omega^{2}\,\frac{d^{2}x}{d\theta^{2}}
$$

$$
\frac{d^{2}x}{d\theta^{2}} = r\cos\theta + \dfrac{r^{2}\cos 2\theta}{l}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\,a = r\,\omega^{2}\,\Big(\cos\theta + \dfrac{r\,\cos 2\theta}{l}\Big)\,}
$$

**Text row explanation:** The first term is the primary SHM acceleration. The second term is a second-harmonic correction. Maximum acceleration occurs near TDC ($\theta \approx 0$); the *reverse* (negative) peak acceleration occurs near BDC.

### Step 6: Numerical verification at key crank angles

| $\theta$ | $\cos\theta$ | $\cos 2\theta$ | $x$ | $v$ | $a$ |
|:--------:|:------------:|:--------------:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0° (TDC) | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | $r\omega^{2}(1 + r/l)$ (max) |
| 90° | 0 | −1 | $r + r^{2}/(2l)$ | $r\omega$ | $-r^{2}\omega^{2}/l$ |
| 180° (BDC) | −1 | 1 | $2r$ | 0 | $-r\omega^{2}(1 - r/l)$ (min) |
| 270° | 0 | −1 | $r + r^{2}/(2l)$ | $-r\omega$ | $-r^{2}\omega^{2}/l$ |

**Engineering insight:** The maximum acceleration (at TDC) is greater than the minimum (at BDC) by an amount equal to $2r^{2}\omega^{2}/l$. This is the **secondary imbalance** that causes the engine to rock.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Radial Pressure of a Piston Ring

### Step 1: Define the free and installed state

A free piston ring (unstressed) has radius $R_{f}$. When installed in a cylinder of radius $R_{w} < R_{f}$, the ring is forced to compress by an amount:
$$
\delta = R_{f} - R_{w}
$$

### Step 2: Treat the ring as a curved beam

The radial pressure $p$ that the ring exerts on the cylinder wall can be obtained from beam-bending theory applied to a thin curved ring:
$$
p = \frac{E\,I\,(R_{f} - R_{w})}{A\,R_{f}^{2}\,R_{w}}
$$

where $E$ is Young's modulus, $I$ is the second moment of area of the ring cross-section, and $A$ is the cross-section area.

### Step 3: Substitute the cross-section properties

For a rectangular cross-section of width $b$ (axial) and radial thickness $t$:
$$
I = \frac{b\,t^{3}}{12}, \quad A = b\,t
$$

### Step 4: Simplify

$$
p = \frac{E \cdot \dfrac{b\,t^{3}}{12} \cdot (R_{f} - R_{w})}{b\,t \cdot R_{f}^{2} \cdot R_{w}}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\,p = \frac{E\,t^{2}\,(R_{f} - R_{w})}{12\,R_{f}^{2}\,R_{w}}\,}
$$

**Text row explanation:** The radial pressure is proportional to the *square* of the ring thickness and the deflection $(R_{f} - R_{w})$, inversely proportional to the *square* of the radius. Thicker rings give more sealing pressure but increase friction.

### Step 5: Tangential (hoop) stress in the ring

The ring is treated as a thin-walled cylinder under internal pressure. Hoop stress:
$$
\sigma = \frac{p\,D}{2\,t}
$$

**Text row explanation:** The hoop stress in the ring must not exceed the yield strength of the material divided by a safety factor. Typical cast iron rings: $\sigma_{\text{yield}} \approx 300 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Numerical Illustration

> **Problem:** A 4-cylinder petrol engine has bore $D = 80$ mm, stroke $L = 90$ mm, connecting rod length $l = 145$ mm, and runs at 3000 rpm. The top compression ring has free radius $R_{f} = 42.0$ mm, width $b = 3.0$ mm, radial thickness $t = 4.0$ mm, and is made of grey cast iron with $E = 1.1 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm².
> 
> **Find:** (a) Mean piston speed, (b) Piston velocity at 90° ATDC, (c) Radial pressure exerted by the ring on the cylinder wall.

### Solution (a) — Mean Piston Speed

$$
\bar{v}_{p} = \frac{2\,L\,N}{60} = \frac{2 \times 0.090 \times 3000}{60} = 9.0 \text{ m/s}
$$

This is well within the **15–22 m/s** typical design range — safe.

### Solution (b) — Piston Velocity at 90° ATDC

Crank radius $r = L/2 = 0.045$ m, $\omega = 2\pi N/60 = 314.16$ rad/s.

$$
v = r\,\omega\,\Big(\sin\theta + \dfrac{r\,\sin 2\theta}{2\,l}\Big)
$$

At $\theta = 90°$: $\sin 90° = 1$, $\sin 180° = 0$
$$
v = 0.045 \times 314.16 \times (1 + 0) = 14.14 \text{ m/s}
$$

### Solution (c) — Radial Ring Pressure

Working radius $R_{w} = D/2 = 40.0$ mm. Deflection $\delta = R_{f} - R_{w} = 2.0$ mm.

$$
p = \frac{E\,t^{2}\,(R_{f} - R_{w})}{12\,R_{f}^{2}\,R_{w}}
$$

$$
p = \frac{1.1 \times 10^{5} \times 4.0^{2} \times 2.0}{12 \times 42.0^{2} \times 40.0}
$$

$$
p = \frac{1.1 \times 10^{5} \times 16 \times 2.0}{12 \times 1764 \times 40.0} = \frac{3.52 \times 10^{6}}{8.4672 \times 10^{5}} \approx 4.16 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,p \approx 4.16 \text{ N/mm}^{2}\,}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** This pressure is sufficient to maintain a gas-tight seal under peak combustion pressure of **40–60 bar** in SI engines, and gives an oil film of $\approx 1\text{–}2\ \mu\text{m}$ thickness.
