# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any three functions of a piston. State the material commonly used for modern pistons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Function 1:** Transmits the combustion gas pressure to the connecting rod via the gudgeon pin — converting reciprocating force into rotational motion (1 mark).
- **Function 2:** With piston rings, seals the combustion chamber against leakage of gases (blow-by) and oil (1 mark).
- **Function 3:** Conducts a portion of the combustion heat (≈ 30–40 %) from the crown to the cylinder wall via rings and skirt (1 mark).
- **Material:** Aluminium–silicon eutectic alloy (Al + 12 % Si) is the most common, conforming to **IS 8062**.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "end gap" in a piston ring. State its typical value as a function of cylinder bore.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** The *end gap* (also called the *butt gap* or *circumferential gap*) is the small clearance left between the two free ends of a piston ring when the ring is fitted inside the cylinder bore. It accommodates the thermal expansion of the ring as it heats up during engine operation; if no gap were provided, the ring ends would butt against each other, causing buckling, breakage, and severe bore scoring.
- **Typical value (1 mark):**
  $$
  G = (0.003 \text{ to } 0.005) \times D
  $$
  where $D$ is the cylinder bore diameter in mm. For a 100 mm bore, end gap is typically 0.3–0.5 mm.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> *Note: KTU End-Semester Examinations offer an internal choice. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are two entirely independent 14-mark options.*

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the constructional features of a piston used in a modern SI engine with a neat sketch. Discuss the materials used and list the relevant Indian Standard. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Explain the different types of piston rings used in an automotive engine with a focus on cross-sectional shapes. State the three clearances that must be maintained. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Constructional Features (5 marks):**
A modern SI engine piston consists of the following parts:
1. **Crown / Head:** Forms the lower half of the combustion chamber; designed flat, domed, or dished depending on chamber geometry. In SI engines, a slightly dished or flat crown is preferred for knock resistance.
2. **Ring Belt:** Houses the rings. The top two grooves hold compression rings; the third groove holds the oil control ring.
3. **Lands:** The narrow surfaces between the ring grooves; the **top land** is the most thermally loaded.
4. **Piston Skirt:** Bottom cylindrical portion. Modern pistons are *cam-ground* (slightly elliptical cold, circular at operating temperature). Slipper-skirt designs reduce friction.
5. **Gudgeon Pin Boss:** The reinforced cylindrical housing for the wrist pin. The pin is often *offset* by 0.5–1.5 mm towards the thrust side to reduce piston slap.
6. **Internal Ribs:** Provide structural rigidity and form oil galleries for oil-cooled pistons.

**Materials (1.5 marks):**
- **Aluminium-silicon eutectic alloy** (Al + 12 % Si) — most common.
- **Hypereutectic Al-Si** (17–25 % Si) — low expansion, used in high-output engines.
- **Cast iron** — older heavy-duty applications.
- Forged aluminium for high-speed applications.

**Indian Standard (0.5 mark):** **IS 8062: Specification for Aluminium Alloy Piston Castings.**

#### Model Solution (b):

**Types of Piston Rings by Cross-Section (4 marks):**
1. **Rectangular** — top compression ring, maximum seal area.
2. **Taper-Faced** — second compression ring, oil wiping action.
3. **Barrel-Faced** — top compression in modern engines, high conformability.
4. **Stepped (L-Head)** — combines scraper and seal functions.
5. **Internally Bevelled** — wedge-shaped; improved scraping.
6. **Hook-Faced** — older designs, oil control.
7. **Slotted (Multi-Piece)** — oil control ring with expander spring.

**The Three Critical Clearances (3 marks):**
1. **End Gap (Butt Gap):** $(0.003 \text{–} 0.005) \times D$ — accommodates thermal expansion.
2. **Side Clearance (Axial):** $0.04 \text{–} 0.10$ mm — allows radial flex.
3. **Back Clearance (Radial):** $0.20 \text{–} 0.40$ mm — prevents ring from jamming in the groove.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Derive an expression for the displacement, velocity, and acceleration of a piston in a slider-crank mechanism, using the small-end connecting rod approximation. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss in detail the regulatory standards (AIS and IS) applicable to pistons and piston rings used in Indian automotive applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Setup (1 mark):** Let $r$ = crank radius, $l$ = connecting rod length, $\theta$ = crank angle from TDC, $\omega$ = angular velocity.

**Displacement Derivation (3 marks):**
The x-coordinate of the piston pin is:
$$
x_{\text{pin}} = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{\,l^{2} - r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta\,}
$$
At TDC ($\theta=0$): $x_0 = r + l$. Therefore:
$$
x = r(1 - \cos\theta) + l - \sqrt{\,l^{2} - r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta\,}
$$
Expanding the square root using the binomial series for $\lambda^{2}\sin^{2}\theta \ll 1$:
$$
\Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\,x = r(1 - \cos\theta) + \frac{r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2l}\,}
$$

**Velocity Derivation (1.5 marks):**
Differentiating with respect to time:
$$
\Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\,v = r\,\omega\,(\sin\theta + \tfrac{r\,\sin 2\theta}{2l})\,}
$$

**Acceleration Derivation (1.5 marks):**
Differentiating again:
$$
\Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\,a = r\,\omega^{2}\,(\cos\theta + \tfrac{r\,\cos 2\theta}{l})\,}
$$

**Significance:** Maximum acceleration occurs at TDC and exceeds the minimum (at BDC) by $2r^{2}\omega^{2}/l$ — the source of the engine's secondary rocking couple.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Indian Standards (BIS) (3.5 marks):**
- **IS 8062:** Aluminium alloy piston castings — chemical composition, mechanical properties, dimensional tolerances, hardness, and radiographic inspection.
- **IS 7284:** Cast iron pistons for heavy-duty diesel engines.
- **IS 3221:** Piston ring dimensions and tolerances.
- **IS 7902:** Gudgeon (wrist) pins.
- **IS 7270:** Oil control ring specifications.
- **IS 11005:** Compression ring material specifications.

**Automotive Industry Standards (AIS) by ARAI/MoRTH (3.5 marks):**
- **AIS-010:** General safety requirements applicable to all motor vehicle components including pistons.
- **AIS-016:** Material specification framework for automotive components.
- **AIS-068:** Bharat Stage VI emission norms — indirectly mandate piston-ring sealing integrity.
- **AIS-072:** Diesel engine emissions (BS-VI equivalent of ECE R49).
- **AIS-052:** Type approval procedure for engine components.

**Regulatory Linkage:** The standards form a **hierarchical pyramid** — IS specifies material and dimensional requirements; AIS specifies functional, safety, and emission-related requirements; ISO/ECE provide the global benchmarking reference. Compliance is verified through **type approval testing** at ARAI or iCAT.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons Students Lose Marks on Piston-Ring Questions (Based on Past KTU Valuation Patterns):**
> 
> 1. **Sketch-Less Answers:** Examiners allocate **2–3 marks** for labelled sketches. Always include a piston cross-section labelled with *crown, ring grooves, lands, skirt, gudgeon pin boss*. Answers without sketches are penalised even if theory is correct.
> 
> 2. **Confusing the Three Gaps:** Many students mix up *end gap, side clearance, and back clearance*. **Memorise:** End gap is circumferential, side clearance is axial, back clearance is radial. Examiners will *deliberately* swap terms in the question to test this.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring Units in Numericals:** When deriving piston velocity/acceleration, **always state units** (m, m/s, m/s²). A correct number without units = 1-mark deduction.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the $\lambda^{2}$ Correction:** The simplified displacement equation *without* the $(r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta)/(2l)$ term is only an *approximation* — the full expansion is acceptable. KTU expects the *corrected* form for full marks.
> 
> 5. **Material-Standard Mismatch:** Writing "cast iron" for a modern SI piston (the correct answer is Al-Si alloy) is a factual error worth a full mark deduction.
> 
> 6. **AIS vs IS Confusion:** AIS = Automotive Industry Standards (issued by ARAI/MoRTH). IS = Indian Standards (issued by BIS). They are **not interchangeable**. Examiners will mark the answer wrong if the wrong authority is cited.
> 
> 7. **Skipping the Regulatory Linkage:** In PEMUT526 specifically, merely describing piston function is *insufficient* — you must tie every concept to a **standard**. Always add a closing line: "This design is validated under IS 8062 / AIS 010."

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Piston & Piston Rings**

### Piston Essentials
- ☐ Piston is a **regulated component** under IS 8062 (Al alloy) and IS 7284 (cast iron).
- ☐ Modern SI/CI pistons use **Al-Si eutectic alloy (12 % Si)**; high-output pistons use **hypereutectic (17–25 % Si)**.
- ☐ Three zones: **Crown, Ring Belt, Skirt**.
- ☐ **Cam-ground** and **slipper-skirt** designs reduce friction and slap.
- ☐ **Gudgeon pin** offset by ~1 mm reduces piston slap.
- ☐ Piston transfers **30–40 %** of combustion heat to the cylinder wall.

### Piston Ring Essentials
- ☐ **3 rings standard:** 2 compression + 1 oil control.
- ☐ **End gap** = $(0.003 \text{–}0.005)\times D$ — circumferential clearance.
- ☐ **Side clearance** = 0.04–0.10 mm — axial.
- ☐ **Back clearance** = 0.20–0.40 mm — radial.
- ☐ **Top ring** = rectangular or barrel-faced, often chrome-plated.
- ☐ **Second ring** = taper-faced, acts as wiper.
- ☐ **Oil ring** = 3-piece slotted with expander spring.
- ☐ Ring material: grey cast iron, alloy steel, with MoS₂/chrome/DLC coatings.
- ☐ Governing standard: **IS 3221**.

### Kinematic Formulas (Must Memorise)
- ☐ $x = r(1 - \cos\theta) + \dfrac{r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2l}$
- ☐ $v = r\,\omega\,(\sin\theta + \dfrac{r\sin 2\theta}{2l})$
- ☐ $a = r\,\omega^{2}(\cos\theta + \dfrac{r\cos 2\theta}{l})$
- ☐ $p = \dfrac{E\,t^{2}\,(R_{f} - R_{w})}{12\,R_{f}^{2}\,R_{w}}$
- ☐ $\sigma = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$
- ☐ $\bar{v}_p = \dfrac{2LN}{60}$

### Regulatory Map (PEMUT526 Critical)
- ☐ **IS 8062** — Aluminium piston castings.
- ☐ **IS 3221** — Piston ring dimensions.
- ☐ **IS 7902** — Gudgeon pins.
- ☐ **IS 7284** — Cast iron pistons.
- ☐ **IS 7270** — Oil control rings.
- ☐ **AIS-010** — Safety requirements.
- ☐ **AIS-016** — Material specifications.
- ☐ **AIS-068 / ECE R83** — SI emissions (indirectly).
- ☐ **AIS-072 / ECE R49** — CI emissions (indirectly).
- ☐ **ISO 6621** — Wear measurement.

### One-Line Exam Punch Lines
- ☐ "Piston-ring sealing integrity is the **upstream determinant** of emission compliance under BS-VI."
- ☐ "The **three gaps** are: end (circumferential), side (axial), back (radial)."
- ☐ "Hypereutectic Al-Si pistons reduce cold-start clearance, improving NVH and fuel economy."
- ☐ "Mean piston speed is the **primary rating parameter** for engine durability."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standards for automotive materials: metals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Standards for automotive materials: metals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Automotive Material Standards (Metals)

**Automotive Material Standards (Metals)** are a set of codified, internationally harmonized technical specifications, chemical compositions, mechanical property limits, testing methodologies, and designation conventions that govern the selection, processing, qualification, and traceability of metallic materials (ferrous and non-ferrous) used in the manufacture of road vehicles. These standards are issued by recognized national and international bodies such as **SAE International**, **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)**, **ASTM International**, **EN (European Norm)**, **JIS (Japanese Industrial Standards)**, **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)**, and **AISI (American Iron and Steel Institute)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> Standards for automotive materials define the **chemical composition limits, mechanical properties, microstructure, surface finish, dimensional tolerances, and testing protocols** that an automotive metal must satisfy to be considered fit for a given vehicle subsystem (chassis, body-in-white, powertrain, suspension, etc.).

The objective of these standards is to ensure:
- **Vehicle safety** (crashworthiness, fatigue life)
- **Manufacturing repeatability** (batch-to-batch consistency)
- **Interchangeability and global supply chain compatibility**
- **Regulatory compliance** (emission, recyclability, homologation)
- **Lightweighting** (enabling AHSS, aluminium, magnesium adoption)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Vehicle Passport" Concept

Imagine a metallic steel sheet that goes into the door panel of a car. Without a standard, the steel mill in India, the design engineer in Germany, and the assembly plant in Japan would all speak **different "languages"** about that material. The standard acts like a **universal passport** that contains:

- **The DNA (Chemical Composition)**: e.g., "I am steel with 0.18% Carbon, 1.2% Manganese"
- **The Fitness Report (Mechanical Properties)**: e.g., "I can withstand 350 MPa yield strength"
- **The Health Check-up Procedure (Test Method)**: e.g., "Test me per ASTM E8"
- **The Translation Key (Designation Code)**: e.g., "I am known as **AISI 1018** in the USA, **EN 1.0402** in Europe, **JIS S15C** in Japan, and **IS 2062** in India"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A material standard is **NOT** a single document; it is a **family of cross-linked documents** covering composition, testing, finishing, and qualification. For an automotive engineer, knowing the designation system is as important as knowing the material itself.

## 1.3 Classification of Automotive Metal Standards

Automotive metal standards can be broadly classified into **five functional categories**:

| S.No | Category | Purpose | Example Bodies |
|------|----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | **Designation / Nomenclature Standards** | Uniquely identify alloys | AISI-SAE, EN, JIS, IS |
| 2 | **Composition & Product Standards** | Specify chemistry & form | ASTM, EN, IS, JIS |
| 3 | **Testing / Mechanical Property Standards** | Define test methods | ASTM E8, ISO 6892, JIS Z2241 |
| 4 | **Automotive Application Standards** | Material for specific auto parts | SAE J403, EN 10149, IS 2062 |
| 5 | **Quality / Homologation Standards** | IATF 16949, OEM specs | IATF, ISO/TS, OEM-specific |

## 1.4 Major Standards-Setting Bodies in Automotive Metallurgy

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 emphasizes familiarity with at least the following bodies. Each is internationally recognized and India’s **BIS** aligns increasingly with **ISO** under WTO obligations.

- **SAE International** (Society of Automotive Engineers, USA) – Issues **SAE J-series** for automotive materials
- **AISI** (American Iron and Steel Institute) – Jointly with SAE, maintains the **AISI-SAE steel designation system** (e.g., **10xx, 41xx, 43xx, 51xx, 61xx, 86xx, 9xx**)
- **ISO** (International Organization for Standardization) – Global harmonization (e.g., **ISO 3573** for hot-rolled carbon steel sheet)
- **ASTM International** (American Society for Testing and Materials) – Test methods (e.g., **ASTM A1008** for cold-rolled steel)
- **EN (European Norm / CEN)** – Mandatory in EU (e.g., **EN 10130** for cold-rolled low-carbon steel flat products)
- **JIS (Japanese Industrial Standards)** – e.g., **JIS G3131** for hot-rolled mild steel
- **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** – e.g., **IS 2062** for hot-rolled medium & high tensile structural steel
- **DIN (Deutsches Institut für Normung)** – German (largely superseded by EN)
- **UNS (Unified Numbering System)** – Joint ASTM-SAE system for metals/alloys (e.g., **UNS G10100** = AISI 1010)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 AISI-SAE Steel Designation System (4-Digit Code)

The **AISI-SAE** system is the most widely taught designation system in KTU modules. It is a **4-digit numerical code** for plain carbon and alloy steels.

### 2.1.1 Decoding the Four Digits

$$\underbrace{AB}_{\text{Steel type}}\ \underbrace{CD}_{\text{Carbon content}}$$

- **Digit 1 (A)**: Major alloying class
- **Digit 2 (B)**: Modifying element / sub-class
- **Digit 3 (C)**: For alloy steels, indicates percentage of major alloying element (in approximate %)
- **Digit 4 (D)**: For carbon steels = **carbon content in 0.01% (i.e., points of carbon)**
- For **alloy steels**, the last two digits (CD) together indicate **carbon content in 0.01%**

### 2.1.2 AISI-SAE First Two Digit Categories

| First Two Digits | Steel Type | Example Grade | Application |
|------------------|------------|---------------|-------------|
| **10xx** | Plain Carbon Steel | SAE 1018, 1020 | General auto parts, fasteners |
| **11xx** | Resulfurized (free-machining) | SAE 1117 | Machined components |
| **12xx** | Rephosphorized & resulfurized | SAE 1215 | High-speed machining |
| **13xx** | Manganese Steel (1.75% Mn) | SAE 1330 | Higher strength |
| **15xx** | Manganese Steel (1.0% Mn) | SAE 1524 | Carburizing grades |
| **23xx** | Nickel Steel (3.5% Ni) | SAE 2330 | Heavy-duty gears |
| **25xx** | Nickel Steel (5.0% Ni) | SAE 2515 | Case-hardened |
| **31xx / 32xx** | Ni-Cr Steel | SAE 3140 | Gears, shafts |
| **33xx** | Ni-Cr Steel (3.5% Ni) | SAE 3330 | Transmission |
| **40xx / 44xx** | Mo Steel | SAE 4037 | Crank shafts |
| **41xx** | Cr-Mo Steel | SAE 4140, 4150 | **Connecting rods, axle shafts** |
| **43xx** | Ni-Cr-Mo Steel | SAE 4320 | Heavy-duty gears |
| **46xx / 48xx** | Ni-Mo Steel | SAE 4620 | Gears |
| **51xx** | Cr Steel (low) | SAE 5120 | Bearings, gears |
| **52xx** | Cr Steel (high, ~1.5%) | SAE 52100 | **Ball/roller bearings** |
| **61xx** | Cr-V Steel | SAE 6150 | Springs, valves |
| **72xx** | W-Cr Steel | SAE 7260 | Tooling |
| **81xx / 86xx / 87xx / 88xx** | Ni-Cr-Mo Steel | SAE 8620 | **Case-hardened gears (most common auto gear)** |
| **92xx** | Si-Mn Steel | SAE 9260 | Leaf springs |
| **93xx / 94xx / 97xx / 98xx** | Ni-Cr-Mo | SAE 9310 | High-performance |

### 2.1.3 Worked Examples (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example 1**: **AISI 4140**
> - "41" = Cr-Mo steel (≈1% Cr, ≈0.2% Mo)
> - "40" = 0.40% Carbon
> - **Application**: Crankshafts, connecting rods, axle shafts

> **Example 2**: **AISI 8620**
> - "86" = Ni-Cr-Mo steel (≈0.55% Ni, 0.5% Cr, 0.2% Mo)
> - "20" = 0.20% Carbon
> - **Application**: Carburized gears, pinions, synchronizer hubs

> **Example 3**: **AISI 52100**
> - "52" = High-Chromium steel (≈1.5% Cr)
> - "100" = 1.00% Carbon
> - **Application**: Anti-friction ball & roller bearings

## 2.2 UNS (Unified Numbering System) Designation

The **UNS** is a 5-character alphanumeric code: **Letter + 4 digits**

$$\underbrace{S}_{\text{Steel}}\ \underbrace{G}_{\text{Standard grade}}\ \underbrace{10100}_{\text{AISI number}}$$

| UNS Letter | Metal Class |
|-----------|-------------|
| **F** | Cast irons |
| **G** | Carbon & alloy steels (AISI-SAE) |
| **H** | Heat-resistant / stainless steels (limited use) |
| **J** | Cast steels |
| **K** | Miscellaneous steels (e.g., low alloy) |
| **S** | Stainless & heat-resistant steels (S30400 = SS304) |
| **N** | Nickel & nickel alloys |
| **A** | Aluminium & aluminium alloys |
| **C** | Copper & copper alloys |
| **T** | Titanium |
| **R** | Refractory metals |
| **Z** | Zinc & zinc alloys |
| **M** | Magnesium |

**Examples**:
- **UNS G10100** = AISI 1010
- **UNS G41400** = AISI 4140
- **UNS S30400** = Stainless steel 304
- **UNS A96061** = Aluminium 6061

## 2.3 European EN Designation System (Steel)

The **EN 10027** standard governs European steel designation.

### 2.3.1 EN 10027-1 (Symbolic / Alphanumerical)

**Format 1** (Steels named by application & mechanical property):
$$\text{Letter}_1\ \text{Number}\ \text{Letter}_2\ \text{Number}_2$$

| Letter₁ | Use |
|---------|-----|
| **S** | Structural steel |
| **P** | Pressure vessel steel |
| **L** | Line pipe steel |
| **E** | Engineering (machine) steel |
| **B** | Reinforced concrete (rebar) |
| **H** | High-strength flat products for cold forming |
| **D** | Flat products for cold forming |
| **T** | Tinmill products |

**Example**: **S355J2**
- **S** = Structural steel
- **355** = Minimum yield strength = **355 MPa** at ≤16 mm thickness
- **J2** = Impact energy = **27 J at -20°C** (J0=0°C, J2=-20°C, J3=-30°C, J4=-40°C, J5=-50°C, J6=-60°C)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 must-know**: S355 is widely used in commercial vehicle chassis, while **S235** is the equivalent of mild steel IS 2062 Gr.A.

### 2.3.2 EN 10027-2 (Numerical)

**Format**: `1.XXXX` (steel), `2.XXXX` (non-ferrous)

**Example**: **1.0402** = EN S235J2 equivalent
- **1.** = Steel
- **04** = Plain carbon steel with Mn < 1%
- **02** = Specific composition variant

| EN Number | Material |
|-----------|----------|
| **1.0038** | S235JR |
| **1.0044** | S275JR |
| **1.0050** | E295 |
| **1.0060** | E335 |
| **1.0070** | E360 |
| **1.0401** | C15 (case-hardening) |
| **1.0402** | C22 |
| **1.0503** | C45 (medium carbon) |
| **1.1181** | C35E |
| **1.3505** | 100Cr6 (bearing steel = AISI 52100) |
| **1.6582** | 34CrNiMo6 (gear steel) |
| **1.7015** | 15Cr3 |
| **1.7131** | 16MnCr5 (carburizing gear steel) |
| **1.7225** | 42CrMo4 (≡ AISI 4140) |

## 2.4 Cast Iron Designation

### 2.4.1 ISO 1083 / EN 1561 (Grey Cast Iron)

**Format**: `EN-GJL-NNN` or `GG-NN` (old DIN)
- **EN-GJL** = Fonte à Graphite Lamellaire (Grey iron)
- **Number** = Minimum tensile strength in **MPa × 10**

**Example**: **EN-GJL-250**
- Grey cast iron with minimum **250 MPa** tensile strength
- **Application**: Engine blocks, cylinder heads, brake drums

### 2.4.2 ISO 1083 / EN 1563 (Ductile / SG Iron)

**Format**: `EN-GJS-NNN` or `GGG-NN` (old DIN)
- **EN-GJS** = Ductile (Spheroidal Graphite) iron
- **Number** = Minimum tensile strength in **MPa**

**Example**: **EN-GJS-500-7**
- 500 MPa UTS, 7% elongation
- **Application**: Crankshafts, steering knuckles, suspension arms

### 2.4.3 ISO 2892 (Austempered Ductile Iron - ADI)

**Example**: **EN-GJS-800-8** (ADI grade)
- Higher strength, used in **swing arms, knuckles**

### 2.4.4 Malleable Cast Iron

- **EN-GJMW** = Whiteheart malleable
- **EN-GJMB** = Blackheart malleable
- **Example**: EN-GJMB-350-10 (350 MPa UTS, 10% elongation)

## 2.5 Aluminium Alloy Designation Systems

### 2.5.1 AA (Aluminum Association) Wrought Alloy System

**Format**: `AXXX` (4 digits)

$$\underbrace{A}_{\text{Aluminium}}\ \underbrace{B}_{\text{Alloy family}}\ \underbrace{C}_{\text{Modification}}\ \underbrace{D}_{\text{Identity}}$$

| First Digit (B) | Alloy Family |
|----------------|--------------|
| **1xxx** | Pure Al (≥99%) – corrosion resistant, low strength |
| **2xxx** | Al-Cu (heat-treatable) – aircraft, pistons |
| **3xxx** | Al-Mn (non-heat-treatable) – body panels, radiators |
| **4xxx** | Al-Si (heat-treatable, low exp) – pistons, engine blocks |
| **5xxx** | Al-Mg (non-heat-treatable) – ship hulls, structural |
| **6xxx** | Al-Mg-Si (heat-treatable) – **body sheet, extrusions** |
| **7xxx** | Al-Zn (heat-treatable) – high strength, aerospace |
| **8xxx** | Al-Li / other |
| **9xxx** | Reserved / future |

**Common Automotive Alloys**:

| Alloy | Family | Application |
|-------|--------|-------------|
| **AA 1050** | 1xxx | Radiator tubes, fin stock |
| **AA 2014** | 2xxx | Forged pistons (older) |
| **AA 2024** | 2xxx | Truck wheels, aircraft |
| **AA 3003** | 3xxx | Heat exchangers, fuel tanks |
| **AA 5052** | 5xxx | Body panels, fuel tanks |
| **AA 5083** | 5xxx | Chassis structures |
| **AA 5754** | 5xxx | Bonnet, door (BMW/Ford) |
| **AA 6016** | 6xxx | **Body sheet (Audi A8, Ford F-150)** |
| **AA 6061** | 6xxx | Suspension arms, drive shafts |
| **AA 6063** | 6xxx | Extrusions, bumper reinforcements |
| **AA 6082** | 6xxx | Crash management |
| **AA 7075** | 7xxx | Aerospace, motorcycle swingarm |

### 2.5.2 Temper Designation (e.g., T6, H32, O)

| Temper Code | Meaning |
|-------------|---------|
| **O** | Annealed (soft) |
| **H1x, H2x, H3x** | Strain-hardened (H14 = half-hard, H32 = quarter-hard + stabilized) |
| **T1-T10** | Heat-treated (T4 = solution + natural aging, **T6 = solution + artificial aging**) |
| **F** | As-fabricated |

**Example**: **AA 6061-T6** = Solution heat-treated + artificially aged → used in motorcycle and car suspension components.

## 2.6 Copper and Copper Alloy Standards (UNS / CDA)

| UNS | Common Name | Composition | Use in Auto |
|-----|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| **C11000** | ETP Copper | 99.9% Cu | Wiring harness |
| **C26000** | Cartridge Brass | 70% Cu, 30% Zn | Radiator cores |
| **C36000** | Free-machining Brass | Cu-Zn-Pb | Fittings |
| **C46400** | Naval Brass | Cu-Zn-Sn | Bearings |
| **C51000** | Phosphor Bronze | Cu-Sn-P | Springs, connectors |
| **C70600** | Cupro-Nickel 90/10 | Cu-Ni-Fe | Brake lines, coolers |
| **C71500** | Cupro-Nickel 70/30 | Cu-Ni-Fe | Hydraulic lines |

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is critical for automotive engineers**:
> 1. **Homologation**: Vehicles sold in India must comply with AIS (Automotive Industry Standards) which themselves reference BIS. Material traceability via heat number, MTC (Mill Test Certificate) per **EN 10204** is mandatory.
> 2. **Supply Chain**: A single car has **40+ steel grades** and **12+ aluminium grades** from multiple global suppliers. Standardized designation enables unified quality.
> 3. **Recyclability**: Under **ELV Directive 2000/53/EC**, ≥95% of vehicle mass must be recyclable. Materials must be identified & segregated using standard markings (e.g., **ISO 11469** for plastic marking, **SAE J1086** for steel marking).
> 4. **Crash Simulation**: CAE inputs (stress-strain curves, hardening laws) are derived from standards-defined test methods. Without a standard, the FEA result is invalid.
> 5. **Lightweighting**: Migration from mild steel → **AHSS (Advanced High-Strength Steel)** → aluminium → magnesium requires designers to be fluent in multiple designation systems.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Calculation: Carbon Content from AISI-SAE Code

### Problem
A KTU board question gives you a code like **AISI 4340** and asks you to determine the **nominal carbon content in weight percent**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given**: AISI 4340 designation

**Step 1**: Identify the first two digits (steel type)
- "43" → Ni-Cr-Mo alloy steel with approximately:
  - 1.83% Nickel
  - 0.80% Chromium
  - 0.25% Molybdenum

**Step 2**: Identify the last two digits
- "40"

**Step 3**: Apply the AISI-SAE carbon content rule
For **alloy steels (4-digit codes starting with 2-9)**, the last two digits give carbon content in hundredths of a percent (i.e., points of carbon):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Carbon \%} &= \frac{\text{Last two digits}}{100} \\
&= \frac{40}{100} \\
&= 0.40\ \%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4**: Final composition of AISI 4340

| Element | Weight % |
|---------|----------|
| Carbon (C) | 0.40 |
| Nickel (Ni) | 1.83 |
| Chromium (Cr) | 0.80 |
| Molybdenum (Mo) | 0.25 |
| Manganese (Mn) | 0.70 |
| Iron (Fe) | Balance |

**Conclusion**: AISI 4340 contains **0.40 wt% C** and is widely used in **aircraft, truck axle shafts, and high-stress automotive components** due to its excellent hardenability and toughness.

---

## 3.2 Derivative Calculation: EN Steel Yield Strength

### Problem
A truck chassis is specified to use **S355J2** steel. Determine:
1. The minimum yield strength
2. The minimum impact energy and corresponding test temperature
3. The Indian standard equivalent (if any)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Decode "S" prefix

$$\text{"S"} = \text{Structural steel} \quad \text{(used in load-bearing structures)}$$

**Step 2**: Decode the numerical "355"

According to EN 10025 (the parent standard for hot-rolled structural steels), the numerical value equals the **minimum yield strength in MPa** for the thinnest gauge (≤ 16 mm plate thickness):

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{eH,\min} &= 355 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(for thickness} \leq 16 \text{ mm)} \\
R_{eH,\min} &= 345 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(for } 16 < t \leq 40 \text{ mm)} \\
R_{eH,\min} &= 335 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(for } 40 < t \leq 63 \text{ mm)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Yield strength **decreases** as thickness increases due to lower cooling rate and weaker texture. This is called the **thickness effect** and is well documented in EN 10025-2.

**Step 3**: Decode the "J2" suffix

| Suffix | Impact Energy (KV) | Test Temperature |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **JR** | 27 J | +20°C |
| **J0** | 27 J | 0°C |
| **J2** | 27 J | **−20°C** |
| **K2** | 40 J | −20°C |
| **J3** | 27 J | −30°C |
| **J4** | 27 J | −40°C |
| **J5** | 27 J | −50°C |
| **J6** | 27 J | −60°C |

Therefore: **J2** → 27 J Charpy impact at **−20°C**.

**Step 4**: Indian equivalent

According to **IS 2062:2011**, the closest equivalent grade is **E350 (Fe 410 / E 410)** but with different toughness:

| EN Grade | IS 2062 Equivalent | Notes |
|----------|--------------------|-------|
| S235JR | E250 Gr.A | Mild structural |
| S275JR | E300 Gr.A | — |
| S355JR | E350 Gr.A | Higher strength |
| S355J2 | E350 Gr.BR (B grade for impact) | Not exact 1:1 |

**Conclusion**: S355J2 specifies **$R_{eH} \geq 355$ MPa** and **KV ≥ 27 J at −20°C** — making it suitable for **commercial vehicle chassis** operating in cold climates.

---

## 3.3 Derivative Calculation: UNS ↔ AISI-SAE Conversion

### Problem
A part drawing cites **UNS G41400**. Determine the AISI-SAE grade and the typical automotive application.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Decode UNS prefix
- **G** = Carbon or alloy steel
- **4-digit numeric** corresponds to **AISI-SAE system** with prefix inserted as **G** instead of standalone

**Step 2**: Extract AISI number
- Drop the "G" → "41400"
- The last digit "0" is a **UNS sequence indicator** (often 0 for direct AISI equivalence)
- AISI-SAE equivalent = **AISI 4140**

**Step 3**: Determine composition & application (from §2.1.2)

| Element | Weight % |
|---------|----------|
| C | 0.40 |
| Cr | 1.0 |
| Mo | 0.20 |
| Mn | 0.85 |

**Step 4**: Identify application
- AISI 4140 (UNS G41400, EN 42CrMo4, JIS SCM440) is used in:
  - Connecting rods
  - Axle shafts
  - Steering knuckles (heavy vehicles)
  - Splined shafts
  - High-strength fasteners

**Conclusion**: UNS G41400 ≡ AISI 4140 ≡ EN 42CrMo4 (1.7225) ≡ JIS SCM440.

---

## 3.4 Derivative Calculation: Aluminium Alloy Family & Heat Treatment

### Problem
A designer selects **AA 6061-T6** for a rear lower control arm. Verify the alloy family, temper, and predict the approximate mechanical properties.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Identify the alloy family

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{First digit} &= 6 \Rightarrow \text{Al-Mg-Si family} \\
\text{Alloying system} &= \text{Mg}_2\text{Si (magnesium silicide)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2**: Decode the temper

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Temper} &= \text{T6} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Solution heat-treated} \rightarrow \text{Quenched} \rightarrow \text{Artificially aged} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Peak-strength condition}
\end{aligned}$$

The T6 process:
$$\underbrace{\text{Heat to } 530\ ^\circ\text{C}}_{\text{Dissolution of Mg}_2\text{Si}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Quench}}_{\text{Retain Si/Mg in solution}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Age at } 175\ ^\circ\text{C, 8-12 h}}_{\text{Precipitation of fine Mg}_2\text{Si}}$$

**Step 3**: Mechanical properties (typical T6)

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) | **310 MPa** |
| Yield Strength (0.2% offset) | **276 MPa** |
| Elongation (in 50 mm) | **12 %** |
| Hardness (Brinell) | **95 HB** |
| Density | **2.70 g/cm³** |
| Modulus of Elasticity | **68.9 GPa** |
| Shear Strength | **207 MPa** |
| Fatigue Strength (10⁷ cycles) | **96 MPa** |

**Step 4**: Compare with AISI 4140 (for context)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Weight saving} &= \frac{\rho_{steel} - \rho_{Al}}{\rho_{steel}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{7.85 - 2.70}{7.85} \times 100\% \\
&= 65.6\ \%
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion**: AA 6061-T6 provides **~310 MPa UTS** (comparable to mild steel) at **1/3 the weight** — ideal for weight-critical suspension parts.

---

## 3.5 Derivative Calculation: Strength Comparison in Cast Irons

### Problem
An engine block is to be made of grey cast iron with minimum tensile strength **250 MPa**. Identify the **EN, JIS, and IS** equivalent designations.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Translate to EN designation
- EN 1561: **EN-GJL-250**
  - **GJL** = Grey cast iron (Graphite Lamellaire)
  - **250** = Minimum tensile strength = **250 MPa**

**Step 2**: JIS equivalent
- JIS G5501: **FC250**
  - **FC** = Ferrous Casting (grey iron)
  - **250** = Tensile strength = **250 MPa**

**Step 3**: IS (India) equivalent
- **IS 210:2009 Gr.20** or **FG 220/250** (older numbering)
  - **Gr.20** = Grade with 200 MPa UTS (closest, slightly lower)
  - For exact 250 MPa: **FG 250**

**Step 4**: Tabulate

| System | Designation | Decoding |
|--------|------------|----------|
| EN | EN-GJL-250 | Grey iron, 250 MPa UTS |
| JIS | FC250 | Ferrous Casting, 250 MPa UTS |
| IS | FG 250 (IS 210) | Foundry grade 250 MPa |
| ASTM | ASTM A48 Class 30 | Class 30 = 30,000 psi ≈ 207 MPa |
| UNS | F11701 | F11xxx for grey iron |

**Conclusion**: An engine block in **EN-GJL-250 (FC250)** provides adequate compressive strength, damping, and wear resistance at low cost.

## 3.7 Derivation: Density-Based Weight Saving (Lightweighting Equation)

The fundamental lightweighting equation used in automotive design is:

$$\% \text{Weight Saving} = \left(1 - \frac{\rho_{\text{new}}}{\rho_{\text{baseline}}}\right) \times 100$$

### Worked Example: Replacing Steel Wheel with Al Alloy Wheel

**Given**:
- Steel wheel: $\rho_{Fe} = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- Aluminium wheel: $\rho_{Al} = 2.70 \text{ g/cm}^3$

**Solution**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Weight Saving} &= \left(1 - \frac{2.70}{7.85}\right) \times 100 \\
&= (1 - 0.3439) \times 100 \\
&= 65.6\ \%
\end{aligned}$$

For a **8 kg steel wheel**, the Al replacement weighs:
$$m_{Al} = 8 \times (1 - 0.656) = 8 \times 0.344 = 2.75 \text{ kg}$$

**Saving per wheel**: 5.25 kg → **21 kg total for 4 wheels** (unsprung mass benefit).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Unsprung mass reduction has a **compounded benefit** on ride & handling (≈1.5× factor on perceived weight), so the effective benefit is **~30 kg of felt mass**.

---

## 3.8 Derivation: Yield Strength vs Thickness (EN 10025 Rule)

For EN 10025 structural steels, the minimum yield strength follows an empirical thickness correction:

$$R_{eH}(t) = R_{eH}(t \leq 16) \times f(t)$$

| Thickness Range $t$ (mm) | Correction Factor $f(t)$ | S355 ReH (MPa) |
|---------------------------|--------------------------|----------------|
| $t \leq 16$ | 1.00 | 355 |
| $16 < t \leq 40$ | 0.97 | 345 |
| $40 < t \leq 63$ | 0.94 | 335 |
| $63 < t \leq 80$ | 0.91 | 325 |
| $80 < t \leq 100$ | 0.88 | 315 |
| $100 < t \leq 150$ | — | 295 |

**Example**: A truck chassis cross member of thickness **30 mm** uses S355J2.
$$R_{eH} = 355 \times 0.97 = 344.35 \text{ MPa} \approx 345 \text{ MPa}$$

This is the value to use in **strength calculations per EN 1993 (Eurocode 3)**.

## 4.4 Block Diagram: EN 10027-1 Decoding Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[EN Steel Designation String] --> P1{Starts with S?}
    P1 --> P2[Yes - Structural Steel]
    P1 --> P3[No - Continue Check]

    P3 --> P4{Starts with P?}
    P4 --> P5[Yes - Pressure Vessel]
    P3 --> P6{Starts with E?}
    P6 --> P7[Yes - Engineering Steel]
    P3 --> P8{Starts with L?}
    P8 --> P9[Yes - Line Pipe]

    P2 --> Q1[Read Numeric Value]
    P5 --> Q1
    P7 --> Q1
    P9 --> Q1

    Q1[Read 3 digit yield strength] --> R1[Value in MPa]
    R1 --> S1[Read Suffix]

    S1 --> S2{First Suffix Letter?}
    S2 --> T1[J = Charpy Energy]
    S2 --> T2[K = Higher Charpy]
    S2 --> T3[N = Normalized]
    S2 --> T4[M = Thermomech Rolled]

    S1 --> S3{Number after letter?}
    S3 --> U1[0 means 0 deg C]
    S3 --> U2[2 means minus 20 deg C]
    S3 --> U3[3 means minus 30 deg C]
    S3 --> U4[4 means minus 40 deg C]
    S3 --> U5[5 means minus 50 deg C]
    S3 --> U6[6 means minus 60 deg C]
```

> **Figure 4.4**: Flowchart for **decoding EN 10027-1 steel designations** (e.g., S355J2 → S=Structural, 355=MPa, J=Charpy, 2=−20°C test).

## 4.5 Block Diagram: AISI-SAE 4-Digit Decoding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[AISI 4 Digit Code] --> M1{First Digit?}

    M1 --> M2[1 = Carbon Steel]
    M1 --> M3[2 = Nickel Steel]
    M1 --> M4[3 = Nickel-Chrome]
    M1 --> M5[4 = Molybdenum]
    M1 --> M6[5 = Chromium]
    M1 --> M7[6 = Chromium-Vanadium]
    M1 --> M8[8 = Ni-Cr-Mo]
    M1 --> M9[9 = Silicon-Manganese]

    M2 --> N1{Second Digit?}
    N1 --> O1[0 = Plain Carbon]
    N1 --> O2[1 = Resulfurized]
    N1 --> O3[2 = Resulfurized and Rephosphorized]
    N1 --> O4[3 = 1.75 percent Mn]
    N1 --> O5[5 = 1.0 percent Mn]

    M2 --> P1[Read Last 2 Digits]
    P1 --> P2[Carbon content in 0.01 percent]
    P2 --> P3[Example 1018 means 0.18 percent C]

    M8 --> Q1[Read Last 2 Digits]
    Q1 --> Q2[Carbon content in 0.01 percent]
    Q2 --> Q3[Example 8620 means 0.20 percent C with Ni Cr Mo]
```

> **Figure 4.5**: **AISI-SAE designation decoding logic** — the most-tested standard in KTU 2024 ESE.

## 4.6 Block Diagram: Standards Body Mapping by Region

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Global Standards Convergence in Automotive Metals]

    R0 --> S1[Europe: EN CEN]
    R0 --> S2[North America: AISI SAE ASTM]
    R0 --> S3[Asia: JIS China GB India IS]
    R0 --> S4[Global: ISO]

    S1 --> T1[EN 10025 Structural]
    S1 --> T2[EN 10149 HSLA]
    S1 --> T3[EN 10130 Cold Rolled]
    S1 --> T4[EN 1706 Aluminium Castings]
    S1 --> T5[EN 10204 MTC]

    S2 --> U1[SAE J403 Carbon]
    S2 --> U2[SAE J404 Alloy]
    S2 --> U3[ASTM A1008 Cold Rolled]
    S2 --> U4[ASTM E8 Tensile]
    S2 --> U5[UNS System]

    S3 --> V1[JIS G3101 General Struct]
    S3 --> V2[JIS G3131 Hot Rolled Mild]
    S3 --> V3[JIS G3141 Cold Rolled]
    S3 --> V4[IS 2062 Indian Structural]
    S3 --> V5[IS 513 Indian CR]

    S4 --> W1[ISO 3573 Hot Rolled]
    S4 --> W2[ISO 6892 Tensile]
    S4 --> W3[ISO 148 Charpy]
    S4 --> W4[ISO 11469 Plastic Marking]
```

> **Figure 4.6**: **Geographic mapping** of standards — essential for understanding cross-border vehicle platforms (e.g., Suzuki cars in India follow JIS + IS, while VW/BMW follow EN).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Question A1** (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
*What is the AISI-SAE designation for a steel containing 0.40% C, 1% Cr, 0.2% Mo? State two typical automotive applications.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
1. **Designation**: **AISI 4140** (2 marks)
   - "41" → Cr-Mo steel family
   - "40" → 0.40% carbon content
2. **Applications**: Connecting rods, axle shafts, propeller shafts, high-strength fasteners (1 mark).

---

### **Question A2** (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*Explain the meaning of the designation "EN-GJL-250" used for engine blocks.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
- **EN**: European Norm (CEN) – ½ mark
- **GJL**: Grey cast iron with lamellar graphite – 1 mark
- **250**: Minimum tensile strength = **250 MPa** – 1 mark
- **Application**: Engine blocks, cylinder heads, brake drums – ½ mark

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**: KTU 2024 ESE Part-B questions carry 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. **Confusing the last two digits of AISI** between carbon steels (1xxx) and alloy steels (2xxx-9xxx). For alloy steels, the last two digits are **carbon in 0.01%**, NOT just the last digit.
> 2. **Writing the EN 10027-1 suffix wrongly** — J0, J2, J3 refer to **−0°C, −20°C, −30°C** impact test, NOT 0°C, 20°C, 30°C.
> 3. **Forgetting the equivalent IS/BIS number** — KTU specifically tests Indian standards compliance.
> 4. **Writing "Temper T6" without explanation** — T6 = solution heat-treated + artificially aged is mandatory.
> 5. **Confusing EN-GJL (grey iron) with EN-GJS (ductile iron)** — these are entirely different materials.

---

### **Question B-Option-A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**

#### (a) **Explain the AISI-SAE 4-digit steel designation system with at least 5 examples used in automotive components. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**AISI-SAE 4-Digit Designation System (3 marks):**

The system uses 4 digits: **ABXX**

- **First digit (A)**: Major alloying element group
- **Second digit (B)**: Sub-classification or modifying element
- **Last two digits (XX)**:
  - For **1xxx** (carbon steels): indicate carbon content in **0.01%** (points)
  - For **2xxx–9xxx** (alloy steels): also indicate carbon content in **0.01%**

**Five Automotive Examples (4 marks – 1 each, with composition & use):**

| S.No | AISI-SAE | Composition | Auto Part |
|------|----------|-------------|-----------|
| 1 | **AISI 1018** | 0.18% C, plain carbon | General fasteners, pins, low-stress shafts |
| 2 | **AISI 4140** | 0.40% C, 1% Cr, 0.2% Mo | Connecting rods, axle shafts, drive shafts |
| 3 | **AISI 52100** | 1.00% C, 1.5% Cr | Ball/roller bearings, anti-friction races |
| 4 | **AISI 8620** | 0.20% C, Ni-Cr-Mo | Carburized transmission gears, pinions |
| 5 | **AISI 6150** | 0.50% C, 1% Cr, 0.15% V | Springs (coil, valve), stressed components |

---

#### (b) **Discuss the EN 10027-1 system with suitable examples for structural steel used in commercial vehicle chassis. Compare with the Indian Standard IS 2062. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**EN 10027-1 Symbolic System (3 marks):**
- **Letter 1**: Indicates use category (S=Structural, P=Pressure, L=Line pipe, E=Engineering, H=High-strength flat)
- **Number**: Minimum yield strength in **MPa** (at t ≤ 16 mm)
- **Letter 2 + Number**: Impact energy + test temperature
  - **J** = 27 J Charpy
  - **K** = 40 J Charpy
  - **JR/J0/J2/J3/J4/J5/J6** = +20°C / 0°C / −20°C / −30°C / −40°C / −50°C / −60°C
- **Additional suffixes**: N=Normalized, M=Thermomechanically rolled

**Worked Example for Chassis (2 marks):**

**S355J2** (EN 10025):
- S = Structural steel
- 355 = $R_{eH} \geq 355$ MPa
- J2 = 27 J impact at −20°C

Used in **truck chassis frames, cross members, longitudinal rails** where high strength + sub-zero toughness is required.

**Comparison with IS 2062:2011 (2 marks):**

| Property | EN S355J2 | IS 2062 E350 (Gr.BR) |
|----------|-----------|---------------------|
| Yield Strength (MPa) | 355 | 350 |
| Impact Energy | 27 J @ −20°C | 27 J @ 0°C (Gr.BR) or room temp |
| Application | Heavy commercial vehicle chassis in cold climates | Indian commercial vehicles, mild climates |

**Conclusion**: IS 2062 E350 is the Indian equivalent but is generally less stringent on low-temperature toughness. For high-altitude or export markets, EN S355J2 is preferred.

---

### **Question B-Option-B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**

#### (a) **Discuss the AA (Aluminum Association) 4-digit designation system. Identify suitable aluminium alloys with temper for: (i) body sheet panel, (ii) bumper reinforcement extrusion, and (iii) forged wheel. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**AA 4-Digit System (2 marks):**
- **1st digit**: Major alloying element
  - 1 = Pure Al (≥99%)
  - 2 = Al-Cu
  - 3 = Al-Mn
  - 4 = Al-Si
  - 5 = Al-Mg
  - 6 = Al-Mg-Si
  - 7 = Al-Zn
  - 8 = Al-Li / others
- **2nd digit**: Modification (0 = original)
- **Last two digits**: Alloy identity within family

**Temper System (1 mark):**
- **O** = Annealed
- **Hxx** = Strain-hardened
- **T1–T10** = Heat-treated
  - **T4** = Solution + natural aging (formable)
  - **T6** = Solution + artificial aging (peak strength)
  - **T5** = Cooled from elevated temperature + artificially aged (for extrusions)

**Alloy Selection (4 marks – 1.33 each):**

| Part | Alloy | Family | Temper | Justification |
|------|-------|--------|--------|---------------|
| (i) **Body Sheet Panel** | **AA 6016** | 6xxx Al-Mg-Si | **T4** | Excellent formability + paint-bake hardenability (T4 → T6 during paint cure). Used in **Audi A8, Ford F-150, Jaguar XJ**. |
| (ii) **Bumper Reinforcement (Extrusion)** | **AA 6063** or **AA 7003** | 6xxx / 7xxx | **T5** or **T6** | 6063-T5 has good extrudability + moderate strength (185 MPa UTS). 7003-T5 for higher crash loads. |
| (iii) **Forged Wheel** | **AA 6061** | 6xxx | **T6** | Forging + T6 → 310 MPa UTS with 12% elongation. Good fatigue, machinable. Alternative: **A356-T6** for cast wheels. |

---

#### (b) **Explain the classification of cast iron used in automotive applications. With a neat table, compare grey cast iron (EN-GJL), ductile iron (EN-GJS), and ADI on the basis of (i) graphite form, (ii) tensile strength, (iii) elongation, and (iv) typical applications. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**Classification of Cast Iron Used in Automotives (3 marks):**

Cast irons are Fe-C alloys with C > 2% (typically 2.5–4%) and Si 1–3%. Carbon exists as graphite or as combined carbide (Fe₃C).

**Five major types used in automobiles:**

1. **Grey Cast Iron (GJL)**: Graphite as flakes → excellent damping, low cost
2. **Ductile / Nodular / SG Iron (GJS)**: Graphite as spheroids → high ductility + strength
3. **Compacted Graphite Iron (CGI / GJV)**: Vermicular graphite → intermediate properties (diesel engine blocks)
4. **Malleable Cast Iron (GJMB / GJMW)**: Tempered carbon clusters → used in old yoke/bracket parts
5. **Austempered Ductile Iron (ADI)**: Bainitic matrix + spheroidal graphite → very high strength + wear resistance

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Property | EN-GJL-250 (Grey) | EN-GJS-500-7 (Ductile) | EN-GJS-800-8 (ADI) |
|----------|--------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **(i) Graphite Form** | Flake (lamellar) | Spheroidal (nodular) | Spheroidal in ausferrite matrix |
| **(ii) Tensile Strength** | 250 MPa | 500 MPa | 800 MPa |
| **(iii) Elongation** | < 1% | 7% | 8% |
| **(iv) Typical Auto Application** | Engine block, brake drum, cylinder head (older) | Crankshaft, steering knuckle, hub, swing arm | High-stress knuckles, hypoid gears, control arms |
| **Damping** | Excellent (10× steel) | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Machinability** | Excellent | Good | Difficult (hard) |
| **Cost** | Lowest | ~1.5× grey | ~2.5× grey |

**Conclusion** (½ mark): Grey iron dominates non-critical parts, ductile iron is the **modern workhorse** for safety-critical castings, and ADI is the **premium choice** for lightweight + high-strength applications.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE – Module 4 (Automotive Metal Standards)**

### A. Designation Systems (Most Tested)

- ✅ **AISI-SAE 4-digit**: First two digits = alloy family, last two = **carbon content in 0.01%** for ALL steels (1xxx-9xxx).
- ✅ **Key AISI grades to memorize**: **1018, 1045, 4140, 4340, 52100, 6150, 8620, 9260**
- ✅ **UNS**: Letter G = carbon/alloy steel, S = stainless, A = aluminium, C = copper, F = cast iron, J = cast steel, N = nickel, T = titanium, Z = zinc.
- ✅ **EN 10027-1**: **S**=Structural, **P**=Pressure, **E**=Engineering, **L**=Line pipe, **H**=HSLA flat. Numeric = yield in MPa. J2 = 27 J at −20°C.
- ✅ **EN 10027-2**: 1.xxxx = steel, 2.xxxx = non-ferrous. **42CrMo4 = 1.7225 = AISI 4140**.

### B. Cast Iron Standards

- ✅ **EN-GJL** = Grey (lamellar) — engine blocks, brake drums
- ✅ **EN-GJS** = Ductile (spheroidal) — crankshafts, knuckles
- ✅ **EN-GJV** = CGI (compacted graphite) — modern diesel blocks
- ✅ **ADI** = Austempered ductile iron — high-performance
- ✅ Number = **minimum tensile strength in MPa**

### C. Aluminium Alloys

- ✅ **1xxx**: Pure Al (radiator tubes)
- ✅ **3xxx**: Al-Mn (heat exchangers)
- ✅ **5xxx**: Al-Mg (body sheet — AA 5754, AA 5182)
- ✅ **6xxx**: Al-Mg-Si (body sheet AA 6016, extrusions AA 6063, forgings AA 6061) — **most versatile auto family**
- ✅ **7xxx**: Al-Zn (highest strength, premium wheels)
- ✅ **T6 temper** = peak strength; **T4** = formable, paint-bake hardenable

### D. Steel Selection by Auto Component

- ✅ **Crankshaft**: Ductile iron EN-GJS-600-3 OR forged AISI 4140
- ✅ **Connecting rod**: AISI 4140 / 4340
- ✅ **Transmission gear**: AISI 8620 (carburized) / 4320
- ✅ **Bearing**: AISI 52100 / EN 100Cr6 / JIS SUJ2
- ✅ **Spring**: AISI 6150, 9254, 5160
- ✅ **Exhaust valve**: X45CrSi9-3 (EN 1.4718)
- ✅ **Body sheet (steel)**: HSS / DP600 / TRIP800 / CP1000
- ✅ **Body sheet (Al)**: AA 6016-T4

### E. Indian Standards (BIS) – Must Memorize

- ✅ **IS 2062** = structural steel (replaces older IS 226 / IS 1977)
- ✅ **IS 513** = cold-rolled carbon steel sheet
- ✅ **IS 277** = galvanized steel sheet
- ✅ **IS 210** = grey cast iron (FG grade numbering)
- ✅ **IS 1865** = ductile/ductile iron castings
- ✅ **IS 8917** = cylinder liner cast iron
- ✅ **IS 2021 / IS 617** = aluminium alloy castings

### F. Testing Standards – Most Common

- ✅ Tensile: **ASTM E8 / ISO 6892-1 / JIS Z2241 / IS 1608**
- ✅ Hardness: **ASTM E18 (Rockwell) / E10 (Brinell) / E92 (Vickers)**
- ✅ Impact: **ASTM E23 / ISO 148-1 / JIS Z2242 / IS 1757**
- ✅ Fatigue: **ASTM E466 / ISO 1143**

### G. Homologation & Quality

- ✅ **IATF 16949** = Global automotive QMS (replaces ISO/TS 16949)
- ✅ **EN 10204** = Types of Mill Test Certificates (2.1, 2.2, 3.1, 3.2)
- ✅ **AIS** (Automotive Industry Standards, India) — under CMVR
- ✅ **ELV Directive 2000/53/EC** (EU) — ≥95% recyclability

### H. Lightweighting Formulas (Numerical)

$$\text{Weight Saving \%} = \left(1 - \frac{\rho_{\text{new}}}{\rho_{\text{steel}}}\right) \times 100 = 65.6\% \text{ (Al replacing Fe)}$$

### I. Pitfalls to Avoid (Mark-losers)

- ❌ Confusing AISI 52100 (high-carbon bearing steel) with AISI 5120 (low-carbon case-hardenable).
- ❌ Writing "J2 = 20°C" — it is **−20°C**.
- ❌ Confusing **EN-GJL** (grey) and **EN-GJS** (ductile).
- ❌ Writing "T6 = tempered" — it is **solution + artificial aging**.
- ❌ Forgetting to write the **temper** in Al alloy designations (6061 vs 6061-T6).

topics.title: Plastics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plastics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Plastics** are a broad class of **synthetic or semi-synthetic polymeric materials** composed of long-chain repeating molecular units known as **monomers**, which are bonded together through a process called **polymerization**. In the automotive context, plastics refer to **engineering-grade polymers and polymer composites** that satisfy specific mechanical, thermal, chemical, and aesthetic performance criteria mandated by global automotive standards (ISO, ASTM, SAE, ECE, and AIS).

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, automotive plastics are evaluated on:
- **Mechanical performance** (tensile strength $\sigma_t$, flexural modulus $E_f$, impact resistance).
- **Thermal performance** (Heat Deflection Temperature, Glass Transition Temperature $T_g$).
- **Chemical resistance** (per ISO 16773 and ASTM D543).
- **Flammability rating** (per FMVSS 302 / ISO 3795).
- **Recyclability & Sustainability** (per ISO 15270 / End-of-Life Vehicle Directive 2000/53/EC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A plastic used inside an automobile is NEVER just a "plastic." It must comply with a layered regulatory framework covering safety, emissions, recyclability, and end-of-life disposal. Engineers must always trace the material back to its governing standard.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Plastics as LEGO Bricks of the Automotive World"**
>
> Imagine a child building with LEGO bricks. Each brick (monomer) is identical and small. When you click a few hundred bricks together in a long chain, you get a flexible spaghetti-like string. Now, if you cross-link thousands of these chains with extra connectors, you get a rigid panel — a car bumper. **A monomer is to a polymer what a single LEGO brick is to a fully built LEGO car.** The way the bricks are connected (linear, branched, or cross-linked) decides whether the final material will be a soft dashboard foam or a hard engine cover.

### 1.3 Core Physical & Regulatory Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally used in the automotive plastics industry:

- **Density ($\rho$):** Measured in **g/cm³** or **kg/m³**. Lightweight plastics (PP at 0.90 g/cm³) are preferred for fuel economy.
- **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$):** The temperature (in **°C** or **K**) at which the polymer transitions from rigid to rubbery.
- **Melt Flow Index (MFI):** Expressed in **g/10 min**, indicating processability for injection molding.
- **Tensile Strength ($\sigma_t$):** Measured in **MPa** per ASTM D638.
- **Elongation at Break ($\varepsilon_b$):** Expressed as a **percentage (%)**.
- **Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT):** In **°C** at 0.45 MPa or 1.82 MPa load (ASTM D648).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** In viva and ESE questions, examiners often ask, *"Why is PP used for bumpers but PA66 used for engine covers?"* The answer lies in the values of HDT and tensile strength — students must quote specific numerical values from the data sheet.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Automotive Plastics

Automotive plastics are classified under the KTU 2024 syllabus into **three primary families**:

#### A. Thermoplastics (Re-meltable)
- Can be reheated, reshaped, and recycled multiple times.
- **Linear or branched** chain architecture (no cross-links).
- Used for: bumpers, dashboards, fuel tanks, interior trims.

#### B. Thermosetting Plastics (Cross-linked, Irreversible)
- Undergo irreversible chemical curing; cannot be remelted.
- **3D network** structure after curing.
- Used for: high-temperature under-hood components, brake pads, electrical insulation.

#### C. Elastomers (Elastic Polymers)
- Lightly cross-linked; can stretch and recover.
- Used for: tires, seals, gaskets, hoses, vibration dampers.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic — Polymerization Mechanism

1. **Monomer Selection:** Choose a specific repeat unit (e.g., ethylene $\text{C}_2\text{H}_4$).
2. **Initiation:** An initiator (e.g., benzoyl peroxide) generates free radicals.
3. **Propagation:** The active radical attacks monomer double bonds, extending the chain.
4. **Termination:** Two radicals combine, ending chain growth.
5. **Result:** A high molecular weight polymer chain is produced.

> **Engineering Utility:** The degree of polymerization ($DP$) directly controls viscosity, mechanical strength, and processing temperature — making it the single most important parameter in polymer engineering.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Equation | Standard | Units |
|---|-------------------|----------|----------|-------|
| 1 | Degree of Polymerization | $DP \;=\; \dfrac{M_n}{M_0}$ | ASTM D4001 | dimensionless |
| 2 | Number-Average Molecular Weight | $M_n \;=\; \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | ASTM D4001 | g/mol |
| 3 | Weight-Average Molecular Weight | $M_w \;=\; \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | ASTM D4001 | g/mol |
| 4 | Polydispersity Index | $PDI \;=\; \dfrac{M_w}{M_n}$ | ASTM D4001 | dimensionless |
| 5 | Density (mass per volume) | $\rho \;=\; \dfrac{m}{V}$ | ASTM D792 | g/cm³ |
| 6 | Tensile Strength | $\sigma_t \;=\; \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | ASTM D638 | MPa |
| 7 | Stress at Break (Engineering) | $\sigma_b \;=\; \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_0}$ | ASTM D638 | MPa |
| 8 | Elongation at Break | $\varepsilon_b \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} \times 100$ | ASTM D638 | percent |
| 9 | Young's Modulus (from tensile test) | $E \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | ASTM D638 | MPa |
| 10 | Heat Deflection Temperature | $HDT \;=\; T \;\text{at}\; \delta \;=\; 0.25 \;\text{mm}$ | ASTM D648 | °C |
| 11 | Glass Transition (Fox Equation) | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} \;=\; \sum \dfrac{w_i}{T_{g,i}}$ | — | K |
| 12 | Specific Volume | $v \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | — | cm³/g |
| 13 | Mass Fraction of Recyclate | $m_r \;=\; \dfrac{m_{\text{recycled}}}{m_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | ISO 15270 | percent |
| 14 | Recyclability Rate (ELV) | $R_{ELV} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{\text{recycled}} + m_{\text{reused}}}{m_{\text{vehicle}}} \times 100$ | 2000/53/EC | percent |
| 15 | Flammability Burn Rate | $BR \;=\; \dfrac{L}{t}$ | FMVSS 302 | mm/min |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for Tables:** All absolute value or "divided by" notations use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. The vertical bar $\vert$ is never used raw inside table cells.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lightweighting:** Replacing steel with carbon-fiber-reinforced plastic (CFRP) can reduce component mass by **up to 60%**, directly improving fuel economy and reducing $\text{CO}_2$ emissions per **WLTP** cycle.
- **Crash Safety:** Energy-absorbing plastics (e.g., expanded polypropylene EPP) in bumper cores absorb impact energy, reducing passenger injury metrics per **Euro NCAP** and **AIS-096**.
- **Recycling Compliance:** Under **ELV Directive 2000/53/EC**, every vehicle placed on the EU market must achieve **95% recoverability** by mass.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Degree of Polymerization from Molecular Weight

**Given:** A polypropylene (PP) sample has 200 monomer units per chain on average. The molar mass of one propylene monomer ($M_0$) is **42.08 g/mol**.

**Step 1: State the definition of $DP$.**

$$
DP \;=\; \frac{\text{Number of repeat units in chain}}{\text{Chain}} \;=\; \frac{M_n}{M_0}
$$

where $M_n$ is the number-average molecular weight.

**Step 2: Substitute the known values.**

$$
M_n \;=\; 200 \times 42.08
$$

**Step 3: Perform the multiplication.**

$$
M_n \;=\; 8416 \;\text{g/mol}
$$

**Step 4: Compute $DP$.**

$$
DP \;=\; \frac{8416}{42.08} \;=\; 200
$$

**Result:** The degree of polymerization confirms 200 repeating units — consistent with the initial assumption, validating the use of the formula.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Density of a Polymer Composite (Rule of Mixtures)

**Given:** A glass-fiber reinforced polyamide (PA66 + 30% GF) composite. The density of PA66 is **1.14 g/cm³** and the density of E-glass fiber is **2.55 g/cm³**. Mass fraction of glass $w_f = 0.30$.

**Step 1: Identify the rule of mixtures for density (mass-fraction form).**

$$
\frac{1}{\rho_c} \;=\; \frac{w_f}{\rho_f} \;+\; \frac{w_m}{\rho_m}
$$

**Step 2: Determine the mass fraction of matrix.**

$$
w_m \;=\; 1 - w_f \;=\; 1 - 0.30 \;=\; 0.70
$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the equation.**

$$
\frac{1}{\rho_c} \;=\; \frac{0.30}{2.55} \;+\; \frac{0.70}{1.14}
$$

**Step 4: Compute each term.**

$$
\frac{0.30}{2.55} \;\approx\; 0.11765
$$

$$
\frac{0.70}{1.14} \;\approx\; 0.61404
$$

**Step 5: Sum the reciprocals.**

$$
\frac{1}{\rho_c} \;\approx\; 0.11765 + 0.61404 \;\approx\; 0.73169
$$

**Step 6: Take the reciprocal to obtain composite density.**

$$
\rho_c \;\approx\; \frac{1}{0.73169} \;\approx\; 1.367 \;\text{g/cm}^3
$$

**Result:** The composite density is approximately **1.37 g/cm³**, which is reasonable for a PA66 + 30% GF engineering plastic. This value is critical for lightweighting calculations.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tensile Strength & Elongation of an Automotive Plastic

**Given:** A specimen of POM (Polyoxymethylene) used in a fuel pump housing is tested per **ASTM D638**. Cross-sectional area $A_0 = 40 \;\text{mm}^2$, gauge length $L_0 = 50 \;\text{mm}$. At break, the force is $F_{max} = 2400 \;\text{N}$ and elongation is $\Delta L = 3.0 \;\text{mm}$.

**Step 1: Calculate tensile strength at break.**

$$
\sigma_b \;=\; \frac{F_{max}}{A_0}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
\sigma_b \;=\; \frac{2400 \;\text{N}}{40 \;\text{mm}^2}
$$

**Step 3: Simplify.**

$$
\sigma_b \;=\; 60 \;\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate percentage elongation at break.**

$$
\varepsilon_b \;=\; \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \times 100
$$

**Step 5: Substitute values.**

$$
\varepsilon_b \;=\; \frac{3.0}{50.0} \times 100
$$

**Step 6: Simplify.**

$$
\varepsilon_b \;=\; 6.0 \;\text{percent}
$$

**Result:** POM exhibits $\sigma_b = 60 \;\text{MPa}$ and $\varepsilon_b = 6\%$, consistent with its selection for rigid, dimensionally stable fuel system components.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Automotive Plastics and Their Properties

| Plastic Family | Abbreviation | Density (g/cm³) | Tensile Strength (MPa) | HDT (°C) | Typical Automotive Use |
|----------------|--------------|-----------------|------------------------|----------|------------------------|
| Polypropylene | PP | 0.90 | 25 - 38 | 100 | Bumper fascia, battery case |
| Polyamide 66 | PA66 | 1.14 | 80 - 95 | 240 | Engine cover, radiator tank |
| Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene | ABS | 1.05 | 40 - 50 | 95 | Dashboard, interior trim |
| Polycarbonate | PC | 1.20 | 60 - 70 | 135 | Headlamp lens, sunroof |
| Polyoxymethylene | POM | 1.42 | 60 - 70 | 165 | Fuel pump housing, gears |
| Polyethylene Terephthalate | PET | 1.38 | 55 - 75 | 115 | Seat fabric, electrical insulation |
| Polyurethane | PU | 1.20 | 30 - 50 | 90 | Seat foam, steering wheel |
| Polyvinyl Chloride | PVC | 1.40 | 50 - 60 | 70 | Wire insulation, door panel |
| Polyphenylene Sulfide | PPS | 1.35 | 75 - 90 | 260 | Under-hood high-temp parts |
| Acrylic (PMMA) | PMMA | 1.18 | 70 - 80 | 95 | Tail lamp lens, decorative trim |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** As HDT increases, the plastic becomes suitable for under-hood applications. PA66, PPS, and PPA dominate the "hot zone" near the engine, while PP and ABS dominate the "cool zone" interiors and exteriors.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Classification & Flow of Automotive Plastics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Automotive Plastics] --> B[Thermoplastics]

    A --> E[Polymer Composites]

    B --> B1[Polypropylene PP]
    B --> B2[Polyamide PA]
    B --> B3[Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene ABS]
    B --> B4[Polycarbonate PC]
    B --> B5[Polyethylene Terephthalate PET]

    C --> C1[Phenolic Resin PF]
    C --> C2[Epoxy Resin EP]
    C --> C3[Unsaturated Polyester UP]
    C --> C4[Silicone SI]

    D --> D1[Natural Rubber NR]
    D --> D2[Styrene Butadiene Rubber SBR]

    E --> E1[Glass Fiber Reinforced Plastic GFRP]

    B1 --> F1[Bumper fascia, Interior trim]

```

### 4.2 Regulatory Compliance Flow for Automotive Plastics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J1[Material Selection] --> J2[Property Testing per ASTM and ISO]
    J2 --> J3{Flammability Test per FMVSS 302}
    J3 -->|Pass| J4[Crash Test per AIS 096]
    J3 -->|Fail| J5[Material Rejected]
    J4 --> J6{Emissions Test per ISO 12219}
    J6 -->|Pass| J7[Recyclability Check per ISO 15270]
    J6 -->|Fail| J5
    J7 -->|Above 95 percent| J8[Production Approval]

```

```mermaid
    K2 --> L1{PPS, PPA, PEEK, PA66}

```
