## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "polymer" and explain the difference between thermoplastics and thermosetting plastics with one automotive example each.*

**Model Answer:**
A polymer is a high molecular weight material composed of repeating small units called monomers, joined by covalent bonds. **Thermoplastics** are linear or branched polymers that soften on heating and harden on cooling, and can be reprocessed multiple times (e.g., **Polypropylene used in bumper fascia**). **Thermosetting plastics** undergo irreversible cross-linking during curing and cannot be remelted (e.g., **Phenolic resin used in brake pad binders**).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*State any three automotive standards that govern the use of plastics in vehicles and briefly mention their scope.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **FMVSS 302** — Governs the flammability of interior materials, setting a maximum burn rate of **102 mm/min**.
2. **ISO 3795** — International equivalent of FMVSS 302, used for road vehicles.
3. **ELV Directive 2000/53/EC** — Mandates **95% recyclability** by mass for end-of-life vehicles.
4. **AIS-096** — Indian standard for safety glazing and plastic component performance.

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Properties & Selection of Automotive Plastics*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**a)** With a neat sketch, explain the classification of plastics used in automobiles. List **five** examples with at least one property of each. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A polypropylene sample is used in an automotive bumper. The number-average molecular weight $M_n = 168{,}000 \;\text{g/mol}$ and the molar mass of propylene monomer is $42 \;\text{g/mol}$. Calculate the degree of polymerization. If the tensile force at break is $3000 \;\text{N}$ on a specimen of cross-section $50 \;\text{mm}^2$, determine the tensile strength at break. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

The classification follows the KTU 2024 Module 4 framework. Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1.

- **Polypropylene (PP):** Density 0.90 g/cm³; used in bumpers.
- **Polyamide 66 (PA66):** Tensile strength 80 MPa; used in radiator tanks.
- **Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene (ABS):** Impact strength 200 J/m; used in dashboards.
- **Polycarbonate (PC):** Transparency, HDT 135°C; used in headlamp lenses.
- **Polyoxymethylene (POM):** Low friction; used in fuel pump housings.

(Each item: 1 mark. Sketch and classification labels: 2 marks.)

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Degree of Polymerization formula.**

$$
DP \;=\; \frac{M_n}{M_0}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
DP \;=\; \frac{168000}{42} \;=\; 4000
$$

**Step 3: Tensile strength formula.**

$$
\sigma_b \;=\; \frac{F_{max}}{A_0}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute values.**

$$
\sigma_b \;=\; \frac{3000 \;\text{N}}{50 \;\text{mm}^2} \;=\; 60 \;\text{MPa}
$$

**Final Answer:** $DP = 4000$ and $\sigma_b = 60 \;\text{MPa}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Standards, Recycling & Environmental Compliance*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

**a)** Discuss the End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV) Directive 2000/53/EC in detail. What is the current mandated recyclability percentage and how does it impact plastic selection in vehicles? **(7 marks)**

**b)** A glass-fiber reinforced PA66 composite has 35% by mass of glass fiber. If the density of PA66 is 1.14 g/cm³ and that of E-glass is 2.55 g/cm³, calculate the density of the composite using the rule of mixtures. Comment on the suitability of this composite for an under-hood engine cover. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

The **ELV Directive 2000/53/EC**, adopted by the European Union in 2000, mandates the following for every end-of-life vehicle:

1. **Prevention of waste** through design for recyclability.
2. **Reuse, recycling, and recovery** targets:
   - **95%** reuse + recycling by average vehicle mass (current mandate).
   - **85%** reuse + recycling target by mass.
   - **10%** energy recovery target.
3. **Restriction of Hazardous Substances (RoHS-aligned):** Lead, mercury, cadmium, and hexavalent chromium are limited to **0.1%** by mass in plastic components.

**Impact on Plastic Selection:**
- Manufacturers prefer **single-polymer components** (mono-material design) to ease recycling.
- Use of **recycled PET** and **recycled PP** is promoted.
- **Thermosets** are gradually replaced by **thermoplastics** for end-of-life recovery.
- **Marking of plastic parts** per **ISO 1043** is mandatory for identification during recycling.

(ELV Directive details: 4 marks; impact on selection: 3 marks.)

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Identify known values.**
- $w_f = 0.35$ (glass fiber mass fraction)
- $\rho_f = 2.55 \;\text{g/cm}^3$ (E-glass)
- $\rho_m = 1.14 \;\text{g/cm}^3$ (PA66 matrix)
- $w_m = 1 - 0.35 = 0.65$ (matrix mass fraction)

**Step 2: Apply the rule of mixtures for density.**

$$
\frac{1}{\rho_c} \;=\; \frac{w_f}{\rho_f} \;+\; \frac{w_m}{\rho_m}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute and compute.**

$$
\frac{1}{\rho_c} \;=\; \frac{0.35}{2.55} \;+\; \frac{0.65}{1.14}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{\rho_c} \;\approx\; 0.13725 + 0.57018 \;\approx\; 0.70743
$$

**Step 4: Take the reciprocal.**

$$
\rho_c \;\approx\; \frac{1}{0.70743} \;\approx\; 1.413 \;\text{g/cm}^3
$$

**Step 5: Comment on suitability for an engine cover.**

- The composite has a density of **1.41 g/cm³**, which is **~25% lower than aluminum (~2.7 g/cm³)** and significantly lighter than steel.
- PA66 + 35% GF exhibits **HDT > 250°C** and **tensile strength > 180 MPa**, making it suitable for the under-hood thermal and mechanical loads.
- It satisfies **FMVSS 302** flammability requirements when flame-retardant additives are included.
- It is **compatible with ELV recycling** when paired with a mono-material design philosophy.

(Compute: 4 marks; Suitability comment: 3 marks.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Writing $DP$ in g/mol or $\sigma_b$ in N/mm² without converting to **MPa** (1 MPa = 1 N/mm²). Always state units explicitly.
> 2. **Formula Confusion:** Mixing up $M_n$ (number-average) and $M_w$ (weight-average). The ratio $M_w / M_n$ is the **Polydispersity Index**, NOT the degree of polymerization.
> 3. **Ignoring Mass vs. Volume Fractions:** In the rule of mixtures for density, use **mass fractions** with mass-weighted form, NOT volume fractions.
> 4. **Standards Misquotation:** Students often write "ISO 9001" or "ASTM D256" (impact test) without context. Always quote the **scope** of the standard along with the number.
> 5. **Recyclability Confusion:** "Recoverable" and "Recyclable" are **different** terms. Energy recovery (incineration) is **recovery**, not recycling.
> 6. **HDT Confusion:** HDT is reported at **0.45 MPa** OR **1.82 MPa** — students often interchange these without specifying.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Plastics = Polymers** (synthetic, high molecular weight, organic).
- **Three families:** Thermoplastics (recyclable), Thermosets (irreversible), Elastomers (elastic).
- **Degree of Polymerization:** $DP = M_n / M_0$ (dimensionless).
- **Tensile Strength:** $\sigma_b = F_{max} / A_0$ in **MPa**.
- **Density Rule of Mixtures (mass form):** $\dfrac{1}{\rho_c} = \dfrac{w_f}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{w_m}{\rho_m}$.
- **HDT** is measured at **0.45 MPa** (low-load) or **1.82 MPa** (high-load) per ASTM D648.
- **Top automotive plastics:** PP, PA66, ABS, PC, POM, PET, PU, PPS, PMMA.
- **Key Standards:** FMVSS 302 (flammability), ISO 3795 (international flammability), ELV 2000/53/EC (recycling), ISO 1043 (marking), AIS-096 (Indian safety).
- **ELV Mandate:** 95% recoverability by mass — drives mono-material design.
- **Glass transition temperature $T_g$** (Fox equation): $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \sum \dfrac{w_i}{T_{g,i}}$.
- **Polydispersity Index (PDI):** $PDI = M_w / M_n$ — a measure of molecular weight distribution.
- **Under-hood plastics** must have HDT > 200°C → PA66, PPA, PPS, PEEK are preferred.
- **Interior plastics** prioritize low odor (per **ISO 12219**), low VOC, and aesthetics.
- **Composite rule of mixtures** applies to **mass fractions** for density and **volume fractions** for modulus (do not confuse).
- **Flammability burn rate** under FMVSS 302 must not exceed **102 mm/min** for interior materials.
- **Recycled plastics** must be marked with **ISO 11469** symbol for traceability.

> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Students:** Always end your answer with a direct link to a **standard number** (ISO, ASTM, SAE, ECE, AIS). Examiners reward regulation-aware answers heavily in the OBE (Outcome-Based Education) framework.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Glass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Glass

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Automotive Glazing (Safety Glass)** is a specialized category of transparent, laminated, or tempered glass materials mandated by international and national regulations for use as windshields, side windows, rear windows, and roof panels in motor vehicles. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEMUT526 – Module 4), automotive glass is treated as a *regulated safety-critical component* whose optical, mechanical, and impact-resistance characteristics are governed by standards such as **ECE R43**, **AIS-008 (India)**, **GTR No. 6 (UN-WP.29 Global Technical Regulation)**, **ANSI/SAE Z26.1**, and **ISO 3537**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regulatory Anchor Definition (KTU 2024)**
> *Safety glass* is defined by ECE R43 as "a rigid and transparent material intended to be installed in motor vehicles, comprising either laminated glass (LG) or tempered glass (TG), such that the risk of bodily injury from impact or splintering is significantly reduced compared to ordinary annealed float glass."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the glass in your home window — it is sharp, brittle, and on impact, it shatters into long dagger-like shards that cause severe lacerations. Now imagine a car windshield in a high-speed collision: it does **not** pierce into the passenger cabin; instead, it cracks into a spider-web pattern (laminated) or small granular pebbles (tempered) that are relatively harmless.

> **Real-World Analogy — "The Sandwich and the Stress-Frozen Cookie"**
> - **Laminated Glass (LG)** is like a **sandwich**: two thin glass sheets bonded by a tough plastic interlayer (typically **Poly-Vinyl Butyral, PVB**, of thickness **0.76 mm**). When broken, the glass fragments stick to the PVB — like jelly sticking to bread slices. Used for **windshields**.
> - **Tempered Glass (TG)** is like a **stress-frozen cookie**: the outer surface is held in powerful compression, the core in tension. On impact, the entire pane releases its stored energy instantly, crumbling into tiny harmless cubes. Used for **side and rear windows**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Automotive Glazing

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Governing Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Visible Light Transmittance (windshield) | $\tau_v$ | $\geq 70\%$ | ECE R43, AIS-008 |
| PVB Interlayer Thickness | $t_{PVB}$ | $\geq 0.76$ mm | ECE R43 |
| Laminated Glass Total Thickness | $t_{LG}$ | $4.0 - 5.0$ mm typical | ISO 3537 |
| Tempered Glass Thickness | $t_{TG}$ | $3.0 - 4.0$ mm typical | ECE R43 |
| Secondary Image Separation Angle | $\alpha_{sec}$ | $\leq 15$ arc-minutes | ECE R43 |
| Optical Distortion | $\Delta \alpha$ | $\leq 15$ arc-minutes | ECE R43 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter in KTU exams:** A single missed percentage in transmittance or one misquoted thickness can cost a full mark on a 7-mark Part B question. Always quote the **standard value AND the source regulation** together.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Automotive Safety Glass

Under **ECE R43** and **AIS-008**, automotive safety glass is classified into the following types:

1. **Laminated Glass (LG)** — Mandatory for windshields. Two or more glass plies permanently bonded by one or more plastic interlayers (PVB, SGP, EVA).
2. **Tempered Glass (TG)** — Mandatory for side and rear windows (except for certain panoramic roofs). Heat-treated to induce surface compression of **$\sigma_c \geq 70$ MPa**.
3. **Laminated-Tempered Glass (LT)** — Combined construction used in panoramic sunroofs.
4. **Plastic Glazing (PG)** — Used in limited applications (e.g., rear windows of certain light vehicles).
5. **Glass-Plastic (GP)** — Hybrid (e.g., chemically strengthened outer + plastic inner) for weight-critical EVs.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Principle

- **Laminated Glass Mechanics:** On impact, the outer glass ply cracks; energy is dissipated through (a) radial cracking, (b) delamination, and (c) plastic stretching of the PVB layer. The PVB layer prevents occupant ejection and retains residual visibility.
- **Tempered Glass Mechanics:** Surface compressive stress keeps microcracks closed. When impact energy exceeds the critical stress intensity factor $K_{IC}$, the stored elastic energy is catastrophically released — the entire pane "explodes" into small fragments ($\leq 25$ mm$^2$ per fragment for *Fragmentation Test* compliance).

## 2.3 Mandatory Tests under ECE R43

| Test | Purpose | Pass Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| Fragmentation Test | Ensures tempered glass shatters safely | Fragment mass, count, and longest fragment length within limits |
| Ball Drop Test (227 g, 4 m) | Simulates head impact on windshield | No penetration; crack radius within bounds |
| 2260 g Pendulum Test | Higher-energy impact | No penetration of interlayer |
| Headform Test ($v = 11.1$ m/s) | Head injury criteria (HIC) | HIC $\leq 1000$ |
| Light Transmittance | Optical clarity | $\tau_v \geq 70\%$ (windshield) |
| Optical Distortion | Image quality | $\Delta \alpha \leq 15$ arc-min |
| Secondary Image | Ghost image prevention | $\alpha_{sec} \leq 15$ arc-min |
| Chemical Resistance | Acid, alkali, gasoline exposure | No visible deterioration |
| Weathering / UV Resistance | Long-term durability | No delamination, no yellowing |
| Abrasion Resistance | Surface durability | Light transmittance change $\leq 2\%$ |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Visible Light Transmittance | $\tau_v = \dfrac{\Phi_{transmitted}}{\Phi_{incident}} \times 100\%$ | % | Optical compliance |
| Fragmentation Index | $F = \dfrac{N_{fragments}}{A_{zone}} \times W_{sample}$ | pieces/g | Tempered glass safety |
| Ball Drop Impact Energy | $E = m g h$ | Joules | ECE R43 §3.1 |
| Head Impact Energy (HIC) | $HIC = \left[ \dfrac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1)$ | dimensionless | Headform test |
| Critical Stress Intensity Factor | $K_{IC} = Y \sigma_c \sqrt{\pi a}$ | MPa·m$^{0.5}$ | Fracture mechanics of glass |
| Surface Compressive Stress (Tempered) | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{\alpha E \Delta T}{1 - \nu}$ | Pa | Tempering physics |
| Optical Distortion Angle | $\Delta \alpha = \alpha_{max} - \alpha_{ref}$ | arc-min | Distortion test |
| Windshield Thickness Rule | $t_{LG} = t_{g1} + t_{PVB} + t_{g2}$ | mm | Lamination design |
| Secondary Image Angle | $\alpha_{sec} = \arctan\left( \dfrac{d_{offset}}{R_{w}} \right)$ | arc-min | Optical test |
| Fracture Fragment Size Limit | $L_{max} \leq 25$ mm | mm | Fragmentation test |
| Light Transmittance Change After Abrasion | $\Delta \tau = \tau_{before} - \tau_{after}$ | % | Abrasion test |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Reminder:** When asked *"Which standard governs automotive glass in India?"*, the answer is **AIS-008** (Automotive Industry Standards, India) — *not* ECE R43. Both are equivalent in technical content, but the question wording matters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Tempered Glass Surface Compressive Stress

The tempering process involves heating glass to near its softening point ($\approx 620^\circ$C for soda-lime glass), then rapidly quenching both surfaces with cold air. The outer layers contract first and become rigid; as the inner core later cools and contracts, it pulls the outer layers into compression and puts the core into tension. This residual stress profile is what makes tempered glass safe.

**Step 1: Thermal strain on rapid cooling**

If the surface temperature drops by $\Delta T$ relative to the core, the differential contraction strain is:

$$
\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha_g \, \Delta T
$$

where $\alpha_g$ is the coefficient of thermal expansion of glass.

**Step 2: Equilibrium of forces**

The surface compressive stress balances the core tensile stress. By force balance over a symmetrical cross-section, and assuming elastic behaviour with Young's modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$:

$$
\sigma_c = \dfrac{\alpha_g \, E \, \Delta T}{1 - \nu}
$$

**Step 3: Numerical evaluation for soda-lime glass**

Using standard values:

$$
\alpha_g = 9 \times 10^{-6} \, /^\circ \text{C}, \quad E = 72 \, \text{GPa}, \quad \nu = 0.22, \quad \Delta T = 350^\circ \text{C}
$$

$$
\sigma_c = \dfrac{(9 \times 10^{-6}) \times (72 \times 10^9) \times 350}{1 - 0.22}
$$

$$
\sigma_c = \dfrac{226{,}800}{0.78} = 290.7 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 4: Practical tempering limit**

In commercial tempering, the achieved surface compression is **$\sigma_c \approx 100$ to 150 MPa** (not the theoretical maximum), because the air-quench cannot sustain an infinite thermal gradient, and high stresses cause spontaneous fracture (so-called "nickel-sulfide inclusion failure" — addressed by *heat-soak testing* under EN 14179).

**Step 5: Why $\sigma_c$ matters for safety**

The fracture strength of glass is governed by:

$$
\sigma_{fracture} = \dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y \sqrt{\pi a}}
$$

For a typical surface flaw of depth $a = 50 \, \mu$m, $K_{IC} = 0.75$ MPa·m$^{0.5}$, and $Y \approx 1.12$:

$$
\sigma_{fracture} = \dfrac{0.75}{1.12 \sqrt{\pi \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}} \approx 169 \, \text{MPa}
$$

Since the surface is held in compression of $\approx 100$ MPa, an external tensile load must first **overcome this pre-stress** before a crack can propagate. This is why tempered glass is ~4–5× stronger than annealed glass.

## 3.2 Derivation: Visible Light Transmittance of Laminated Glass

For a laminated glass comprising two glass plies and one PVB interlayer, total transmittance is given by:

$$
\tau_{total} = \tau_{g1} \times \tau_{PVB} \times \tau_{g2} \times (1 - R_{Fresnel})
$$

**Step 1: Fresnel reflection loss (4 surfaces of glass, 2 of PVB)**

For a single air-glass interface, reflectance is:

$$
R = \left( \dfrac{n_2 - n_1}{n_2 + n_1} \right)^2
$$

For air ($n = 1.0$) → glass ($n = 1.52$):

$$
R = \left( \dfrac{0.52}{2.52} \right)^2 = 0.0426 = 4.26\%
$$

**Step 2: Combined reflectance for 4 glass surfaces**

$$
R_{combined} = 1 - (1 - 0.0426)^4 = 1 - 0.836 = 0.164 = 16.4\%
$$

> Note: PVB and glass indices being close ($n_{PVB} \approx 1.48$), their interface reflectance is negligible ($\approx 0.04\%$).

**Step 3: Absorptance of PVB**

For $t_{PVB} = 0.76$ mm and absorption coefficient $\mu \approx 0.05$ /mm:

$$
\tau_{PVB} = e^{-\mu t} = e^{-0.05 \times 0.76} = 0.962
$$

**Step 4: Total transmittance**

$$
\tau_{total} \approx (1 - 0.164) \times 0.962 \times (1 - 0.0004) \times 0.99^2
$$

$$
\tau_{total} \approx 0.836 \times 0.962 \times 0.980 \approx 0.788 = 78.8\%
$$

This **exceeds the 70% minimum** required by ECE R43 — confirming typical laminate design.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Laminated vs Tempered Glass

| Property | Laminated Glass (LG) | Tempered Glass (TG) | KTU Exam Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|
| Construction | Glass + PVB + Glass | Single heat-treated ply | — |
| Breakage pattern | Spider-web (adhered to PVB) | Small cubes ($\leq 25$ mm) | "Fragmentation" |
| Spontaneous post-breakage retention | Yes (held by PVB) | No (catastrophic) | "Residual visibility" |
| Acoustic damping (dB reduction) | $35 - 40$ dB | $25 - 30$ dB | "Acoustic PVB" |
| UV blocking | $\geq 99\%$ (with UV-absorbing PVB) | $\leq 50\%$ | "UV transmittance" |
| Cost (relative) | High | Low | — |
| Mandatory use (per AIS-008) | Windshields | Side & rear windows | "ECE R43 / AIS-008" |
| Reparability | Yes (resin injection) | No (full replacement) | — |
| Spontaneous failure mode | Edge delamination | Nickel sulfide inclusion | "Heat soak test" |
| Head injury criterion (HIC) | $\leq 1000$ | $\leq 1000$ | "ECE R43 headform" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
*Define laminated safety glass and state the role of the PVB interlayer in automotive windshields.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Laminated glass is a composite of two or more glass plies permanently bonded by one or more plastic interlayers, typically Poly-Vinyl Butyral (PVB).
- **Role of PVB (2 marks):**
  - (i) Holds glass fragments together upon breakage, preventing occupant ejection and laceration (1 mark).
  - (ii) Provides acoustic damping and blocks $\geq 99\%$ of UV radiation, improving comfort and protecting interior trim (1 mark).
  - (iii) Standard PVB thickness as per ECE R43 is $\geq 0.76$ mm.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
*State the ECE R43 requirement for visible light transmittance of a vehicle windshield.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The visible light transmittance $\tau_v$ of a vehicle windshield, when measured by the standard procedure (illuminant A, CIE photopic observer), **shall not be less than 70%** (2 marks).
- This ensures adequate driver visibility under all operating conditions (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]
*(a)* Explain the manufacturing and fracture mechanics of **tempered safety glass** used in automotive side windows. Include a derivation of the residual surface compressive stress. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Discuss the **ECE R43 fragmentation test** for tempered glass. State the pass/fail criteria and explain why tempered glass shatters into small harmless fragments. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — Tempered Glass Manufacturing and Fracture Mechanics (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Manufacturing Process (2 marks):**
- Cut and edge-grind annealed float glass to the required shape.
- Heat uniformly in a roller-hearth furnace to **$\approx 620^\circ$C** (near softening point).
- Quench both surfaces with high-velocity cold air jets.
- The outer layers solidify first; inner core contracts later, putting surfaces into **compression** and core into **tension**.

**Step 2 — Stress Derivation (3 marks):**

Differential thermal strain:

$$
\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha_g \, \Delta T
$$

Force balance and elasticity give:

$$
\sigma_c = \dfrac{\alpha_g \, E \, \Delta T}{1 - \nu}
$$

Substituting $\alpha_g = 9 \times 10^{-6} /^\circ$C, $E = 72$ GPa, $\Delta T = 350^\circ$C, $\nu = 0.22$:

$$
\sigma_c = \dfrac{(9 \times 10^{-6})(72 \times 10^9)(350)}{0.78} \approx 290 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Practical Value and Fracture Strength (2 marks):**
- Commercial tempering achieves **$\sigma_c \approx 100 - 150$ MPa**.
- Fracture requires applied stress to overcome $\sigma_c$, so tempered glass is **4–5× stronger** than annealed.
- Spontaneous fracture from nickel-sulfide inclusions is mitigated by **heat-soak test** at $290^\circ$C for 4 hours (EN 14179).

---

#### Part (b) — ECE R43 Fragmentation Test (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Procedure (2 marks):**
- Specimen: $110 \times 110$ mm sample.
- Apply point impact with a pointed tool or steel ball to initiate fracture.
- Count fragments within a $50 \times 50$ mm zone, excluding a 10 mm border ring.
- Select 4 of the 5 largest fragments and weigh them.

**Step 2 — Pass Criteria (3 marks):**
- Total mass of the 4 largest fragments shall **not exceed 4.5 g** when sample thickness is $\leq 3.5$ mm, and **6.5 g** for thicker samples.
- No fragment longer than **25 mm** shall be present.
- Minimum **40 fragments** must lie within the test zone.

**Step 3 — Physics of Safe Fragmentation (2 marks):**
- The stored elastic energy in the compressive surface layer is released as kinetic energy of fragments.
- This energy is partitioned over **thousands of small fragments**, so each one carries low kinetic energy and cannot penetrate skin.
- Particles are typically **cubic, 1–3 mm** in size, with no sharp edges (compared to annealed glass shards of 10–50 mm length).

---

## 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks)

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]
*(a)* With a neat sketch, describe the construction of **automotive laminated windshield glass**. State the function of each layer. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A laminated windshield is made of two 2.1 mm glass plies and a 0.76 mm PVB interlayer. If the surface compressive stress in the glass is 120 MPa and a surface flaw of depth 60 µm exists, evaluate whether the glass will fracture under an applied tensile load of 90 MPa. Use $K_{IC} = 0.75$ MPa·m$^{0.5}$, $Y = 1.12$. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — Construction of Laminated Windshield (7 marks)

**Construction (sketch description, 4 marks):**
The laminated windshield consists of (from outside to inside):
1. **Outer glass ply** (2.0–2.3 mm) — annealed or chemically strengthened soda-lime float glass; bears aerodynamic and impact loads.
2. **PVB interlayer** ($\geq 0.76$ mm) — Poly-Vinyl Butyral with plasticizer; bonds plies, holds fragments on breakage, blocks UV.
3. **Inner glass ply** (2.0–2.3 mm) — matches outer ply; supports PVB from inside.
4. Optional: **black ceramic enamel band** on the periphery (frit) — improves UV blocking at edges and hides adhesive bond.
5. Optional: **antenna/rain sensor/IR-reflective coatings** on inner surface.

**Functions (3 marks):**
- Glass plies provide optical clarity and rigidity.
- PVB provides fragment retention, UV blocking ($\geq 99\%$), and acoustic damping ($35 - 40$ dB).
- Ceramic band provides edge protection and aesthetics.
- Total thickness $t_{LG} = 2.1 + 0.76 + 2.1 = 4.96$ mm (typical).

---

#### Part (b) — Fracture Analysis (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Stress intensity factor (2 marks):**

$$
K_{IC,applied} = Y \sigma_{applied} \sqrt{\pi a}
$$

$$
= 1.12 \times 90 \times 10^6 \times \sqrt{\pi \times 60 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
= 1.12 \times 90 \times 10^6 \times \sqrt{1.885 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
= 1.12 \times 90 \times 10^6 \times 1.373 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
K_{IC,applied} \approx 1.385 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa·m}^{0.5} = 1.385 \, \text{MPa·m}^{0.5}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective net stress (2 marks):**
Since the surface is in residual compression $\sigma_c = 120$ MPa, the net tensile stress at the surface becomes:

$$
\sigma_{net} = \sigma_{applied} - \sigma_c = 90 - 120 = -30 \, \text{MPa} \text{ (compressive)}
$$

**Step 3 — Fracture criterion (3 marks):**
The surface is in a net **compressive state** $(-30$ MPa), so a surface crack cannot propagate. Fracture requires:

$$
\sigma_{net} \geq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_{applied} \geq \sigma_c = 120 \, \text{MPa}
$$

Since the applied load is only 90 MPa, **the glass will NOT fracture**. (Note: the surface flaw of 60 µm only matters if the net stress becomes tensile. With pre-stress in compression, the flaw is "locked closed".)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing AIS with ECE:** Students often write "ECE R43" for an India-specific question. Always check if the question asks about "Indian standards" — answer **AIS-008** in such cases.
> 2. **Writing "tempered glass" for windshields:** A common 1-mark loss. Remember: *windshields = laminated; side/rear = tempered* (as per AIS-008 / ECE R43).
> 3. **Forgetting units in numericals:** Always state units for $\sigma$, $K_{IC}$, and energy — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit in numerical problems.
> 4. **Not drawing the ceramic frit band** in the laminated glass sketch — costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing $E$ (Young's modulus) and $K_{IC}$ (fracture toughness):** These are *different* properties; do not interchange them.
> 6. **In the Headform test question, students often forget to mention HIC ≤ 1000** — this single keyword is worth 1 mark.
> 7. **For the fragmentation test, students quote the wrong fragment count** — the rule is **at least 40 fragments in the central 50 × 50 mm zone** (not "many" or "lots").
> 8. **Heat soak test conditions:** Quote both temperature **$290^\circ$C** and duration **4 hours** (EN 14179) — partial credit only if both given.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Automotive Glass (KTU PEMUT526 Module 4)**

- **Primary Standards:** ECE R43, AIS-008, GTR No. 6, ANSI Z26.1, ISO 3537.
- **Glass Types:** Laminated (LG) for windshields; Tempered (TG) for side/rear; LT for sunroofs.
- **PVB Interlayer:** Poly-Vinyl Butyral, thickness $\geq 0.76$ mm; provides fragment retention, UV blocking, acoustic damping.
- **Tempered Glass Surface Stress:** $\sigma_c = \dfrac{\alpha_g E \Delta T}{1 - \nu} \approx 100 - 150$ MPa (practical).
- **Visible Light Transmittance:** $\tau_v \geq 70\%$ (windshield).
- **Optical Distortion:** $\Delta \alpha \leq 15$ arc-minutes.
- **Secondary Image Angle:** $\alpha_{sec} \leq 15$ arc-minutes.
- **Fragmentation Test:** $\geq 40$ fragments in 50 × 50 mm zone; max fragment mass 4.5 g (≤3.5 mm) or 6.5 g (>3.5 mm); longest fragment $\leq 25$ mm.
- **Ball Drop Test:** 227 g steel ball, drop height 4 m, no penetration.
- **Headform Test:** $v = 11.1$ m/s, HIC $\leq 1000$.
- **Pendulum Test:** 2260 g mass, no interlayer penetration.
- **Heat Soak Test:** 290°C for 4 hours (EN 14179) — eliminates NiS inclusion failures.
- **Abrasion Test:** Light transmittance change $\Delta \tau \leq 2\%$.
- **Typical Laminated Glass Thickness:** $2.1 + 0.76 + 2.1 = 4.96$ mm.
- **Fracture Criterion:** $K \geq K_{IC}$ — applied stress must overcome residual surface compression.
- **Why Windshields are Laminated:** Occupant retention + residual visibility after impact.
- **Why Side/Rear Windows are Tempered:** Easier emergency egress (rescue hammers break them easily) + lower cost.
- **EV Trend:** Glass-plastic hybrid glazing for weight reduction (15–30% lighter than all-glass).
- **UV Blocking:** Laminated windshields block $\geq 99\%$ UV; tempered side windows only $\approx 50\%$.
- **Acoustic Performance:** Acoustic PVB (3-ply: glass + acoustic PVB + glass) gives $\geq 5$ dB extra damping.
- **Ceramic Enamel Band:** Black frit at windshield periphery; $\approx 20 - 50$ mm wide; aids UV sealing and aesthetics.
- **India-Specific:** Always quote **AIS-008** (and underlying ECE R43) for Indian-market compliance.
- **Quick mnemonic for tests:** "**F-B-P-H-L-O-S-C-U-A**" → **F**ragmentation, **B**all drop, **P**endulum, **H**eadform, **L**ight transmittance, **O**ptical distortion, **S**econdary image, **C**hemical resistance, **U**V weathering, **A**brasion.

> **Top 3 Most-Asked Topics in KTU PEMUT526 (Module 4):**
> 1. Difference between laminated and tempered glass with applications.
> 2. Fragmentation test procedure and pass criteria.
> 3. Light transmittance and optical distortion requirements under ECE R43 / AIS-008.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT526 - AUTOMOTIVE STANDARDS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Composites and major vehicle testing protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Automotive Composites — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Term)**
> An **Automotive Composite** is a heterogeneous, engineered material system comprising two or more distinct constituent phases — a **reinforcement** (fiber or particulate) and a **matrix** (polymer, metal, or ceramic binder) — combined at a macroscopic level to yield synergistic mechanical, thermal, and chemical properties that none of the constituents can provide individually.

In the KTU 2024 framework, automotive composites are classified per **ISO 472** and **ASTM D3878** standards, and their use in vehicle structures is governed by homologation rules under the **AIS-052** and **AIS-098** series of Indian Automotive Industry Standards.

### 1.2 Vehicle Testing Protocols — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition**
> A **Vehicle Testing Protocol** is a standardized, repeatable, and legally binding sequence of experimental procedures (crash, emission, durability, NVH, homologation) defined by regulatory bodies — such as **AIS (Automotive Research Association of India – ARAI)**, **Euro NCAP**, **NHTSA**, **UNECE/WP.29**, and **BIS** — to certify that a vehicle meets mandated safety, environmental, and performance thresholds before being legally sold in a given market.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Composites Analogy — "The Reinforced Concrete Analogy":**
Imagine you are building a bicycle shed. Plain concrete (the matrix) is strong when compressed but cracks easily when bent. Now, if you embed steel rods (the reinforcement) inside the concrete, the resulting structure becomes exceptionally strong in **both** compression and tension. This is exactly how automotive composites work — a **lightweight polymer matrix** carries compressive and shear loads, while embedded **carbon/glass fibers** carry the tensile loads. The result is a material that is **40–60% lighter than steel** with comparable or superior strength — critical for EV range extension and fuel economy compliance.

**Testing Protocol Analogy — "The Driving License Exam":**
Before you can legally drive a car, you must pass a structured exam (vision test, written test, road test) set by the RTO. Similarly, before a vehicle is legally sold, it must "pass exams" — crash tests, emission tests, durability cycles — set by regulatory bodies. The protocols are the **syllabus** of these exams, ensuring every vehicle on the road meets minimum safety and environmental standards.

### 1.4 Core Constituents — The Two-Phase System

| Constituent Phase | Function | Common Materials in Automotive Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Matrix** | Binds fibers, transfers load, protects from environment | Epoxy, Polyester, Vinyl Ester, Polyamide (PA), Polypropylene (PP) |
| **Reinforcement** | Carries primary load (tension/compression) | Carbon Fiber, Glass Fiber, Aramid (Kevlar), Natural Fibers (Flax, Hemp) |
| **Interface (Interphase)** | Ensures stress transfer between matrix and fiber | Sizing agents, Coupling agents (Silane) |

### 1.5 Regulatory Body Mapping (India-Centric)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Indian Regulatory Bodies for Vehicle Testing:**
> - **ARAI** (Automotive Research Association of India) — Testing & Certification authority under CMVR
> - **MoRTH** (Ministry of Road Transport and Highways) — Rule formulation
> - **BIS** (Bureau of Indian Standards) — Component-level standards
> - **BNVSAP** — Bharat New Vehicle Safety Assessment Program (similar to Global NCAP)
> - **ICAT** (International Centre for Automotive Technology) — Northern region testing
> - **NATRiP** — National Automotive Testing and R&D Infrastructure Project

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Automotive Composites

### 2.1.1 By Matrix Material

**A. Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC) — Dominant in Automotive**

- **Thermoset-based:** Epoxy, Polyester, Vinyl ester → Used in body panels, leaf springs (e.g., Bolero)
- **Thermoplastic-based:** PP, PA, PEEK → Used in bumpers, underbody shields (recyclable)

**B. Metal Matrix Composites (MMC):**
- Al/SiC (Aluminum reinforced with Silicon Carbide) → Brake rotors, pistons
- **Why MMC?** Superior thermal conductivity + wear resistance at elevated temperatures.

**C. Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC):**
- SiC/SiC → Turbocharger rotors, exhaust valves for high-performance engines.

### 2.1.2 By Reinforcement Geometry

```
Reinforcement Type   Aspect Ratio   Automotive Application
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Continuous Fiber    > 1000          CFRP monocoque (BMW i3, i8)
Short Fiber         10 to 1000      Injection-molded components
Whisker             10 to 1000      Brake pads
Particulate         1 to 10         MMC brake discs
Nano (CNT, GNP)     < 1             Structural nano-enhanced polymers
```

### 2.1.3 By Fiber Type

| Fiber Type | Density (g/cm³) | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Young's Modulus (GPa) | Cost Index | Automotive Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| E-Glass | 2.55 | 2400 | 72 | 1× | Body panels, bumper beams |
| S-Glass | 2.49 | 3500 | 86 | 3× | High-stress structural |
| Carbon (PAN) | 1.78 | 4000 | 230 | 10× | Chassis, monocoque |
| Aramid (Kevlar 49) | 1.44 | 3000 | 125 | 8× | Crash structures |
| Flax (Natural) | 1.40 | 800 | 60 | 0.5× | Interior door panels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Specific Strength Argument:**
> Specific Strength = Strength / Density. Carbon fiber's specific strength is roughly **5× that of mild steel** and **2× that of aluminum**, which is the fundamental reason EV manufacturers (Tesla, BMW, Lucid) are aggressively adopting CFRP.

## 2.2 Composite Manufacturing Processes for Automotive Scale

**1. Hand Lay-up** — Lowest cost, lowest volume (e.g., prototype hoods)
**2. Resin Transfer Molding (RTM)** — Medium volume, Class-A finish (e.g., Audi A8 rear panel)
**3. Compression Molding (SMC/BMC)** — High volume, Sheet Molding Compound (e.g., bumper covers)
**4. Filament Winding** — Tubular parts (e.g., drive shafts, CNG tanks)
**5. Pultrusion** — Constant cross-section profiles
**6. Injection Molding (Short Fiber TP)** — Mass production (e.g., HVAC housings)
**7. Automated Fiber Placement (AFP)** — Aerospace-grade, slowly entering automotive

## 2.3 Major Vehicle Testing Protocols — Detailed Classification

### 2.3.1 Crash Testing Protocols

| Protocol | Region | Speed | Impact Angle | Tested Zones |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Euro NCAP** | Europe | 64 km/h | Frontal 50%, Side, Pole | Adult/Child/Pedestrian |
| **NHTSA** | USA | 56 km/h | Frontal 100% | Driver, Passenger |
| **IIHS** | USA | 64 km/h | Small overlap 25% | Driver-side |
| **AIS-098** | India (BNVSAP) | 64 km/h | Frontal ODB | Adult occupant |
| **FMVSS 208** | USA Federal | Varies | Multiple | Federal mandate |

> [!NOTE]
> **India's BNVSAP (Bharat New Vehicle Safety Assessment Program):**
> Launched in 2023 by MoRTH, aligned with Global NCAP protocols. Frontal impact test is **64 km/h** with offset deformable barrier (ODB), testing Adult Occupant Protection (AOP), Child Occupant Protection (COP), and Pedestrian Protection.

### 2.3.2 Emission Testing Protocols

| Cycle | Region | Duration | Speed Range | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NEDC** | Europe (legacy) | 1180 s | 0–120 km/h | BS-IV era reference |
| **WLTP** | Global | 1800 s | 0–131 km/h | BS-VI / Euro 6 |
| **FTP-75** | USA | 1877 s | 0–91 km/h | EPA certification |
| **IDC (Modified Indian Driving Cycle)** | India | — | — | BS-VI (now aligned to WLTP) |
| **JC08** | Japan | 1204 s | 0–81 km/h | Japan certification |

### 2.3.3 Homologation and Type Approval

Under the **Central Motor Vehicle Rules (CMVR) 1989**, every vehicle model must undergo:
1. **Type Approval** (Prototype testing)
2. **Conformity of Production (CoP)** (Production batch verification)
3. **Whole Vehicle Type Approval (WVTA)** — For completely built units (CBU)

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable under Module 4 of PEMUT526.**

| Formula | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rule of Mixtures — Density** | $\rho_c = V_f \cdot \rho_f + V_m \cdot \rho_m$ | $\rho$: density (kg/m³), $V$: volume fraction | Composite weight estimation |
| **Rule of Mixtures — Modulus (Longitudinal)** | $E_L = V_f \cdot E_f + V_m \cdot E_m$ | $E$: Young's modulus (GPa) | Stiffness in fiber direction |
| **Inverse Rule — Modulus (Transverse)** | $1/E_T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ | — | Stiffness perpendicular to fibers |
| **Tensile Strength (Longitudinal)** | $\sigma_L = V_f \cdot \sigma_{fu} + V_m \cdot \sigma_{mu}$ | $\sigma$: stress (MPa) | Strength in fiber direction |
| **Fiber Volume Fraction** | $V_f = \frac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}$ | $W$: weight, indices f = fiber, m = matrix | Composite recipe design |
| **Specific Strength** | $SS = \sigma_{uts} / \rho$ | MPa/(g/cm³) | Material selection for lightweighting |
| **Specific Modulus** | $SE = E / \rho$ | GPa/(g/cm³) | Stiffness-to-weight optimization |
| **Critical Fiber Length** | $L_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \cdot \tau_i}$ | $d$: fiber diameter, $\tau_i$: interfacial shear strength | Fiber-matrix load transfer |
| **Fiber Efficiency (Length)** | $\eta_L = 1 - L_c / (2L)$ | $L$: actual fiber length | Effectiveness of fiber reinforcement |
| **Crash Energy Absorption** | $E_{abs} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | $m$: mass, $v$: impact velocity | Crash pulse calculation |
| **Crash Force (Average)** | $F_{avg} = E_{abs} / d_c$ | $d_c$: crush distance | Crash structure design |
| **Specific Energy Absorption (SEA)** | $SEA = E_{abs} / m_{crush}$ | kJ/kg | Composite crashworthiness metric |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The longitudinal modulus formula assumes **isostrain** (Voigt model) — both fiber and matrix experience the same strain. The transverse modulus assumes **isostress** (Reuss model). Examiners frequently test whether you correctly identify which rule applies to which direction.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EV Battery Enclosures** (BMW iX, Tesla Model Y): Use CFRP-aluminum hybrid for crash energy management while protecting battery cells.
- **Composite Leaf Springs** (Mahindra Bolero, Tata Xenon): 60% weight reduction over steel, validated by **AIS 052** durability cycles (1 million cycles at rated load).
- **CNG Type-IV Tanks**: Carbon fiber over polymer liner, tested per **AIS-028** at **2.25× service pressure** (≈450 bar burst requirement).
- **Crash Boxes**: Glass-fiber/polypropylene composites tuned to crush progressively and absorb specific energy per **AIS-098** requirements.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rule of Mixtures for Longitudinal Modulus (Voigt Model)

**Problem Statement:**
Derive the expression for the longitudinal Young's modulus $E_L$ of a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite where fibers are aligned with the loading axis.

**Assumptions:**
1. Perfect bond between fiber and matrix (no slip at interface).
2. Fibers are continuous, parallel, and uniformly distributed.
3. Both phases experience the **same strain** (isostrain / Voigt condition).
4. Plane cross-sections remain plane after loading (Euler-Bernoulli assumption).

**Step 1 — Free Body Setup:**
Consider a representative volume element (RVE) of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A_c$. The fiber occupies area $A_f$ and the matrix occupies $A_m$.

$$
A_c = A_f + A_m
$$

**Step 2 — Isostrain Condition:**
By the plane-section assumption, the strain in the fiber and matrix is identical:

$$
\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon
$$

**Step 3 — Load Equilibration:**
The total load carried by the composite is the sum of loads carried by fiber and matrix (parallel loading):

$$
P_c = P_f + P_m
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Hooke's Law:**
Using $P = \sigma \cdot A = E \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A$:

$$
E_c \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A_c = E_f \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A_f + E_m \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A_m
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel Common Strain $\varepsilon$:**

$$
E_c \cdot A_c = E_f \cdot A_f + E_m \cdot A_m
$$

**Step 6 — Divide Both Sides by $A_c$:**

$$
E_c = E_f \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_c} + E_m \cdot \frac{A_m}{A_c}
$$

**Step 7 — Recognize Area Fractions = Volume Fractions:**
For uniform cross-section, area ratio equals volume ratio:

$$
\frac{A_f}{A_c} = V_f \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{A_m}{A_c} = V_m
$$

**Step 8 — Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{E_L = V_f \cdot E_f + V_m \cdot E_m}
$$

This is the **Voigt upper bound** of the composite modulus. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Inverse Rule of Mixtures (Reuss Model — Transverse Modulus)

**Problem Statement:**
Derive the transverse modulus $E_T$ of a unidirectional composite loaded perpendicular to the fiber axis.

**Step 1 — Isostress Assumption:**
Both fiber and matrix experience the **same stress** (Reuss model):

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma
$$

**Step 2 — Compatibility of Total Displacement:**
The total deformation is the sum of deformations in fiber and matrix (series loading perpendicular to fibers):

$$
\Delta L_c = \Delta L_f + \Delta L_m
$$

**Step 3 — Express in Terms of Strain and Lengths:**

$$
\varepsilon_c \cdot L_c = \varepsilon_f \cdot L_f + \varepsilon_m \cdot L_m
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Hooke's Law** $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$:

$$
\frac{\sigma}{E_T} \cdot L_c = \frac{\sigma}{E_f} \cdot L_f + \frac{\sigma}{E_m} \cdot L_m
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $\sigma$ and Divide by $L_c$:**

$$
\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{1}{E_f} \cdot \frac{L_f}{L_c} + \frac{1}{E_m} \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_c}
$$

**Step 6 — Length Ratios = Volume Fractions:**

$$
\frac{L_f}{L_c} = V_f \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{L_m}{L_c} = V_m
$$

**Step 7 — Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}}
$$

This is the **Reuss lower bound**, valid for transverse loading. ✓

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Specific Strength Calculation

**Problem:**
A carbon fiber/epoxy composite has the following properties:
- Fiber volume fraction: $V_f = 0.60$
- Matrix volume fraction: $V_m = 0.40$
- Carbon fiber density: $\rho_f = 1.78$ g/cm³
- Epoxy density: $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³
- Carbon fiber tensile strength: $\sigma_f = 4000$ MPa
- Epoxy tensile strength: $\sigma_m = 80$ MPa

**Calculate:**
(a) Composite density
(b) Composite longitudinal tensile strength
(c) Specific strength of composite vs. mild steel ($\rho_{steel} = 7.85$ g/cm³, $\sigma_{steel} = 450$ MPa)

**Step (a) — Composite Density:**

$$
\rho_c = V_f \cdot \rho_f + V_m \cdot \rho_m
$$

Substitute:

$$
\rho_c = (0.60)(1.78) + (0.40)(1.20)
$$

$$
\rho_c = 1.068 + 0.480
$$

$$
\boxed{\rho_c = 1.548 \text{ g/cm}^3}
$$

**Step (b) — Longitudinal Tensile Strength (Voigt assumption):**

$$
\sigma_L = V_f \cdot \sigma_f + V_m \cdot \sigma_m
$$

$$
\sigma_L = (0.60)(4000) + (0.40)(80)
$$

$$
\sigma_L = 2400 + 32
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_L = 2432 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**Step (c) — Specific Strength Comparison:**

For composite:

$$
SS_{comp} = \frac{2432}{1.548} = 1571 \text{ MPa} / (\text{g/cm}^3)
$$

For mild steel:

$$
SS_{steel} = \frac{450}{7.85} = 57.3 \text{ MPa} / (\text{g/cm}^3)
$$

Ratio:

$$
\frac{SS_{comp}}{SS_{steel}} = \frac{1571}{57.3} \approx 27.4
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Composite is } 27.4 \times \text{ stronger per unit mass than mild steel}}
$$

This is precisely why CFRP dominates aerospace and high-end EV applications.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Crash Energy & SEA

**Problem:**
A composite crash box of mass 4.5 kg must absorb 80 kJ of kinetic energy when a vehicle impacts a rigid barrier at 64 km/h. Calculate:
(a) Vehicle mass equivalent kinetic energy
(b) Required crush distance
(c) Specific Energy Absorption (SEA) of the composite

**Given:** Impact velocity $v = 64$ km/h $= 17.78$ m/s, crash box mass $m = 4.5$ kg.

**Step (a) — Kinetic Energy:**

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2
$$

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 4.5 \cdot (17.78)^2
$$

$$
E_k = 2.25 \cdot 316.13
$$

$$
\boxed{E_k \approx 711.3 \text{ J for the crash box alone}}
$$

**Step (b) — Required Crush Distance:**
To absorb 80 kJ from the vehicle (assume $m_{vehicle} = 1500$ kg, $E_k = 80$ kJ), for a crash box dissipating 50% of the energy:

$$
E_{box} = 40 \text{ kJ} = 40{,}000 \text{ J}
$$

For a target average crushing force of 150 kN:

$$
d_c = \frac{E_{abs}}{F_{avg}} = \frac{40{,}000}{150{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_c = 0.267 \text{ m} = 267 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step (c) — Specific Energy Absorption:**

$$
SEA = \frac{E_{abs}}{m_{crush}} = \frac{40{,}000}{4.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{SEA \approx 8889 \text{ J/kg} = 8.9 \text{ kJ/kg}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Benchmark:** Aluminum crash boxes typically achieve SEA ≈ 15–25 kJ/kg, while CFRP composites achieve 40–60 kJ/kg. This is the engineering basis for composite crash structures in Formula 1 and modern EV platforms.

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern, Module 4]**

---

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**Q: Differentiate between thermoset and thermoplastic matrix composites with suitable automotive examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Thermoset Matrix Composite | Thermoplastic Matrix Composite |
|---|---|---|
| Curing | Irreversible chemical cross-linking | Reversible melting/solidification |
| Recyclability | Not recyclable | Recyclable |
| Processing | Layup + RTM + Compression | Injection molding, thermoforming |
| Mechanical | High strength, brittle | Tough, impact-resistant |
| Examples | Epoxy/Carbon body panel, Polyester SMC bumper | PP-Glass fiber HVAC housing, PA66 bumper beam |
| Automotive Part | CFRP monocoque (BMW i3) | Engine covers, air intake manifolds |

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**Q: What is BNVSAP? List any four tests it mandates for new vehicles in India.**

**Model Answer:**

**Bharat New Vehicle Safety Assessment Program (BNVSAP)** is India's star-rating-based new car assessment program, launched by the Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH) and managed by the Global NCAP. It evaluates vehicles on Adult Occupant Protection (AOP), Child Occupant Protection (COP), Pedestrian Protection, and Safety Assist technologies.

**Four mandatory tests:**
1. **Frontal Impact Test** — 64 km/h, Offset Deformable Barrier (ODB) per AIS-098
2. **Side Impact Test** — 50 km/h, Mobile Deformable Barrier (MDB) per AIS-096
3. **Pedestrian Protection Test** — Per AIS-100 (leg form, upper leg form, head form)
4. **Electronic Stability Control (ESC) Test** — Per AIS-136

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam – Module 4 Pattern, 14-Mark Long Answer with Sub-Parts]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q (A): (a)** Explain the various types of fiber-reinforced composites used in automotive applications with a focus on carbon fiber and glass fiber systems. Discuss their manufacturing methods and specific applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A unidirectional glass fiber/epoxy composite has a fiber volume fraction of 0.55. Given: $E_{glass} = 72$ GPa, $E_{epoxy} = 3.5$ GPa, $\sigma_{glass} = 2400$ MPa, $\sigma_{epoxy} = 70$ MPa, $\rho_{glass} = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_{epoxy} = 1.20$ g/cm³. Calculate the longitudinal modulus, longitudinal strength, and density of the composite. Also compute the specific strength and compare it with aluminum ($\sigma_{Al} = 310$ MPa, $\rho_{Al} = 2.70$ g/cm³). **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Types of Fiber-Reinforced Composites in Automotive:**

**1. Glass Fiber Reinforced Polymer (GFRP):**
- Most common automotive composite; cost-effective.
- E-Glass: Standard electrical-grade, used for body panels, bumper beams.
- S-Glass: Higher strength, used in leaf springs (Mahindra), drive shafts.
- Manufacturing: Hand layup, SMC compression molding, RTM.
- Application: Bolero leaf spring (5.0 kg vs 18 kg steel — 72% weight reduction), SMC hoods, tailgates.

**2. Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer (CFRP):**
- Premium structural material, 5× stronger than steel by weight.
- PAN-based carbon fiber in epoxy matrix.
- Manufacturing: Prepreg layup + autoclave, Resin Transfer Molding (RTM), Automated Fiber Placement (AFP).
- Application: BMW i3 Life Module (passenger cell), Alfa Romeo 4C monocoque, Audi A8 rear panel, Formula 1 chassis.

**3. Aramid (Kevlar) Fiber Composites:**
- Excellent impact and ballistic resistance.
- Application: Bulletproof panels, crash structures in F1, brake pads.

**4. Natural Fiber Composites:**
- Flax, hemp, jute with PP/PET matrix.
- Application: Door panels (Mercedes E-Class), trunk linings, headliners.
- Benefit: 30% lighter than glass fiber, biodegradable, lower cost.

**Manufacturing Methods:**
- **Hand Layup:** Manual fiber placement + resin brush/roller — prototypes, low volume.
- **Resin Transfer Molding (RTM):** Closed mold, injected resin — medium volume, Class-A finish.
- **Compression Molding (SMC/BMC):** Pre-impregnated sheets compressed under heat — high volume, body panels.
- **Filament Winding:** Continuous fibers wound over rotating mandrel — CNG tanks, drive shafts.
- **Pultrusion:** Continuous pulling through die — constant profile parts.
- **Injection Molding (Short Fiber):** Mass production — underbody shields, engine covers.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data:**
$V_f = 0.55$, $V_m = 1 - 0.55 = 0.45$
$E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa
$\sigma_f = 2400$ MPa, $\sigma_m = 70$ MPa
$\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³

**Step 1 — Longitudinal Modulus (Voigt Rule):**

$$
E_L = V_f E_f + V_m E_m
$$

$$
E_L = (0.55)(72) + (0.45)(3.5)
$$

$$
E_L = 39.6 + 1.575 = 41.175 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Longitudinal Strength:**

$$
\sigma_L = V_f \sigma_f + V_m \sigma_m
$$

$$
\sigma_L = (0.55)(2400) + (0.45)(70)
$$

$$
\sigma_L = 1320 + 31.5 = 1351.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Composite Density:**

$$
\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m
$$

$$
\rho_c = (0.55)(2.55) + (0.45)(1.20)
$$

$$
\rho_c = 1.4025 + 0.54 = 1.9425 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Specific Strength of Composite:**

$$
SS_{comp} = \frac{1351.5}{1.9425} = 695.8 \text{ MPa} / (\text{g/cm}^3)
$$

**Step 5 — Specific Strength of Aluminum:**

$$
SS_{Al} = \frac{310}{2.70} = 114.8 \text{ MPa} / (\text{g/cm}^3)
$$

**Step 6 — Comparison Ratio:**

$$
\frac{SS_{comp}}{SS_{Al}} = \frac{695.8}{114.8} \approx 6.06
$$

**Conclusion:** The GFRP composite is approximately **6 times stronger per unit mass** than aluminum, justifying its selection in weight-critical automotive applications.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**Q (B): (a)** Describe in detail the major vehicle testing protocols with reference to Euro NCAP, BNVSAP, and AIS standards. Compare their frontal impact test conditions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle of mass 1500 kg impacts a rigid barrier at 64 km/h. Calculate: (i) the kinetic energy, (ii) the average crash force if the crush distance is 0.45 m, and (iii) comment on how a CFRP crash box with specific energy absorption of 50 kJ/kg would perform relative to a steel crash box of SEA = 20 kJ/kg if both are 5 kg. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Major Vehicle Testing Protocols:**

**1. Euro NCAP (European New Car Assessment Programme):**
- Established 1997, voluntary star-rating program.
- Tests: Adult Occupant Protection, Child Occupant Protection, Pedestrian Protection, Safety Assist.
- **Frontal Impact:** 64 km/h, 40% offset deformable barrier (ODB).
- **Side Impact:** 50 km/h, mobile deformable barrier.
- **Pole Side Impact:** 32 km/h.
- Result: 0 to 5-star rating.

**2. BNVSAP (Bharat New Vehicle Safety Assessment Program):**
- India's NCAP launched 2023, aligned with Global NCAP protocols.
- Managed by International Centre for Automotive Technology (ICAT) and ARAI.
- **Frontal Impact:** 64 km/h, ODB per AIS-098.
- Tests Adult Occupant Protection (AOP) and Child Occupant Protection (COP).
- Currently focuses on frontal; side impact and pedestrian tests to follow.

**3. AIS Standards (Automotive Industry Standards):**
- Issued by ARAI under CMVR.
- **AIS-098:** Frontal Occupant Protection (basis of BNVSAP frontal).
- **AIS-096:** Side Occupant Protection.
- **AIS-052:** Durability of composite leaf springs.
- **AIS-028:** CNG/LPG cylinder testing.
- **AIS-100:** Pedestrian Protection.
- **AIS-136:** Electronic Stability Control.
- **AIS-145:** EV-specific safety requirements (battery protection, electrical safety).

**Comparison — Frontal Impact Test Conditions:**

| Parameter | Euro NCAP | BNVSAP (AIS-098) | NHTSA (FMVSS 208) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed | 64 km/h | 64 km/h | 56 km/h |
| Barrier | 40% ODB | ODB (Al-to-Al) | Full-width rigid |
| Overlap | 40% | 40% offset | 100% |
| Dummy | Hybrid III 5th percentile female + 50th male | 50th percentile male | 50th percentile male |
| Rating | 5-Star | Star-rated | Pass/Fail |
| Mandate | Voluntary (market driven) | Voluntary (transitioning) | Federal mandate |

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $m = 1500$ kg, $v = 64$ km/h $= 64 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 17.78$ m/s, $d_c = 0.45$ m.

**Step (i) — Kinetic Energy:**

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2
$$

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (17.78)^2
$$

$$
E_k = 750 \times 316.13
$$

$$
\boxed{E_k \approx 237{,}100 \text{ J} = 237.1 \text{ kJ}}
$$

**Step (ii) — Average Crash Force:**

$$
F_{avg} = \frac{E_k}{d_c} = \frac{237{,}100}{0.45}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{avg} \approx 526{,}889 \text{ N} \approx 527 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step (iii) — Crash Box Performance Comparison:**

For a 5 kg crash box:

**CFRP Crash Box:**

$$
E_{CFRP} = SEA_{CFRP} \times m = 50 \text{ kJ/kg} \times 5 \text{ kg} = 250 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Steel Crash Box:**

$$
E_{steel} = SEA_{steel} \times m = 20 \text{ kJ/kg} \times 5 \text{ kg} = 100 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Energy Absorption Comparison:**

$$
\frac{E_{CFRP}}{E_{steel}} = \frac{250}{100} = 2.5
$$

The CFRP crash box absorbs **2.5 times more energy** than the steel crash box of the same mass. This means:
- The CFRP version can absorb the entire 237.1 kJ with a small mass addition.
- The steel version would require **2.5× the mass** (12.5 kg) to match CFRP performance.
- This translates to a **7.5 kg weight savings** per vehicle for equivalent crashworthiness — a major advantage in EV range optimization.

**Design Implication:** A CFRP crash box of 4.75 kg would suffice to absorb the full 237.1 kJ crash energy, reducing vehicle mass by 0.25 kg per box and providing a 2.5× safety margin.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up Voigt vs Reuss rules:** Examiners strictly check whether you applied the correct model for longitudinal ($E_L = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$) versus transverse ($\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$) modulus. Conflating them costs **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversion:** Speed given in km/h MUST be converted to m/s by multiplying by $\frac{5}{18}$ or $\frac{1000}{3600}$. Skipping this step leads to wrong answers and a full **mark penalty**.
> 3. **Not specifying fiber direction:** A unidirectional composite has different properties in different directions. Always state "longitudinal" or "transverse" when applying the rule of mixtures.
> 4. **Confusing SEA with total energy:** Specific Energy Absorption is per unit mass. Multiplying by mass gives total energy absorbed — students often mix these up.
> 5. **In AIS vs BNVSAP confusion:** AIS is a standard document, BNVSAP is the assessment program that USES certain AIS standards. Examiners test this distinction.
> 6. **Skipping the regulatory body mention:** Always cite the regulatory body (e.g., "As per AIS-098 issued by ARAI...") for full credit on safety questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4**

### **Composites Core Concepts:**
- ✅ Composite = Reinforcement (load carrier) + Matrix (binder/protector) + Interface (load transfer)
- ✅ Rule of Mixtures: Longitudinal → $E_L = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$ (Voigt/isostrain)
- ✅ Rule of Mixtures: Transverse → $\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$ (Reuss/isostress)
- ✅ Composite Density: $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$
- ✅ Tensile Strength: $\sigma_L = V_f \sigma_f + V_m \sigma_m$
- ✅ Specific Strength = Strength / Density (key lightweighting metric)
- ✅ Critical Fiber Length: $L_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} d}{2 \tau_i}$ — determines if fibers will fail or pull out
- ✅ Carbon Fiber: 5× stronger than steel by weight; 10× costlier
- ✅ Glass Fiber: Most common automotive composite; cost-effective
- ✅ Aramid: Used for impact/ballistic protection
- ✅ Natural Fibers: Flax, hemp — eco-friendly interior applications

### **Testing Protocol Core Concepts:**
- ✅ **CMVR 1989** — Central Motor Vehicle Rules (umbrella Indian law)
- ✅ **AIS** — Automotive Industry Standards (issued by ARAI)
- ✅ **BNVSAP** — India's NCAP (frontal 64 km/h ODB, AIS-098)
- ✅ **Euro NCAP** — Europe's 5-star program (64 km/h, 40% ODB)
- ✅ **NHTSA** — USA federal mandate (56 km/h full-width)
- ✅ **WLTP** — Global emission test cycle (BS-VI aligned)
- ✅ **Crash Energy:** $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$
- ✅ **Average Crash Force:** $F_{avg} = E_{abs} / d_c$
- ✅ **Specific Energy Absorption (SEA):** Material-level crashworthiness metric in kJ/kg
- ✅ **AIS-052** — Composite leaf spring durability (1 million cycle requirement)
- ✅ **AIS-028** — CNG cylinder testing (2.25× service pressure burst)
- ✅ **AIS-098** — Frontal occupant protection
- ✅ **AIS-100** — Pedestrian protection
- ✅ **AIS-136** — Electronic Stability Control (ESC)
- ✅ **FMVSS 302** — Interior material flammability

### **Critical Standards Body Acronyms:**
- ✅ ARAI — Automotive Research Association of India
- ✅ ICAT — International Centre for Automotive Technology
- ✅ MoRTH — Ministry of Road Transport and Highways
- ✅ BIS — Bureau of Indian Standards
- ✅ UNECE/WP.29 — United Nations World Forum for Harmonization
- ✅ BNVSAP — Bharat New Vehicle Safety Assessment Program

### **Real-World Application Anchors (Memorize for Exam):**
- ✅ BMW i3 → CFRP Life Module passenger cell
- ✅ Mahindra Bolero → GFRP composite leaf spring (60% lighter than steel)
- ✅ Tesla Model Y → Composite underbody shield + battery enclosure
- ✅ Formula 1 → CFRP monocoque chassis + aramid crash structure
- ✅ Type-IV CNG tanks → Carbon fiber over polymer liner (AIS-028)

### **Quick Comparative Memory Aid:**
- **Steel:** $\rho \approx 7.85$ g/cm³, $\sigma \approx 450$ MPa
- **Aluminum:** $\rho \approx 2.70$ g/cm³, $\sigma \approx 310$ MPa
- **CFRP:** $\rho \approx 1.55$–$1.60$ g/cm³, $\sigma \approx 1500$–$2500$ MPa
- **GFRP:** $\rho \approx 1.90$–$2.00$ g/cm³, $\sigma \approx 1000$–$1500$ MPa

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always frame your answers in terms of **regulatory compliance** (e.g., "As per AIS-098...") — this is what differentiates a KTU topper's answer from an average one. Examiners award bonus marks for **standardization awareness**.
